===== PAGE 15 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 15 
1. 
Introduction 
 
SPx is a modular software framework for the receipt, processing and display of 
primary radar video. It comprises a set of software libraries, servers, utilities and 
client applications that provide solutions and tools for radar processing and display 
under Windows or Linux. 
 
SPx is designed to be flexible to accommodate different system architectures and 
operating systems. It supports high-performance client-server configurations in which 
the processing is split from the display across a network, and it supports low-cost, 
integrated solutions in which acquisition through to display is implemented on a single 
platform, which may range from a basic laptop through to a multi-processor system. 
 
 
For client-server configurations, SPx supports a number of options for the split 
between the server and the client. The server can be responsible for radar acquisition 
and network distribution, leaving the client to perform the scan conversion and display 
processing, or else the scan conversion itself can be handled in the server leaving the 
client to handle just the display rendering. 
 
The capabilities of the SPx software are made available in self-contained C++ classes. 
These classes can be incorporated into server or client applications using the 
capabilities as required. The SPx framework is also designed to support the addition of 
user-supplied capabilities, so if a new processing function is needed that is not in the 
SPx library, it can be easily incorporated into the processing chain. In this way, SPx 
provides a framework that can be employed in an open-architecture computing 
framework. 


===== PAGE 16 =====
Page 16 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The SPx product family also includes complete ready-to-run server and client 
applications including SPxServer for radar distribution and tracking, and RadarView for 
Windows-based radar display capability. 
 
Some sample applications built using SPx are shown in the screen shots below: 
 
Figure 1 - SPx Server application 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPx Server is a complete server application built from the SPx 
library to provide radar capture, processing, plot extraction, tracking 
and recording. SPx Server is a standard product supplied by 
Cambridge Pixel for Linux or Windows. Customers can build similar 
server applications from the SPx library or use the standard SPx 
Server product if the need is for a ready-to-run radar video 
processor. 


===== PAGE 17 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 17 
 
Figure 2 -The SPx library supports multiple colour radar 
 
 
Figure 3 – SPx-Frame-X sample application 
 
 
Figure 4 – A-Scan Video Display 
Radar Video may be presented 
in PPI, B-Scan and A-Scan 
display formats. Shown here is 
the A-Scan format, which 
shows radar video amplitude 
as a function of time (range). 
The A-Scan display component 
is a standard Windows or 
Linux class that may be 
included in a custom 
application. The display 
updates in real-time to show 
the incoming radar video, 
optionally with persistence 
(fading) of old video.

1.1. SPx Overview 
 
The SPx software offers: 
 
 
Server applications. These servers support radar video capture, 
distribution and tracking. An SPx Server runs under Linux or Windows and is 
supplied as a complete ready-to-run application program. License options 
enable the software to provide capabilities from radar video distribution, 
recording, plot extraction and target tracking. The software runs on industry-
standard computing hardware from PCs to VME or cPCI configurations. 
 
 
Client applications. An SPx client application provides a complete ready-to-
run application program. The RadarView application, for example, is a 
Windows-based application that displays radar video from a network source 
or through local signals using a HPx radar acquisition card. 
 
 
Development Toolkit. The SPx library offers a toolkit of components for 
radar video processing and scan conversion. These software components can 
be incorporated into custom Windows or Linux applications to provide radar 
scan conversion, network distribution, recording or processing. 
 
 
The SPx library allows for 
the simultaneous display of 
raw and processed video, 
here shown in different 
colours. Each radar video is 
a separate layer in the 
display. Only the resources 
of the host computer limits 
the number of radar layers 
that can be displayed. 


===== PAGE 20 =====
Page 20 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 8 - SPx Software 
 
As shown in Figure 8, the SPx software may be used as pre-built server and client 
applications (which are built using the SPx libraries), or else may be custom 
applications written to use the SPx Library functions. These may be mixed, so that a 
standard SPx Server application, for example, provides radar video to a customised 
client application. 
 
This manual describes the SPx library software. If you are using standard SPx server 
or client applications then this manual is generally not relevant, and separate user 
manuals for these applications are available. This manual is relevant for programmers 
developing customised server or client applications using the SPx library. 
 
1.2. Key Concepts 
 
This manual is primarily concerned with the use of SPx by a software developer. When 
using standard SPx applications such as SPx Server and RadarView, user manuals for 
those products are applicable. 
 
An SPx application generally comprises one or more radar sources, processing and 
display modules. These may be configured as part of a server or client application.

The SPx library presents a set of C++ classes. These may be used by an application 
developer working in Linux or Windows to add radar processing capabilities into a 
custom application. For example, radar scan conversion can be added into an existing 
application program by using the SPx class libraries. Similarly, a radar server may be 
constructed using the SPx classes to provide the core capabilities of radar receipt, 
processing, compression and distribution, whilst leaving the customised application 
code to implement all the peripheral, management and control functions of the server. 
The SPx library is designed to provide core capabilities, leaving the application 
developer to focus on the custom requirements of the application. 
 
1.3. Terminology 
 
Radar video is derived as a sequence of returns, sometimes called spokes, which 
contains a set of samples of video intensity as a function of range for a specific 
azimuth. The radar may rotate or scan, and in so-doing creates a sequence of returns. 
In the situation of a search radar, for example, the rotating radar creates returns at a 
rate defined by the pulse repetition frequency (PRF) as the rotation of the radar 
increases the azimuth from 0 to 360 degrees. 
 
ACP/ARP 
 
The azimuth (angle) of the radar for each return may be provided using a set of 
pulses called the ACP and ARP. The ACP (Azimuth Change Pulse) is a pulse train 
where the interval between pulses is a fixed angle. Commonly there are 2048, 4096 
or 8192 pulses per 360 degrees. The ARP (Azimuth Reset Pulse) is a reset pulse that 
occurs at a reference point. The angle of the radar is therefore derived by counting 
the number of ACP pulses since the last ARP pulse. The ACP count is not related to the 
radar’s PRF. 


===== PAGE 22 =====
Page 22 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
A-Scan (A-Scope) 
 
A-Scan view of radar video is traditionally associated with an oscilloscope display. It 
shows signal intensity on the vertical axis and range (time) along the horizontal axis. 
The trace is updated for each azimuth at the pulse rate of the radar. 
 
Azimuth 
 
The azimuth is the angle that the radar is pointing, defined relative to a reference 
such as North. In SPx terms, the azimuth is a number in the range 0 .. 65536, which 
represents an angle in the range 0 to 360 degrees. 
 
Azimuth Correlation 
 
If there are more input returns than required for display or processing, or if some 
degree of processing is required for filtering, then azimuth correlation may be 
employed. The process combines a number of input returns for each output using 
either a peak-picking, averaging or other method of combination. 
 
B-Scan (B-Scope) 
 
B-Scan is a view of radar video that shows azimuth along the horizontal axis and 
range along the vertical axis. It is commonly seen in fire control radars. 
 
Compositing 
 
Compositing is the process by which image are built up from a number of layers. The 
compositing operators support overlay and blending of layers. The seminal work on 
the subject is a paper by Porter and Duff. 
 
Compression 
 
Radar video may be compressed for the purposes of storage or distribution on a 
network. Strictly speaking, the radar is subject to a process of coding, which is chosen 
to achieve a degree of compression. The coding process may be lossy or lossless, and 
the degree of compression is affected by the level of noise in the signal. 
 
Fast Time Constant (FTC) 
 
An FTC operation can be applied to a radar video signal to remove low frequency 
components, for example due to weather effects. 
 
The FTC processing will filter these low frequency components, so that only pulses 
that rise and fall quickly will be displayed. In technical terms, FTC is a differentiator. 
 
PPI 
 
The Plan Position Indicator is the view of radar video that shows the radar as it would 
appear on a plan view, which is a polar coordinate display of the area surrounding the 
radar platform. The radar position is represented as the origin of the sweep, which is 
normally located in the centre of the scope, but may be offset from the centre.  
 
 
 


===== PAGE 23 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 23 
Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) 
 
The PRF is the rate at which the radar generates new returns. In a pulse radar it is the 
frequency of transmission of new pulses. The maximum number of pulses per second 
is related to the range of radar, since time must be allowed for the pulse to get to 
target and back before sending the next one. 
 
Range Correlation 
 
If there are more samples per return than required for display or processing, then 
range correlation may be used to affect a reduction. A number of range samples are 
combined using either a peak-picking, averaging or other method of combination. 
 
Range resolution 
 
The ability of the radar to discriminate two targets that are closely spaced in range. 
For example, a range resolution of 10 metres means that two targets that are on the 
same azimuth and 10 metres apart in range can be resolved. 
 
Range sampling 
 
Radar video provided as an analogue signal is sampled at discrete intervals. The 
frequency of sampling is limited by the capture hardware and is ideally chosen to 
ensure that the full bandwidth of the radar video is captured. For example, a radar 
video signal that is bandwidth limited to B Hz can be fully reproduced by sampling at 
2B Hz. Oversampling the video produces no new information and only serves to 
overload the processing activities. 
 
Scan Rate/period 
 
The rotation rate/period of a search radar, for example as the time between two North 
crossings. 
 
Sector Blanking 
 
Sector blanking is the process by which an interval of ranges of azimuth values are 
blanked, meaning that the video is forced to value 0. This might be used to suppress 
video over the superstructure of a ship, for example. 
 
Sparse azimuths 
 
If the number of returns being generated by the radar is less than a pre-defined store 
dimension, then the radar can be said to be generating sparse azimuths. For example, 
if a store dimension is set of 2000 but the radar only generates 500 returns per scan, 
then only 1 in 4 of the azimuths in the store is populated. Special processing can be 
invoked to fill-in the sparse azimuths. 
 
Random scan 
 
If the radar can switch azimuths, for example because it is electronically rather than 
mechanically steered, then it can generate azimuth numbers that are not necessarily 
increasing steadily over time. In general, such a radar can produce any arbitrary 
azimuth sequence and the display is expected to show this effect. In this situation, the 
radar is said to be working in random scan mode. 
 


===== PAGE 24 =====
Page 24 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Sector scan 
 
In the situation where the radar is scanning between a start and stop azimuth, rather 
than a continuous 360 degree scan, the radar is said to be operating in sector-scan 
mode. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 25 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 25 
2. 
SPx Overview 
 
SPx is designed to solve a wide range of radar processing problems. This section is 
aimed at providing the general processing sequence and not all stages may be 
relevant for a given application. 
 
The general sequence of activities supported by SPx is receive, process and display. In 
SPx terminology, the different software components are called objects, which are 
provided as reusable C++ classes. 
 
2.1. SPx Objects 
 
SPx objects exist in a hierarchy with SPxObj at the root. The Test Generator class, for 
example, is a type of Radar Source class. Other Radar Sources include the Scenario 
Generator, Network Interface and File Replay. These different types of radar source 
are said to be derived from, or inherit from, the Radar Source parent class. The class 
relationships in Figure 9 show the SPxRadarSource class and its sub-classes. 
 
Figure 9 - Class Relationships for SPxRadarSource 
 
When objects are organised in this way it is possible to use a Radar Source object as a 
collective term of any of the objects that derive from it. So if another object takes 
data from a Radar Source, it really means that it can take data from any class that is 
derived from Radar Source. 
 
The inheritance link is just one kind of relationship between SPx objects. Another 
relationship is based on data flow. For example, all Radar Source objects can pass 
data to a Radar Interface Buffer, or a RIB, object. 
 
The RIB, represented by the class SPxRIB, provides a buffer that allows a radar source 
to write video independently of an object reading data. 
 
 
 
SPxRadarSource 
SPxTestGenerator 
SPxScenarioGenerator 
SPxRadarReplay 
SPxNetworkReceive 
SPxHPx100Source 
SPxObj 


===== PAGE 26 =====
Page 26 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Radar video is removed from the RIB into a Polar Interface Module (PIM). Any number 
of PIMs are connected to the RIB, and each is responsible for formatting the radar 
video to the size and structure required by the downstream processing. The PIM 
specifies how it wants the data formatted (number of azimuths, number of samples 
per return, type of data reduction etc.) and the PIM is responsible for formatting and 
reporting the availability of new data to downstream processes. 
 
When the PIM receives and processes new video it reports that to one or more 
processes that can be executed to further process the video. 
 
SPx supports different forms of radar scan conversion: PPI, A-Scan and B-Scan. The 
scan conversion process is split between data processing and a display module, 
allowing the two activities to run on the same hardware, or across a network link. 
 
An overview of the SPx classes is shown in Figure 10. A set of connected SPx software 
objects are shown in Figure 11. The diagram shows the data flows between the 
software objects, and also indicates the processing threads that each activity runs in.

2.2. Scan conversion Configurations 
 
SPx supports a number of configurations for scan conversion: 
 
The integrated scan converter has the scan conversion running on the same machine 
as part of the same Windows/Linux process. The client application uses the SPx C++ 
class library to add scan conversion capabilities into the process. 
 
The RDC (Radar Display Coprocess) approach uses a separate process to handle the 
scan converter. The client application communicates to the RDC using a socket 
interface. The RDC runs on the same processor as the client application, but it runs in 
a different process. The RDC is described in the “Radar Display Coprocess User 
Manual”. 
 
The distributed scan converter has the scan converter running on different machines 
separated by a network. The remote scan converter delivers regular updates of scan 
converted data which are displayed in the client. The scan converter in the server is 
fully controlled by the client application, so that there is a one-to-one correspondence 
between the scan conversion server and a client application that uses it. 
 
The RIS (Radar Image Server) approach has the scan converter running on a different 
machine as above. This time, however, the RIS distributes scan conversion images to 
client applications. The client does not control the server, but just receives fixed radar 
views that the server delivers. A RIS can send data to any number of clients and no 
client licenses are needed to display the data.

PIM 
Display 
Window 
Scenario 
Generator

Generator

SPxLib Programming Manual

2.3. An Overview of Scan Conversion Classes 
 
The SPx class SPxScSourceLocal provides a PPI or B-Scan radar scan converter. It 
accepts a source of polar-format radar video and creates a PPI or B-Scan view. The 
output of the class is a set of small patches, a set of which creates an updating sector 
of radar video. 
 
The scan converter class is normally used with a display class that takes the scan 
converted data and either presents it as a bitmap to an application 
(SPxScDestBitmap) , or else handles the graphical display itself 
(SPxScDestDisplayWin for Windows and SPxPPIwindow for X Windows). Alternatively, 
the scan converted bitmap can be moved across a network to a display elsewhere. In 
this case, the output of SPxScSourceLocal is connected to SPxScDestNet, which 
handles the networking. 
 
SPx offers considerable flexibility in system architecture, with the integrated solution 
supporting scan conversion and display on a single machine, and the distributed 
solution separating the scan conversion and display across a network. 
 
Under Linux, the option exists to use the SPx Radar Display Coprocess (RDC). This is 
a Linux process that handles the radar acquisition and scan conversion. The process 
requires commands through a socket-based interface from the application and inserts 
the scan converted video into a nominated X Window identified by the application. The 
RDC offers the benefit of completely de-coupling the application from the scan 
converter: they can even run on separate processing cards with a network interface 
for communication. The only constraint is that the RDC must run on the same 
processor as the X Server. The RDC is fully described in “Radar Display Coprocess 
User Manual”. 
 
2.4. Image Compositing 
 
A key concept in displaying radar video is compositing. Compositing is the process of 
combining layers of graphics information. The basic mathematics of compositing allow 
images to be combined with logical operations and blending. For example, The Over 
operator allows data from one layer to be layered above data from a lower layer. In 
this way scenes can be built up by displaying the layers in sequence starting from the 
lowest and finishing with the topmost.  
 
The Alpha operator allows a layer to be partially transparent so that the layers 
underneath are partially visible. The degree of transparency is controlled by the alpha 
value, which is set for each pixel.  
 
SPx can use compositing capabilities of X Windows and Microsoft Windows to combine 
multiple images into a single display. These functions support the ability to combine 
layers using both the Over and Alpha operations, therefore providing a capability to 
implement a multi-layer display. 
 
There are a number of approaches to compositing that SPx applications can use: 
 
Application Compositing – In application compositing, the application (with the help 
of the SPx library) is responsible for combining graphics. The inputs to the 
compositing process can be either Pixmaps (Application Pixmap Compositing) or 
Windows (Application Window Compositing). 


===== PAGE 29 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 29 
Window Manager Compositing – In window manager compositing, the application 
leaves the task of image combination to the window system. The application presents 
a set of stacked windows that may have alpha transparency and the window manager 
combines the window contents on to the display. Microsoft Windows has built-in 
capabilities for this form of window compositing. For X11, the compositing function is 
performed by a compositing window manager. The recommended window manager 
for X11 is Compiz. 
 
2.4.1. 
Example Image Compositing 
 
To illustrate the compositing process, we consider the presentation of radar video with 
an underlay map and overlay symbology. This is shown in Figure 13. Firstly the map 
comprises a set of layers that, when displayed in the correct sequence, will form a 
chart containing different thematic layers. For example, the image shown in (a) is an 
S57 chart that comprises 62 layers. In (b) there is a scan converted radar image, and 
in (c) an overlay image. We require the data to be combined in a manner that 
preserves the display of the chart underlay through the radar video, and presents the 
overlay graphics as opaque to both the radar video and underlay. As illustrated, the 
overlay graphics image contains two layers, comprising range rings and a grey mask. 
 
The construction of the desired picture requires two compositing operations. Firstly 
the underlay image is combined with the radar image to form the combined 
underlay+radar as shown in (d). This is a blending process that provides a semi-
transparent mix of the two pictures. Where the radar image is black, there is no effect 
on the underlay, but for non-black pixels the effect is to combine the corresponding 
pixels in the underlay and radar to form a new colour that is, simplistically, a mix of 
the two. An alpha value is derived from the colour component of the radar and used 
to combine the RGB colour values of the underlay and the radar. 
 
It is important to note that this calculation is carried out for each pixel, and alpha will, 
in general, have a different value at each pixel. A simple way of calculating alpha for a 
pixel is from the modulus of the radar colour, i.e. the normalised radar intensity at 
that point. For a radar intensity of 0, alpha will be 0 so the combination yields only the 
Underlay. As the radar intensity increases, the radar component in the combination 
progressively increases at the expense of the Underlay component, which decreases. 
 
The second compositing operation is the addition of the overlay image. This is an Over 
function with a transparent background. Where the overlay is transparent (appears as 
black in (c)), the combination shows the radar+underlay mix. Where the overlay is a 
non-transparent pixel (i.e. a range ring or mask) then that pixel colour is visible and 
the radar+underlay is masked. Figure (e) shows the final composited image. 
 
2.4.2. 
Two Layer Compositing 
 
Whereas the example shown in Figure 13 shows the construction of a multi-layer 
image with separate underlays and overlays to the radar video, the same effect can 
also be achieved with a single layer of graphics. This time, the alpha component of the 
colour of the single layer of graphics can be used to define the positioning (underlay 
or overlay) of that colour with respect to the radar. 
For example, in Figure 12 a raster satellite map is being overlaid with a real-time, 
rotating radar image. The two applications generating the graphics and the radar are 
quite independent. 


===== PAGE 30 =====
Page 30 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 12 - Radar image overlaid on a raster map, as a single layer composite. 
 


===== PAGE 31 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 31 
 
Figure 13 - Compositing on an underlay + radar + overlay scene 
 
 
(a) Underlay Scene – layers of an 
S57 chart 
(c) Overlay Scene 
(d) Composition of underlay and 
radar with alpha-blending 
(b) Scan Converted Radar Picture 
(e) Composition of overlay with underlay and 
radar with Over function 


===== PAGE 32 =====
Page 32 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
2.4.3. 
Implementation of Image Compositing 
 
Although the implementation of image compositing under Microsoft Windows and X11 
differs in its details, the principles are similar. In terms of the example of Figure 13, 
the underlay, radar and overlay may be created as separate windows (Microsoft 
windows or X11 windows, but we’ll use the term window in this section to mean 
either). 
 
The windows can be stacked so that the Underlay is below the radar and the Overlay 
is above both. Again, library software can be used to enforce this stacking order. With 
the Windows of the same size, located at the same location and maintained in the 
right stacking order we can use the capabilities of the graphics library to implement 
the compositing functions identified in the previous section. 
 
Additionally, the same effect of positioning some graphics as an underlay to the radar 
and some graphics as an overlay to the radar may be achieved using a single layer of 
graphics. In this mode, the 32-bit value of the graphics (ARGB) is used to control the 
mixing with the radar. 
 
It should be noted that the construction of the composited scene, for example (e) of 
Figure 13, needs to be performed at a rate that is high enough to capture the real-
time changes in the presentation of the data. For the radar sweep, this will imply a 
rate of 10-50 times per second, depending on various parameters. The whole of the 
compositing process needs to be repeated at this rate, and for large window sizes, this 
can imply processing rates of several hundred Mpixels per second, a requirement that 
becomes feasible with modern graphics architectures that support hardware 
accelerated alpha-blending and high-speed copies. 
 
2.5. Programming an SPx Application 
 
SPx is supplied as a set of include files and C++ class libraries with associated C 
functions. A development release comprises: 
 
 
Include files 
 
Libraries 
 
Example code 
 
Pre-built application servers 
 
Demonstration programs 
 
The libraries supplied may be for Windows or Linux, with other operating systems on 
request. 
 
You develop your application by including the SPx classes to provide the capabilities 
for radar processing and display. The SPx framework is open and extensible, so you 
can add your own processing modules and access the data at any stage of the 
processing. 
 
When you have developed your application and linked with the SPx libraries, the 
executable program runs as a normal C/C++ program. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 33 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 33 
SPx is licensed through a developer licence, which supports the process of writing the 
application and includes support from Cambridge Pixel, and run-time licenses for the 
deployed modules. A dongle may be required in your deployed system to enable the 
software to run correctly. 
 
2.6. An Overview of Major SPx Classes 
 
The major C++ classes that SPx uses are as follows: 
 
SPxRIB 
 
A RIB is a Radar Input Buffer. It allows data to be written and read from different 
activities (specifically different threads of execution) and provides the buffering and 
synchronisation primitives to support this. 
 
SPxPIM 
 
The SPx PIM is the Polar Interface Module. It provides a store for radar returns and 
supports range and azimuth correlation to reduce the incoming data to a desired 
format. Many processing objects take their input from a PIM and provide their output 
to either the same or a different PIM. Under normal circumstances, data is copied into 
a PIM from a RIB, so a PIM is constructed by providing the RIB as an input argument. 
 
SPxRadarSource 
 
The SPxRadarSource object provides the root object class for a number of radar input 
sources, as already illustrated in Figure 9. A radar source provides its data into a RIB 
object, so a radar source is normally constructed by providing the associated RIB. 
 
SPxProcess 
 
An SPxProcess is an activity that manipulates data. The library contains a number of 
built-in processing options, such as thresholding, scan-to-scan integration, FTC etc. 
Additionally, new processes can be created and incorporated into the processing 
chain. 
 
SPxSc 
 
The SPxSc class is the root of a set of classes that handle PPI radar scan conversion. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 34 =====
Page 34 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 35 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 35 
3. 
Radar Sources 
 
SPx supports a number of classes to acquire radar video from different sources: 
 
 
Video from an HPx radar interface card 
 
Video from network 
 
Video from a test generator 
 
Video from a scenario generator 
 
Video from file replay 
 
Video from a user defined source 
 
The class SPxRadarSource is the parent class of all radar sources. 
 
3.1. Radar Video Format 
 
The source objects are responsible for providing radar data in SPx format so that the 
rest of the SPx processing objects can work on it. The source object may generate 
data in the correct format, or otherwise converts data from the input format to the 
standard SPx format.

3.1.1. 
Radar Video Samples  
 
The radar video samples stored in each SPx return may be raw or compressed. The 
actual format of the data stored is defined by the Packing Type in the data structure 
and the Data Flags (SPx V1.43 and later) – refer to Table 1. 
 
The Packing Type is set to one of the following values. With the exception of 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_ORC (10) and SPX_RIB_PACKING_ZLIB (20), all packing types 
define the format of the uncompressed data (see Note 1 below): 
 
Value Name 
Description 
0 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8 
8-bits per sample, data is packed as one sample 
per byte. 
1 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW4 
4-bits per sample, data is packed as two samples 
per byte, with the first sample being in the lower 
four bits.  
This packing type is included for reference only 
and is not currently supported. 
2 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW2 
2-bits per sample, data is packed as four samples 
per byte, with the first sample being in the lower 
two bits.  
This packing type is included for reference only 
and is not currently supported. 
3 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW1 
1-bit per sample, data is packed as eight samples 
per byte, with the first sample being in the lowest 
bit.  
This packing type is included for reference only 
and is not currently supported. 
4 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW4_1111 
4+1+1+1+1 bits per sample. The top 4-bits 
contain the sample value. The lower four bits are 
digital/control bits. 
5 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW5_111 
5+1+1+1 bits per sample. The top 5-bits contain 
the sample value. The lower three bits are 
digital/control bits. 
6 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW6_11 
6+1+1 bits per sample. The top 6-bits contain the 
sample value. The lower two bits are 
digital/control bits. 
7 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW7_1 
7+1 bits per sample. The top 7-bits contain the 
sample value. The lowest bit is a digital/control 
bit. 
8 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW16 
16-bits per sample. Byte ordering is defined by 
the Data Flags field.  
This packing type is included for reference only 
and is not currently supported. 
10 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_ORC 
ORC compressed SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8 data. 
Refer to Note 1 below. 
20 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_ZLIB 
ZLIB compressed SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8 data. 
Refer to Note 1 below.

Description 
30

12-bits per sample packed into two bytes with 
sample value contained in the top 12-bits. The 
lower four bits are unused and set to zero. Byte 
ordering is defined by the Data Flags field. 
31 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW12_1111 
12+1+1+1+1 bits per sample. The top 12-bits

The Data Flags field contains the following bits and subfields: 
 
Bits 
Name 
Description 
0 
SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAG_BIG_ENDIAN (0x01) 
Defines the byte ordering of 
uncompressed multi-byte samples. 
This bit is set if multi-byte samples 
are in big-endian byte order and not 
set if samples are in little-endian 
byte order. 
2:1 
SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAG_COMPRESSED_ORC (0x02) 
SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAG_COMPRESSED_ZLIB 
(0x04) 
Reserved (0x06) 
Data compression type. If the data 
is uncompressed then this field is 
set to 0, otherwise it is set to 
indicate ORC compression or ZLIB 
compression. Refer to Note 1 below. 
4 
SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAG_UPSIDE_DOWN 
This flag is set if the radar is 
mounted upside down, so appears 
to rotate anti-clockwise if viewed 
from above. Various SPx processes 
are sensitive to the setting of this 
flag to control the radar processing.  
 
Note 1: The Data Flags field was introduced in V1.43 of the SPx library. Before V1.43, 
the data compression type was defined by the Packing Type field. V1.43 of the library 
and later uses bits set in the Data Flags field to define the data compression type and 
the Packing Type field to define the format of the uncompressed data. To ensure 
backward compatibility, the Packing Type field is set to SPX_RIB_PACKING_ORC (10) 
for ORC compressed 8-bit samples and SPX_RIB_PACKING_ZLIB (20) for ZLIB 
compressed 8-bit samples. This is in addition to the compression bits set in the Data 
Flags field. For both of these compressed packing types, it may be assumed that the 
packing type of the uncompressed data is SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8 (0). 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 40 =====
Page 40 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.1.2. 
ORC Compression Standard 
 
ORC (Open Radar Coding) format is a coding algorithm for radar video that provides a 
good level of compression, yet consumes relatively little CPU resource in both the 
compression and decompression.  
 
Although an ORC compressed data stream is generally never larger than the original 
raw data stream, the degree of compression achieved depends on the nature of the 
data being compressed. ORC is a lossless coding format that achieves compression by 
reducing the number of bits used to represent the video values. It does this by 
dividing the return into a number of 1-dimensional windows and calculates the 
distribution of video in each window. Typically, even though the video may be 
nominally 8-bits, local regions use far fewer levels than the maximum permits. ORC 
exploits this by replacing the data with values at a lower bit resolution and adding a 
look-up table that can be used to return the data to the original values on the 
decompression side. For example, if examination of a window of nominally 8-bit video 
identifies that there are only 7 different values within the window, ORC recodes the 
video as 3-bit and adds a 7-element look-up table that can be used to restore the 3-
bit values to the original 8-bit values on the decompression side.  
 
3.1.3. 
ZLIB Compression 
 
The ZLIB compression standard is a variant of Lempel-Ziv LZ77. The compression 
process retains a history of already-processed data and attempts to identify similar 
sequences that can be represented as repeats of the previous data.  
 
In terms of radar compression, ZLIB typically achieves somewhat higher compression 
than ORC but does so at the expense of additional CPU time in both the compression 
and decompression. 
 
3.1.4. 
Comparing ORC and ZLIB 
 
ORC has the benefit of providing a good level of compression with very low demands 
on the compression and decompression resources. For mid or high performance 
machines, ZLIB offers additional compression at the expense of more processing. 
 
It should be emphasised that both ORC and ZLIB are lossless compression processes.

The Cambridge Pixel HPx series of Radar Interface Cards provide an acquisition 
capability for analogue and digital radar video signals. The class SPxHPx100Source 
provides a complete API for initialisation, control and data retrieval for the HPx family 
cards. Refer to the companion documents:

The SPxHPx100Source class is a sub-class of SPxRadarSource, and therefore provides

A single HPx card provides data into a RIB, from where it may be processed,

3.3. Radar Input from Network 
 
The class SPxNetworkReceive provides the top-level interface for receiving polar-
format network radar video from a remote server in SPx format.  
 
The class SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix is derived from SPxNetworkReceive and 
provides an interface for receiving ASTERIX Category 240 network video from a 
remote server. ASTERIX Cat-240 is a Eurocontrol standard for the transmission of 
rotating radar video. 
 
The server may be another SPx application, for example, that is receiving radar video, 
compressing it and then delivering it to the network. Alternatively, the server may be 
a custom application, or even an existing radar that is providing data in its own 
proprietary format (received using customised network receive class derived from 
SPxNetworkReceive). 
 
You can use SPxNetworkSend to deliver a network stream in SPx format or use 
SPxNetworkSendAsterix to deliver a network stream in ASTERIX Cat-240 format (see 
Section 4).  
 
RIB 
PIM 
Processing 
And 
Display 
SPxHPx100Source 


===== PAGE 42 =====
Page 42 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
You can also develop your own software to generate network radar video. If using SPx 
network format, the data on the network must conform to the structure defined in this 
document. If using ASTERIX Cat-240 network format, the data on the network must 
conform to the Eurocontrol standard. There are additional requirements relating to the 
transport and resolution of the video. Please refer to the ASTERIX Cat-240 Network 
Data Format manual (CP-25-110-83) for further details. 
 
ASTERIX Cat-240 has the advantage of being a recognised standard for network radar 
video; however, the SPx network format provides a number of advantages when used 
with SPx applications. SPx network video accommodates a variety of different data 
packing formats enabling additional data, such as the state of digital inputs, to be 
transported with the radar data. It also supports multiple compression types and non-
rotating scan modes. 
 
3.3.1. 
Format of SPx Network Radar Video 
 
Radar video is delivered over the network through standard IP protocols in a sequence 
of returns, where each return comprises a header and a data block. The data block 
comprises the radar video samples for the return, and may be of variable length. The 
data block may be in a compressed or raw format.  
 
Although the length of a return (size of header + size of data block) is variable, it is 
likely to be many Kbytes long. The low-level network interface needs to transmit 
smaller packets of data, so the header + data block of the original return is 
fragmented into a number of “chunks” that are then distributed and reassembled into 
the header + data block for processing by the SPx classes. This process is illustrated 
in Figure 15. 
 
If your application is using an SPx server to deliver radar video across the network, 
the format of the radar video is entirely encapsulated by the SPxNetworkReceive and 
SPxNetworkSend classes. However, if you have your own video format, then some 
form of “bridge” is needed to interface that format to the standard expected by SPx. 
There are two options for this. Either this interface module can be written by the 
application developer or else Cambridge Pixel can develop a custom interface module 
that receives your video. This effectively becomes a sub-class of SPxNetworkReceive, 
specialised to receive your network video. Consult the factory if this option is of 
interest. 
 
If you have a source of radar video that you need to connect to SPx running on a 
remote server then there are a number of options:  
 
1. Use the SPxNetworkSend class on the remote server. You will need to provide 
your radar returns in the format required by the class – see Table 1 – but then 
the fragmentation and distribution of the returns is handled by the class and 
the corresponding receiver SPxNetworkReceive. 
 
2. If you do not want to use the SPx classes on the server then you will need to 
format the radar video at two levels. First of all, the header and data block will 
need to be formatted as defined in Table 1. Secondly, that data structure will 
need to be fragmented and encoded as described in Section 3.3.2. When this is 
done, data can be sent to a designated IP address and port on the SPx 
processor, where the class SPxNetworkReceive will receive it and recover the 
data. 


===== PAGE 43 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 43 
 
3. Deliver your format of radar video from the server to the SPx processor then 
implement software to convert it to the SPx format. Using this approach you 
will not be using SPxNetworkSend or SPxNetworkReceive, as you will be taking 
full responsibility for all network interfacing. 
 
4. Define your network format to Cambridge Pixel and ask us to develop a custom 
interface module. 
 
These four options are summarised in Figure 16, which shows the software 
components that you may need to develop. 
 
Figure 15 - Distribution of video across network

3.3.2. 
Packetising the SPx Network Radar Video Data Structures 
 
As discussed previously, the data structure defined in Table 1 needs to be divided into 
smaller packets for transmission on the network. A variable number of these packets 
are transmitted to deliver each return. 
 
The packeting process does not interpret the contents of the data it transmits, so the 
data structure from Table 1 is simply treated as a byte stream with breaks at arbitrary 
offsets. 
 
The format of the packet structure is shown in Table 2.

3.3.3. 
SPxNetworkReceive Class 
 
The SPxNetworkReceive class provides the client-side receiver that accepts network 
video in SPx network format, as transmitted by an instance of SPxNetworkSend. It 
may also be used as a base class when deriving receiver classes to handle other 
network formats. 
 
Creating an instance of SPxNetworkReceive will create a new thread that listens for 
data arriving on a specified port. When data is received, it is decoded and copied into 
an RIB, where it may be processed through a PIM by the normal SPx processing and 
display functions. 
 
SPxNetworkReceive is a sub-class of SPxRadarSource, so may be used wherever an 
SPxRadarSource instance is needed. 
 
3.3.3.1 Construction, Destruction and Creation 
 
SPxNetworkReceive::SPxNetworkReceive(SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
Construct an instance of a network receive source which will provide data to the 
existing RIB defined by buffer. Call the Create() function to initialise the class 
instance. 
 
 
SPxNetworkReceive::~SPxNetworkReceive() 
 
Delete an instance of the class. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::Create(char *addr=NULL,  
                                       int port=0,  
                                       char *ifAddr = NULL) 
 
Initialise the class instance with a network address and port number to receive 
radar video. For most situations, the address will be a multicast address that 
matches the address used in the server for distribution. For unicast 
applications, use “0.0.0.0” as the address. 
 
The "ifAddr" argument can be used to optionally specify the interface address 
on which multicast groups should be joined, either as dotted IP, or a network 
name that can be looked up, but NOT the name of the interface itself (e.g. not 
"eth0" or "eth1"). 
 
3.3.3.2 Set Address and Port for Video Receive 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::SetAddress(char *addr, int port=0) 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::SetAddress(UINT32, int port=0) 
 
Set a new address and port. Refer to the Create() function for an explanation 
of the address and port. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 47 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 47 
UINT32 SPxNetworkReceive::GetAddress(void) 
 
Returns the current IP address as a 32-bit number. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkReceive::GetPort(void) 
 
 
 
Returns the current port number. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::SetPort(int port) 
 
Set a new port number for receiving radar video. The address is unchanged. 
 
 
3.3.3.3 Enable 
 
void SPxNetworkReceive::Enable(int enableState) 
 
This function enables or disable the source. Calling this function with a value of 
1 causes any other sources that are connected to the same RIB to be disabled. 
Likewise, calling Enable with 1 on any other source connected to the same RIB 
causes the network receive object to be disabled. 
 
 
3.3.3.4 Socket Receive Buffer 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::SetRcvBufSize(unsigned int numBytes) 
  
Set the size of the SO_RCVBUF socket buffer used for receiving network packets. 
A value of 0 will cause the SPx default value of 128KB to be requested. In some 
situations, increasing the size of the buffer may be necessary to ensure that all 
network packets are captured. Note that there is an operating system-imposed 
limit on the size of the buffer. If the requested size cannot be provided, the 
buffer size will be set to the 128KB default.  
 
 
unsigned int SPxNetworkReceive::GetRcvBufSize(void) 
 
This function returns the size of the socket buffer, as requested by the most 
recent call of SetRcvBufSize(), which will be 0 (to indicate that the default size 
is being used) if the SetRcvBufSize function has not been called. The function 
does not return the actual size of the socket buffer. 
 
 


===== PAGE 48 =====
Page 48 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.3.3.5 Data Handler callback 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::InstallDataFn( 
void(*fn)(SPxNetworkReceive *, void *userData, 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxReturnHeader *returnHeader, 
 
 
 
 
 
unsigned char *returnData), 
 
 
 
 
 
void *userData) 
 
This function registers a user-defined callback function to be invoked for each 
return that is received and decompressed by the object. It is not necessary to 
use this function if the SPxNetworkReceive object is being used with the 
normal RIB, PIM and process chain. However, in situations where it is required 
to use the SPxNetworkReceive object to receive and decompress returns and 
then pass them into client software (with no further SPx processing) then this 
function may be used. 
 
The function is called with a function pointer, fn, which is the callback function 
to be invoked, and a general-purpose arg pointer. When a return is received 
and decompressed, fn is called with: 
 
 
the instance of the SPxNetworkReceive object,  
 
the value of arg provided to InstallDataFn,  
 
the return’s header pointer (see section 3.1), 
 
the address of the start of the uncompressed video data block. 
 
In the Development release of SPx, refer to SPxExamples/SPxNetDataDirect 
project for a worked example of how to use this function. 
 
3.3.3.6 
Status 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceive::GetInitialised(void) 
 
This function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the network receiver object is initialised, 
that is interface is bound or connected. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxNetworkReceive::GetMsecsSinceLastReturn(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the network receiver 
object received the last radar return. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxNetworkReceive::GetMsecsSinceLastAziChange(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the network receiver 
object received a change of azimuth in a return. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 49 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 49 
3.3.3.7 Multicast Group Drop 
 
The class can be configured to optionally drop membership of a multicast group 
if the input source is disabled (i.e. because another radar source type is active). 
The option is disabled by default, which means that the multicast group is still 
used, even if the class is not the current radar source. The following functions 
control the behaviour. 
 
 
void SPxNetworkReceive::SetDropMcastGroup(int drop); 
 
Enable (if "drop" is true) or disable (if "drop" is false) dropping of the multicast 
group membership when the source object is disabled. In this case, when the 
source becomes active again the multicast group is rejoined automatically. 
 
 
int GetDropMcastGroup(void) 
 
Retrieve the current setting of the SetDropMcastGroup() option. 
 
 
3.3.3.8 Set and Get Parameter Values 
 
int SPxNetworkReceive::SetParameter(char *parameterName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterValue) 
 
Set a named parameter on the object. This function provides an ASCII-based 
interface to change a number of parameters of the object including over a 
network connection.

Cambridge Pixel Ltd.

Page 51 
3.3.4. 
SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix Class 
 
The SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix class provides a client-side receiver that accepts 
ASTERIX Category 240 network video as UDP network packets. ASTERIX Cat-240 is a 
Eurocontrol standard for the transmission of rotating radar video.  
 
The SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix class will receive radar video transmitted by an 
instance of SPxNetworkSendAsterix as well as most third party sources of ASTERIX 
Category 240 network video. A number of caveats apply for third party sources, 
namely the network transport must be UDP, the radar video sample size must be 
compatible with SPx and the compression algorithm (if used) must be ZLIB. Please 
refer to the ASTERIX Cat-240 Network Data Format manual (CP-25-110-83) for 
further details. 
 
SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix is a sub-class of SPxNetworkReceive, so may be used 
wherever an SPxNetworkReceive instance is needed. 
 
The SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix class supports all public functions provided by the 
SPxNetworkReceive base class. In addition, the following functions are available. 
 
 
3.3.4.1 Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix(SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
Construct an instance of a network receive source which will provide data to the 
existing RIB defined by buffer. Call the SPxNetworkReceive::Create() function 
to initialise the class instance. 
 
 
SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::~SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix() 
 
Delete an instance of the class. 
 
3.3.4.2 Message Filtering 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetExpectedSAC(UINT8 sac) 
 
Set the expected System Area Code. If sac is non-zero and the SAC of an 
incoming message does not match the specified value, then the message is 
silently discarded. If sac is set to zero then no filtering is performed. This is the 
default setting.  
 
 
UINT8 SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetExpectedSAC(void) 
 
Returns the current expected System Area Code or 0 if filtering is disabled.  
 
 
 


===== PAGE 52 =====
Page 52 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetExpectedSIC(UINT8 sic) 
 
Set the expected System Identification Code. If the sic is non-zero and the SIC 
of an incoming message does not match the specified value, then the message 
is silently discarded. If sic is set to zero, then no filtering is performed. This is 
the default setting.  
 
 
UINT8 SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetExpectedSIC(void) 
 
Returns the current expected System Identification Code or 0 if filtering is 
disabled.  
 
 
3.3.4.3 Azimuth Reference Mode 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetAziRefModeEnabled(int enable) 
 
The ASTERIX Category 240 network video does not support the concept of 
heading referenced data. By default, SPx radar returns generated by this class 
will not have the azimuth reference mode set. If the azimuth reference mode 
setting for a PIM receiving data from the source requires the reference mode to 
be set, then this function can be used. The reference mode set will be north-up 
if SetNorthUp() has been called a TRUE argument, or heading-up if 
SetNorthUp() has been called with a FALSE argument. 
 
Starting with V1.79.1, SPx server applications may be configured to include 
azimuth reference information in the Special Purpose Field (SPF). If this 
information is included, and SPF decoding is enabled, the received azimuth 
reference mode will be used in preference to the mode configured by these 
functions. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetAziRefModeEnabled(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if the azimuth reference mode is being set in the generated SPx 
radar returns, or FALSE otherwise. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetNorthUp(int northUp) 
 
If the SetAziRefModeEnabled() function has been called with a TRUE 
argument, then this function sets the azimuth reference mode to set in 
generated SPx radar returns. North-up if this function is called with a TRUE 
argument, and heading-up if called with a FALSE argument. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetNorthUp(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if the azimuth reference mode should be north-up, and FALSE if 
heading-up. This setting is only used if SetAziRefModeEnabled() has been 
called with a TRUE argument. 
 
 


===== PAGE 53 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 53 
3.3.4.4 Azimuth Input Mode 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetAziInputRefMode 
                                      (AziInputRefMode_t mode) 
 
Configure the network sender to use the END_AZ field to set the azimuth 
position (0) or the START_AZ field to set azimuth position (1) in generated radar 
video messages. 
 
 
AziInputRefMode_t SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetAziInputRefMode(void) 
 
Retrieve the current azimuth input reference mode setting. 
 
 
3.3.4.5 Special Purpose Field 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::SetSPFEnabled(int isEnabled) 
 
Enable or disable the decoding of supported custom Special Purpose Field (SPF) 
data. Starting with V1.79.1, SPx server applications may be configured to 
include a custom SPF in generated ASTERIX network radar video. This field 
includes azimuth reference information and data type information. Decoding of 
custom SPF data is enabled by default. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::IsSPFEnabled(void) const 
 
Query if decoding of custom Special Purpose field is enabled (TRUE) or disabled 
(FALSE). 
 
 
3.3.4.6 
Data Retrieval 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetLastSAC(UINT8 *sacRtn, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
        
 
 UINT32 expiryMsecs=0) 
 
Returns the System Area Code from the last Video Summary Message or the 
last Video Message received. The expiryMsecs argument is used to specify an 
optional data expiry time in milliseconds.  
 
If no message has been received then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_NO_DATA is returned. 
 
If expiryMsecs is set greater than zero and the time since the last message 
exceeds expiryMsecs, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_DATA_EXPIRED is returned. 
 
If the call is successful, then SPX_NO_ERROR is returned. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 54 =====
Page 54 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd.

or the last Video Message received. The expiryMsecs argument is used to 
specify an optional data expiry time in milliseconds.  
 
If no message has been received, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_NO_DATA is returned.  
 
If expiryMsecs is set greater than zero and the time since the last message 
exceeds expiryMsecs, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_DATA_EXPIRED is returned.  
 
If the call is successful then SPX_NO_ERROR is returned. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetLastSummaryText 
                                     (char *bufPtr,

UINT32 expiryMsecs=0) 
 
Returns the meta text received in the last Video Summary Message into a 
buffer referenced by bufPtr. A maximum of bufSizeBytes characters will be 
written including a null terminator. The maximum buffer size required is given 
by SPX_CAT240_MAX_SUMMARY_TEXT_BYTES (256 bytes). The expiryMsecs 
argument is used to specify an optional data expiry time in milliseconds.  
 
If no Video Summary Message has been received, then 
SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_NO_DATA is returned.  
 
If expiryMsecs is set greater than zero and the time since the last Video 
Summary Message exceeds expiryMsecs, then 
SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_DATA_EXPIRED is returned. 
 
If the call is successful, then SPX_NO_ERROR is returned. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix::GetLastSummaryTimeOfDayMsecs 
                                              (UINT32 timeOfDayRtn, 
                                               UINT32 expiryMsecs=0) 
 
Returns the time-of-day from the last Video Summary Message received, 
defined as milliseconds since midnight UTC. Please note that this is an optional 
component in a Video Summary Message and may have been omitted from the 
message by the sender. The expiryMsecs argument is used to specify an 
optional data expiry time in milliseconds.  
 
If no Video Summary Message has been received or the optional Time of Day 
component was not present, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_NO_DATA is returned.  
 
If expiryMsecs is set greater than zero and the time since the last Video 
Summary Message exceeds expiryMsecs, then 
SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_DATA_EXPIRED is returned.  
 
If the call is successful, then SPX_NO_ERROR is returned.

If no Video Message has been received, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_NO_DATA is 
returned.

If expiryMsecs is set greater than zero and the time since the last Video 
Message exceeds expiryMsecs, then SPX_ERR_ASTERIX_DATA_EXPIRED is 
returned.  
 
If the call is successful, then SPX_NO_ERROR is returned.

3.4. Input from the Radar Test Generator 
 
The test generator is a radar source that provides static or dynamic test patterns into 
a RIB.  
 
The class SPxTestGenerator may be configured to set the scan-rate, pulse rate, 
return length and contents of the test pattern. 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 58 =====
Page 58 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.4.1. 
SPxTestGenerator Class 
 
An instance of SPxTestGenerator encapsulates the capabilities of the test generator 
with the following interface functions: 
 
3.4.1.1 Construction and destruction 
 
SPxTestGenerator::SPxTestGenerator (SPxRIB *buffer, 
 
 
 
int rangeSamples, 
 
 
 
double scanPeriod, 
 
 
 
int returnsPerSecond, 
 
 
 
int testPattern. 
 
 
 
int arg1=0) 
 
Creates a new instance of a test generator, which will provide radar video to 
the specified Radar Input buffer, previously created. The rangeSamples 
argument defines the number of rangeSamples per return. The scanPeriod is 
the rotation period in seconds. The returnsPerSecond argument specifies the 
number of returns per second that will be generated. The selected testPattern 
is as defined in Table 3. Some of the test patterns have a parameter and this 
can be provided in an optional argument provided to the constructor. After 
construction, the test pattern generator is disabled and only becomes active 
after a call to Enable(). 
 
 
SPxTestGenerator::~ SPxTestGenerator() 
 
Deletes an existing instance of a test generator. 
 
3.4.1.2 Information Retrieval 
 
int SPxTestGenerator::GetRangeSamples(void) 
 
Returns the number of range samples in a return. This is the value provided to 
the constructor of the class. 
 
 
SPxRIB* SPxTestGenerator::GetBuffer(void) 
 
Returns the RIB being used by this test generator. At the current time, an 
instance of the test generator can only write to a single RIB.  
 
 
3.4.1.3 Set/Get Period 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetScanPeriod(double newScanPeriod) 
 
Changes the rotation period of the test generator. The specified value is the 
new period in seconds. The azimuth value of each radar return is calculated to 
ensure that a complete 360 scan is made in the specified period. 
 


===== PAGE 59 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 59 
double SPxTestGenerator::GetScanPeriod(void) 
 
Return the current scan period in seconds. 
 
 
3.4.1.4 Set/Get Radar Range 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetRangeMetres(double rangeMetres) 
 
Sets the maximum range of each return in metres. 
 
 
double SPxTestGenerator::GetRangeMetres(void) 
 
Returns the range in metres of the last sample for a return. 
 
 
3.4.1.5 Set/Get Radar PRF 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetReturnsPerSecond(int newReturnsPerSecond) 
 
Changes the number of returns per second that the test generator sends to the 
radar input buffer. Although there is no upper limit on this value, a practical 
limitation derives from the processing implications of the quantity of data being 
delivered.  
 
 
int SPxTestGenerator::GetReturnsPerSec(void) 
 
Returns the number of returns per second (the PRF) of the test generator. 
 
 
3.4.1.6 
Set/Get Test Pattern  
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetPattern(int newTestPattern) 
 
Changes the current test pattern being sent by the test generator. The test 
patterns are defined in Table 3. Some test patterns fix the scan rate and PRF to 
a known value, irrespective of the current user settings (refer to Table 3). Test 
pattern 0 is the user-defined test pattern. When this is configured, the class 
calls the function supplied in SetUserFunction() for every output azimuth it 
creates. 
 
 
int SPxTestGenerator::GetPattern(void) 
 
Return the current test pattern number. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 60 =====
Page 60 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetArg1(int arg) 
 
Set the argument that some of the test patterns use. The argument can be 
changed at any time and is effective on the next azimuth that is processed. 
 
 
int SPxTestGenerator::GetArg1(void) 
 
Return the current value of the argument used by some of the test patterns. 
 
 
3.4.1.7 Source Enable 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::Enable (int enableState) 
 
The function disables (enableState = 0) or enables the test generator. If any 
other radar sources are providing data into the RIB, then they will be disabled 
before enabling the test generator. When called with 0, the test generator stops 
sending data to the Radar Input buffer, effectively entering a pause mode. 
 
 
int SPxTestGenerator::GetEnable(void) 
 
Returns the current enable state of the test generator as either 0 (meaning it is 
disabled), or 1 (meaning it is enabled). 
 
 
3.4.1.8 User Callbacks 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetUserFunction( void (*fn) 
                             (SPxTestGenerator *testGen,  
 
 
 
 
 
int azimuth, unsigned char *dataPtr),    
                              void *userArg=NULL)  
 
Defines a function to call when the user-defined test-pattern (code 0) is 
selected. The function gets called with the instance of the TestGenerator, the 
azimuth for the new return (0 to 65536 to represent 0 to 360 degrees), and the 
memory location where the return should be constructed. The user’s function is 
responsible for supplying the data to create the test pattern. The class calls the 
user’s function for every new return to be generated. 
 
Note that the value of azimuth provided to the function may be difficult to 
predict, as it depends on PRF and scan rate. The user’s function should not rely 
on getting specific values for the azimuth. If it needs to do something special at 
the North crossing, for example, then it should look for the azimuth value 
crossing zero, rather than the specific value of zero. 
 
The user’s function should use GetRangeSamples() to get the number of range 
samples to write to dataPtr. The range represented by those samples is 0 to 
GetRangeMetres() metres. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 61 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 61 
If required, the function can call GetUserSetFunctionArg() to retrieve the 
“userArg” parameter that was specified when the function was set. This can be 
used to pass user-specific data to the callback, such as a pointer to another 
object, or other permanent data required by the user function. 
 
 
void SetUserPacking(UCHAR packing) 
  
If the user-defined test pattern is providing radar data in something other than 
8-bits per sample, then this function can be used to specify the format being 
used. This tells downstream objects how to store and handle the data where it 
includes more than one sub-stream of data, e.g. SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW6_11, or 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8_8 as defined in section 3.1.1. The GetUserPacking() 
function can be used to retrieve the current setting. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxTestGenerator::InstallDataFn( 
void(*fn)(SPxTestGenerator *, void *userData, 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxReturnHeader *returnHeader, 
 
 
 
 
 
unsigned char *returnData), 
 
 
 
 
 
void *userData) 
 
This function registers a user-defined callback function to be invoked for each 
return that is created by the object. It is not necessary to use this function if 
the SPxTestGenerator object is being used with the normal RIB, PIM and 
process chain. However, in situations where it is desired to use the 
SPxTestGenerator object to create returns (with no further SPx processing) 
then this function may be used. 
 
The function is called with a function pointer, fn, which is the callback function 
to be invoked, and a general-purpose arg pointer. When a return is received 
and decompressed, fn is called with: 
 
 
the instance of the SPxNetworkReceive object,  
 
the value of arg provided to SPxTestGenerator,  
 
the return’s header pointer (see section 3.1), 
 
the address of the start of the video data block.

char *parameterValue)

3.4.1.10 Azimuth Scanning Mode 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetAzimuthScanMode(SPxScanMode mode) 
 
This function defines the scanning mode for the test generator. The scanning 
mode defines the sequence of azimuths generated by the test generator. The 
value of mode is one of the following: 
 
mode = SPX_SCAN_MODE_ROTATING (value 0) for normal rotating scan mode. 
This is the default mode. 


===== PAGE 63 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 63 
mode = SPX_SCAN_MODE_COMPENSATED (value 1) for normal rotating situation 
but with compensated azimuths that may appear to go backwards for short 
intervals. This is not currently supported in the test generator. 
 
mode = SPX_SCAN_MODE_SECTOR (value = 2) for sector scan, in which the 
sweep scans alternately forwards and backwards between limits. The limits are 
set with SetAzimuthSector(). 
 
mode = SPX_SCAN_MODE_RANDOM (value = 3) for random scan, in which each 
azimuth has no specific relationship to the previous. The test generator 
generates pseudo-random azimuths around the scan. 
mode = SPX_SCAN_MODE_SECTOR_FLY_BACK (value = 4) for sector scan, in 
which the sweep scans forwards and then flies back to repeat the scan in the 
sector (no backward scanning). The limits are set with SetAzimuthSector(). 
 
 
SPxScanMode SPxTestGenerator::GetAzimuthScanMode(void) 
 
Returns the current value of the azimuth scan mode. 
 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetAzimuthSector(int startSector, int endSector) 
 
When the test generator is used in sector scanning mode (set with 
SetAzimuthScanMode), the start and end sector of the scanning are set with 
this function. The startSector and endSector values define the start and end 
azimuth of the sector in the range 0..65536 to represent 0 to 360 degrees. 
 
3.4.1.11 Data Endian 
 
void SPxTestGenerator::SetDataEndian(SPxTestGenDataEndian_t de) 
 
By default the test generator will output multi-byte samples in native endian 
order (the same endian order as the host). If required, the data can be forced 
to either big-endian or little-endian order using this function: 
 
de = SPX_TEST_GEN_DATA_NATIVE_ENDIAN (value 0) for native endian byte 
order. This is the default mode. 
 
de = SPX_TEST_GEN_DATA_LITTLE_ENDIAN (value 1) for little-endian byte 
order (least significant byte first). 
 
de = SPX_TEST_GEN_DATA_BIG_ENDIAN (value 2) for big-endian byte order 
(most significant byte first). 
 
 
SPxTestGenDataEndian_t SPxTestGenerator::GetDataEndian(void) 
 
Get the current data endian order setting. 
 
 


===== PAGE 64 =====
Page 64 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.4.1.12 Status 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxTestGenerator::GetInitialised(void) 
 
This function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the test generator object is initialised. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxTestGenerator::GetMsecsSinceLastReturn(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the test generator object 
generated the last radar return. 
 
UINT32 SPxTestGenerator::GetMsecsSinceLastAziChange(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the test generator object 
generated a change of azimuth in a return. 
 
 
3.4.2. 
Implementation Details 
 
When a new instance of class SPxTestGenerator is created, a new thread is created 
to handle the radar transfers to the RIB. The thread provides data to the RIB at the 
rate implied by the parameters. Between transfers, the thread is idle. 
 
Consideration needs to be given to the amount of time spent by the thread copying 
data into the buffer. This depends on the parameters provided to the constructor. The 
average number of bytes per second is returnsPerSecond * rangeSamples. The 
scanPeriod does not affect the data rate, but it does affect the azimuth numbering. 
 
Generally, the thread created for an instance of the class will not be running on a real-
time operating system, which means it is not possible to generate data at exactly the 
rate implied by the PRF setting. The class can generate data at a rate that averages to 
the correct value by generating data to the RIB in bursts. In normal operation, this 
will be transparent to the processing and display. However, the RIB must be large 
enough to hold the bursts. As a rule of thumb, under Windows and Linux the transfer 
of data from the test generator to the RIB occurs at rates no larger than 10ms (a PRF 
of 100). For required PRFs in excess of this, a burst of several returns is sent. The size 
of the burst is equal to the product of the number of rangeSamples and the PRF/100. 
So, for example, a PRF of 5 kHz and a return length of 8192 samples will require a 
burst size of 5000/100 * 8192 = 410kbytes. The RIB size should be significantly 
larger than this, say by a factor of at least 5. 
 
3.4.3. 
User-Defined Test Patterns 
 
By selecting test pattern 0 using SetPattern(0), a user-defined test pattern may be 
created. Client software needs to register a function with SetUserFunction(), and 
this function is called by the test generator class when a new azimuth of data is to be 
generated. Your function is responsible for creating the data for each azimuth. 
 
For example, the following code fragment installs the function myTestGenerator on 
the class, and then the function is defined to create a range ring at ½ maximum range 
and full range. 


===== PAGE 65 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 65 
static void myTestGenerator(SPxTestGenerator *, int azimuth,  
unsigned char *); 
  
… 
/* Create our test generator – assume the RIB already exists */ 
SPxTestGenerator *testGen = new SPxTestGenerator(rib, 1024, 2, 500, 0); 
/* User-defined test pattern */ 
testGen->SetPattern(0); 
/* Register our function to get called */ 
testGen->SetUserFunction(myTestGenerator); 
TestGen->Enable(1); 
 
/* Define the function that gets called for each azimuth */ 
static void myTestGenerator(SPxTestGenerator * testGen,  
int azimuth,  
unsigned char *dataPtr) 
{ 
 
/* Create test pattern with a range ring at ½ and full range */ 
 
 
/* The azimuth number is in the range 0 to 65536 (0 to 360 degs) 
 
   The data is to be written at dataPtr. 
 
*/ 
 
 
int numSamples = testGen->GetRangeSamples(); 
 
/* Clear the return */ 
 
memset(dataPtr, 0, numSamples); 
 
 
/* Now write data at ½ and full range. Note azimuth independent. */ 
 
dataPtr[numSamples/2-1] = 0xff; 
 
dataPtr[numSamples-1] = 0xff; 
 
} 
 
3.4.4. 
Scan periods 
 
Each return written into the Radar Input Buffer has an azimuth number expressed in 
the range 0 .. 65536, where 65536 corresponds to 360 degrees. If R is the number of 
returns per second, and S is the scan period, then the number of returns generated 
per scan is R*S. On the assumption that each scan is equally located around the 
rotation, the azimuth count for each scan is then set as i * 65536 /(R*S), where i 
indexes from 0 to R*S-1. 
 
Attention must be given to the choice of values for scanPeriod and 
returnsPerSecond. In practice, there is generally an implied relationship between 
them because radars with long scan periods are looking at long ranges and this puts 
an upper limit to the number of returns per second that can be generated. For 
example, a radar having a range of 250 NM must allow a minimum of 3.1ms between 
consecutive pulses (250 NM * 1852 M/NM / 3 x 108 x 2), giving a maximum returns 
per second of about 320. Allowing 5 returns per degree of rotation, gives 1800 hits 
per scan and a scan period of 5.6 seconds.
