3.5. Input from the Scenario Generator 
 
Like the test pattern generator, the Scenario Generator provides synthetic radar video 
that may be used for testing and performance verification. The Scenario Generator is 
a radar generator object that provides a source of radar video to the Radar Interface 
Buffers (RIBs). 
 
The Scenario Generator, represented by the class 
SPxScenarioGenerator, allows simple moving targets to be 
created and updated, generating video that contains 
representations of these targets as a function of the target 
and the radar’s position. The Scenario Generator can support 
a number of radars, allowing a single set of targets to 
generate video that is consistent with observation from 
different radar locations. This allows multi-radar installations 
to be simulated. 
The Scenario Generator can read a file containing target 
positions and speeds and will then automatically generate 
radar video that contains representations for these targets as a function of time. The 
characteristics of the radar (period, PRF, range etc.) are defined to the scenario 
generator through a Radar Database object (see Section 11.10). 
 
The Scenario Generator can be configured to distribute its configuration (including 
target sizes and positions) onto the network. Other Scenario Generators can be set up 
to receive this configuration, rather than using a local configuration. Multiple Scenario 
Generators can receive the same configuration from a single distributing Scenario 
Generator if multicast distribution is used. 
 
The SPxScenarioGenerator class is described in the SPx Scenario Generator User 
manual (document CP-25-110-89). 
 
A separate product, SPx Simulator, is available to support complex simulations of 
multiple targets and radars. 
 
3.6. Radar Input From File 
 
Radar video may be read from a file, where it may have been stored from a previous 
recording with SPx, or where it may have been created by another application. To 
record radar video to a file refer to the SPXProRecord process (Section 6.7.25). The 
file, which normally has a .cpr extension, may contain packets of video and 
navigation data. 
 
The class for handling radar replay is SPxRadarReplay, which is a sub-class of 
SPxRadarSource, and provides radar video data to a RIB and optionally navigation 
data to a SPxNavData object.  
 
3.6.1. 
Coded Radar Input from File 
 
The class SPxRadarReplay is a sub-class of SPxRadarSource and may be used to read 
coded radar video from a file into a RIB. The file will typically, although not 


===== PAGE 74 =====
Page 74 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
necessarily, have a .cpr extension and will contain data in either ORC, ZLIB or 
uncompressed raw format. 
 
3.6.1.1 
Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxRadarReplay::SPxRadarReplay(SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
Creates a new instance of a SPxRadarReplay object, which will be used to read 
radar video from a file and write into buffer.  
 
The specified buffer may be NULL if it is not desired to use the SPx RIB and PIM 
objects to handle the video. In this mode, the replay object will report new 
returns by calling a user-supplied callback function which is set with 
InstallDataFn(). 
 
 
SPxRadarReplay::~SPxRadarReplay() 
 
Delete a SPxRadarReplay object. 
 
3.6.1.2 
Information Retrieval 
 
SPxRIB * SPxRadarReplay::GetBuffer(void) 
 
Returns the RIB buffer being used by the replay object. This is the RIB that was 
provided in the class constructor. The buffer may be NULL if no RIB was 
specified in the constructor. 
 
 
int SPxRadarReplay::GetNumSamples(void) 
 
Returns the number of samples per return in the data. 
 
 
double SPxRadarReplay::GetStartRange(void) 
 
Returns the range in metres of the first sample in the return. 
 
 
double SPxRadarReplay::GetEndRange(void) 
 
Returns the range in metres of the last sample in the return. 
 
 
int SPxRadarReplay::IsTOCAvailable(void) 
 
This function returns TRUE if the recording has a table of contents. The table of 
contents allows rapid movement to a time in the file using the GotoFileTime() 
functions. The table of contents is created automatically for all recordings done 
with SPx 1.17.4 or later. For recordings taken with an earlier version there will 
be no table of contents. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 75 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 75 
3.6.1.3 
File Name 
 
int SPxRadarReplay::SetFileName(char *fileName) 
 
Change the file being replayed. Calling this function causes the replay to stop, 
and a new file opened at the beginning. The function returns an error code, 
which is SPX_NO_ERROR if there is no error. 
 
 
const char * SPxRadarReplay::GetFileName(void) 
 
Returns the current filename being used for recording. 
 
 
const char *SPxRadarReplay::GetFullPath(void) 
 
Returns the full path name of the file that has been set for replay. 
 
3.6.1.4 
Replay Speed 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::SetSpeedupFactor(double factor) 
 
Set a speed-up factor for the replay. The nominal replay speed is calculated 
from the information in the infoBlock in the original Create function. This 
function allows the replay speed to be adjusted by specifying a value larger 
than 1 to speed-up replay (for example a value of 2 corresponds to x2 fast 
forward) and specifying a value of less than 1 to slow down replay (for 
example, a value of 0.5 corresponds to a ½ speed replay). 
 
 
double SPxRadarReplay::GetSpeedupFactor(void) 
 
Return the current speed-up factor for replay. 
 
 
3.6.1.5 
Replay Controls 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::Rewind(void) 
 
Rewind the replay back to the beginning of the file. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::Play(void) 
 
Play the replay. This happens by default after a successful Create and if the 
replay source is enabled. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::Pause(void) 
 
Pause the replay. No further data is sent to the RIB until Play() is called. 
 


===== PAGE 76 =====
Page 76 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxRadarReplay::SetAutoLoop(int enable) 
 
The function sets the behaviour of the replay object when an end of file is 
reached. The default is to loop back to the beginning of the file. By using 
SetAutoLoop(FALSE), the radar replay class will stop at the end of the 
recording. 
 
 
int SPxRadarReplay::GetAutoLoop(void)  
 
Returns the status of the auto loop value. A value of TRUE is returned if the 
replay class is configured to automatically loop back to the beginning of the file 
and start playing. A value of FALSE is returned if the behaviour is to stop at the 
end of the file. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::GotoFileTimePercent(double percent) 
 
This function moves the current replay time to the given percentage through 
the file (e.g., 0 for start of file, 100 for end of file, 50 for halfway). This function 
is effective if the recording file has a table of contents, or if the replay has 
previously completed a start to end cycle. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::GetFileTimePercent(double *percentPtr) 
 
Get the current replay position of a percentage through the file. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::GetFileTimeStart(SPxTime_t *ptr, int inHandler = 
FALSE) 
void SPxRadarReplay::GetFileTimeCur(SPxTime_t *ptr, int inHandler = 
FALSE) 
void SPxRadarReplay::GetFileTimeEnd(SPxTime_t *ptr, int inHandler = 
FALSE) 
 
These functions retrieve the start, current and end file times through the 
provided SPxTime_t pointer. The inHandler argument should be set to TRUE if 
the functions are being called within a callback handler installed with the 
InstallDataFn() routine. 
 
Refer to Section 11.11 for details of the Time functions. 
 
3.6.1.6 
Enable 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::Enable (int enableState) 
 
The function disables (enableState = 0) or enables the replay object. If any 
other radar sources are providing data into the RIB then they will be disabled 
before enabling the radar replay generator.  
 
int SPxRadarReplay::GetEnable(void) 
 
Return the current enable state of the radar replay object. 
 


===== PAGE 77 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 77 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::GotoFileTime(const SPxTime_t *timestamp) 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::GotoFileTime(UINT32 secs) 
 
These functions move the current replay point to the specified epoch time, 
which may be provided as either a SPxTime_t structure, or else as seconds 
since 1st Jan 1970. Refer to Section 11.11 for details of the Time functions. 
 
3.6.1.7 
Callback on Data Read 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::InstallDataFn(void (*fn) 
                                          (SPxRadarReplay *, 
                                           void *arg,  
                                           SPxReturnHeader * header,  
                                           unsigned char* data), void  
                                           *userArg); 
 
This function is normally only used if the SPxRadarReplay object was created 
with NULL for the RIB, i.e. the normal SPx processing software is not being 
used. In this mode, the user-defined function, fn, gets called as data is read 
and decompressed from the recording. The function is called with the following 
arguments:  
 
 
The instance of SPxRadarReplay,  
 
The value of userArg which is the second argument to InstallDataFn(), 
The return header, 
 
The start of the data block of uncompressed video packed according to 
the details in the header. 
 
This function may be used in conjunction with an SPxRIB radar input buffer 
passed to the SPxRadarReplay object on construction, in which case the routine 
is invoked before the data is written to the RIB for further processing. Or, it 
may be used in a stand-alone application that does not use the SPx processing 
library and hence does not have a RIB object for the data but processes the 
data itself. 
 
Handling functions may modify the radar data if desired (in which case the data 
written to the RIB is the modified set of values), or may simply use the data for 
some other processing, diagnostic or display purpose. 
 
3.6.1.8 
Status 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarReplay::GetInitialised(void) 
 
This function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the radar replay object is initialised. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxRadarReplay::GetMsecsSinceLastReturn(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the object generated the 
last radar return. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 78 =====
Page 78 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
UINT32 SPxRadarReplay::GetMsecsSinceLastAziChange(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the object generated a 
change of azimuth in a return. 
 
3.6.1.9 
Navigation Data 
 
If the recording contains navigation data, the replay class may provide this to a 
SPxNavData object. It is not necessary to establish an explicit connection between the 
SPxRadarReplay object and an SPxNavData object. By default, if a SPxNavData object 
has been created, the SPxRadarReplay object will invoke that (global) object with 
each packet of navigation data read from the file. Nav data in the recording file will be 
discarded if an SPxNavData object has not been created. Alternatively, a specific 
SPxNavData object may be provided to the SPxRadarReplay object which it will invoke 
with each packet of navigation data read from the file. Navigation data replay may be 
disabled or re-enabled using the SPxRadarReplay::SetReplayNavData() function. 
 
In the example of Figure 17, the SPxRadarReplay object reads and decodes the 
contents of the recording file. Video data is sent to the RIB defined in the object’s 
constructor. From that RIB, the video is passed to a PIM and a scan conversion 
process. When SPxRadarReplay decodes navigation data from the file this is sent to 
the first (and generally the only) SPxNavData object created in the same software 
process (unless a specific SPxNavData object has been set). The PIM associated with 
the scan conversion chain is then connected to the same SPxNavData object using the 
SPxPIM::SetNorthReference() function (see 5.5.9). This means that the PIM will 
query the SPxNavData object to ensure that the correct azimuth adjustment is made 
on the video to achieve the requested north-up or heading-up display. It is possible 
that the application will also want to query the SPxNavData object to control the 
positioning of the radar image relative to the map. 
 
Figure 17 - Object relationships for SPxRadarReplay and navigation data 
 
 
 
 
SPxRadarReplay 
 
SPxNavData 
 
File 
 
SPxPIM 
 
SPxRIB 
Application 
 
SPxScSourceLocal 
(or other processing) 


===== PAGE 79 =====

SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 79 
void SPxRadarReplay::SetNavData(SPxNavData *navData,  
                                unsigned int chanID=0,  
                                unsigned int navDataID=0) 
 
Associate a specific SPxNavData object with the SPxRadarReplay object. This is 
only required when multiple navigation data objects are used in an application 
and navigation data should be replayed to the specified SPxNavData object 
rather than the default. 
 
chanID is the channel ID the navigation data applies to, for use when the 
SPxRadarReplay object is replaying multiple channels of radar video. 
 
navDataID is an ID to allow multiple navigation data objects to be supplied with 
navigation data. Each navigation data object that requires data should be 
installed with a different navDataID. 
 
 
SPxNavData *SPxRadarReplay::GetNavData(unsigned int chanID=0,   
                                       unsigned int navDataID=0) const 
 
Retrieve the SPxNavData object associated with the SPxRadarReplay object. 
This function will return NULL if there is no SPxNavData object or the 
SPxRadarReplay object is replaying navigation data using the global 
SPxNavData object. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplay::SetReplayNavData(int replayNavData) 
 
Enable or disable the replay of navigation data from the file. By default, 
navigation data replay is enabled. 
 
 
int SPxRadarReplay::GetReplayNavData(void) const 
 
Query if navigation data replay is enabled. Returns TRUE if enabled, and FALSE 
if disabled. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxRadarReplay::GetNumNavData(unsigned int chanID=0) const 
 
Retrieve the number of SPxNavData objects that have been installed for a 
particular channel, ignoring the global SPxNavData object.

3.6.2. 
Raw Radar Input from File 
 
The simplest format of radar video that can be read is raw video, which is 
uncompressed radar returns coded as 8-bits per sample, with a fixed number of 
samples per return. The use of this raw format is not recommended. The preferred 
class for handle file-based replay is SPxRadarReplay, as described in the previous 
section. 
 
The raw file, which typically but not necessarily has a .dat extension, contains binary 
data, organised as a sequence of {azimuth, data, marker} blocks. The file length is 
limited by the operating system and normal file handling restrictions. The format is 
{Azimuth : UINT32, Data : UCHAR[], Marker : UINT32} repeated to the end of 
the file. 
 
The azimuth number is a 32-bit number that defines the azimuth number of the 
return that follows. After a pre-set number of data bytes, an end-of-return marker is 
read as a 32-bit value. The next azimuth follows. Within each 32-bit word, the 
azimuth and marker are stored in network (big-endian) byte format. 
 
The class SPxRadarReplayRaw provides the capability to read this data into a RIB. 
 
 
SPxRadarReplayRaw::SPxRadarReplayRaw(SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
Creates a new instance of a SPxRadarReplayRaw object, which will be used to 
read radar video from a file and write into the already-existing buffer. When the 
constructor has been called, use the Create function to initialise the object. 
 
int SPxRadarReplayRaw::Create(char *fileName, SPxReplayRawInfo 
*infoBlock) 
 
Initialise a newly created radar replay object. The input fileName is assumed to 
be the name of a file where the data can be found, expressed as either an 
absolute filename or a name relative to the directory of execution of the 
program. The infoBlock is filled-out by the caller of the function to define 
various properties of the recorded data. The SPxReplayRaw structure has the 
following form: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
    int rangeSamplesPerReturn; 
    int azimuthsPerScan; 
 
  
    UINT16 timeBetweenReturns; 
 
  
    int flags; 
 
 
 
 
    REAL32 rangeOfFirstSample; 
    REAL32 rangeOfLastSample;  
 
    UINT32 endOfDataMarker; 
 
 
    void (*eventHandler)(SPxRadarReplay*, int reason); 
 
 
 
} SPxReplayRawInfo; 
 
The components of the structure have the following meaning: 
 
rangeSamplesPerReturn is the number of 8-bit samples per return. For 
example, a value of 2048 indicates there are 2048 samples in each azimuth 
block.  
 


===== PAGE 83 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 83 
azimuthsPerScan is the number of azimuths that represent one revolution. For 
example, if the input data uses 0-4096 to indicate an azimuth number in the 
range 0..360 degrees, then azimuthsPerScan is set to 4096. Note that this 
value is only used to convert azimuth numbers in the file into SPx azimuth 
codes. It does not imply that the input data contains contiguous azimuths, for 
example. 
 
timeBetweenReturns is the nominal time interval in microseconds between 
returns. When replaying the data, the class will try to get the average interval 
between returns to be this value. 
 
flags supports a number of optional controls: 
 
SPX_REPLAY_AUTO_RESTART – when the recording gets to the end do an 
automatic rewind and continue from the beginning. 
 
SPX_REPLAY_LITTLE_ENDIAN – the azimuth and end marker are read as little-
endian values, rather than the default network (big-endian) format. 
 
rangeOfFirstSample is the range in metres of the first range sample in the 
return. This could be negative in the situation where the return contains a 
preamble of data before range 0. 
 
rangeOfLastSample is the range in metres of the last sample in the return. 
 
endOfDataMarker is the 32-bit number that marks the end of a return. This 
isn’t used for anything other than basic error checking. If the value is specified 
as 0 the checking is not done. 
 
eventHandler is a user-supplied function that gets called on a significant event. 
It may be NULL if no event handling is required. The event handler is called 
with a pointer to the class instance and a reason code, which is one of: 
 
SPX_REPLAY_EOF – The replay reached the end of the file. 
SPX_REPLAY_BAD_AZIMUTH – The azimuth could not be read. 
SPX_REPLAY_BAD_DATA – End-of-file whilst reading the data. 
SPX_REPLAY_BAD_EOD – Error reading the end-of-data marker. 
SPX_REPLAY_BAD_MARKER – The end-of-data marker was not as defined. 
 
When the error handler is called, the replay process is automatically disabled. 
The user’s event handler may restart the recording by calling Rewind() and 
Enable() on the class instance. Note that if a normal end-of-file situation arises 
and the flag SPX_REPLAY_AUTO_RESTART is set, the replay restarts from the 
beginning and the user’s event handler is not called. 
 
 
SPxRadarReplayRaw::~SPxRadarReplayRaw() 
 
Delete a SPxRadarReplayRaw object. 
 
 
SPxRIB * SPxRadarReplayRaw::GetBuffer(void) 
 
Returns the RIB buffer being used by the replay object. 


===== PAGE 84 =====
Page 84 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxRadarReplayRaw::SetFileName(char *fileName) 
 
Change the file being replayed. Calling this function causes the replay to stop, 
and a new file to be opened at the beginning. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplayRaw::SetSpeedupFactor(double factor) 
 
Set a speed-up factor for the replay. The nominal replay speed is calculated 
from the information in the infoBlock in the original Create function. This 
function allows the replay speed to be adjusted by specifying a value larger 
than 1 to speed-up replay (for example a value of 2 corresponds to x2 fast 
forward) and specifying a value of less than 1 to slow down replay (for 
example, a value of 0.5 corresponds to a ½ speed replay). 
 
 
double SPxRadarReplayRaw::GetSpeedupFactor(void) 
 
Return the current speed-up factor for replay. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplayRaw::Rewind(void) 
 
Rewind the replay back to the beginning of the file. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplayRaw::Play(void) 
 
Play the replay. This happens by default after a successful Create() and if the 
replay source is enabled. 
 
 
void SPxRadarReplayRaw::Pause(void) 
 
Pause the replay. No further data is sent to the RIB until Play() is called. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 85 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 85 
3.6.2.1 
Sample Code Fragment for Raw Radar Replay 
 
static void handleEvents (SPxRadarReplayRaw*, int reason); 
 
… 
 
// Radar Replay 
 
SPxReplayRawInfo replayInfo; 
 
replayInfo.rangeSamplesPerReturn = 2048; 
 
replayInfo.azimuthsPerScan = 2048; 
 
replayInfo.eventHandler = handleEvents; 
 
replayInfo.flags = 0; 
 
replayInfo.rangeOfFirstSample = 0.0; 
 
replayInfo.rangeOfLastSample = 100.0; 
 
replayInfo.endOfDataMarker = 0xbbbb; 
 
replayInfo.timeBetweenReturns  = (4.0 / 2048 ) * 1000 * 1000; 
 
 
// Create the replay object (ribBuffer already exists). 
 
RadarReplay = new SPxRadarReplayRaw(ribBuffer); 
 
 
// Initialise it. 
 
RadarReplay->Create("C:\\CambridgePixel\\RadarData\\Recording1.dat".   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
&replayInfo ); 
 
RadarReplay->Enable (1);  
 
… 
 
static void handleEvents (SPxRadarReplayRaw* replayObject, int reason) 
{ 
 
if (reason == SPX_REPLAY_EOF) 
 
{ 
 
 
// Rewind and re-enable. 
 
 
replayObject->Rewind(); 
 
 
replayObject->Enable (1); 
 
} 
 
else 
 
{ 
 
 
// Report the error 
 
} 
} 
 
3.7. Input from a User Defined Source 
 
If an existing application is generating radar video programmatically, it can provide 
that video into SPx using the class SPxExternalSource as a convenient interface 
point. 
 
To use the class, create an instance of SPxExternalSource, then call 
SetNumSamples(), SetStartRange() and SetEndRange() to configure the size of the 
returns. Then provide data to the class using NewReturn(), where the return data is 
created by your software as a data block with 8 bits per sample. An example of this 
class being used is provided in Section 3.7.2. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 86 =====
Page 86 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.7.1. 
SPxExternalSource Class Interface 
 
 
SPxExternalSource::SPxExternalSource (SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
Creates a new instance of a SPxExternalSource object, which will be used to 
read radar video from a user-defined source and write into the already-existing 
RIB buffer.  
 
 
SPxExternalSource::~SPxExternalSource() 
 
Destructor for a SPxExternalSource object 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::SetNumSamples(unsigned int numSamples) 
 
Use this function to set the number of samples per return. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxExternalSource::GetNumSamples(unsigned int numSamples) 
 
Return the number of samples per return, as set from the SetNumSamples 
function. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::SetStartRangeMetres(double metres) 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::SetEndRangeMetres(double metres) 
 
Set the start/end range of the return. The units are metres. 
 
 
double SPxExternalSource::GetStartRangeMetres(void) 
double SPxExternalSource::GetEndRangeMetres(void) 
 
Get the start/end range of the return. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::NewReturn(SPxReturnHeader *hdr,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
    unsigned char *data) 
 
Call this function to provide the radar return. The arguments are the formatted 
return header and the data block. See the alternate interface below which fills 
the header out automatically. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 87 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 87 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::NewReturn(UINT16 azi16Bits,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 gapUsecs, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 numBytes, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
unsigned char *data) 
 
Call this function to provide the radar return. The header is automatically filled 
out by the class using the information provided by the client. The minimal 
information required is azi16Bits, which is a 16-bit number representing the 
azimuth value (0..65536 representing 0 to 360 degrees), gapUsecs, which is 
the time in microseconds between returns, numBytes, which is the number of 
bytes of 8-bit data provided, and data, which is the start of the return data. 
 
 
void SPxExternalSource::Enable (int enableState) 
 
The function disables (enableState = 0) or enables the replay object. If any 
other radar sources are providing data into the RIB then they will be disabled 
before enabling the radar replay generator.  
 
 
int SPxExternalSource::GetEnable(void) 
 
Return the current enable state of the radar replay object. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxExternalSource::GetInitialised(void) 
 
This function return SPX_NO_ERROR if the object is initialised. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxExternalSource::GetMsecsSinceLastReturn(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the object generated the 
last radar return. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxExternalSource::GetMsecsSinceLastAziChange(void) 
 
This function returns the number of milliseconds since the object generated a 
change of azimuth in a return. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 88 =====
Page 88 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3.7.2. 
Example Code using SPxExternalSource 
 
This sample code is provided in the example spxextsource.cpp in the 
SPxExamples/SPxExtSource directory (for the purposes of clarity, error checking is 
removed from the code fragment shown).  
 
The code uses SPxExternalSource object as a source of radar video for a RIB. The 
NewReturn function is called to provide the user-created video return.  This example 
assumes that samples are 8-bit. 
 
    /* Create our RIB */ 
    SPxRIB *rib = new SPxRIB(numSamples * 1000, NULL); 
 
    /* 
     * Create our PIM.  In this example, we hardcode 2048 azimuths, but 
     * that should probably depend on the source etc. 
     */ 
    SPxPIM *pim = new SPxPIM(rib, numSamples, 2048, 
 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK, SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, 
 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_OUTPUT(1)); 
 
    /* 
     * Create the network sender. 
     */ 
    SPxNetworkSend *netSend = new SPxNetworkSend(); 
    netSend->Create(hostName); 
 
    /* 
     * Create the standard process that takes the output of the PIM and 
     * passes it to the network sender. 
     */ 
    SPxRunProcess *runProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProNetworkSend, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
NULL, pim, netSend); 
 
    /* 
     * Create our external source object. 
     */ 
    SPxExternalSource *src = new SPxExternalSource(rib); 
 
    /* Configure it. */ 
    src->SetNumSamples(numSamples);   
 
    src->SetEndRangeMetres(rangeMetres); 
    src->Enable(1); 
 
    /* Allocate some memory for building our test data. */ 
    unsigned char *buf = (unsigned char *)malloc(numSamples); 
 
    /* 
     * Run the main loop until/unless we decide to finish. 
     * 
     * This is where an application should acquire/generate the radar data 
     * it wants to feed into the SPx library, calling NewReturn() at the 
     * required PRF. 
     */ 
    int finish = 0; 
    UINT16 azimuth = 0; 
    UCHAR value = 0; 
    while(!finish) 
    { 
 
/* Decide what azimuth to send.  This should be a 16-bit number 
 
 * in the range 0 to 0xFFFF corresponding to 0 to 360 degrees, 
 
 * but note that you don't need to fill every azimuth if the 


===== PAGE 89 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 89 
 
 * source does not have a 16-bit resolution.  In this example, 
 
 * we generate every 32nd azimuth. 
 
 */ 
 
azimuth += 32; 
/* Will wrap around at 0xFFFF */ 
 
 
/* Generate a new return of data, based on an incrementing value.*/ 
 
memset(buf, value++, numSamples); 
 
 
/* Input the return to the SPx library using our source object. */ 
 
if( src->NewReturn(azimuth, 0, numSamples, buf) != SPX_NO_ERROR ) 
 
{ 
 
    printf("Failed to input radar return.\n"); 
 
    break; 
 
} 
 
 
/* Delay a bit before the next return, rather than sending as 
 
 * fast as we can go. 
 
 * 
 
 * NB: This is a very naive way of generating the timing. The 
 
 * sleep function will almost certainly sleep for longer than 
 
 * the requested 1 msec, and thus we will not be generating a 
 
 * 1KHz PRF.  In practice, an application should get the current 
 
 * time after it has slept and calculate how many more returns 
 
 * need to be output based on that time and the desired PRF, but 
 
 * this is just a basic example to demonstrate the use of the 
 
 * SPxExternalSource object, not to demonstrate a radar simulator! 
 
 */ 
 
SPxTimeSleepMsecs(1); 
    }  
}  
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 90 =====
Page 90 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 91 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 91 
4. 
Radar Network Distribution 
 
Radar video may be compressed and distributed on an Ethernet network. The SPx 
classes SPxNetworkSend and SPxNetworkReceive encapsulate the send and receive 
activities for SPx format network radar video.  The SPx classes 
SPxNetworkSendAsterix and SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix encapsulate the send and 
receive activities for ASTERIX Cat-240 format network radar video.  Both sending 
classes support compression and multicast distribution of the video. Any number of 
clients may receive data distributed from a server. 
 
There are two options when distributing radar video onto a network. 
 
Firstly, the SPx classes SPxNetworkSend and SPxNetworkSendAsterix provide a 
programmatic interface that may be used to deliver video onto the network. The 
classes are used with other SPx objects to create a processing chain. Typically, a 
sending class will receive its video from a Radar Source object via a RIB, PIM and 
possibly other processing objects. 
 
Alternatively, radar video may be distributed using the SPx Server application. This is 
a complete server application that is provided as a ready-to-run executable for Linux 
or Windows. The SPx Server accepts radar video from one of a number of standard 
sources (network, hardware etc.), then processes and distributes the video. For most 
applications, using SPx Server is the easiest method to implement radar distribution. 
Internally, SPx Server uses SPxNetworkSend / SPxNetworkSendAsterix as required. A 
requirement needing a customised server configuration may wish to implement a 
server from either of these two classes. 
 
ASTERIX Cat-240 has the advantage of being a recognised standard for network radar 
video; however, the SPx network format provides a number of advantages when used 
with SPx applications.  SPx network video accommodates a variety of different data 
packing formats enabling additional data, such as the state of digital inputs, to be 
transported with the radar data.  It also supports multiple compression types and 
non-rotating scan modes. 
 
4.1. SPxNetworkSend Class 
 
The SPxNetworkSend class provides the base-level compression and network 
transmission of SPx format network radar video. It may also be used as a base class 
when deriving sender classes to handle other network formats. 
 
The class can be used as part of a radar distribution system, with distributed video 
being sent to multiple client machines using a multicast addressing scheme. In this 
way, the total network bandwidth is dependent only on the number of transmitting 
servers, and not on the number of active clients. On each client machine, the class 
SPxNetworkReceive (see Section 3.3.3) provides the receipt and decompression 
capability that is fully compatible with the SPx processing and display objects. 
 
 


===== PAGE 92 =====
Page 92 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
4.1.1. 
Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxNetworkSend::SPxNetworkSend(void) 
 
Constructs a new network sender object. After construction, call Create() to 
initialise the object. 
 
 
SPxNetworkSend::~SPxNetworkSend() 
 
Deletes an existing network sender object. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::Create(char *addr=NULL,  
int port=0, char *ifAddr = NULL) 
 
Initialises a network send object. After construction, the Create function 
initialises the sender to output video to a specified port and IP address. If NULL 
is specified for the address the default value of 
SPX_SCNET_DEFAULT_ADDR_RADAR is used, otherwise addr is an IP address or 
machine name that can be resolved from a name look-up.. If 0 is specified for 
the port, the default value of SPX_SCNET_DEFAULT_PORT_RADAR is used.  
 
The ifAddr argument can be used to optionally specify the address of the local 
network interface through which multicast packets should be sent, either in 
dotted-IP notation, or as a network name that can be looked up, but NOT the 
name of the interface itself (e.g. not “eth0” or “eth1”). If this parameter is 
NULL (which is the default), the operating system is responsible for routing the 
packets to the multicast group according to the configured network routing. 
 
4.1.2. 
Enable 
 
void SPxNetworkSend::SetEnable(int enable) 
 
Enables/disables the transmission of new data from a NewData() call. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkSend::GetEnable(void) 
 
Return the current enable/disable state of the object. 
 
4.1.3. 
Address and Port 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetAddress(char *addr, int port=0) 
 
Set a new address and port for network distribution. The address may be an IP 
address in the form “a.b.c.d”, or a machine name that can be resolved from a 
name look-up server.  
 
 


===== PAGE 93 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 93 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetAddress(UINT32 addr, int port=0) 
 
Set a new address and port number for network distribution. The address is 
given as a 32-bit number in native byte order. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetPort(int port) 
 
 
Set a new port number for distribution. 
 
4.1.4. 
Socket Buffer 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetSndBufSize(unsigned int numBytes) 
 
This function sets the size of the socket buffer for the network send. The socket 
buffer holds data before transmission onto the network. The function sets the 
size of the SO_SNDBUF socket buffer used for sending network packets. A value 
of 0 will cause the SPx default value of 128KB to be requested. There is an 
operating-system imposed limit on the size of the buffer. If a value is requested 
that is larger than the limit, the default value of 128 KB will be used. The actual 
size of the buffer can be retrieved with GetSndBufSize(). 
 
 
unsigned int SPxNetworkSend::GetSndBufSize(void) 
 
This function returns the size of the buffer requested with the most recent 
SetSndBufSize call, or 0 if nothing has been set and so the default buffer size 
(128KB) is being used. Note that the function does not return the actual size of 
the buffer. 
 
4.1.5. 
Repeat Azimuth Control 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetSendRepeatEnable(int isEnabled) 
 
The PIM associated with an SPxNetworkSend object will normally fill any missing 
azimuths by repeating the last azimuth received prior to the missing azimuths.  
By default, the SPxNetworkSend class will not output repeated azimuths created 
by a PIM azimuth fill.  In most applications, the radar video is received into a 
PIM on the client and any missing azimuths are recreated here.  This avoids 
sending unnecessary data across the network.  If required, an SPxNetworkSend 
object may be configured to send repeated azimuths using this function.  This is 
useful if data is received directly from an SPxNetworkReceive object on the 
client without the use of a PIM. To enable the transmission of repeated 
azimuths, set isEnabled to 1. The default is the repeated azimuths are not 
transmitted. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 94 =====
Page 94 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxNetworkSend::GetSendRepeatEnable(void) 
 
Call this function to determine if the sending of repeated azimuths (created by 
an azimuth fill operation in the PIM) is enabled or disabled. The value returned 
is 0 if repeated azimuths are not transmitted (this is the default) and 1 if they 
are transmitted. 
 
4.1.6. 
TTL Control 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetTTL(int ttl) 
  
This function sets the time-to-live setting in the UDP multicast network packets 
being sent by the object.  The default setting of -1 means "not set", in which 
case the value is chosen by the operating system. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkSend::GetTTL(void) 
  
This function returns the current time-to-live setting in the UDP 
multicast network packets being sent by the object.   
 
4.1.7. 
MTU Control 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSend::SetMTU(int sizeBytes) 
  
This function sets the maximum data size of a radar video chunk sent on the 
network.  The default is 1400 bytes.  A value of 0 indicates that the maximum 
size should be used. 
 
 
int SPxNetworkSend::GetMTU(void) 
  
This function returns the current maximum data size of radar video chunks.

void SPxNetworkSend::InstallBandwidthFunc  
                   (void (*fn)(void *userArg, 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxNetworkSend *obj,

This function registers a user-defined function which will be called at regular 
intervals to report network bandwidth. The arguments to 
InstallBandwidthFunc are the function to be called, a user argument (which 
will be passed into the function), and a reporting interval in seconds. The user-
supplied function will be called with a user argument, the Network Send object, 
the encoded bytes per second, and the compression ratio.  
 
 
4.2. SPxNetworkSendAsterix Class 
 
The SPxNetworkSendAsterix class provides the compression and network 
transmission of ASTERIX Cat-240 format radar video. ASTERIX Cat-240 is a 
Eurocontrol standard for the transmission of rotating radar video. 
 
This class can be used as part of a radar distribution system, with distributed video 
being sent to multiple client machines using a multicast addressing scheme. In this 
way, the total network bandwidth is dependent only on the number of transmitting 
servers, and not on the number of active clients. On each client machine the class 
SPxNetworkReceiveAsterix (see Section 3.3.4) provides the receipt and 
decompression capability that is fully compatible with the SPx processing and display 
objects.   
 
The network radar video sent by this class may be received by a third party ASTERIX 
Cat-240 receiver, provided that it supports receipt from a UDP network socket.  If the 
radar video is compressed, the receiver must support ZLIB decompression. Please 
refer to the ASTERIX Cat-240 Network Data Format manual (CP-25-110-83) for 
further details. 


===== PAGE 98 =====
Page 98 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxNetworkSendAsterix is a sub-class of SPxNetworkSend, so may be used wherever 
an SPxNetworkSend instance is needed. 
 
The SPxNetworkSendAsterix class supports all public functions provided by the  
SPxNetworkSend base class. In addition, the following functions are available. 
 
 
4.2.1. 
Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SPxNetworkSendAsterix(void) 
 
Constructs a new ASTERIX Cat-240 network sender object. After construction, 
call SPxNetworkSend::Create() to initialise the object. 
 
 
SPxNetworkSendAsterix::~SPxNetworkSendAsterix() 
 
Deletes an existing network sender object. 
 
 
4.2.2. 
Configuration 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSAC(UINT8 sac) 
 
Set the System Area Code (SAC) that will be included in Video Messages and 
Video Summary Messages. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSIC(UINT8 sic) 
 
Set the System Identification Code (SIC) that will be included in Video 
Messages and Video Summary Messages. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSummaryText(const char *text) 
 
Set the text string that will be included in Video Summary Messages. The 
maximum string length (excluding the null terminator) is 254 characters. If not 
configured, then the string defaults to “Cambridge Pixel SPx.”.   
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::GetSummaryText(char *bufPtr,  
       
 
 
 
 
unsigned int bufSizeBytes) const 
 
Returns the current Video Summary Message text into a buffer referenced by 
bufPtr.  A maximum of bufSizeBytes characters will be written including a 
null terminator. The buffer should therefore be a minimum of 255 bytes to 
enable the largest string to be retrieved. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 99 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 99 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSummaryIntervalMsecs(unsigned int msecs) 
 
Set the interval at which Video Summary Messages are sent when the object is 
enabled (via SetEnable()). The interval is defined in milliseconds. The default 
interval is 1000 milliseconds. Message are sent by a separate worker thread 
and therefore the sending of Video Summary Messages is not dependent on 
SPxNetworkSend::NewData() being called. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetAzimuthLimitDegs(double degs) 
 
This is an advanced setting used to limit the maximum angular size of a cell 
group contained in a single Video Message. The nominal angular size of a cell 
group will be approximately equal to the number of azimuths per sweep divided 
by 360.  However a jump in the perceived antenna position may result in a cell 
group with a larger or smaller angular size. The angular size will not exceed the 
specified limit. The default limit is 5 degrees. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSkipBackJumpMaxDegs(double degs) 
 
This is an advanced setting used to set the maximum backwards jump in 
azimuth that will result in spokes being dropped. Backwards jumps larger than 
this value will be treated as large forward jumps. This setting is useful for 
receivers that cannot handle backwards azimuth jumps. The default value is 5 
degrees. 
 
 
double SPxNetworkSendAsterix::GetSkipBackJumpMaxDegs(void) const 
 
Returns the current maximum backwards jump that will result in skipping 
spokes, in degrees. 
 
 
double SPxNetworkSendAsterix::GetAzimuthLimitDegs(void) const 
 
Returns the current azimuth limit in degrees. 
 
 
void SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetEncodeFormat(int format) 
 
Set the encoding format for the data being transmitted. The options for format 
are: 
 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW 
Uncompressed samples with resolution determined by sample size received 
from the PIM, typically 8-bit or 16-bit samples (although only the top 12 msbs 
are used). 
 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW4 
Uncompressed 4-bit samples. 
 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW2 
Uncompressed 2-bit samples. 
 


===== PAGE 100 =====
Page 100 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW1 
Uncompressed 1-bit samples. 
 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_ZLIB 
Z-Lib compressed samples with resolution determined by sample size received 
from the PIM, typically 8-bit or 16-bit samples (although only the top 12 msbs 
are used). 
 
Please note that ORC compression is not supported for ASTERIX Cat-240 
network radar video. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetRangeResolution 
                                   (RangeResolution rangeRes) 
 
ASTERIX radar video supports two different resolutions for the field that 
specifies the cell size in range. By default, SPxNetworkSendAsterix will use the 
Femto header, unless the radar range implies that the Nano header must be 
used - this corresponds to the RANGE_RES_AUTO (0) range resolution setting. 
Alternatively, the header to use may be explicitly specified as RANGE_RES_FEMTO 
(1) or RANGE_RES_NANO (2). Attempting to use a header that is not suitable for 
the radar range will result in errors being generated and possibly corrupt data. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNetworkSendAsterix::SetSPFEnabled(int isEnabled) 
 
Enable or disable the inclusion of a custom Special Purpose Field (SPF) in 
generated radar video messages. The custom SPF will include azimuth 
reference information and detailed packing type, which may be used by SPx or 
custom client applications. Inclusion of custom SPF data is disabled by default.

4.3. Using SPxNetworkSend and SPxNetworkSendAsterix 
 
The recommended way of using SPxNetworkSend and SPxNetworkSendAsterix is with 
the built-in SPx Process SPxProNetworkSend. Refer to Section 6 for details of the 
processing functions in general and Section 6.7.13 for the network distrbution in 
particular. 
 
The SPxProNetworkSend process will call the NewData() function of a specified 
instance of SPxNetworkSend, allowing the network distribution to be easily combined 
with other SPx objects in an acquisition and processing chain. As an example, a 
simple processing sequence that gets radar video from a test generator, thresholds it 
then distributes the compressed video is accomplished by the following code 
fragment: 
 
 
SPxInit(); 
 
// Create a RIB 
SPxRIB spxRib = new SPxRIB(1024*1024, NULL); 
 
// Create a PIM 
SPxPIM *spxPIM = new SPxPIM(spxRib, 2048, 2048, 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK, SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, 1); 
 
// Create a test generator. 
SPxTestGen *spxTestGen = new SPxTestGenerator(spxRib, 2048, 4.0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1000, 35, 20); 
 
// Create a network send object – use default addressing. 
SPxNetworkSend *spxNet = new SPxNetworkSend(); 
spxNet->Create(); 
spxNet->SetEncodeFormat(SPX_RIB_PACKING_ORC); 
 


===== PAGE 102 =====
Page 102 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
// Create a process to take data from the PIM and threshold it. 
SPxRunProcess *thresholdProcess = new SPxRunProcess(  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxProDynamicThreshold, 
 
 
 
 
 
NULL, spxPim); 
 
// Create a process that takes the thesholded data and distributes it 
SPxRunProcess *netProcess = new SPxRunProcess( SPxProNetworkSend,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
thresholdProcess,  
 
 
 
 
 
spxPim, spxNet); 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 103 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 103 
5. 
Radar Processing 
 
5.1. SPxRIB - Radar Interface Buffer 
 
The Radar Interface Buffer (RIB) is the mechanism for suppliers of radar video (test 
generator, scenario generator, network receiver, record/replay) to interface to 
consumers of that video (processing, display, recording etc.). The RIB provides a 
memory buffer that allows separate software threads to write and read the video. 
 
Data written into the RIB has the format of an SPxReturn, which comprises a header 
and data block. See Section 5.3.2 for details of the SPxReturn structure. 
 
5.2. SPxRIB - Class Interface 
 
The class SPxRIB encapsulates the Radar Input Buffer (RIB), which provides the 
interface between the radar sources and the processing or display modules. The built-
in radar sources include the test pattern generator, scenario generator, radar network 
interface, radar replay and hardware interfacing. These sources handle their own data 
transfers to a RIB that is provided to them. Application code will create the RIB and 
then pass that object to the construction of the appropriate radar source.  
 
It is only necessary to use the RIB read and write functions yourself if you are 
developing a new radar source or a special-purpose processor. The normal way of 
writing radar processing modules does not require direct access to the Read and Write 
functions of the RIB as that is handled by the SPx framework. 
 
SPxRIB::SPxRIB(int size, unsigned char *memory = NULL) 
 
Constructor to create a buffer for receiving radar video. The specified size of the 
buffer is in bytes and should be chosen to allow for storage of a number of 
returns. If memory is non-null it is assumed to be an area of existing memory 
managed by the caller. If memory is NULL, memory is allocated by the routine 
from the heap. 
 
The size of the RIB buffer is important. Radar sources may need to write large 
blocks of data to the RIB to achieve the necessary data throughput. The non 
real-time nature of Windows and Linux, for example, means that the processing 
will happen in discrete timeslices, which requires bursts of data to be 
transferred. The RIB provides the buffering to absorb this but the memory must 
be large enough to store the data.  As a rule of thumb, compute the size of 
data that will be passed to the RIB from a radar source in a 100ms interval and 
set the RIB size to be 5 times that quantity.  
 
Suggested RIB size = PRF * samplesPerReturn / 10 * 5 
 
For example, for a 8 kHz PRF and 2048 samples per return, there is 1640 
kbytes per 100ms interval, giving a suggested RIB size of 8 Mbytes. 
 
 


===== PAGE 104 =====
Page 104 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Other than general resource issues, there is no problem in allocating more 
memory for the RIB size. A larger-than-ideal size has no effect on performance 
or latency. 
 
 
SPxRIB::~SPxRIB() 
 
Deletes a RIB object. If memory was allocated in the constructor, it is deleted in 
the destructor. The RIB should not be deleted until all Radar Source objects 
that could be writing into the RIB have themselves been deleted. This does not 
happen automatically. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRIB::Write(int sizeToWrite,  
 
 
 
unsigned char *dataToWrite,  
 
 
 
int flags=0) 
 
Writes a block of data to a previously created buffer. This routine does not 
block if the buffer is full. The caller is responsible for rescheduling as necessary. 
The length of data being written in bytes is supplied as sizeToWrite and the 
data itself is stored at dataToWrite. Flags is currently unsupported and should 
be set to 0. 
 
The return value is an error code – see below – or SPX_NO_ERROR on success. 
 
SPX_ERR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL – The buffer is too small to accommodate the 
input block. 
 
SPX_ERR_WOULD_BLOCK – There is insufficient free space in the buffer. The caller 
should wait and try again. 
 
SPX_ERR_BAD_ARGUMENT – An input argument is out of range. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRIB::Write(SPxReturnHeader *hdr,  
 
 
 
 
   unsigned char *data, 
 
 
 
 
   int dataLenBytes, int flags = 0) 
 
This is a variant if the Write() function that takes a separate header, hdr, and 
data block, data, and writes them into the buffer in a single operation. The flags 
and error codes are the same as Write(). Refer to Section 5.3.2 for a definition 
of the radar header. 
 
  
int SPxRIB::Read(int sizeToRead,  
 
 
     unsigned char *bufferMemory,  
 
 
     int flags = 0) 
 
Read a block of data from a Radar Input buffer. The number of bytes to read is 
supplied as sizeToRead, and bufferMemory is an area of caller-managed 
memory that the data will be read into. The flags parameter may be set to 
SPX_RIB_READ_ALL_OR_NOTHING to force the routine to read the specified 
number of bytes or else read nothing if that number of bytes is not available. 
 


===== PAGE 105 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 105 
The return value from the function is the number of bytes read or -1 if there 
was an error. 
 
 
int SPxRIB::BytesWaitingInBuffer(void) 
 
Returns the number of bytes waiting to be read from the buffer. Note that after 
calling this function, the data size may change if other threads are reading or 
writing to the buffer. 
 
 
int SPxRIB::SpaceLeftInBuffer(void) 
 
Returns the number of bytes of space in the buffer. 
 
 
SPxThread *SPxRIB::GetThread(void) 
 
Returns the SPx object that represents the thread that this RIB and all 
associated processing runs in. 
 
 
5.3. Format of Radar Video Returns 
 
A radar video return is written into the Radar Input Buffer by a supplier. Radar returns 
are formatted as shown in Table 1. 
 
5.3.1. 
Byte Ordering  
 
The byte order of the header block is big-endian when the data is external to the 
application, i.e. saved to disk or distributed on a network.  The byte ordering of 
samples in the data is specified by the header Data Flags.  
 
For all internal processing, the byte order of the header and data is native. 
 
5.3.2. 
Radar Video Return Header 
 
The data type SPxReturnHeader stores the header information, as defined in Table 1. 
 
    /* Bytes 0 to 3 */ 
    UINT32 magic1; 
 
// Constant magic number for integrity checks 
 
    /* Bytes 4 to 7 */ 
    UINT16 protocolVersion;// Protocol version number 
    UINT8 reserved06; 
    UINT8 headerSize; // Size of header. 
 
    /* Bytes 8 to 11 */ 
    UINT16 radarVideoSize; // Size of encoded radar video data. 
    UINT8 reserved10; 
    UINT8 sourceType; // Source where this return came from 
 


===== PAGE 106 =====
Page 106 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
    /* Bytes 12 to 15 */ 
    UINT8 reserved12; 
    UINT8 sourceCode; // Source-specific code. 
    UINT16 count; 
 
// Incrementing count from source 
 
    /* Bytes 16 to 19 */ 
    UINT16 nominalLength; // Nominal length of the return. 
    UINT16 thisLength; 
// Length of this return (<= nominal length) 
    /* Bytes 20 to 23 */ 
    UINT16 azimuth; 
 
// The azimuth (0..65536) 
    UCHAR packing; 
 
// Packing type, one of SPX_RIB_PACKING_... 
    UCHAR scanMode; 
 
// One of the SPX_SCAN_MODE_... modes. 
 
    /* Bytes 24 to 27 */ 
    UINT32 totalSize; // headerSize + radarVideoSize + extraBytes 
 
    /* Bytes 28 to 31 */ 
    UINT16 heading; 
 
// Platform heading (0..65536 = 0..360degs) 
    UINT16 reserved30; 
 
    /* Bytes 32 to 35 */ 
    UINT16 timeInterval; 
// Interval between returns (microseconds) 
    UINT8 pimFlags; 
 
// Bitwise combination of SPX_PIM_FLAGS_... 
    UINT8 dataFlags; // Bitwise combination of SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAGS_... 
 
    /* Bytes 36 to 39 */ 
    REAL32 startRange; 
// Start range in world units for first 
sample 
 
    /* Bytes 40 to 43 */ 
    REAL32 endRange; // End range in world units for nominalLength. 
 
    /* Bytes 44 to 47 */ 
    UINT32 magic2; 
 
// Magic number for integrity checking 
} SPxReturnHeader; 
 
 
5.3.3. 
Radar Return Structure 
 
The type SPxReturn is defined as: 
 
typedef struct  
{ 
    SPxReturnHeader header; 
    unsigned char data[1]; 
} SPxReturn; 
 
The actual size of the data field is variable, and is specified in the header. 
 
5.3.4. 
Utility Functions 
 
The following utility function may be used to determine the sample size sample based 
on the packing type defined in SPxReturnHeader: 
 
 


===== PAGE 107 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 107 
unsigned int SPxGetPackingBytesPerSample(UCHAR packing) 
 
The packing argument is set to one of the SPX_RIB_PACKING_... packing types.  
The returned value defines the number of bytes per sample for the specified 
packing type.  A value of zero indicates less than one byte per sample or an 
unrecognised packing type (in which case, call 
SPxGetPackingSamplesPerByte() to determine if there is more than one 
sample per byte). 
 
 
unsigned int SPxGetPackingSamplesPerByte(UCHAR packing) 
 
The packing argument is set to one of the SPX_RIB_PACKING_... packing types.  
The returned value defines the number of samples per byte for the specified 
packing type.  A value of zero indicates less than one sample per byte or an 
unrecognised packing type. 
 
5.4. SPxPIM - Polar Interface Modules (PIM) 
 
Although the RIB is a general-purpose circular memory buffer, in the context of SPx it 
is closely coupled with the Polar Interface Module (PIM). The PIM is a module that 
takes video packets out of the RIB and formats and pre-processes them. The specific 
formatting implemented by the PIM is correlation of the video in range and azimuth to 
combine the input data. For example, the PIM can format the video into a defined 
polar-store dimension, correlating returns in azimuth and range using either highest 
wins, lowest wins, averaging or a user-defined method. The PIM also handles 
interference suppression. 
 
A PIM can perform both range and azimuth correlation, although for some situations it 
may do neither. It is possible to configure the PIM to simply pass along every return 
arriving from the source, allowing the subsequent processing to see all the data 
captured from the sensor. 
 
A RIB can serve any number of PIMs and it would be usual to see PIMs working at 
different resolutions to meet the needs of their downstream processes. For example, 
one PIM may be creating a high-resolution polar-store for scan conversion, whereas 
another might be generating a low-resolution store with sub-sampled data to allow 
the display of a snapshot of input video. 
 
5.4.1. 
PIM Input and Outputs 
 
The PIM receives data provided by the RIB, which will have arrived from the radar 
source connected to that RIB. For a radar generating returns at a rate of 500 Hz (the 
PRF), this figure defines the rate of arrival of returns into the RIB and hence into the 
PIM. If the radar has a rotation period of 6 seconds, the PIM will be receiving 3000 
returns per revolution (500Hz x 6 seconds).  
 
The PIM is created with a fixed dimension in azimuth and range, and the input data is 
then processed to fit that size. The PIM reports the receipt of new data to downstream 
processing at an interval that may be configured. 
 


===== PAGE 108 =====
Page 108 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
For example, if the PIM is configured for a range resolution of 2048 and an azimuth 
resolution of 2048, then each input return is stored at the closest azimuth location. In 
the example above, where there were 3000 input returns, these will be stored in 2048 
output positions, so there will be a degree of compression. This is achieved by 
combining a number of returns together using a method such as highest wins or 
averaging. When data is written into the PIM in this way, the PIM reports the arrival of 
new data at a programmable rate called the update interval. If this was set to 32, for 
example, the PIM would report the availability of new data only when it had updated 
32 new positions in its store.  
 
In contrast, the PIM may be configured with an azimuth dimension of 1, which means 
that it provides no azimuth correlation. It reports the arrival of every new return 
(which may still be range correlated if desired), to the downstream processing. 
 
A PIM has a single output process. That process may have one or more output 
processes to form a chain of parallel and serial elements that start from the PIM. 
When the PIM is updated, the attached output process is invoked to process the data 
in the PIM. Once that process has been executed, its output processes will be 
executed in sequence. Refer to Section 6.1 for details of process flow. 
 
5.4.2. 
PIM Scaling 
 
If the number of input returns per scan exceeds the dimension of the PIM store (and 
that dimension is greater than 1) then the PIM uses azimuth correlation to reduce the 
data. The correlation mode may be either overwrite, highest-wins, lowest-wins or 
average. 
 
If the number of input returns per scan is less than the dimension of the store, the 
PIM may be configured to automatically fill in the missing entries by duplicating data. 
This is called azimuth filling.  
 
In range, the PIM will similarly reduce a return that is larger than the PIM dimension 
using sub-sampling, highest-wins, lowest-wins or averaging. If an input return is 
smaller than the PIM size the return will be copied in full, leaving the remainder of the 
line in the PIM as empty. Significantly, the PIM will not expand a return to fill its range 
dimension. 
 
5.4.3. 
Sample Scenarios 
 
The capabilities of the SPx PIM to read and process radar video are explained with 
reference to a number of scenarios.  
 
The scenarios in the table below all work on the same form of input data. The radar is 
assumed to be rotating and providing 3000 returns per scan, where each return is 
2048 samples. This data is written into a RIB with a correctly built header, including 
an incrementing count and azimuth field. We create a PIM to handle processing and 
formatting of the video ahead of our processing function being called.  
 
To understand the different sample modes, the input data is assumed to have its first 
data value in each input return changing in the cycle 0, 1, 2, 3, 0, 1, 2, 3 etc., and 
subsequent samples in each azimuth incrementing from the start value. The 
organisation of processing modules is show in Figure 18.

5.4.4. 
Sparse Azimuths and Azimuth Repeat 
 
The scenarios considered previously assumed that the incoming data was providing a 
steady stream of contiguous azimuths. In practice, it is possible that the azimuth 
count is not contiguous. Two situations are considered. In the first, the incoming 
azimuth count does not increase in a uniform manner, although for the purposes of 
this analysis we will assume that it does change in the same direction. In the second 
situation, the azimuths increases as before, but the polar store is larger than the 
number of incoming azimuths. 
 
Both of these situations generate the same problem. Without further consideration, 
they would cause some azimuths in the PIM polar-store not to be updated. Consider 
the situation shown in Figure 19. For this example, the test generator is configured to 
generate 500 returns per scan, but the polar-store is configured for 2048 azimuths. 
With azimuth repeat disabled (right side picture), the display shows the discrete 
spokes corresponding to the returns. With azimuth repeat enabled (left side picture) 
the spacing is filled in by copying the previous azimuth. In this situation, an azimuth 
fill value of at least 4 is required to ensure that all azimuths are filled. 
 
Azimuth repeat is the method of compensating for missing returns by inserting copies 
of the previous one. Suppose the PIM receives requests to update azimuths 2, 3 then 
5, i.e. missing 4. With azimuth repeat disabled, this would mean that azimuth 4 was 
automatically filled with zero. With Azimuth Repeat enabled, azimuth 4 would be filled 
with a copy of azimuth 3. 
 
Azimuth repeat is enabled by default for a new PIM. It can be changed with the class 
member SetAzimuthRepeat. The repeat number defines the maximum number of 
copies of an azimuth. Although, in principle, azimuth repeat can be used for any 
number of missing azimuths, it generally makes sense to use a small value, as 
repeating too much data is often not the right solution (for example, a smaller 
azimuth size for the PIM might be more appropriate). 
 
Figure 19 - Azimuth fill enabled (left) and disabled (right) 
 
The control of azimuth filling is affected by the following quantities: the Azimuth Clear 
Limit (ACL), Azimuth Filling Enable (AFE) and the Azimuth Repeat Limit (ARL). This is 
summarised by the flow chart shown in Figure 20. In terms of configuring the 
parameters that affect this process, the following API functions on the PIM are used: 
ACL is set through SetAzimuthClearLimit(). The default value is 32 PIM azimuths. 


===== PAGE 113 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 113 
AFE is set through EnableFill(). The default value is TRUE. This is almost always the 
desired value. There are very few cases where fillign should be disabled. 
 
ARL is set through SetAzimuthRepeat(). The default value is 10 PIM azimuths. 
 
With the default values of the parameters, the behaviour of the filling is as follows. If 
the azimuth jump is larger than or equal to 32 azimuths forward, no filling is done. If 
the azimuth jump is less than 32 then the last azimuth is repeated up to 10 times to 
close the gap, with any additional gap filled by black. 
 
Figure 20 - Flow Chart for Azimuth Filling

remaining azimuths up 
to A2. 
 
Finish 
N 
Y 
N

forward from A1 to A2

ACL is set through 
SetAzimuthClearLimit().

ARL is set through

It should be noted that azimuth repeat is generally only needed for display-related 
functions. The SPx library marks repeated azimuths (using the pimFlags in the 
header) so that processes (such as network distribution and recording) do not need to 
process duplicated data. 
 
5.4.5. 
Scanning Modes 
 
The radar video presented as input to the PIM may be derived from a number of 
different radar types having different scanning modes. The simplest form of scanning 
mode is a radar that rotates and presents azimuth numbers that increase. The general 
form of scanning mode is random scan, in which there is no particular pattern to the 
sequence of azimuths. The behaviour of the PIM (and other SPx processing) can be 
different for different scanning modes.  
 
The scanning mode is indicated in the SPx header of the radar video using the 
ScanMode field. This field is set automatically when using Cambridge Pixel’s input 
classes for the HPx radar input cards or SPx Network input for custom radar 
interfaces. If using a user-defined input source (for example, SPxExternalSource) 
then the correct value of ScanMode must be set in the radar header. If it is not set, 
the default value of SPX_SCAN_MODE_ROTATING is assumed. 
 
The supported options for ScanMode and the corresponding behaviour in the PIM are 
summarised in Table 5. 
 
ScanMode  
Symbolic Name (Value) 
Description 
PIM Behaviour 
Azimuth 
Filling 
Available 
SPX_SCAN_MODE_ROTATING (0) 
Normal rotating 
situation.

Table 5 - Scan Mode Options for PIM Processing 


===== PAGE 115 =====
