Page 115 
5.4.6. 
Random Scan Radar Input 
 
For this next set of scenarios, the radar input data is assumed to derive from a 
random scan radar, meaning that the azimuth number of the returns will not be 
following an increasing count. In principle, each return may have a different and 
unpredictable azimuth. For the purposes of illustration, the azimuth sequence is 
assumed to cycle around the following list: 
 
{0, 1000, 1000, 2000, 4000, 0, 8000, 3000, 16000} 
 
(Azimuths are scaled in the range 0 .. 64k representing 0 to 360 degrees.) 
 
Consider the scenarios from Table 4 again: 
 
Scenario 1 – The processing function is called for every return as expected. 
 
Scenario 2 -  The processing function sees only azimuth numbers 1000 and 
8000, corresponding to azimuths in the sequence 2, 6, 10, 14 etc. 
 
Scenario 6 – The processing function gets called for every new azimuth that is 
written into the PIM. The two returns at azimuth 1000 are integrated before 
they get reported. The processing function is called with the new PIM azimuth 
that has changed. Unlike with scenario 1, the processing function has the 
opportunity to defer processing of this new azimuth until later – since it has 
been written and retained into the polar-store. However, it is likely that the 
processing function would want to store an indication that the azimuth has been 
updated.  
 
Scenario 7 – With the request to generate reports every 64 azimuths 
combined with the sub-sampling of the input azimuths, the effect is to never 
generate a processing function. The report delivery software is waiting for the 
PIM azimuth to advance by 64, but it never does. 
 
When using random scan inputs, it is necessary to generate a report on every source 
or PIM azimuth. Data can be sub-sampled or correlated as desired, but a report must 
be generated on every azimuth to avoid missing data or even not generating a report 
at all. 
 
Note that if the radar is known to be random or sector scanning then it is necessary to 
turn off the automatic azimuth filling that goes on for missing azimuths. Otherwise, a 
step change in azimuth would cause the processing to fill all missing azimuths with 
zero data. 
 
5.4.7. 
Interference Suppression 
 
The PIM can be used for removal of interference from nearby radars. In the example 
shown in Figure 21, the left-hand picture shows a radar image with interference from 
adjacent radars. The exact nature of the interference display is affected by the 
transmission frequency of the adjacent radars. In the example shown, the near radial 
nature of the lines indicates that the nearby radars are transmitting on a very similar 
frequency (probably similar radar types). The image on the right shows the same 
radar image after the PIM has been used for interference removal. The processing is 
to eliminate samples which only occur on a single return. These returns are assumed 


===== PAGE 116 =====
Page 116 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
to be transmit pulses received from nearby radars, as target echoes returned from our 
radar would appear in multiple returns. 
 
The SPx PIM function SetInterferenceLevel, which controls the interference 
suppression process, is described in Section 5.5.5. 
 
Figure 21 - Example of Interference Suppression and removal using PIM Processing 
 
5.5. SPxPIM Class Interface 
5.5.1. 
PIM Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxPIM::SPxPIM(SPxRIB *buffer, 
 
 
unsigned rangeDim,  
 
 
unsigned azimuthDim,  
 
 
SPxPIMrangeCombine rangeSubSample = SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK,  
 
 
SPxPIMazimuthCombine azimuthSubSample = SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, 
 
 
SPxPIMupdateInterval updateInterval = SPX_PIM_OUTPUT(1), 
 
 
unsigned int maxBytesPerSample=1); 
 
Creates a PIM object to provide polar storage for radar video. The dimensions 
of the polar-store are defined in the constructor using rangeDim and 
azimuthDim. The PIM is responsible for transferring the data from the radar 
input buffer and reducing (or expanding) the data to fit in the polar-store 
dimensions. The PIM implements various forms of data reduction to map the 
incoming video into the selected polar-store size. The arguments are as follows: 
 
buffer – A previously created SPxRIB for the situation where this PIM is 
updated from a radar source. If a PIM is being created as an intermediate data 
store, for example to hold the output of a process, then provide NULL for this 
parameter.  
 
rangeDim – The range dimension of the polar-store. 
 
azimuthDim – The azimuth dimension of the polar-store. 
 
rangeSubSample – The mode of range combination to map the incoming 
samples into the polar-store. Permissible values are: 
 
 


===== PAGE 117 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 117 
SPX_PIM_RAN_SUBSAMPLE 
SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK 
SPX_PIM_RAN_MIN 
SPX_PIM_RAN_SMOOTH 
 
If there are more samples in the incoming video than required in the polar-
store, the mode SPX_PIM_RAN_SUBSAMPLE causes the incoming data to be 
reduced by sub-sampling. For example, if the incoming data has 4096 sample 
per return and rangeDim is 1024 then SPX_PIM_RAN_SUBSAMPLE will cause every 
1 in 4 samples to be used and the remainder to be discarded. With the other 
modes, every range sample from the input is considered. For example, with 
4096 input samples and 1024 output samples, 4 samples are read and 
considered as a group. For SPX_PIM_RAN_MIN the lowest sample from the group 
is used. For SPX_PIM_RAN_SMOOTH, an average of the sample is calculated. In 
addition to these built-in modes, it is possible to create additional combine 
modes through the function SPxSetUserCombineTable().  
 
azimuthSubSample – The mode of azimuth combination to map the incoming 
azimuths into rows of the polar-store. Permissible values are: 
 
SPX_PIM_AZI_SUBSAMPLE 
SPX_PIM_AZI_OVERWRITE 
SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK 
SPX_PIM_AZI_MIN  
SPX_PIM_AZI_SMOOTH  
 
As for the range dimension, azimuth sub-sampling of SPX_PIM_AZI_SUBSAMPLE 
means that incoming azimuths are dropped to reduce the quantity to the 
dimension of the polar-store. For SPX_PIM_AZI_OVERWRITE, no combination 
occurs, but every azimuth is written into the polar-store. For 
SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, a number of azimuths are combined using a peak-picking 
algorithm, For SPX_PIM_AZI_MIN, a lowest-wins combination is used, and for 
SPX_PIM_AZI_SMOOTH data across a number of azimuths is averaged. 
 
updateInterval – The interval between report notifications being generated 
and whether these reports are done in input or output azimuth updates. To get 
a report generated every nth input returns use SPX_PIM_INPUT(val), where a 
val of 1 means a report for every input azimuth, a value 2 means every other 
input return. Use SPX_PIM_OUTPUT to get a report generated on every new 
azimuth updated in the PIM polar-store, with a value of 4, for example, 
meaning that a report is generated every the update of every 4th azimuth of the 
PIM store.  
 
maxBytesPerSample – Use this argument to set the maxium sample size that 
the PIM will accept and therefore the amount of memory that will be allocated 
by the PIM.  If the PIM is required to process only one-byte samples then this 
argument can be set to 1 or omitted.  This is the default setting.  If the PIM is 
required to process  two-byte samples then this argument should be set to 2.  A 
PIM configured with a maximum sample size of 2 will handle both one-byte and 
two-byte samples.  A PIM configured with a maximum sample size of 1 will 
reject two-byte samples and report error #166. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 118 =====
Page 118 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxPIM::~SPxPIM(void) 
 
Deletes a PIM. 
 
5.5.2. 
PIM Clearing 
 
void SPxPIM::Clear(void) 
 
Clears the PIM. The contents of the PIM are set to zero. 
 
5.5.3. 
PIM State Querying 
 
int SPxPIM::GetAzimuthDim(void) 
 
Return the azimuth dimension of the PIM. 
 
 
SPxPIMazimuthCombine SPxPIM::GetAzimuthSubSample(void) 
 
Returns the current azimuth sub-sample mode. Refer to the documentation for 
the class constructor for permissible values for the mode. 
 
 
double SPxPIM::GetPRF(void) 
 
Returns the instantaneous PRF estimate based on the timing information 
provided in the header of the most recent return. Not all sources set the PRF (in 
which case 0 will be returned). 
 
 
int SPxPIM::GetRangeDim(void) 
 
Return the range dimension of the PIM. 
 
 
SPxPIMrangeCombine SPxPIM::GetRangeSubsample(void) 
 
Return the current range sub-sample mode. Refer to the documentation for the 
class constructor for permissible values for the mode. 
 
 
int SPxPIM::GetActive(void) const 
 
Return the current activity state of the PIM as 0 for inactive and 1 for active. 
 
 
int SPxPIM::GetLastInputAzimuth(void) 
 
Returns the azimuth of the last radar return that was processed by the PIM. 
The azimuth number is in the range 0 to 65536, representing 0 to 360 degrees. 
The value of SPX_PIM_LAST_AZI_START_CODE is returned if no returns have 
been received by the PIM. 
 


===== PAGE 119 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 119 
int SPxPIM::GetLastUpdatedAzimuth(void)  
 
Returns the number of the azimuth in the PIM store that was most recently 
updated. The azimuth number is normally in the range 0 to GetAzimuthDim(), 
although the value SPX_PIM_LAST_AZI_START_CODE will be returned if no data has yet 
been written into the PIM store. 
 
 
SPxRunProcess *SPxPIM::GetOutputProcess(void) 
 
Return the SPxRunProcess object that is the first process connected to this PIM. 
There can only be one process connected directly to a PIM. 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetActive(int active) 
 
If active = 0, the PIM is inactive, meaning it does not process new returns. Set 
active to 1 to enable the PIM (the default mode). Note that if a PIM is made 
inactive, all processes that are downstream of that PIM will not receive data. 
 
5.5.4. 
PIM Correlation and Combination Modes 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxSetUserCombineTable(int index,  
 
 
 
unsigned char (*userTable)[256][256]) 
  
This function, which is global and not a member function for the PIM, allows the 
creation of user-defined combination modes. The index specifies the table to 
change (from 0 to 31). The input data is a 256 x 256 table that is indexed by 
the existing value (first index) and the new value (second index). One 
application of a user-defined table is to address the situation where the input 
video is composed of multiple channels encoded in an 8-bit value. For example, 
two 4-video videos or a 6-bit video and 2 x 1-bit videos. In this situation, a 
user-defined table is needed to implement the correct highest wins processing 
on each sample. 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetAzimuthSubsample(SPxPIMazimuthCombine mode) 
 
Changes the azimuth sub-sampling mode for the PIM. Refer to the 
documentation for the class constructor for permissible values for the mode. 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetRangeSubsample(SPxPIMrangeCombine mode) 
 
Changes the range sub-sampling mode for the PIM. Refer to the documentation 
for the class constructor for permissible values for the mode. 
 
 


===== PAGE 120 =====
Page 120 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
5.5.5. 
Interference Suppression 
 
The PIM can be used to remove video that does not correlate from pulse to pulse. This 
video could be derived from an adjacent radar and therefore appears as interference. 
See Figure 21 for an example. 
 
void SPxPIM::SetInterferenceLevel(unsigned int level) 
 
The interference suppression is controlled by this function. Setting the level to 0 (the 
default) disables interference suppression. Setting it to 1, 2 or 3 (the max) enables 
increasingly strong interference suppression. The value of level defines the number of 
preceding spokes in which a range sample must be high to allow it to be high in the 
output spoke. 
 
The side-effect of this suppression is that the leading edge in azimuth of 
objects/targets/coastlines etc. will be suppressed too, slightly changing the weighted 
centre of targets and making them smaller. 
 
5.5.6. 
PIM Update Reporting 
 
void SPxPIM::SetUpdateInterval(int updateInterval) 
 
The azimuth interval between reports being generated to the Process Manager. 
The value can be specified as either the number of input returns or the number 
of output azimuths. Use SPX_PIM_INPUT() or SPX_PIM_OUTPUT() macros with 
the update interval as an argument. 
 
 
int SPxPIM::GetUpdateInterval(void) 
 
Return the current update interval for the PIM. The value is returned as a 
positive number for reporting that is on the input returns, or negative for 
reporting that is on the output azimuths. 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetConstantAziLimit(unsigned int limit) 
 
This functions sets a limit on the number of returns that will be combined at the 
same position if the azimuth is not changing (when in output-based reporting 
mode).  If the limit is reached, the combined return is output for processing 
even if the azimuth stays constant. 
 
If set to zero (the default), this feature is disabled, i.e. no spokes will be output 
until the azimuth changes. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxPIM::GetConstantAziLimit(void) 
 
Returns the number of returns that will be combined at the same azimuth, as 
set from SetConstantAziLimit(). 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 121 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 121 
5.5.7. 
PIM Azimuth Filling and Clearing 
 
void SPxPIM::EnableFill(int enable) 
 
If enable = 1 (the default) the PIM will fill missing azimuths by copying the 
most recent one (up to the repeat limit set with SetAzimuthRepeat) and then 
clearing the remainder (up to the limit set with SetAzimuthClearLimit). If 
enable = 0, both the copying of azimuths and the clearing of missing ones is 
disabled. There are very few situations where EnableFill should be disabled. For 
most situations (including normal rotating radars and random scan), the 
internal logic of the PIM handles the clearing correctly. Turning off filling 
(enable = 0) can cause undesirable effects where azimuths in the PIM are not 
updated with incoming data (yet are potentially affected by subsequent 
processing, results in undesirable consequences). 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetAzimuthClearLimit(unsigned azimuthClearLimit) 
 
Set the maximum number of azimuths that can be jumped for azimuth filling to 
occur. If the azimuth of returns entered into the PIM are not increasing by 1, 
such that an azimuth in the PIM could miss an update, the PIM can 
automatically clear the affected azimuths and possibly fill them from the more 
recent azimuth. If the observed azimuth jump exceeds or equals the value set 
by this function then no azimuth filling is done. If the azimuth jump is less than 
this value then a number of azimuths (as defined by the SetAzimuthRepeat() 
function) are filled, and then the remaining azimuths up to the end of the jump 
are filled with black. See the flow chart in Figure 20 for details. 
 
For random or sector scan radars, where the azimuth number is known to shift 
around, it is undesirable to attempt to fill in missing azimuths. In this situation 
it is desirable to turn off azimuth clearing. 
 
 
void SPxPIM::SetAzimuthRepeat(unsigned azimuthRepeat) 
 
Set the azimuth repeat value on the PIM object. If azimuths are missing from 
the incoming data, the use of azimuth fill allows for automatic insertion of data 
as copies of the previous one. For example, if the input azimuth count jumps 
from 20 to 25, a value of azimuth fill of at least 4 would cause the PIM to 
automatically copy azimuth 20 into the next 4 locations. Note that the azimuth 
fill value is the number of azimuths in the PIM polar-store, not the incoming 
data. Azimuth filling is only effective if EnableFill(TRUE) is called and if the 
azimuth jump is less than the limit set by SetAzimuthClearLimit(). See the 
flow chart in Figure 20 for details. 
 
5.5.8. 
PIM Azimuth Offset 
 
void SPxPIM::SetAzimuthOffset(int offset) 
 
This function defines an azimuth offset to be added to each azimuth that is 
subsequently processed by the PIM. The input offset is in the range -32768 to 
+32768 to represent angles in the interval -180 to +180 degrees. For example, 
to apply an azimuth offset of 5 degrees, use an argument of 5 * 32768 / 180, 


===== PAGE 122 =====
Page 122 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
and -2 degrees would be -2 * 32768 / 180. Supply an offset of 0 to return to 
the default state of no azimuth offset. If North Offset adjustment is being used, 
the total azimuth adjustment is the sum of the two components. 
 
 
int SPxPIM::GetAzimuthOffset(void) 
 
This function returns the current azimuth offset. The value will be in the range -
32768 to +32768, representing angles in the range -180 degrees to +180 
degrees. 
 
5.5.9. 
PIM North Offset Adjustment for Moving Platforms 
 
void SPxPIM::NorthReference(SPxNavData *navData, SPxAziRefMode mode) 
 
This function associates the PIM with a SPxNavData object (see Section 
6.8.13.1), which allows the PIM to reference its azimuth data according to the 
ship’s course or heading provided by the NavData object. Use this function if 
the video data provided to the PIM is ship-heading relative and it is desired to 
convert it into north-relative data or vice versa. Note that the azimuth 
adjustment implied by this function operates in addition to the azimuth 
adjustment set through the SetAzimuthOffset function. The mode options are 
defined in Table 6.

5.5.10. 
PIM Copy 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPIM::CopyFromPIM(SPxPIM *src) 
 
This function copies data from the specified source PIM, src, into the PIM. The 
PIMs must be the same size or else an SPX_ERR_PIM_MATCH results. The video 
data and the header are copied for each azimuth. 
 
5.5.11. 
 Saving and Loading PIMs to File 
 
The following functions save a PIM to file and restore from a file. The format of the file 
is not defined. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPIM::SaveToFile(const char *filename) 
 
This function saves the PIM to a file. The specified filename should be provided 
as a relative or absolute path. A PIM saved to file using this function may be 
read using the related LoadFromFile() function. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPIM::LoadFromFile(const char *filename) 
 
This function loads a PIM from the contents of a file. The file should have been 
created using the SaveToFile() function. The specified file name should be 
provided as a relative or absolute path.

5.5.13. 
PIM Debug 
 
void SPxPIM::SetDebug(UINT32 debug) 
 
This function enables debug on this instance of the PIM. If SetLogFile() has 
not been previously called then debug output is sent to the process stdout. 
Otherwise debug is sent to the FILE specified in the most recent call to 
SetLogFile(). Note that SetLogFile() is class static, meaning that the same 
log file will be used for all PIM debugging.  
 
The debug flag provided as an input argument may be a bitwise combination of 
the following flags (with associated numerical values) 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_INPUT_SUMMARY (value 0x01 ) 
Provides a summary of the PIM update for each input azimuth. 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_UPDATES (value 0x02) 
Provides a detailed log of PIM processing activity 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_UPDATES_HEADER (value 0x04)  
Provides additional details on the contents of the return header on each update 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_UPDATE_STATE (value 0x08)  
Displays additional details on the state of the PIM at each update 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_NAV_DATA (value 0x10) 
Displays additional details relating to azimuth adjustment from navigation data. 
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_VIDEO_QUICKLOOK (value 0x40) 
Displays a quick summary of details from the video data on the desitination set 
by SetLogFile(). The summary includes the minimum and maximum sample 
values in the data and a print out of the first 16 samples of the return.  
 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_VIDEO (value 0x20) 
Write the header and video data to the logfile specified by SetDataLogFile(). 
This debugging option can generate very large amount of data and in general 
should be used for short durations. The debugging mode is only effective if 
SetDataLogFile() has been called to set the file to be used for output. Note 
that since the data to be dumped in binary, it is not sensible to use stdout for 
the field destination. 
 
 
static void SPxPIM::SetLogFile(FILE *logFile) 
static FILE * SPxPIM::GetLogFile(void 
 
Sets/Gets the current log file to be used for debugging. If SetLogFile() has 
not been called when SetDebug() is called then stdout is used for the debug 
output. Note that the logfile is class static, so that all instances of PIM will have 
the same logfile. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 126 =====
Page 126 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxPIM::SetDataLogFile(FILE *file) 
 
This sets the log file to be used for output of video data when the 
SPX_PIM_DEBUG_VIDEO flag is used.

SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 127 
6. 
Processing Modules 
 
SPx has a number of pre-defined processing modules and you can add to these by 
defining your own. The processing modules can be connected in a user-defined way 
and are logically processed in a chain from the output of the PIM. Often, but not 
necessarily, the processing terminates in a display process. A processing module can 
have one input from another process, but can supply its output to a number of 
processes. 
 
In SPx a processing element is called a Process. An SPx Process is not the same as a 
process in the operating system. An SPx application may run as a single process in 
the operating system (Linux or Windows), or it may run as a number of processes, 
possibly with a network between. Within an operating system process, there are 
typically multiple threads of execution. Some SPx processing elements create their 
own threads and the user has control over how SPx Processes are mapped into 
threads. On a modern multi-core processor, the operating system is able to run 
different threads on different cores. This allows SPx to fully exploit the ability of the 
processor to run multiple activities concurrently.  
 
The two principal SPx classes that support processing are SPxProcess and 
SPxRunProcess. The SPxProcess class describes a processing function. The 
SPxRunProcess class represents an invocation of a process. For example, the object 
SPxProThreshold is a pre-defined instance of SPxProcess, which is part of the SPx 
signal processing library. To use the SPxProThreshold object to process data we 
create an instance of SPxRunProcess and associate it with the both the 
SPxProThreshold object and the PIM or other process that provides the data input. 
 
6.1. Process Control and Data Flow 
 
The SPx data processing chain starts from a PIM. The chain connects a number of 
processes together and concludes with a process that has no output process (for 
example, a display process). Each process in the chain can be a SPx library process or 
a user-defined process. A process accepts data from one source, but may connect to 
multiple processes on the output side. In Figure 22, the processes A, B, C are 
connected as a chain and the processing sequence is A, B, C. 
 
Figure 22 - Simple Processing Chain 
 
 
RIB 
PIM 
Process 
A 
From radar 
source 
Process 
B 
Process 
C 


===== PAGE 128 =====
Page 128 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
If there are multiple outputs for a process, the order of execution of those output 
processes is the order that they were created. The processing executes depth first. 
For example, with reference to Figure 23, the process execution order is A, B, E, C, F, 
D (assuming that the processes are created in the sequence B, C, D). 
 
Figure 23 - Process Execution Order 
 
Consider the sequence of processes shown in Figure 24. The first process, Sector 
Blank, is connected to the PIM. The output of this process goes to a Threshold process 
and then to a Scan Conversion process. 
 
Figure 24 - Example Process Flow 
 
The solid lines linking the PIM and processes in Figure 24 show the processing 
sequence. The dash lines show the data flow, which indicates that each process reads 
and modifies the single PIM. 
 
Consider the processing sequence shown in Figure 25. In this situation, the output of 
PIM 1 goes into a Threshold process, which provides output to PIM-2. PIM-2 must 
have been created by the application and provided to the Threshold process as its 
output PIM. The two scan conversion processes then work on PIM-1 and PIM-2 
respectively. The dash lines in the diagram show the data flow. It is important to note 
although Scan Conversion 2 processes the data from PIM-2 it must still be attached to 
the Scan Conversion 1 process to achieve the necessary control flow. Significantly, 
Scan Conversion 2 would not get executed if it were connected to PIM 2 in the control 
sequence (for the purposes of control flow, a process must take input from another 
process or from a PIM that is driven by a RIB).

6.2. Process Activity 
 
A process may be active or inactive. If it is inactive, it means the process is not 
executed, but this does not break the processing chain. For example, in Figure 23 if 
Process A is made inactive the remaining processes B to F are still executed (unless 
they themselves are inactive). 
6.3. Processing Threads 
 
All processing downstream of a RIB runs in a thread created by the RIB. The function 
GetThread() may be used to retrieve the thread object from RIB. This thread object 
is of type SPxThread and the underlying thread handle may be retrieved using 
GetHandle() defined as: 
 
HANDLE SPxThread::GetHandle(void) 
 
 
for Windows and  
 
pthread_t SPxThread::GetHandle(void) 
 
 
for Linux. 
 
A new thread may be created using the process SPxProToRIB (see Section 6.7.19), 
which copies data from a PIM through a RIB into another PIM. The second PIM has a 
new thread associated with it and so all processing downstream of that RIB runs in 
that thread. 
 
6.4. Process Data Formats 
 
SPx processes can support data formats of 8 bits, 12 bits and 16 bits per sample. The 
standard is 8 bits and all process support this format.  
 
For each of these data word sizes, there can be multiple videos represented in the 
word. For example, an 8-bit radar format could represent one video having 8 bits of 
resolution, or alternatively a “7+1” format with one video having 7 bits and one video 
having 1 bit (for example to represent a background video and 1 bit of MTI or IFF). 
The significance of the 7+1 organisation is that a process needs to treat the two video 
RIB 
PIM-1 
Process 
Threshold 
Process 
Scan 
Conversion 
1 
From radar 
source 
Process 
Scan 
Conversion 
2 
PIM-2 


===== PAGE 130 =====
Page 130 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
components separately when processing the 8-bit sample. Other possible data formats 
in an 8-bit sample are 6+2, 6+1+1, 5+3, 5+2+1 etc. 
 
SPx processes are sensitive to the format of the radar video supplied to them. In 
general, all of the processes support a standard format of 8 bits, representing one 
channel, of video. Selected processes support 12 or 16-bit samples. 
 
The following formats are supported by SPx processes: 
 
Format name 
Interpretation 
8 
Single video, 8 bits per sample 
<8 
8 bits per sample with multiple videos, e.g. 7+1, 6+1+1 etc. 
<8* 
8 bits per sample with multiple videos, e.g. 7+1, 6+2, 5+3, 4+4 etc. 
Only the ‘main’ video is processed, i.e. the 7, 6 or 5-bit video. 
 
(Note: If a process supports <8, it will support <8*) 
4+4 
Two 4-bit samples, derived from two channels, packed into a byte. 
12 
Single video, 12 bits per sample 
12+1+1+1+1 
16 bits per sample organised as 12 +1 + 1 +1 (4 videos) 
12+1+1+1+1* 
16 bits per sample organised as 12 +1 + 1 +1 (4 videos). Only the 
12-bit video is processed. 
 
(Note: If a process supports 12+1+1+1+1, it will support 
12+1+1+1+1*) 
16 
Single video, 16 bits per sample 
8+8 
16 bits per sample, organised as 8 + 8 (2 videos, 8 bits each). 
8+8* 
16 bits per sample, organised as 8 + 8 (2 videos, 8 bits each). Only 
the first 8-bit video is processed. 
 
(Note: If a process supports 8+8, it will support 8+8*) 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 131 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 131 
6.5. SPxProcess Class 
 
The SPxProcess class encapsulates a description of a process. You will only need to 
create new instances of SPxProcess if you are creating new processes. If you are 
using existing processes in the library, then create an instance of SPxRunProcess (see 
Section 6.6). 
 
 
SPxProcess::SPxProcess(char *name, SPxProcFunc processingFunction, 
                                   SPxProcFuncChanges changeFn=NULL, 
                                   SPxProcFuncCleanup cleanupFn=NULL) 
 
Creates a new processing module. A processing module can subsequently be 
used to create an SPxRunProcess object, which invokes a SPxProcess to 
processes data. 
 
The specified process name is the generic name for the process. 
 
The processingFunction is a pointer to a function that executes the process. 
The function will be called with arguments set to the SPXRunProcess context, 
the first return to be processed, the azimuth location in the PIM containing the 
data to process, and the size of that data in azimuths. The function is defined 
by: 
 
typedef void (*SPxProcFunc)(SPxRunProcess*,  
               SPxReturn*, unsigned startAzimuth,  
                           unsigned azimuthSize) 
 
The changeFn is a pointer to a function that executes when a change is 
reported in the RIB source for this process. The function is optional and may be 
omitted if the process does not need to respond to change events. The type of 
the function is defined as: 
 
typedef int (*SPxProcFuncChanges)(SPxRunProcess *rp, UINT32 flag) 
 
The flag is reported as a bitwise combination of the following: 
 
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_INIT 
Initialisation  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_TYPE 
File/net/hw etc.  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_CONTENT 
Filename, hw chan  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_DIMENSIONS 
Num azis/samples  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_RANGE 
Start/end range  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_TIMING 
Period, PRF  
SPX_RIB_CHANGED_SCANMODE 
Random rotate  
 
The cleanupFn is a pointer to a function that executes when an SPxRunProcess 
object using this Process is deleted. The function is optional and may be 
omitted if no special cleanup is required by the processing. The function is 
defined by: 
 
 


===== PAGE 132 =====
Page 132 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
typedef void (*SPxProcFuncCleanup)(SPxRunProcess *rp) 
and is called in the destructor for a SPxRunProcess object. One use for the 
cleanup function is when the processingFunction of the Process creates per-
instance data to support the processing. For example, an Integration function 
might need to create an internal datastore to hold the intermediate result of the 
clutter processing. The process would create this memory using malloc or free 
on its first time of execution. A pointer to the allocated memory could be stored 
in the Arg2 field of the SPxRunProcess (see Section 6.6). Then, on deletion of 
the RunProcess, the cleanup function can free the memory pointer held in the 
Arg2 field. 
 
 
void SPxProcess::AddParameterI(char *name, int defaultValue); 
void SPxProcess::AddParameterR(char *name, double defaultValue); 
void SPxProcess::AddParameterS(char *name, char *defaultValue); 
 
Adds a new parameter to the class description. The parameter is either an 
integer, a real or a null-terminated character string and a default value is 
provided. The specified name will be used to reference the parameter to set and 
retrieve its value. 
 
 
SPxProcessParameter *SPxProcess::GetFirstParameter(void) 
 
Returns an object representing the first parameter defined for this process. 
Subsequent parameters may be retrieved by calling GetNext() on the 
SPxProcessParameter, until the next one is NULL. The SPxProcessParameter 
instance that is returned from this call supports the GetType() and GetName() 
operations to retrieve the type and name of the parameter. The permissible 
values for type are SPX_PROC_PARAM_TYPE_INT, SPX_PROC_PARAM_TYPE_REAL 
and SPX_PROC_PARAM_TYPE_STRING. The name of the parameter is the name 
supplied in the original AddParameter function.  
 
 
char * SPxProcess::GetName(void) 
 
Returns the name of the process. The string returned in owned by the class and 
should not be modified or deleted. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxProcess::SetMaxBytesPerSample(unsigned int sizeBytes) 
 
Set the maximum sample size in bytes supported by this process (either 1 or 2 
bytes).  If a return is received by the process that exceeds the maximum 
sample size then it will be rejected and an error reported.  The default 
maximum sample size is 1 byte.  The sample size may be set to 0 to disable 
sample size validation. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxProcess::GetMaxBytesPerSample(void) 
 
Get the maximum sample size supported by the process.  A value of 0 indicates 
that sample size validation is disabled. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 133 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 133 
6.5.1. 
Process Parameters 
 
A process can have parameters which have values that can be retrieved and used 
when the process is executed. The SPxProcess class defines the names, types and 
default values for the parameters. When the class is used in a SPxRunProcess 
instance, the parameters can be assigned values in the context of the execution. The 
functions AddParameterI, AddParameterR and AddParameterS are used to create 
integer, real and string parameters for a process. 
 
6.5.2. 
Defining custom processing modules 
 
You can construct new processing modules and add them into the processing chain. 
These user-defined modules can be freely mixed with the built-in functions. The class 
that encapsulates a new process is SPxProcess. You’ll need to supply a function that 
actually does the useful work – this is called the process handler. 
 
A process handler is a function that gets called by the SPx framework when data 
becomes available from a PIM. In the constructor for SPxProcess, a user-supplied 
function is provided. The framework calls this function to execute the processing. 
 
A process handler has the form: 
 
void myProcess(SPxRunProcess* proc,  
               SPxReturn  *firstNewReturn,  
               unsigned firstNewAzimuthInPim, 
               unsigned azimuthBlockSize) 
 
In addition to the data that is provided to the function, the routine can retrieve 
various data relating through the proc pointer. The arguments provided to the 
function are as follows: 
 
proc – This is the SPxRunProcess object that holds information about the 
process execution. Your application can use proc to get access to data that the 
routine might need to do its processing, including parameters. 
 
firstNewReturn – This is a pointer to the first new return that has been 
updated. 
 
firstNewAzimuthInPim – This is the PIM index of the first new return that has 
been updated, i.e. the index of firstNewReturn in the PIM’s return store. 
 
azimuthBlockSize – This is the number of PIM azimuths that are being 
presented. Note that when processing each azimuth from the PIM, the 
azimuthBlockSize gives the number of azimuths starting at 
firstNewAzimuthInPim that can be processed. However, it is possible that the 
start and end azimuth cross 0 and provision must be made for this. The end 
azimuth of the interval to be updated is not firstNewAzimuthInPim + 
azimuthBlockSize, but rather this quantity modulus the polar-store dimension 
found from proc->GetPIMA()->GetAzimuthDim() 
 
If the process will be used to handle multi-byte samples then 
SetMaxBytesPerSample() must be called to set the maximum sample size in bytes.  
By default, the process will reject returns with a sample size exceeding one byte. 


===== PAGE 134 =====
Page 134 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.5.3. 
Example Processing Function 
 
As an example of the processing function associated with a SPxProcess object, we 
consider the built-in process SPxProThreshold. Note that this section is describing 
how the library implements the SPxProThreshold function, as an example of the 
processing involved. If you want to create your processing you can use the basic data 
processing as a template for your own function. If, however, you just want to use the 
Threshold function then skip this section. 
 
The library creates an instance of SPxProcess as: 
 
SPxProThreshold = new SPxProcess("SPx_PRO_THRESHOLD", 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxProFuncThreshold); 
 
This constructor provides a name for the process and a function, 
SPxProFuncThreshold, that will do the work. There is an additional, optional, 
argument to SPxProcess that provides a notification if something changes that the 
process might be interested in. Refer to the SPxProcess document in Section 6.4 for 
further information. As the simple threshold process does not need to handle changes, 
this argument is not considered here. 
 
The library adds an integer parameter, Threshold, to the process and provides a 
default value of 32 using: 
 
SPxProThreshold->AddParameterI("Threshold",32); 
 
The implementation of the function will read the value of Threshold and use it as a 
parameter in the processing. 
 
The library provides an implementation of the processing function 
SPxProFuncThreshold that supports both 8-bit and 12-bit samples as follows: 
 
void SPxProFuncThreshold(SPxRunProcess *rp, SPxReturn *r, 
 
 
 
 
unsigned firstAzi, unsigned size) 
{ 
    /* Threshold. Data less than or equal to the threshold value is 
     * replaced by zero. 
     */ 
    UINT16 threshold16 = (UINT16)rp->GetParamValueI("Threshold"); 
 
    /* Output value.  Data above the threshold is set to this if not zero, 
     * or preserved at input value otherwise. 
     */ 
    UINT16 thresholdOutput16 =  
        (UINT16)rp->GetParamValueI("ThresholdOutputValue"); 
 
    /* Set maximum 8-bit threshold and output value, limiting 
     * value to the maximum 8-bit sample value if needed. 
     */ 
    UINT8 threshold8 = (UINT8)threshold16; 
    if( threshold16 > 255)  
    { 
        threshold8 = 255; 
    } 
    UINT8 thresholdOutput8 = (UINT8)thresholdOutput16; 
    if( thresholdOutput16 > 255)  
    { 
        thresholdOutput8 = 255; 


===== PAGE 135 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 135 
    } 
 
    /* For 12-bit samples, the sample value is stored in the top  
     * 12-bits of each 16-bit word. Therefore we shift threshold16 and  
     * thresholdOutput16 values so that the value is also contained  
     * in the top 12-bits. 
     */ 
    threshold16 <<= 4; 
    thresholdOutput16 <<= 4; 
 
    /* Get the PIMs that we will use for input and output. */ 
    SPxPIM *inputPIM  = rp->GetPIMA(); 
    SPxPIM *outputPIM = rp->GetPIMB(); 
 
    /* Check that we have an input and output PIM. */ 
    if( inputPIM == 0 ) 
    { 
 
SPxError(SPX_ERR_USER, SPX_ERR_PROCESS_PIMS, 
                  0, 0, "SPxProFuncThreshold",  
                  "Input PIM not defined"); 
 
return; 
    } 
    if( outputPIM == 0 ) 
    { 
 
SPxError(SPX_ERR_USER, SPX_ERR_PROCESS_PIMS, 
                  0, 0, "SPxProFuncThreshold", 
                  "Output PIM not defined"); 
 
return; 
    } 
 
    /* Check that the destination PIM supports the same sample size  
     * or larger than the source PIM. 
     */ 
    if( outputPIM->GetMaxBytesPerSample() < inputPIM-
>GetMaxBytesPerSample() ) 
    { 
        SPxError(SPX_ERR_USER, SPX_ERR_PROCESS_PIMS ,  
                 0, 0, "SPxProFuncThreshold", 
                 "Output PIM maximum sample size too small"); 
 
return;  
    }  
 
    /* Process each return received. */ 
    SPxReturn **inputStore = inputPIM->GetStoreMemory(); 
    SPxReturn **outputStore = outputPIM->GetStoreMemory(); 
    for (unsigned  i = firstAzi; i < (firstAzi + size) ; i++) 
    { 
        /* Get azimuth index and pointer to return data. */ 
 
 
int azimuth = i % inputPIM->GetAzimuthDim(); 
 
 
SPxReturn *rtn = inputStore[azimuth]; 
 
        /* Determine the sample size for data in this return 
         * from the packing type defined in the return header.  
         */ 
        if( rtn->header.packing == SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8 ) 
        { 
            /*  
             * Process 8-bit samples. 
             */ 
 
            /* Get 8-bit input and output pointers. */ 
            UINT8 *in8Ptr  = inputStore[azimuth]->data; 
            UINT8 *out8Ptr = outputStore[azimuth]->data; 
            UINT8 *maxIn8Ptr = in8Ptr + rtn->header.thisLength; 
            UINT8 val8; 


===== PAGE 136 =====
Page 136 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
            /* Process each sample in the return. */ 
            while (in8Ptr < maxIn8Ptr) 
            { 
                /* Get the 8-bit input value. */ 
                val8 = *in8Ptr++; 
 
                /* Check it against the threshold. */ 
                if( val8 <= threshold8 ) 
                { 
                    /* It does not exceed the threshold, so clear it. */ 
                    val8 = 0; 
                } 
                else if( thresholdOutput8 != 0 ) 
                { 
                    /* It exceeds the threshold AND we have a specified 
output 
                     * value, so set it to that (otherwise just preserve 
it). 
                     */ 
                    val8 = thresholdOutput8; 
                } 
                /* Write it out. */ 
                *out8Ptr++ = val8; 
            } 
        } 
        else if( rtn->header.packing == SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW12 ) 
        { 
            /*  
             * Process 12-bit samples. 
             */ 
 
            /* Get 16-bit input and output pointers. */ 
            UINT16 *in16Ptr  = (UINT16 *)inputStore[azimuth]->data; 
            UINT16 *out16Ptr = (UINT16 *)outputStore[azimuth]->data; 
            UINT16 *maxIn16Ptr = in16Ptr + rtn->header.thisLength; 
            UINT16 val16; 
 
            /* Process each sample in the return. Note that the 
             * sample value is contained in the top 12-bits 
             * of each 16-bit value. 
             */ 
            while (in16Ptr < maxIn16Ptr) 
            { 
                /* Get the 16-bit input value. */ 
                val16 = *in16Ptr++; 
 
                /* Check it against the threshold. */ 
                if( val16 <= threshold16 ) 
                { 
                    /* It does not exceed the threshold, so clear it. */ 
                    val16 = 0; 
                } 
                else if( thresholdOutput16 != 0 ) 
                { 
                    /* It exceeds the threshold AND we have a specified 
output 
                     * value, so set it to that (otherwise just preserve 
it). 
                     */ 
                    val16 = thresholdOutput16; 
                } 
 
                /* Write it out. */ 
                *out16Ptr++ = val16; 


===== PAGE 137 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 137 
            } 
        } 
        else 
        { 
            /* Packing type not supported by this function. */ 
            SPxError(SPX_ERR_USER, SPX_ERR_BAD_PACKING_TYPE,  
                     0, 0, "SPxProFuncThreshold", 
                     "Packing type not supported"); 
            return; 
        } 
 
 
/* If the output PIM is different to the input PIM copy the header 
 
 * for the return across. 
 
 */ 
 
if (outputPIM != inputPIM) 
 
{ 
 
    /* Copy the header. */ 
 
    memcpy(&outputStore[azimuth]->header, 
 
 
    &inputStore[azimuth]->header, 
 
 
    sizeof(SPxReturnHeader)); 
 
} 
  } 
 
} /* SPxProFuncThreshold() */ 
 


===== PAGE 138 =====
Page 138 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
A simplified version of this function that supports only 8-bit samples is as follows: 
 
void SPxProFuncThreshold(SPxRunProcess *rp, SPxReturn *r, 
 
 
 
 
unsigned firstAzi, unsigned size) 
{ 
    /* Threshold. Data less than or equal to the threshold value is 
     * replaced by zero. 
     */ 
    SPxPIM *inPIM  = rp->GetPIMA(); 
    SPxPIM *outPIM = rp->GetPIMB(); 
    int threshold = rp->GetParamValueI("Threshold");   
    for (unsigned  i = firstAzi; i < (firstAzi + size) ; i++) 
    { 
 
/* Get the azimuth number, allowing for crossings of North. */ 
 
int azimuth = i % inPIM->GetAzimuthDim(); 
 
SPxReturn *rtn = inPIM->GetStoreMemory()[azimuth]; 
 
unsigned char *inPtr = rtn->data; 
 
unsigned char *outPtr = outPIM->GetStoreMemory()[azimuth]->data; 
 
/* Threshold all samples in the return. */ 
 
for (int j = 0 ; j < rtn->header.thisLength ;  
 
 
 
 
j++, inPtr ++, outPtr++) 
 
{ 
 
    if (*inPtr <= threshold) 
    { 
 
 
*outPtr = 0; 
 
    } 
 
    else 
 
    { 
 
 
*outPtr = 0xff; 
 
    } 
 
} 
    } 
 
    /* If the output PIM is different to the input PIM copy the header 
 
 * for the return across. 
    */ 
 
    if (outPIM != inPIM  ) 
    { 
 
/* Copy the header. */ 
     SPxReturn **inputStore = inputPIM->GetStoreMemory(); 
SPxReturn **outputStore = outPIM ->GetStoreMemory(); 
 
memcpy(&outputStore[azimuth]->header, 
&inputStore[azimuth]->header, sizeof(SPxReturnHeader)); 
} 
}

SPxLib Programming Manual

6.6. SPxRunProcess Class 
 
The SPxRunProcess class is created to invoke a Process represented by the 
SPxProcess class and attach it to another process or a PIM source. There are three 
interfaces to create a SPxRunProcess object, which serve to provide an interface for 
processes that use one, two or three PIMs in their processing. 
 
6.6.1. 
Construction 
 
SPxRunProcess::SPxRunProcess(SPxProcess *process,  
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxRunProcess *input, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimA, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimB, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimC, 
 
 
 
 
 
  void *userArgs = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
  int initialActivityState = 1) 
 
SPxRunProcess::SPxRunProcess(SPxProcess *process,  
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxRunProcess *input, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimA, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimB, 
 
 
 
 
 
  void *userArgs = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
  int initialActivityState = 1) 
 
SPxRunProcess::SPxRunProcess(SPxProcess *process,  
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxRunProcess *input, 
 
 
 
 
 
  SPxPIM *pimA, 
 
 
 
 
 
  void *userArgs = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
  int initialActivityState = 1) 
 
Creates an instance of a Process to process data from the specified source. 
 
process – The process type to run, which may be either a user-created one 
with an instance of SPxProcess, or else may be a pre-defined system process. 
 
input – A pointer to the input process. For the first process in the chain this 
value will be NULL. 
 
pimA, pimB, pimC – Defines the PIMs for processing. Up to three PIMs are 
associated with a process. See the documentation for specific processes. 
Generally, all processes need PIM A at least. Note that if the input process is 
NULL, PIM A must be driven by a RIB. A process will not be executed if its input 
process is NULL and its input PIM-A is not driven by a RIB. 
 
userArgs is an optional pointer argument that is passed to the process. Refer 
to specific process documentation to see if this argument is needed and the 
appropriate data type. 
 
initialActivityState defines the initial activity state of the process. If 0 the 
process is inactive and requires a subsequent SetActive() command. Note 
that some processes may need to be started in the inactive state to allow initial 
parameter values to be set.  
 
 


===== PAGE 140 =====
Page 140 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.6.2. 
Activity State 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetActive(int state) 
 
Set the activity state of the process. If state = 0 the process is inactive. The 
process is active if state is set to 1. Note that if a process is set to inactive the 
downstream processes are still executed, in other words an inactive process 
does not break the processing chain. 
 
 
int SPxRunProcess::GetActive(void) const 
 
Return the current activity state of the process as 0 or 1. 
 
6.6.1. 
User Arguments 
 
void * SPxRunProcess::GetUserArgs(void) 
 
Returns the argument, if any, supplied to the process. 
 
 
void * SPxRunProcess::SetUserArgs2(void *ptr) 
 
The SPxRunProcess instance provides a general-purpose field to hold a pointer. 
This may be used by the SPxProcess processing function to store data. When 
the SPxRunProcess instance is deleted, the destructor calls free on this pointer 
if there is no cleanup function defined on the Process and if the pointer is non-
NULL. 
 
 
void * SPxRunProcess::GetUserArgs2(void) 
 
Return the current UserArgs2 pointer. See documentation for SetUserArgs2() 
above. The only place this function should be called is in the implementation of 
the processing function for an SPxProcess function. 
 
6.6.2. 
Information Retrieval 
 
SPxPIM * SPxRunProcess::GetPIMA(void) 
 
Return PIM A for the process. 
 
 
SPxPIM * SPxRunProcess::GetPIMB(void) 
 
Return PIM B for the process. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 141 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 141 
SPxPIM * SPxRunProcess::GetPIMC(void) 
 
Return PIM C for the process. 
 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetPIMD(SPxPIM *pim) 
 
This function defines PIM D for the RunProcess object. Note that PIM D cannot 
be assigned with a constructor and must be set using this function. Some SPx 
processes may use PIM D. 
 
 
SPxPIM * SPxRunProcess::GetPIMD(void) 
 
Return PIM D for the process.  
 
 
SPxProcess * SPxRunProcess::GetProcess(void) 
 
Return the SPxProcess object for this object. 
 
 
int SPxRunProcess::GetNumOutputProcesses(void) 
 
Returns the number of processes connected to the output of this process.  
 
 
SPxRunProcess *SPxRunProcess::GetOutput(int index) 
 
Returns the output process at the specified index. The value of index should be 
in the range 0 to GetNumOutputProcesses() – 1. The order of the processes is 
the order in which they were added to the class. This is also the order that they 
will be executed. 
 
 
SPxRunProcess *SPxRunProcess::GetInput(void) 
 
Returns the input process, which will be NULL if this process is the beginning of 
the chain. 
 
6.6.3. 
Parameter Assignment and Retrieval 
 
int SPxRunProcess::GetParamValueI(char *parameterName) 
 
Returns the current value of the specified parameter as an integer. The 
supported parameters are defined for the SPxProcess. If the type of the 
underlying parameter is real, this function returns the integer part of the value. 
 
 
double SPxRunProcess::GetParamValueR(char *parameterName) 
 
Returns the current value of the specified parameter as a Real number. The 
supported parameters are defined for the SPxProcess. 
 


===== PAGE 142 =====
Page 142 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
char * SPxRunProcess::GetParamValueS(char *parameterName, 
                                     char *buffer, int buflen) 
 
Returns the current value of the specified parameter as a null-terminated 
character string. The caller must provide a buffer memory into which the 
function will copy the value of the string parameter. The caller provides the 
length of the buffer as buflen bytes. If the buffer is too small for the string 
value no copy occurs and the function returns NULL. Otherwise the string is 
copied and the function returns buffer. The supported parameters are defined 
for the SPxProcess. It is permissible to call GetParamValueS for a parameter 
that is of type real or integer, as well as string. In the case of reals and ints 
they are converted to a string representation using the %d and %lf format 
specifiers of sprintf. 
 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetParamValueI(char *parameterName, int newValue) 
 
Sets a new integer value for the specified parameter. It is permissible to use 
this function if the underlying type of the parameter is integer or real. 
 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetParamValueR(char *parameterName, double newValue) 
 
Sets a new value for the specified parameter. It is permissible to use this 
function if the underlying type of the parameter is integer or real. 
 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetParamValueS(char *parameterName, char * newValue) 
 
Sets a new string value for a parameter of type SPX_PROC_PARAM_TYPE_STRING. 
The function maintains memory for the string value, which is copied by the 
function.  
 
 
SPxProcessRunParameter* SPxRunProcess::GetFirstRunParameter(void) 
 
This function returns the first parameter associated with the SPxRunProcess 
object. Subsequent parameters can be found by calling GetNext() on the 
returned object until NULL is returned. The SPxProcessRunParameter object 
supports GetParameter(), GetValueI(), GetValueR() to retrieve information 
for the process parameter. 
 
 
void SPxRunProcess::SetParamsFromConfig(void) 
 
This function initialises process parameters using configuration parameters 
previously read using SPxLoadConfig (see Section 11.9). Parameters must be 
formatted as "<procName><paramName>". This function may only be called after 
the run process name and parameters have been defined.

SPxLib Programming Manual

6.7. Built-in Processing Modules 
 
The following processing modules are instances of SPxProcess and are available as 
standard in the SPx framework.  Unless stated otherwise, built-in processing modules 
may only be used to process 8-bit (SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8) data.  
 
6.7.1. 
Null Process 
 
Name 
SPxProNull 
Description 
This is a null process that performs no actions. 
Parameters 
None 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
None 
User Args 
None 
Details 
This process performs no processing on the input data. Since a 
PIM can only output data to one process, SPxProNull may be 
used as the first process after the PIM to allow multiple parallel 
processes to be connected. 
 
6.7.2. 
Sector Blanking 
 
Name 
SPxProSectorBlank 
Description 
Blanks video in an range/azimuth sector 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
StartAzimuth : Real (0.0 .. 360.0) 
EndAzimuth : Real (0.0 .. 360.0) 
StartRangeMetres : Real 
EndRangeMetres : Real 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Modified PIM A 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
Radar video between StartAzimuth and EndAzimuth (each in 
degrees) is set to zero in the input PIM. In range, radar video at 
a range less than StartRangeMetres and video with a range 
greater than EndRangeMetres is set to zero. If EndRangeMetres 
is -1 (the default), there is no upper range limit. 
 
Note that any number of sectors can be blanked in this way by 
cascading multiple processes of this type. The routine handles 
the situation of the start and end azimuth crossing the origin.  
 
See Figure 26 for an example of using SPxProSectorBlank to 
blank video in the interval 170 to 190 degrees. For this example, 
the parameters are: 
 
 


===== PAGE 144 =====
Page 144 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
StartAzimuth = 170 
EndAzimuth = 190 
StartRangeMetres = 0 
EndRangeMetres = -1 
 
Figure 26 - Sector Blanking with SPxProSectorBlank 
 
To enable the display of video in a specific range-azimuth sector, specify the start and 
end azimuths as the end and start of the blanking interval respectively. For example, 
to enable the display of video in a sector from 30 to 120 degrees over a range of 5km 
to 10km, specify StartAzimuth = 120, EndAzimuth = 30, StartRangeMetres = 
5000, EndRangeMetres = 10000. See Figure 27 for an example, which shows the 
input video on left in green and the output video in red on right (with input video as 
well at reduced intensity). 
 
Figure 27 - Sector and range blanking to show video (red) in sector only

6.7.3. 
Range Blanking 
 
Name 
SPxProRangeBlank 
Description 
Blanks video in a range interval, where the range interval can be 
specified in samples or metres. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
StartRangeSample : Integer 
EndRangeSample : Integer 
StartRangeWorld : Real 
EndRangeWorld : Real 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Modified PIM A 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process clears radar in a defined range interval. The range 
specification can either be in samples or metres. If the parameters 
StartRangeSample and EndRangeSample are used, the 
specification is in terms of samples. If StartRangeSample is 
assigned a value, radar video is cleared from sample 0 to sample 
StartRangeSample. If EndRangeSample is assigned a value, radar 
video is cleared from that sample to the end of the video. The 
behaviour is similar for StartRangeWorld and EndRangeWorld, 
except the values are given in world coordinates, which are then 
converted into samples using the range information in the header. 
See Figure 28 for an example of SPxProRangeBlank. 
 
Figure 28 - Range blanking with SPxProRangeBlank 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 146 =====
Page 146 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.7.4. 
Range Adjust 
 
Name 
SPxProRangeAdjust 
Description 
Adjusts start and end range of radar video 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
RangeScale : Real 
RangeDelta : Real 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, 12 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process allows the start and end range of each radar return 
to be adjusted to compensate for any anomalies. The radar video 
data itself remains unchanged; only the information in the header 
of each radar return is modified. If RangeScale is non-zero, the 
start and end range of each radar return is multiplied by the scale 
factor. The value of RangeDelta (expressed in metres) is then 
added to the start and end range. If PIM A and PIM B are 
different, the modified returns are copied to the output PIM. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 147 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 147 
6.7.5. 
Constant Threshold  
 
Name 
SPxProThreshold 
Description 
Thresholds the video at a constant level 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Threshold : Integer (0..255) 
ThresholdOutputValue : Integer (0 .. 255) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, 12 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The input video is thresholded at the constant level defined by the 
parameter Threshold, which must be in the range 0..255. 
 
All video above the threshold is replaced by 
ThresholdOutputValue, unless ThresholdOutputValue = 0, in 
which case the input value is preserved. Samples that are less 
than or equal to the threshold are set to 0 in the output. See 
Figure 29 for an example of SPxProThreshold. 
This process supports multi-byte samples. 
 
Figure 29 - Constant thresholding with SPxProThreshold

6.7.6. 
Sensitivity Time Constant (STC) 
 
Name 
SPxProSTC 
Description 
Reduces the signal intensity for near ranges 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
GainAtRange0 : Real 
StartRangeWorld : Real 
StartRangeSample : Integer 
ActiveRangeWorld : Real  
ActiveRangeSample : Integer 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Modified PIM A 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The input video is processed to attenuate video data close to the 
radar centre. This is typically used to reduce the effect of sea 
clutter close to the radar. 
 
The gain profile is shown in Figure 30. In the case where 
StartRangeSample = 0, the profile is just a linear gain change 
from GainAtRange0 at range 0 to 1.0 at range 
ActiveRangeWorld. 
 
 
Figure 30 - Gain Profile for STC gain function 
 
The value of GainAtRange0 is in the range 0 to 1 and is the gain that is used to 
multiply the sample at range = 0 and then all other samples up to start range, 
which is specified as follows. 
 
If StartRangeSample is set (i.e. has a value other than -1) it is the sample 
number for the start range. Otherwise, if StartRangeWorld is set it is the start 
range value in metres. 
 
Samples beyond start range are then subject to a gain that linearly increases 
from GainAtRange0 to 1.0 at the selected range. The selected range beyond 
which STC is disabled is set from either ActiveRangeWorld or 
ActiveRangeSample. If ActiveRangeSample is set (i.e. has a value other than -
1.0 
GainAtRange0 
StartRangeWorld or 
StartRangeSample 
ActiveRangeWorld or 
ActiveRangeSample 
range 


===== PAGE 149 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 149 
1) it is the maximum sample number for STC. Otherwise, if ActiveRangeWorld 
is set it is the maximum range in metres to be used for STC. See Figure 31 for 
an example of SPxProSTC. 
 
Figure 31 – Sensitivity Time Constant with SPxProSTC 
 
In many situations, a single control is required to adjust the STC (a “Sea Clutter” 
adjustment). One way to implement this is as follows. 
 
Let S be the Sea Clutter adjustment, which is a real number value between 0 (no sea 
clutter removal) and 1.0 (maximum removal). Then S is mapped to the process 
parameters as follows: 
 
GainAtRange0 = 1.0-S 
ActiveRangeWorld = S.MaxRange 
 
This assumes that ActiveRangeSample = -1 and StartRangeSample = 0. The value 
MaxRange is the maximum range of the radar. 
 
The gain profiles for values of S of 0, 0.5 and 1.0 are shown in Figure 32. 
 
For maximum sea clutter (S=1), it might be desirable to permit the gain to increase 
from 0 to 1 over somewhat less than the full range. This can be accomplished by 
adding an additional term, so that: 
 
ActiveRangeWorld = S.MaxRange * N 
 
Where N is the multiplier that reduces the effective range of the clutter adjustment. 
For example, with N =1/2, the gain is returned to 1 at half the maximum range, and 
for N = 1/4, the gain is returned to 1 at a quarter of the maximum range. 
 
This gives two controls for the STC: S (0 to 1) adjusts the intensity of the sea clutter 
reduction and N (0 to 1) adjusts the effective range. 


===== PAGE 150 =====
Page 150 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 32 - Gain profile for different S values for simplified STC control (N=1)

S = 1 
(no clutter

6.7.7. 
Dynamic Thresholding 
 
Name 
SPxProDynamicThreshold 
Description 
Dynamic thresholding and filtering of video. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
WindowSizeSample : Integer 
WindowSizeWorld : Real 
ThreshDelta : Integer (0..255) 
ThreshDeltaStop : Integer (-255 .. + 255) 
ThreshOutputValue : Integer (-1 .. 255) 
SDfactor : Real (0.0 .. 10.0) 
 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
The dynamic thresholding process provides automatic adaption to 
changing background levels and implements the Cell Averaging 
Constant False Alarm Rate (CA-CFAR) detector. The principle is to 
create a detection if a signal is sufficiently different from the local 
background around the signal. The background video is computed 
using a low frequency filter, whose time constant is chosen so that 
the size of a real target will have minimal contribution to the 
calculation of the background level. As a rule of thumb, the 
dimension of the window used for the averaging should be 5 times 
the expected maximum target dimension. 
 
A sample that is different from the local background will pass 
through the process as appear as either a full level output sample 
(thresholding) or may appear with its level retained. In this case, 
the process serves to remove low-frequency signals across a 
return and therefore implements an adaptive high-pass filter. 
 
The input video is dynamically thresholded by calculating a local 
average over a window size. If a value is assigned to the 
parameter WindowSizeSample, that value is used as the width of 
the window in samples. If a value is assigned to WindowSizeWorld 
(with WindowSizeSample = -1), that value is the window width in 
metres. 
 
The window is centred over a test sample and the average signal 
intensity over the window dimension is calculated. The WindowSize 
parameters effectively defines the bandwidth of the filter.  
 
For the sample at position s, the average, A(s), is calculated for a 
window centred on s and having width, w, where w is set by the 
WindowSize parameter. The threshold value is then derived as 
ThreshDelta plus the maximum of A(s-w/2) and A(s+w/2). 
 
Optionally, (if SDfactor > 0.0) the standard deviation of samples 
around the test point can be used to further raise the threshold. 
This is useful as a way of raising the threshold (reducing the 


===== PAGE 152 =====
Page 152 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
sensitivity) if there is a high level of noise around the test point, 
where that noise might not otherwise increase the threshold based 
on mean. The threshold level is raised by an amount equal to 
SDfactor times the measured standard deviation. 
 
In summary, the threshold, T(s), for a sample at range s is 
calculated as: 
 
T(s) = A(s) + ThreshDelta + SDfactor*SD(s) 
 
Where A(s) is the average sample value around s (in range only) 
and SD(s) is the standard deviation of sample values around s (in 
range). 
 
For the special case of WindowSizeSample = 0, the thresholding 
works as a fixed-level threshold, with the threshold value set by 
ThreshDelta. If requiring a fixed-level thresholder, it is preferable 
to use the constant-level threshold process SPxProThreshold. 
 
If the video sample is less than the threshold, then the output 
sample is set to 0. If the video sample exceeds the threshold, then 
the output sample is computed according to the value of 
ThreshOutputValue, as follows: 
 
If ThreshOutputValue = -1, the output value is set to the 
difference between the sample value and the local average. 
This means that the output level is the relative strength of a 
sample compared to the local background.  
 
If ThreshOutputValue = 0, the output value is set to the 
input value. The background is removed, but the level of the 
signal in the output is the original level (versus the level 
relative to the background, as for the previous case of 
ThreshOutputValue = -1).  
 
Otherwise the output value is defined by the value of 
ThreshOutputValue. 
 
An example of this is shown in Figure 33. 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 153 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 153 
 
Figure 33 - Effect of ThreshOutputValue in the Dynamic Threshold Process 
 
The parameter ThreshDeltaStop can be used to control the hysteresis level of 
the detection. With ThreshDeltaStop = 0 (the default), a single threshold 
delta, as set by ThreshDelta is used. If ThreshDeltaStop is > 0 it defines a 
value of the threshold delta to stop the detection (this value should be less than 
ThreshDelta). If ThreshDeltaStop is < 0 it defines the stop value as a 
difference from ThreshDelta. For example, if ThreshDelta = 32 and 
ThreshDeltaStop = 20, the signal will initially be thresholded at level 32, but 
then the threshold level drops to 20 for subsequent samples. When the signal 
level drops below 20, the threshold value of 32 is restored. If ThreshDelta = 
Range 
Range 
Range 
Range 
Input signal – comprises three targets 
superimposed on a background video 
whose intensity is a function of range 
ThreshOutputValue = -1 
The background is removed and the 
targets appear at an intensity consistent 
with their intensity relative to the 
background 
ThreshOutputValue = 0 
The background is removed and the 
targets appear at an intensity consistent 
with their original strength 
ThreshOutputValue = 255 
The background is removed and the 
targets appear at a constant intensity of 
255 (thresholded) 


===== PAGE 154 =====
Page 154 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
32 and ThreshDeltaStop = -8, the initial threshold is 32 and the subsequent 
threshold is 24. 
 
6.7.8. 
Fast Time Constant (FTC)  
 
Name 
SPxProFTC 
Description 
Fast Time Constant 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
WindowSizeSample : Integer 
WindowSizeWorld : Real 
Level : Real (0.0 .. 1.0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The input video is processed with a high-pass filter. This feature is 
intended to reduce the effect of long duration returns, e.g. from 
weather effects. If a value is assigned to WindowSizeSample, that 
value is used as the width of the window in samples. If a value is 
assigned to WindowSizeWorld, that value is the window width in 
metres. 
 
A fraction, defined by Level, of the local average is subtracted 
from the input signal to achieve a high-pass filter. 
 
 
Figure 34 - FTC shown on A-Scan window. FTC data is shown in yellow with original (test 
generator pattern 102) video in white. 
Window width is 500 metres. 
 
 


===== PAGE 155 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 155 
6.7.9. 
Averaging 
 
Name 
SPxProAverage 
Description 
Averages the video in range using a low-pass filter 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
WindowSizeSample : Integer 
WindowSizeWorld : Real 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The video is smoothed in range using the specified window size to 
define the bandwidth of the filter. If a value is assigned to 
WindowSizeSample, that value is used as the width of the window in 
samples. If a value is assigned to WindowSizeWorld, that value is 
the window width in metres. A typical effect is shown in Figure 35. 
 
 
Figure 35 - Averaging shown on A-Scan window. Averaged data is shown in yellow with 
original (test generator pattern 102) video in white. 
Window width is 500 metres. 
 
 


===== PAGE 156 =====
Page 156 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.7.10. 
Notch Filter 
 
Name 
SPxProFilterNotch 
Description 
Removes notches, i.e. short dips in video intensity. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
WindowSizeSample : Integer 
WindowSizeWorld : Real 
NotchSize : Integer 
Invert : Integer 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The specified window size is used to compute an average signal 
level in a sliding window.  If a value is assigned to 
WindowSizeSample, that value is used as the width of the window in 
samples.  If a value is assigned to WindowSizeWorld, that value is 
the window width in metres. 
 
In normal use (with Invert set to zero), when the input signal 
exceeds the average it is preserved without change.  Where the 
input signal is less than the average AND the signal is more than 
the average at a further NotchSize/2 samples before and after the 
test sample, then the input signal is replaced by the previous value. 
 
The intention of this process is to preserve target-like signals that 
exceed the local average, and remove small notches or dips. This 
filter will generally have minimal impact on the appearance of the 
video on the display, but it can have a significant impact on video 
being compressed for recording or distribution. One possible 
consequence for display is a loss of discrimination of two targets 
closely separated in range. The filter will tend to smooth out the 
separation. 
 
If the Invert parameter is non-zero, the logic comparing the input 
signal against the average is inverted and the process aims to 
smooth out small spikes in the video instead. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 157 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 157 
6.7.11. 
Scan Conversion (PPI and B-Scan) 
 
Name 
SPxProScanConv 
Description 
Radar Scan Conversion for PPI or B-Scan display 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
User Args 
SPxScSourceLocal * 
Input Data 
PIM A 
PIM B (for optional history data) 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
Provides an interface from the processing chain into the scan 
conversion. The SPxRunProcess object is created with a user 
argument that is the scan conversion object. For example, the 
code: 
 
SPxRunProcess *scPro = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
NULL, spxPim, spxSc) 
 
will link the PIM spxPIM (created as an instance of SPxPIM) with the 
scan conversion object spxSc (created as an instance of 
SPxScSourceLocal). To link the scan conversion process to the 
output of another process, for example a prior FTC function, the 
two processes would be: 
 
SPxRunProcess *ftcPro = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProFTC, 
 
 
 
           NULL, spxPim, spxPim); 
 
SPxRunProcess *scProcess = new SPxRunProcess 
                              (SPxProScanConv,  
                               ftcPro, spxPim, spxSc); 
 
This process can be used with SPxProHistory which provides trail 
history retention of view changes. Refer to Section 6.7.28 for 
details of SPxProHistory. When using this feature, provide PIM B 
as the history PIM.

6.7.13. 
Radar Distribution on Network 
 
Name 
SPxProNetworkSend 
Description 
Radar Network Distribution 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Input Data 
PIM A, PIM B, PIM C (PIM B and C are optional) 
Input 
Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
None 
User Arg

Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process provides an interface from the processing chain for the 
network distribution of video. The SPxRunProcess object is created 
with a user argument that is an instance of SPxNetworkSend (see 
Section 4.1). For example the code: 
 
SPxRunProcess *netProcess = new SPxRunProcess 
                            (SPxProNetworkSend,  
 
 
 
 
 
 NULL, spxPim, sxNet)

process uses. The value is 0 to use PIM A, 1 to use PIM B and 2 to 
use PIM C. Refer to the description of the SPxNetworkSend object in 
Section 4.1 for details. 
 
This process supports multi-byte samples.

6.7.14. 
Remap 
 
Name 
SPxProRemap 
Description 
Clip low and high video and remap video to fewer bits. 
Parameters 
Active: Integer (0 or 1) 
Level1 : Integer (0..255) 
Level2 : Integer (0..255) 
Bits : Integer (1..8) 
Input Data 
PIM A

PIM B (may be same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
Video having values from 0 to Level1 is set to 0. Video having 
values from Level2 to 255 is set to 255. The value of Bits (in the 
range 1 to 8) defines the number of bits that the processed data 
will use. For example, Bits = 1 means that the output values are 0 
or 255. See the following diagram for the mapping function. 
This process can be used to remove low-level noise from video. For 
example, Level1 = 32, Level2=255, Bits = 8 has the effect of 
setting video from 0 to 12.5% of full-scale to be replaced by 0. 
 
Figure 36 - Mapping function for SPxProRemap Process

Output 
Level1 
Level2
