6.7.15. 
Integration 
 
Name 
SPxProIntegrate 
Description 
Integrate input data over time to build up a clutter map 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
TimeConstant : Real (0.0 .. 1.0) 
TimeConstantStartup : Real (0.0, 1.0) 
SlowDecay : Real (0 to 100) 
SlowDecayStartup : Real (0 to 100) 
StartupRevs : Integer  
HoldRangeMult : Real 
HoldAzimuthMult : Real 
HoldShadowMult : Real 
HoldShadowAdd : Real 
ScrollNumCells : Integer 
LoadFromFile : String 
UseFloor : Integer 
SampleMin : Integer 
SampleMax : Integer 
 
Input Data 
PIM A 
PIM C (for optional floor data) 
Input Formats 
8, <8* , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1*, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
SPxNavData (Optional) 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process integrates the input data over successive scans and 
stores the result in PIM B. Internally, the process uses a 16-bit 
clutter map to preserve accuracy. 
 
The basic update function is: 
 
 
I(+) = k.I(-) + (1-k).D 
 
where I(-) is the data in the PIM before the update, D is the new 
data, which is PIM A of this process, and k is the time constant 
(0 <= k <= 1).  
 
If PIM C is specified and the process parameter UseFloor is 1, 
the contents of PIM C define a floor of minimum values. The 
decay of the clutter map does not reduce values below the floor. 
In this case the update equation becomes: 
 
 
I(+) = MAX( C, k.I(-) + (1-k).D) 
 
Where C is the value of the floor from PIM C at a sample. 
 
If the new data sample is less than SampleMin, or the new data 
sample is greater than SampleMax (and SampleMax is non-zero), 
then the new data is ignored and the integrated value is 
unchanged.  This allows outliers to be ignored if desired. 


===== PAGE 162 =====
Page 162 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
For the first StartupRevs scans of the process the value of the 
time constant is TimeConstantStartup, after then the time 
constant becomes TimeConstant. The time constant is reset to 
the starting value again if the radar source object changes. 
Typically the start-up time constant will be smaller than the 
steady state value, as this allows a more rapid initialisation of 
the clutter map. 
 
The update equation above can be modified using the SlowDecay 
parameter. This affects the decay of the clutter map and has the 
effect of holding the clutter value higher in the face of 
intermittent noise. For the first StartupRevs scans of the 
process, the parameter value is taken from SlowDecayStartup. 
The parameter has value 0 (the default) to disable the slow 
decay feature. With a value of 100, which is the maximum, the 
clutter never decays and therefore has infinite persistence. 
Increasing the value from 0 to 100 causes progressively more 
clutter to be retained. The implementation of SlowDecay is to 
compute another gain Kd, such that Kd = 1.0 if SlowDelay = 0 
and Kd = 1/k if SlowDecay = 100. The value of Kd is then used 
in a modification of the clutter update equation as: 
 
 
I(+) = MAX( Kd.k.I(-) + (1-k).D, limit) 
 
where limit = MAX(I(-), D) 
 
If PIM C is specified as a floor and parameter UseFloor is 1, the 
update becomes: 
 
 
I(+) = MAX (C, MAX( Kd.k.I(-) + (1-k).D, limit)) 
 
If the parameter LoadFromFile is set, it defines the name of the 
file to load to initialise the clutter map. 
 
The process can be created with an optional user argument as a 
SPxNavData pointer. This may be provided where the clutter 
integration is on a moving platform. The motion of the platform 
is used to scroll the clutter map according to the value of 
ScollNumCells. If using a SPxNavData object, set 
ScrollNumCells to 1 (or set to 2 to reduce CPU load in 
exceptional cases). 
 
The Hold parameters can be used to force areas of the clutter 
map to be held at the current value and not updated by the 
normal processing. This might be used by a tracker, for 
example, which wants to prevent update of the clutter map 
around known targets. The global function 
SPxProIntegrateHoldArea is used to control this process. The 
function is defined as: 
 
 
void SPxProIntegrateHoldArea(SPxRunProcess *rp, 
 
 
 
 
     double rangeMetres,  


===== PAGE 163 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 163 
 
 
 
 
     double azimuthDegrees, 
 
 
 
 
     double sizeMetres, 
 
 
 
 
     double sizeDegrees) 
 
The instance of SPxRunProcess, rp, is the process of type 
SPxProIntegrate. The values of rangeMetres, azimuthDegrees 
define the centre point of a range azimuth region that is of size 
sizeMetres and sizeDegrees. The function causes the clutter 
map updating for the specified region to be stopped for one 
scan. The geometry of the area concerned is derived from the 
area reported in the function call and the parameters of the 
process. The parameter HoldRangeMult defines a multiplier on 
the value of sizeMetres, and HoldAzimuthMult is a multiplier 
on sizeDegrees. A shadow area is then added as an additional 
range dimension derived from the sum of HoldShadowMult times 
sizeMetres and HoldShadowAdd. See Figure 37 for an example 
of the calculation. 
 
Any number of hold-off regions may be set, for example 
responding to reports generated by a tracker. Note that the 
regions are automatically cleared after the hold-off has occurred 
and the the new areas need to be set again, for example by the 
next track report. 
 
Figure 37 - Calculating the Geometry of the Clutter Hold-Off region 
 
 
The output, PIM B, will contain video whose strength depends on 
the correlation of the data over time. This is one method of 
identifying clutter or stationary targets. 
 
The output PIM is automatically cleared when the source of input 
data is changed. 
 
The clutter map associated with the SPxProIntegrate process 
may be saved and loaded from a file using the following 
functions: 
sizeMetres/2 
sizeAzimuth/2 
sizeAzimuth/2 * HAM 
sizeMetres/2 * HRM + 
HSA + HSM * sizeMetres  
HRM = HoldRangeMult 
HAM = HoldAzimuthMult 
HSM = HoldShadowMult 
HAS = HoldShadowAdd  
sizeMetres/2 * HRM 
Clutter hold-
off region 


===== PAGE 164 =====
Page 164 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxProIntegrateSaveToFile( 
                                SPxRunProcess *rp,  
                                const char *fileName) 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxProIntegrateLoadFromFile( 
                                SPxRunProcess *rp,  
                                const char *fileName) 
 
where rp is the instance of SPxRunprocess and fileName is the 
name of the file, which is either an absolute or relative path. 
 
In the simple mode, the TimeConstant and SlowDecay 
parameters are applied globally. However, the process may be 
configured to apply different rates in up to 8 different areas.   
This is configured using the RegionNameN and RegionValueN 
parameters, each associated with a different TimeConstant and 
optionally SlowDecay value. 
 
The parameter RegionNameN , where N is 0-7, defines the name 
of the existing SpxActiveRegion.   The parameter RegionValueN 
defines the desired values to use in the matching region in a 
string with the form of “timeconstant=x” or “timeconstant=x 
slowdecay=y”, where x and y are floating point numbers. Note 
the names inside the string must be lower case.  The time 
constant must be specified for every region, but the slow decay 
value can be omitted if the global value is to be used. 
 
For example, with: 
 
RegionName0 = “myregion” 
RegionValue0 = “timeconstant=0.9 slowdecay=95.0” 
 
the time constant and slow decay in the SPxActiveRegion having 
name myregion will have values 0.9 and 95 respectively 
(assuming the named active region is active).  
 
 
6.7.16. 
Signal Enhancement 
 
Name 
SPxProBoost 
Description 
Estimate range-dependent system noise and boost the signal 
part of the video separately to the noise, e.g. for sea clutter 
removal. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
NoiseMode : Integer (Default = 0) 
Threshold : Integer (Default = 32) 
NoiseGain: Real (Default = 1.0) 
SignalGain: Real (Default = 1.0) 
SignalOffset: Integer (Default = 0) 
SignalSpread: Integer (Default = 0) 
NoiseMinValue: Integer (Default = 0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 


===== PAGE 165 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 165 
PIM B (Optional input for system noise estimation) 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details.) 
Output Data 
Modified PIM A 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The process takes either one or two PIMs on creation. PIM A 
contains the radar video to be processed, and modified data will 
be written back to PIM A by the process. An optional PIM B 
contains the radar video to estimate the system noise from, 
which may be different from PIM A or omitted if it is the same. 
 
The parameters are as follows: 
 
NoiseMode - This controls how the system noise is estimated. 
Currently, only mode 0 is supported, which estimates the noise 
at each range sample by calculating the median of all radar 
samples at the same range around the 360 degree coverage. 
 
Threshold - Radar samples that are at least this amount higher 
than the noise estimate are deemed to be signal rather than 
noise. 
 
NoiseGain - Gain to apply to the noise component of every radar 
sample. For example, 1.0 leaves the noise unchanged, but 0.0 
removes the noise from each sample. 
 
SignalGain - Gain to apply to the signal component of every 
radar sample exceeding the threshold. 
 
SignalOffset - Offset to add to every radar sample exceeding 
the threshold. 
 
SignalSpread - Number of samples to spread the gain/offset by 
in the range dimension where a signal is detected.  For example, 
if this is set to 1 and a signal is detected at range sample 10, the 
gain and offset would be applied to samples 9, 10 and 11.  If it is 
set to 0, the gain and offset would be applied to sample 10 only. 
 
NoiseMinValue – Only radar samples greater than or equal to 
this value are used to estimate the range-dependent noise. 
Samples less than this value are ignored and not included in the 
median calculation. 
 
When enabled, the process constantly estimates range-
dependent system noise, e.g. modelling whether noise is greater 
at short range (such as sea clutter around a ship in open water), 
or increasing at longer range (such as some FMCW radars). 
 
Each radar sample is then compared against the estimated noise 
for the particular range. 
 
If the sample is at least "Threshold" levels higher than the noise, 


===== PAGE 166 =====
Page 166 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
then the signal component of the sample (i.e. the amount by 
which the sample exceeds the noise) is multiplied by 
"SignalGain" and "SignalOffset" is added to the value.   If 
"SignalSpread" is greater than zero, the same gain and offset is 
applied to that number of adjacent samples in increasing and 
decreasing range. 
 
In addition, for all samples, the estimated noise component of 
the sample is multiplied by "NoiseGain". 
 
This process may be used in a number of typical ways.  For 
example, to use it to remove range-dependent noise (such as 
sea clutter around a ship in open water), set PIM A to be the PIM 
containing the video to be processed and PIM B to be the PIM 
containing raw (unprocessed) video, then use the following 
parameters: 
 
NoiseMode = 0 
Threshold = 255 
NoiseGain = 0.0 
SignalGain = 1.0 
SignalOffset = 0 
SignalSpread = 0 
 
To use the process to boost the value of signals in a clutter map, 
without boosting the range-dependent noise in the same clutter 
map, set PIM A to be the clutter map and PIM B to be the live 
video, then use the following parameters: 
 
NoiseMode = 0 
Threshold = 32 
(for example) 
NoiseGain = 1.0 
SignalGain = 2.0 (for example) 
SignalOffset = 16 
(for example) 
SignalSpread = 0 (or non-zero for spreading) 
 
Note that in this situation the process should be added to a 
process chain for the live video, between the SPxProIntegrate 
process and the SPxProCombine process, so that it is invoked 
after the clutter map is updated but before the clutter map is 
combined (e.g. subtracted) from the live video. 
 
6.7.17. 
Combine 
 
Name 
SPxProCombine 
Description 
Combine input data from two PIMs 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Mode : Integer (Default = 0) 
GainA : Real (Default = 1.0) 
GainB : Real (Default = 1.0) 
MinA : Integer (Default 0) 
MinB : Integer (Default 0) 
RangeMode : Integer (Default 0) 


===== PAGE 167 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 167 
UseLSBIndicator : Integer (Default 0) 
InfillStartAziDegs : Real (Default = 90.0) 
InfillStopAziDegs : Real (Default = 270.0) 
InfillStartRangeMetres : Real (Default = 0.0) 
InfillStopRangeMetres : Real (Default = 999999.0) 
 
Input Data 
PIM A 
PIM B 
Input Formats 
8, <8* , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1*, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM C (may be the same as PIM A or PIM B) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This function combines two PIMs A and B to give an output C, 
according to the Mode. PIMs A, B and C must be the same size 
(same number of azimuths and range samples). 
 
The supported combinations are as follows: 
 
Mode  = 0: Linear Combine. The PIMs are combined as a linear 
combination of the two inputs. In the simple case (MinA and MinB 
are both 0), the output is: 
 
 
C = GainA*A + GainB*B 
 
This simple case applies if parameters MinA and MinB are both 0. 
Setting MinA or MinB to a value greater than zero means that 
any video below the specified level is ignored. So the general 
form of the combination is: 
 
 
C = A* + B* 
 
 
 
Where 
 
 
A* = GainA*A for the situation where A ≥ MinA 
 
A* = 0 for the situation where A < MinA 
 
 
B* = GainB*B for the situation where B ≥ MinB 
 
B* = 0 for the situation where B < MinB 
 
This function can be used to add or subtract PIMs by specifying 
appropriate values for Gain1 and Gain2. The values of C are 
clipped to the interval 0 to 255 for the standard 8-bit PIM. 
 
A typical use of this process is in association with clutter map 
generation, which is input to the combiner as PIM B, with the 
current video in PIM A. In this situation, the following 
configurations may be used: 
 
Scan-to-scan integration – Set GainA = 1, GainB = 1, MinB = 0 
and MinA to a mid-range level such as 128. In this situation, the 
combining process adds the input video to the clutter map. With 
MinA at 128, only recent video exceeding 50% of the full-scale 


===== PAGE 168 =====
Page 168 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
input is passed through. As MinA is adjusted between 0 and 255, 
the output is affected by showing either all or none of the input 
video added to the scan integrated video. 
 
Moving target detection – Set GainA = 1, Gain B = -1, MinA = 0, 
MinB = 0. In this mode, the clutter is subtrated from the input 
video, meaning any correlated clutter is removed. The output is 
therefore video that is changing from scan to scan (potentially 
moving targets). This mode can only be used on a video that has 
clean (i.e., not changing from scan to scan) clutter. 
 
Mode = 1: Highest Wins. The PIMs are combined by setting the 
output for each sample to be the largest of the gain-adjusted 
samples from the two input PIMs. In simple terms (when MinA 
and MinB are both 0), the output is: 
 
 
C = MAX(GainA.A, GainB.B) 
 
Or, if using MinA and MinB: 
 
 
 
C = MAX(GainA.A*, GainB.B*) 
 
 
Where 
 
 
A* = GainA.A for the situation where A ≥ MinA 
 
A* = 0 for the situation where A < MinA 
 
 
B* = GainB.B for the situation where B ≥ MinB 
 
B* = 0 for the situation where B < MinB 
 
Mode = 2: Lowest Wins. The PIMs are combined by setting the 
output for each sample to be the smallest of the gain-adjusted 
samples from the two input PIMs: 
 
 
C = MIN(GainA.A, GainB.B) 
 
As previously, MinA and MinB may be used. 
 
When the range of the radar video from each of the two PIMs are 
different, optional range matching can be used to preserve the 
correct range for both inputs in the generated output radar video. 
By default, range matching is disabled and the PIM B input will be 
assumed to have the same range as the PIM A input. 
 
Mode = 3: Infill. The PIMs are combined in a mosaic manner, 
using values from PIM A by default, but PIM B in a configurable 
area, defined by the following parameters: 
 
InfillStartAziDegs – the start azimuth of the infill 
sector, defaulting to 90.0 degrees. 
 
InfillStopAziDegs – the stop azimuth of the infill sector, 
defaulting to 270.0 degrees. 
 
 


===== PAGE 169 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 169 
InfillStartRangeMetres – the start range of the infill 
sector, defaulting to 0.0 metres. 
 
InfillStopRangeMetres – the stop range of the infill 
sector, defaulting to 999999.0 metres. 
  
The Min and Gain parameters are applied to the A and B values 
prior to the combination. 
 
The supported range combination modes are defined by the 
RangeMode parameter and are: 
 
RangeMode = 0: Disabled. No range matching is performed. 
The generated radar video has a range equal to the PIM A range. 
The PIM B radar video is assumed to have a range equal to the 
PIM A input. 
 
RangeMode = 1: Subsample. The generated radar video has a 
range equal to the larger of the PIM A and PIM B ranges. The 
input PIM with the shorter range will be sub-sampled before 
combination. The range of the video from each PIM in the output 
video will match the input ranges. 
 
RangeMode = 2: Highest Wins. The generated radar video has 
a range equal to the larger of the PIM A and PIM B ranges. When 
samples from the input PIM with the shorter range must be 
combined, the larger of the contributing values will be used. The 
range of the video from each PIM in the output video will match 
the input ranges. 
 
UseLSBIndicator. Disabled if set to 0 (default). If set to 1, the 
radar samples in the combined PIM have the Least Significant Bit 
cleared to 0 if the sample came from PIM A, or set to 1 if the 
sample came from PIM B. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 170 =====
Page 170 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.7.18. 
PIM Copy 
 
Name 
SPxProPimCopy 
Description 
Copy data into a different PIM, optionally truncating multi-byte 
samples down to 8-bits. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
MaxOutBytesPerSample: Integer (0 to 1, default = 0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8* 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This function copies data from PIM A to PIM B. This is a simple 
copy. The output PIM can subsequently be used as a source of 
input data in another process. 
 
Note the other method of copying a PIM using SPxProToRIB. This 
achieves a smilar result of copying a PIM to another PIM (in this 
case via a RIB), but has the additional properties of allowing the 
output PIM to be a different size to the input, and allowing the 
output PIM and all its subsequent processing to run in a different 
processing thread. See 6.7.19 for details). 
 
This process supports multi-byte samples. If a return contains 
two-byte samples and PIM B supports two-byte samples then the 
return is copied without being modified.  If the return contains 
two-byte sample but PIM B only supports one-byte samples, then 
the return data is truncated to one-byte samples before being 
written to PIM B.  The return header is modified to reflect the 
reduction in data size and change the packing type to 
SPX_RIB_PACKING_RAW8. 
 
The parameter MaxOutBytesPerSample may be set to force this 
process to truncate samples, even if the sample size is supported 
by PIM B.  If set to 0 then the sample size is limited to the 
maximum sample size support by PIM B. This is the default 
setting.  If set to 1 then two-byte samples are always truncated 
to one-byte. This feature may be used to reduce multi-byte 
samples down to one-byte for downstream processing by 
modules that only support one-byte samples.  If PIM A and PIM B 
are set to the same PIM, this process can be used to truncate 
samples and write them back to the same PIM. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 171 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 171 
6.7.19. 
Process To RIB 
 
Name 
SPxProToRIB 
Description 
Copy a PIM into a RIB to allow resizing and/or create a new 
thread of execution. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
StartRangeMetres : (default 0.0) 
EndRangeMetres : (-1.0) 
StartAzimuthDegrees : (0.0) 
EndAzimuthDegrees : (360.0) 
LatencyDegrees (default 0.0) 
 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
User Arg RIB 
User Arg 
SPxRIB * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has a value of 1. 
Copies a PIM into a user-defined RIB, which must have been 
created before constructing the SPxRunProcess object and is 
supplied as the User Arg. The RIB may in turn be connected to 
another PIM to achieve the effect of a PIM to PIM copy. 
 
The process may be used to provide data for a RIB. If that RIB 
is connected to a downstream PIM then the RIB is the conduit 
for a PIM to PIM copy. Since the PIM downstream of the RIB 
may have a different dimension to the input PIM, the effect can 
be to rescale the polar-store.  
 
Furthermore, when a RIB is created, a new thread of execution 
will be associated with all processes created downstream of the 
PIM connected to that RIB. So this function can be used to 
create a separate thread of execution and establish parallel 
processing branches. If this is done, care needs to be taken in 
the sharing of PIMs between the two threads, as there are no 
synchronisation methods between processes running in different 
threads. This is in contrast to the processing of data from a PIM 
in the same thread, where the order of processing is well 
defined. 
 
Regarding the StartRangeMetres, EndRangeMetres, 
StartAzimuthDegrees and EndAzimuthDegrees parameters - 
These parameters can be used to define a sub-area of the 
source data that restricts what will be written to the RIB. Only 
data inside the area is passed on to the destination RIB. Outside 
the azimuth area, blank data is written. A negative 
EndRangeMetres is treated as a special case to automatically 
represent the real end range of the incoming data, i.e. no 
restriction is used. 
Regarding the LatencyDegrees parameter - if greater than 
zero, this defines an angle used to delay the output of azimuths 
behind the source of the data.  For example, a latency of 90 
degrees would mean that the data written to the destination 
RIB is a quarter of a revolution behind the incoming data, so 


===== PAGE 172 =====
Page 172 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
when the source azimuth is at 180 degrees (for example), the 
data from 90 degrees is output. This relies on the source PIM 
having a suitably large dimension to store the data until it is 
output, and will therefore not work with a single azimuth PIM. 
 
Correct application of the SPxProToRIB process is important in 
getting best system performance on multi-processor systems. 
This process supports multi-byte samples. 
 
 
6.7.20. 
LUT Manipulation 
 
Name 
SPxProLUT 
Description 
Pass data through a LUT 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details.) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
SPxLUT * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This function passes the video in PIM A through the specified 
look-up table and provides the result to the output PIM B (which 
may be the same as PIM A if desired). Refer to Section 6.8.3 for 
details of the SPxLUT object). 
 
Note that it is the responsibility of the LUT to handle the 
different input formats, as needed. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 173 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 173 
6.7.21. 
Scan to Scan Integration (Version 1) 
 
Name 
SPxProSSI 
Description 
Integrate thresholded video between scans with an M:N 
detector (See also Scan to Scan Integration in Section 6.7.31, 
which for most cases is the preferred option for integration of 
small targets). 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Threshold : Integer (0..255 or -1) 
HistorySize : Integer (1 .. 15) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
PIM B (for optional thresholded data) 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM C 
User Arg 
SPxLUT12 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This function accumulates up to 15 scans of thresholded video 
as history. The actual number of scans accumulated is set 
through the parameter HistorySize. The output video is then 
set as a combination of the number of historical returns that 
exceed a threshold and the most recent video level. 
The input video is supplied through PIM A. PIM B is an optional 
input and if supplied can contain a processed (for example 
thresholded) version of the input. Otherwise PIM A is 
thresholded by this process at the constant level defined by 
Threshold (0 to 255). To enable the use of the PIM B to define 
the threshold, set the value of Threshold to -1.  
 
For simple thresholding, it is sufficient to define a constant 
Theshold, which will cause the process to ignore PIM B, and let 
the process do the thresholding. However, there is more 
flexibility in the thresholding process by implementing it 
externally, perhaps with SPxProDynamicThreshold, and 
providing the thresholded data into this process.  
 
The output value for each sample is derived by considering: 
 
 
the number of historical returns that exceeded the 
threshold. 
 
the current video sample. 
 
Let J be the number of detections for which the video intensity 
execeeds the Threshold (J = 0..15). 
 
Let X be the current video intensity (X = 0..255). 
 
Then the output is derived by combining J and X into a 12-bit 
look-up table, where J is the top four bits, to give an 8-bit 
output value.The look-up table is provided to the process as an 
instance of type SPxLUT12 provided through the user argument. 
The table can implement any combination of the historical data 
and current signal level. 
 


===== PAGE 174 =====
Page 174 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
An M from N detector is implemented by setting HistorySize 
to the required value of N and using M in the construction of 
the SPxLUT12 object. Refer to Section 6.8.4 for an explanation 
of the SPxLUT12 object. 
 
The integration store is a 16-bit deep bitmap that is 
dimensioned to be the same size as PIM A. This is internal to 
the process and is not externally available. 
 
See also SPxProSSI64 in Section 6.7.31. 
 
6.7.22. 
Area Select 
 
Name 
SPxProAreaSelect 
Description 
Process video in selected areas 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Modified PIM A 
User Arg 
SPxAreaSelect * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1 and 
the active region associated with the SPxAreaSelect object is 
active. 
 
This process allows area-dependent manipulation of the video, 
typically with the objective of eliminating video in specified areas 
to improve the dislay presentation or to eliminate unnecessary 
video prior to compression. An area is defined as a set of 
complex polygons or range-azimuth segments. Video that falls 
inside (or outside) this area is passed through a look-up table, 
whereas the opposite video is left unaffected. The affected video 
is changed in PIM A and is therefore visible to all processes that 
are downstream of this one. 
 
The process may be used with the SPx World map object to 
permit automatic blanking of video over land using a high-
resolution world vector shoreline (40m resolution). 
 
Figure 38 - Processing video in different areas with SPxProAreaSelect 
Highlighting a sector 
Show video only in defined area 


===== PAGE 175 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 175 
This process may be used to implement a number of processing functions, 
including: 
 
 
Video can be eliminated from selected areas, which can either be defined 
as polygons or else defined automatically from a world shoreline 
database. 
 
 
Video can be highlighted in an area. 
 
 
Video can be reduced in intensity in selected areas. 
 
The definition of an area for this process is through a set of complex polygons 
and range-azimuth segments.  
 
The SPx object SPxAreaSelect is provided to the constructor of the RunProcess 
as the user argument. The SPxAreaSelect object is constructed from an 
SPxActiveRegion, an SPxLUT8 object and, optionally, an SPxNavData object. 
Refer to Section 6.8.1 for a description of the SPxAreaSelect object. 
 
This process can be used to process video for a static or a moving platform. In 
the case of a moving platform, an SPxNavData object must be provided to the 
SPxAreaSelect object. The associated SPxActiveRegion object is then 
assumed to contain areas defined in lat/long copordinates. The radar position, 
as provided by the SPxNavData object, is periodically read by the process and 
used to update the SPxActiveRegion object. 
 
When using the process with the SPxWorldMap, the polygons are automatically 
defined by the world map. Refer to Section 6.8.17 for details of this. This 
process supports automatic blanking of video over land areas given a lat/long 
coordinate of a radar (static radar applications only). Sample code for world 
map blanking is shown in Section 6.8.17.2. This creates an area selection 
process that removes video over land areas, given a lat/long location of the 
radar. 
 
 


===== PAGE 176 =====
Page 176 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.7.23. 
Insertion of Range Rings 
 
Name 
SPxProRangeRings 
Description 
Inserts range rings into radar video 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Metres : Real 
MetresList : String 
Percent : Integer 
Replace : Integer 
Style : Integer 
StyleArg1 : Real 
StyleArg2: Real 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
This process inserts range rings into the input video. The spacing 
between the rings may be set with the Metres or MetresList 
parameter. If set, MetresList takes priority and sets the rings 
from a list of up to 32 comma-separated ranges (in metres). For 
example, if MetresList is set to “1000,2000, 5000,10000”, the 
process will add range rings at 1km, 2km, 5km and 10km. 
Otherwise, the Metres parameter defines the interval between 
the rings in metres. To switch from using MetresList to Metres, 
set MetresList to the string “disable”. 
 
The Percent parameter defines the video intensity of the ring, 
with 100 being a ring of maximum brightness. The Replace 
parameter is non-zero if the range ring is to replace the incoming 
video, or is zero if the range ring is to be added to the incoming 
video. 
 
By default, rings are created at 10 km intervals, with a 50% 
intensity (i.e. video level 128) with “replace” disabled, so the ring 
is added to the existing video intensity and hence bright targets 
will still be visible. 
 
The rings are drawn in a solid line if Style = 0 and in a dash line 
if Style = 1. For dash lines, the parameter StyleArg1 defines the 
angle of the dash. For example, a value of Style = 1 and  
StyleArg1 of 2.0 means that the dash line is drawn with 2 
degrees of line then 2 degrees of space repeating around the 
circle. 
 
See Figure 39 for an example of this process. 


===== PAGE 177 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 177 
 
Figure 39 – Scan conversion before and after the insertion of Range Rings  
(10km spacing for this example)

Page 178 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.7.24. 
Channel Synchronisation 
 
Name 
SPxProSyncCombine 
Description 
Synchronise video from two channels 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
RIB provided in SPxSyncCombineSrc User Arg. 
User Arg 
SPxSyncCombineSrc * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process, in conjunction with the SPxSyncCombineSrc, which is 
provided as a User Arg, synchronises video from two inputs. A 
RunProcess object of type SPxProSyncCombine must be created for 
each of the two channels to be combined. The source PIM for each 
process must have the same dimension and must be fed with data 
from a RIB at nominally the same timing, but with the allowance of 
a time difference between the azimuths. 
 
The data for the two input channels is combined using highest-wins 
and written to an output RIB. Refer to Section 6.8.10 for details of 
the SPxSyncCombineSrc object. 
 
This process is designed to combine two data streams that are of 
the same timing and differ only in their phase. 
 
Figure 40 - Operation of SPxProSyncCombine process

6.7.25. 
Radar Recording 
 
Name 
SPxProRecord 
Description 
Record radar video and optionally navigation data to a file 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
MaxFileSizeMB : Integer 
MaxFileSizeSeconds : Integer 
EncodeFormat : Integer 
FilePrefix : String 
StatusBandwidth : Real 
StatusRatio : Real 
StatusFilename : String 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16, 8+8 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
None 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process records radar video to a file. The data from the input 
PIM is compressed using either ZLIB or ORC compression and 
written to a file (or sequence of files).  This process supports the 
recording of radar video containing multi-byte samples. 
 
Ensure that SPxProRecordInit() is called before using the 
recording process (this explicit call to the initialisation function is 
needed so that an application built without the recording option 
does not need to be linked with the external zlib libraries). Your 
application will need to be linked with the zlib libraries when using 
the record process, even if you are using ORC encoding (see 
Section 11.2 for link options). 
The parameter MaxFileSizeMB defines the maximum size of a 
single recording file. If the current recording exceeds this size a 
new file is automatically created. Similarly, the parameter 
MaxFileSizeSeconds defines the recording time in seconds before 
a new file is automatically created. 
 
The encoding format is set with EncodeFormat, which may be 
either SPX_RIB_PACKING_ORC for ORC coding (lower CPU use but 
lower compression) or SPX_RIB_PACKING_ZLIB (higher cpu use but 
higher compression) for zlib coding. 
 
The name of the file is derived from the current date and time of 
day prefixed by the value of FilePrefix (which defaults to an 
empty string). 
 
The actual filename used for recording may be read from the 
parameter StatusFilename (this is a read-only parameter). 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 180 =====
Page 180 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Note that the default activity state of a process in the 
SPxRunProcess constructor is active, meaning that recording will 
start as soon as the process is created. If this is not desired, 
ensure that the initialActivityState of the process is 0 in the 
constructor. 
 
The parameter StatusBandwidth provides an indication of the 
data rate being written to the recording device (bytes/sec). 
 
The parameter StatusRatio provides an indication of the 
compression ratio being achieved if compression is active. 
 
See Section 3.6 for details of how to replay the recording. 
 
By default the process does not record navigation data. To record 
navigation data call the following function: 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxProRecordUseNavData(SPxRunProcess *rp) 
 
where rp is the instance of SPxProRecord. After this function has 
been called, the global object SPxNavData is used to retrieve the 
navigation data and store into the radar recording file. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 181 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 181 
6.7.26. 
Target Extraction 
 
Name 
SPxProSpanDetect 
Description 
Extract plots from input video (primary or IFF) 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
StartRangeMetres : Real 
ThresholdStart : Integer (Default 64) 
ThresholdStop : Integer (Default 0) 
MinSpanLengthSamples : Integer (Default 2) 
MinBlobRangeMetres : Real (Default 0.0) 
MaxBlobRangeMetres : Real (Default 99999999.0) 
MinBlobAzimuthDegrees : Real (Default 0.5) 
MinBlobAzimuthMetres : Real (Default 0.0) 
MaxBlobAzimuthDegrees : Real (Default 180.0) 
MaxBlobAzimuthMetres : Real (Default 0.0) 
MinBlobWeight : Integer (Default 0) 
WorldMapInhibitLandMetres : Real (Default 0.0) 
WorldMapInhibitSeaMetres : Real (Default 0.0) 
SmallBlobSectorLimit : Integer (Default 0) 
SmallBlobWeight : Integer (Default 0) 
CentroidMode : Integer (Default 0) 
IFFPattern : Integer (Default 0) 
IFFStartSample : Integer (Default 1)  
AllowNonQualifiers : Integer (Default 0) 
AllowAnonIFF : Integer (Default 1) 
DopplerMinSpeed : Real (Default 0.0) 
DopplerMaxVariance : Real (Default 0.0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Target Database of Blob Detector 
User Arg 
SPxBlobDetector * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The process may be used to extract plots from primary or OFF 
video. 
This process examines the video in the input PIM and identifies 
target-like shapes as plots. These plots are characterised by a set 
of video samples that pass a set of tests for minimum size and 
intensity. 
 
The process only considers video whose range is greater than 
StartRangeMetres metres.  
 
For each return, video is examined to find a sample having an 
intensity that is greater than level ThresholdStart. A “span” of 
video is then identified such that all subsequent samples in the 
span have a level greater than ThresholdStop. If ThresholdStop = 
0 then it means use the value of ThresholdStart. The span is 
accepted for further consideration if it is greater than 
MinSpanLengthSamples samples. 
The Blob Detector object, which is passed in the User Arg field, is 
an instance of SPxBlobDetector and must be created by the caller 
before the process is created. The Blob Detector is responsible for 


===== PAGE 182 =====
Page 182 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
combing spans across multiple azimuths to identify connected 
shapes. Refer to Section 6.8.11 for details of the Blob Extractor. 
 
The blob is acceptable if it passes a number of size checks, positin 
checks and there are not too many blobs in a sector (which is a 
mechanism to optionally limit the number of blobs in a small 
area). 
 
The following conditions must be met for a blob to be acceptable: 
 
1. The blob’s range must exceed MinBlobRangeMetres. 
 
2. The blob’s range must be less than MaxBlobRangeMetres 
metres. 
 
3. The blob’s azimuth must be less than either 
MaxBlobAzimuthDegrees or MaxBlobAzimuthMetres, which 
represents the measured dimension of the blob at its range, 
i.e. 2 x range x tan(azimuth/2). If MaxBlobAzimuthMetres is 
0, this check is disabled. 
 
4. The blob’s weight (the number of samples above the 
threshold) must be more than than MinBlobWeight. This 
parameter is sometimes used when it is desired to capture 
blobs that may be a single azimuth or single range sample 
in size, yet for which we require at least two samples in 
either range or azimuth. In this case, the minimum azimuth 
and range dimension of the plot is set to correspond to one 
sample, yet MinBlobWeight is set to 2. 
 
5. If the blob’s position is too close to a coastline the blob can 
be ignored. If the parameter WorldMapInhibitLandMetres is 
non-zero, then the blob is rejected if it is over land and less 
than WorldMapInhibitLandMetres from the coast (for 
example set this parameter to a large number to suppress 
any blob over land). Similarly, if the parameter 
WorldMapInhibitSeaMetres is non-zero, then the blob is 
rejected if it is over sea and less than 
WorldMapInhibitSeaMetres from the coast (for example set 
the parameter to 500 metres to eliminate blobs that are 
within 500 metres of the coastline. The definition of the 
coast is handled by associating a world map object in the 
Blob Detector attached to this span detector process.  
 
In addition to the blob’s size and weight, the blob may also be 
rejected if there are too many blobs in a small area. The 
SmallBlobSectorLimit parameter specifies the maximum number 
of small plots in a sector (32 sectors in 360 degrees).  The 
definition of a "small" plot is controlled by the SmallBlobWeight 
parameter (see below). 
 
If SmallBlobSectorLimit parameter is set to 0, there is no limit on 
the number of small blobs. If it is more than 0, then the sector is 
split into 8 range bins and the parameter is linearly split across 
the range bins, although there is a minimum count of 4 in each 


===== PAGE 183 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 183 
bin as a safety measure (in case there are several targets close in 
range). 
If there are more plots in the range bin than the limit allows, the 
largest ones are kept and the smallest ones are discarded.  This is 
based on the weight of the plot, i.e. the number of significant 
samples that make it up. 
The parameter SmallBlobWeight specifies the weight limit for what 
is deemed a small plot for the above per-sector limit.  If 0, all 
plots are checked against the per-sector limit.  If non-zero, only 
plots that have a weight less than this parameter are checked 
against the limit. In other words, if the parameter is non-zero, any 
plot with a weight greater than or equal to this parameter will be 
accepted regardless of the normal per-sector limit above. This 
ensures that large plots can bypass the limit. 
 
If the blob passes the size tests, it is inserted into the blob 
database associated with the blob detector object. Refer to section 
6.8.11 for details of the blob detection object and the blob 
database. 
 
The AllowNonQualifiers parameter can be set to 1 to allow 
blobs that do not pass the size tests to be inserted into the 
database anyway, with a flag indicating their status. 
 
The AllowAnonIFF parameter can be set to 0 to suppress IFF 
blobs that do not have any valid codes extracted for them.  (This 
is ignored if the process is not in IFF mode.) 
 
In normal situations, the plot extractor reports the centre position 
of a plot as the weighted centre assuming all samples above the 
detection threshold are equal. For specialised applications, other 
ways of reporting the plot position are available. 
 
The parameter CentroidMode controls how the position of the plot 
is reported. There are three options: 
 
CentroidMode = 0 (the default). Report the weighted centre 
of the plot assuming all samples are equal. 
 
CentroidMode = 1. Report the intensity-based centroid, 
giving additional emphasis to samples with higher signal 
levels. When using this option, incoming samples must 
have intensity preserved by the earlier process. Specifically, 
the Thresholding process should not be configured to set all 
samples to 255 
 
CentroidMode = 2. Report the leading edge of the plot in 
range dimension and weighted centre in azimuth). 
 
CentroidMode = 3. Extract the video as IFF. The value of 
the parameter IFFPattern is used to indicate the 
interrogation pattern of the IFF. 
 
The parameter IFFPattern defines the interrogation pattern for IFF 
decoding. This may be one of the following values: 


===== PAGE 184 =====
Page 184 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
IFFPattern = 0. The decoder will automatically detect the IFF 
pattern from the spacing of the P1 and P3 pulses, if present at the 
start of the radar video data. The zero range point will be 
automatically set according to the position of the P3 pulse. 
 
IFFPattern = 1. IFF video contains mode 3/A interrogation 
only. 
 
 
 
IFFPattern = 2. IFF video contains mode 3/A and mode C 
interrogation replies on alternate spokes (i.e. 3,A,3,A,3 
etc.). 
 
IFFPattern = 3. IFF video contains mode 3/A and mode C 
interrogation replies in an AAC repeating pattern. 
 
IFFpattern = 4. IFF video contains mode 3/A, C and 1 or 2 
in a three-way repeating pattern. 
 
The parameter IFFStartSample defines the index of the first 
sample searched for the P1/P3 pair of pulses when the decoder is 
set to IFFPattern 0 (the automatic mode). This parameter was 
introduced in SPx version V1.76.5 and has a default value of 1 to 
preserve the existing behaviour of earlier versions. 
 
If IFFStartSample is set to zero, the decoder will start 
searching immediately and will assume that the first sample 
above the threshold is the start of the P1 pulse. It does not 
need to detect a rising edge (just a high level), so this 
setting is appropriate when the P1 pulse is being used as 
the trigger pulse itself. 
 
If IFFStartSample is not zero, the decoder will delay 
searching until the specified sample index is reached. In 
this mode it needs to detect a rising edge and will therefore 
require the signal to be low before it goes high for the P1 
pulse. This is appropriate for situations where a separate 
trigger pulse is used and the signal from that pulse (or 
another source) may appear in the first few samples of 
radar video. Delaying the search and waiting until the signal 
is low avoids this video being wrongly treated as the P1 
pulse. 
 
When using the IFF extraction, it is important to ensure that the 
range resolution of the radar video is high enough for the coded 
bit patterns to be detected. The bits can clearly be seen in the 
sample display of Figure 41. This would typically require a PIM 
range dimension of 4k samples per 100km of radar coverage, , 
but for enhanced de-garbling better results can be obtained with 
higher resolutions, e.g. 8000 samples per 100km of range. It is 
also recommended that a plot merging process is used after plot 
extraction to combine plots with up to 0.5 degree gaps in azimuth. 
 


===== PAGE 185 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 185 
 
Figure 41 - IFF Video showing bit pattern for coded interrogation 
 
Plots that are extracted by this process are accessible through the 
plots database, which is described in Section 6.8.12. 
 
If the radar video contains Doppler information in a supported 
format, the DopplerMinSpeed and DopplerMaxVariance 
parameters can be used to suppress plots that fall below the 
speed threshold (i.e. are moving too slowly) or are above the 
variance threshold (i.e. have too much inconsistency within their 
data) respectively. 
 
It is typically the case that the SPxProSpanDetect process is 
preceded by one or more other SPx processes to pre-process the 
signal. This pre-processing may include thresholding or area 
selection to define an area of interest for the processing. The 
example shown in Figure 42 shows a number of target aircraft 
extracted. The blob detection has been restricted to runways and 
taxiways using the SPxProAreaSelect process ahead of 
SPxProSpanDetect, for example using the typical processing 
sequence shown in Figure 43. 
 
Figure 42 - SPxProSpanDetector identifies target blobs (plots) in the input video 
 


===== PAGE 186 =====
Page 186 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 43 - Typical processing sequence for blob extraction with SPxProSpanDetect 
6.7.27. 
Target (Plot) Extraction – Plot Merging 
 
Name 
SPxProBlobMerge 
Description 
Process plots to merge together those that are close. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Mode: Integer (Default 0) 
KeepComponents: Integer (Default 0) 
MinMergedRangeMetres: Real (Default 0.0) 
MaxMergedRangeMetres: Real (Default 99999999) 
MinMergedAzimuthDegrees: Real (Default 0.5) 
MinMergedAzimuthMetres: Real (Default 0.0) 
MaxMergedAzimuthDegrees: Real (Default 180.0) 
MaxMergedAzimuthMetres: Real (Default 0.0) 
MergeMarginDegrees: Real (Default 1.0) 
MergeMarginMetres: Real (Default 100.0) 
Input Data 
Target Database specified in User Arg 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
Target Database 
User Arg 
SPxTargetDB * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process would normally follow SPxProSpanDetect. The 
identification of plots in SPxProSpanDetect may create many 
small plots for a target, for example because the target appears 
fragmented in small pieces due to the reflective characteristics of 
the target.  
 
For the purposes of target tracking, it is desirable to have a 
single consistent plot representing the target. The purpose of the 
SPxProBlobMerge process is to combine together the small plots 
into larger plots. This combination process may proceed in a 
number of modes, with each mode supporting parameters to 
control the angles and distances between plots that may be 
merged. 
 
Even if plots are merged, the process can optionally retain the 
original unmerged plots in the database.  
 
The value of Mode defines the operation of the process as follows: 
 
Mode = 0 – No merging is done. 
 
Mode = 1 – Azimuth merging is done for plots that are 
RIB 
PIM 
SPxProSpanDetect 
SPxBlobDetect 
 
SPxTargetDatabase 
 
SPxProAreaSelect 


===== PAGE 187 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 187 
correlated in range. This mode is intended to be used for 
longer range radars where a plot might appear fragmented 
into small eyebrows – see Figure 44. 
 
Mode = 2 – Distance merging is done using the general 
proximities of plots in range and azimuth. This mode is 
intended to be used for shorter range radars where a plot 
might appear fragmented due to different return strength 
across the target structure – see Figure 44. 
 
Mode = 3 – Shape Distance Merging is done based on the 
estimated separation between the plots considering their 
oriented shape, rather than just the range/azimuth 
bounding box. This mode is intended to be used for 
medium range radars such as VTS installations where very 
large ships can appear as distinguishable rectangular 
shapes unaligned to the range/azimuth axis. In these 
situations, merging based on centroid proximity or the gap 
between bounding boxes could result in false positives. 
 
If KeepComponents is 1, the process retains the original 
plots in the database. If KeepComponents is 0, the plots 
that are used for merging are removed. 
 
For Mode 1 merging, the plots must have a range centre 
within MergeMarginMetres and an azimuth gap of less 
than MergeMarginDegrees – see Figure 45. This mode is 
commonly associated with air surveillance radars where 
the plots exhibit some break-up in azimuth, but are 
otherwise highly correlated in range. 
 
For Mode 2 merging, the distance between the centre 
points must be less than MergeMarginMetres for merging 
– see Figure 45. This mode is commonly associated with 
airport ground-movement radars, where targets may be 
observed as a number of separate pieces. 
 
For Mode 3 merging, the MergeMarginMetres and 
MergeMarginDegrees parameters define the mazimum 
allowable gap in the range and azimuth directions 
respectively. Plots with an estimated separation that 
exceeds either of these margins will not be merged. 
 
When plots are merged, they are only retained if the 
merged plot has have a range dimension that is at least 
MinMergedRangeMetres and no greater than 
MaxMergedRangeMetres. Also, the plot’s azimuth must 
exceed both MinMergedAzimuthDegrees and 
MinMergedAzimithMetres, and be less than either 
MaxMergedAzimuthDegrees or MaxMergedAzimuthMetres. 
 
The parameters MinMergedAzimuthMetres and 
MaxMergedAzimuthMetres are the minimum and maximum 
dimension of a plot subtended by its azimuth extent. 


===== PAGE 188 =====
Page 188 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
A typical processing sequence involving SPxProBlobMerge is 
shown in Figure 46. 
 
 
Figure 44 - Plot Merging Modes

highly correlated in range. 
Mode 2 Plot Merging – Plots 
are separated according to 
the target structure (close 
radar, for example ground 
movement).

are separated according to

not)

6.7.28. 
Trail History Retention 
 
Name 
SPxProHistory 
Description 
Enable trail history retention on PPI scan conversion 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
FadeType: Integer 
FadeRate: Integer 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (the history PIM) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process is used with PPI scan conversion, SPxProScanConv, 
to provide trail history retention on view changes. The normal 
behaviour of PPI scan conversion process is that trail history is 
lost when the view changes. The SPxProHistory process manages 
a history store that automatically refreshes the display at the new 
view. 
 
Note that the function of this process is to preserve the video 
trails on a view change. The process does not create the trail 
history. The scan converter must be configured to create the trails 
in the first place, for example by using sweep fade with a fade 
time of many sweeps. Once the trails are present, the use of this 
process will keep the trails even after the view has changed. 
 
To use the process, create a SPxProHistory object that connects 
to the PIM being used for scan conversion, and connects to a 
second PIM, the history PIM, which has the same dimensions as 
the input. When creating the scan conversion process, provide the 
same history PIM to that process. Finally tell the scan converter 
about the history process with the function 
SetRedrawHistoryProcess (see Section 7.10.1). The parameters 
of SPxProHistory are manipulated automatically by the 
connected scan conversion process so that changes to the fading 
of the scan converter are reported to the history process.  
 
 
 
SPxBlobDetect 
 
SPxTargetDatabase 
 
From RIB and 
PIM 
SPxProSpanDetect 
SPxProAreaSelect 
SPxProBlobMerge 


===== PAGE 191 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 191 
 
Trail history retention does not work with random scan radars. On 
moving platforms, this form of trail history retention preserves 
Relative Trails only.  To support True Trails on moving platforms, 
refer to section 7.10.1.11 and 7.10.1.12. 
 
A typical configuration is shown in Figure 47. 
 
Figure 47 - Using SPxProHistory with SPxProSourceLocal for trail history retention 
 
For example: 
 
    /* Create a new PIM to hold the history data (must  
 
 * be the same size as the normal PIM). 
     */ 
    historyPIM = new SPxPIM(NULL, rangeDim, aziDim); 
  
    /* Create a history process to update the PIM. */ 
    SPxRunProcess *historyProcess = new SPxRunProcess( 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxProHistory, NULL, 
                              spxPIM, historyPIM); 
  
    /* Give both PIMs to the scan converter process. */ 
    SPxRunProcess *scProcess = new SPxRunProcess( 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxProScanConv, 
                              historyProcess,spxPIM,   
 
 
 
 
historyPIM, spxSc); 
  
    /* Tell the scan converter about the history process, 
     * so that it can keep fade rates in sync etc. 
     */ 
 
    
spxSc->SetRedrawHistoryProcess(historyProcess);
