6.7.29. 
Land Mass Mapping (LMM) 
 
Name 
SPxProLMM 
Description 
Land Mass Mapping process to automatically eliminate large 
areas of video (land, weather etc.). 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
StartRangeMetres: Real  (Default 0.0) 
ThresholdStart: Integer  (Default 64) 
ThresholdStop: Integer  (Default 0) 
MinBlobRangeMetres : Real (Default 500.0) 
MinBlobAzimuthDegrees: Real (Default 10.0) 
MinBlobAzimuthMetres : Real (Default 5000.0) 
MinBlobSizeMode : Integer (Default 0) 
LandMassOutputGain : Real (0.0) 
LandMassOutputOffset : Real (0) 
LandMassSpreadNearMetres : Real (0.0) 
LandMassSpreadFarMetres : Real (0.0) 
LandMassSpreadDegrees : Real (0.0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM A 
User Arg 
SPxBlobDetector * 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The Land Mass Mapping process can be used to remove land 
mass from the radar video signal. This can be especially useful 
when a plot extraction or tracking process is to follow, since the 
spurious ground-based returns that occur on and around land 
masses can easily saturate a plot extraction or tracking process. 
 
The Land Mass Mapping is generally used only on moving 
platform applications. For stationary platforms it is possible to 
use a static blanking mask to eliminate video using a polygon 
(see process SPxProAreaSelect in Section 6.7.22).  
 
The Land Mass Mapping process examines video to identify large 
connected shapes that have a land-like appearance. The data is 
processed from PIM A and the same PIM is updated with the 
processed video. A number of parameters control the process. 
 
StartRangeMetres is the minimum range at which the land mass 
mapping is effective.  
 
ThresholdStart defines the threshold level (0 to 255) for 
identification of land area. Once video exceeds this level, the 
remainder of the return is processed until the level drops below 
ThresholdStop. If ThresholdStop = 0, then just a single level 
threshold is used. It is possible to use a more sophisticated form 
of detection using the dynamic thresholder 
SPxProDynamicThreshold (see Section 6.7.7) as a processing 
stage before SPxProLMM. 
 


===== PAGE 193 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 193 
 
An area of video is considered to be land if the range size 
exceeds MinBlobRangeMetres and/or the azimuth size exceeds 
MinBlobAzimuthDegrees and MinBlobAzimuthMetres (being the 
azimuth extent of the video block in metres) - see Figure 48. The 
decision between AND or OR in the combination of the range and 
azimuth sizes is affected by the parameter MinBlobSizeMode. 
Setting this parameter to 0 means AND; setting it to 1 means 
OR. For the OR mode, the blob is classified as land if either the 
range or azimuth minimums are exceeded. This allows a land 
area that has a large azimuth extent but short range extent to be 
classified. 
 
Figure 48 - Parameters for Land Mass Mapping 
 
An area of video that is identified as land is enlarged by 
“spreading” the area. This allows for a margin of error in the 
identification of land. The spreading occurs for 
LandMassSpreadNearMetres metres ahead of the video, 
LandMassSpreadFarMetres behind the video and for 
LandMassSpreadDegrees on either side of the identified video 
area.  In most situations, it is desirable that the video is blanked 
to the end of the range once land has been identified. This is 
accomplished by setting LandMassSpreadFarMetres to be a 
large number – see example later. 
 
If an area of video passes the criteria of being land, the video in 
that area and the surrounding margin is manipulated by 
multiplying by a gain, LandMassOutputGain, and adding an 
offset LandMassOutputOffset. Each sample is updated using: 
Output = Gain * Input + Offset 
 
To blank the land video to 0 simply set LandMassOutputGain to 
0.0 and LandMassOutputOffset to 0. 
 
When instantiating a Land Mass Mapping function, a previously 
created SPxBlobDetector object needs to be provided as the 
MinBlobRangeMetres 
MinBlobAzimuthDegrees 
MinBlobAzimuthMetres 
Radar centre 
LandMassSpreadFarMetres 
LandMassSpreadNearMetres 
LandMassSpreadDegrees 
 


===== PAGE 194 =====
Page 194 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
user argument. This blob detector is configured by the 
parameters of the land mass mapping process. 
 
An example output from the Land Mass Mapping process is 
shown in Figure 49. The first image shows the original data, the 
middle image shows the effect of using LMM to suppress the 
land (with minimal margin), and the right image shows the 
effect of using LandMassSpreadFarMetres to suppress video 
from the end of the land to the end of the return. 
 
An example of creating and using a land mass mapping process 
is as follows: 
 
 
/* Create the blob detector that LMM will use */ 
SPxBlobDetector *blobDetector = new SPxBlobDetector(NULL); 
 
 
/* Create the land mass mapping process – assume first Process after 
input PIM. */ 
 
m_proLMM = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProLMM, NULL, spxPIM, blobDetector); 
 
 
/* Set parameters for LMM */ 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("StartRangeMetres", 0.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("MinBlobRangeMetres", 500.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("MinBlobAzimuthDegrees", 5.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("MinBlobAzimuthMetres", 500.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("LandMassOutputGain", 0.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueI("LandMassOutputOffset", 0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("LandMassSpreadNearMetres", 100.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("LandMassSpreadFarMetres", 100000.0); 
 
m_proLMM->SetParamValueR("LandMassSpreadDegrees", 2.0); 
 
 
Figure 49 - Example of land mass mapping (left is input data, middle is blanked land, right 
is land blanked to end of return with LandMassSpreadFarMetres  
set to large value). 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 195 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 195 
6.7.30. 
Debug Process 
 
Name 
SPxProDebug 
Description 
Reports debugging information. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
FilePrefix : String (default NULL) 
MaxFileSizeMB : Integer (default 50) 
Flags : Integer (default 0x00000000) 
AzimuthStart : Integer (default 0) 
AzimuthEnd : Integer (default 0) 
SampleStart : Integer (default 0) 
SampleEnd : Integer (default 15) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
None 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
This process is used to generate debug reports from the data 
contained in radar returns.  The debug data may be written to a 
log file (if FilePrefix is set) or written to ‘standard out’.  The 
name of the log file is created from the defined prefix, which may 
be an absolute or relative path name and is appended with the 
current date and time. 
 
The MaxFileSizeMB parameter is used to define the maximum 
size of the log file in megabytes.  When the file size limit is 
reached, the log file is closed and a new log file is created.  If an 
existing log file is present, then it is deleted so that only the 
previous and current log files are preserved. 
 
The Flags parameter is used to define ‘trigger’ and ‘report’ flags.  
Trigger flags are used to control when debug reports are output.  
Report flags are used to define the content of debug reports. 
 
The following trigger flags are supported: 
 
0x00000001 
Trigger on Azimuth Gate 
Trigger debug report when azimuth number is between 
AzimuthStart and AzimuthEnd (inclusive).  These parameters 
define the start and end azimuth number to capture.  If 
AzimuthEnd is set to -1, this is interpreted as the maximum 
azimuth number. 
 
0x00000002 
Trigger on Missing Azimuth 
Trigger debug report when non-consecutive azimuth numbers 
are detected. 
 
0x00000004 
Trigger on Repeated Azimuth 
Trigger debug report when consecutive azimuths have identical 
azimuth numbers. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 196 =====
Page 196 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
0x00000008 
Trigger on Short Return 
Trigger debug report when return length is less than the 
nominal return length. 
 
0x00000010 
Trigger on Return Header Change 
Trigger debug report when return header changes.  Fields that 
change in every return, such as azimuth number and count are 
excluded. 
 
 
The following report flags are supported: 
 
0x00010000 
Report Return Header 
Print return header fields in a debug report. 
 
0x00020000 
Report Min/Max Samples 
Show the minimum and maximum range sample values in a 
debug report. 
 
0x00040000 
Report Sample Distribution 
Show distribution of range sample values in a debug report. 
 
0x00080000 
Report Values Within Sample Gate 
Print range samples between SampleStart and SampleEnd 
(inclusive) in a debug report.  If SampleEnd is set to -1, this is 
interpreted as the maximum range sample. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 197 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 197 
6.7.31. 
Scan to Scan Integration (SSI) Process 
 
Name 
SPxProSSI64 
Description 
Implements scan-to-scan integration for the emphasis of slow-
moving, weak targets in the video of static radars. This may be 
used for display enhancement or part of a processing chain for 
target extraction. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
FullWindowSize : Integer (Default 32) 
RecentWindowSize : Integer (Default 0) 
FullHitCountThreshold : Integer (Default 0) 
RecentHitCountThreshold : Integer (Default 0) 
IntegrationVideoThreshold : Integer (Default 0) 
InstantVideoThreshold : Integer (Default 255) 
RecursiveAverageGain : Real (0.0 .. 1.0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
Optional SPxLUT* 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The SPxProSSI64 process is only relevant for static radars (not 
moving platforms). It provides a capability to enhance the video 
by emphasising slow-moving, weak targets. These targets have 
the characteristic that they are hard to differentiate from noise on 
a given scan, and would be very difficult to acquire a track on 
without the long-term integration. 
 
Scan-to-scan integration works by considering up to 64 scans of 
video data to look for video that appears, on average, to be higher 
than the background noise level. This is a very sensitive process 
that can generate enhanced video where normal viewing of the 
video would hardly differentiate target from background. 
 
In the example of Figure 50, the original video is shown (in green) 
in five scans. The processed video (in red) after SSI is shown in 
the final picture. The target is observed by emphasising the 
correlated video, which is near the centre of the window. The 
higher than average level of video in this area, relative to the 
background noise, eventually reveals the presence of the target. 
 


===== PAGE 198 =====
Page 198 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 50 - Use of Scan-to-Scan Integration (SSI) to emphasise slow, weak targets 
 
The SSI process can work on input video that has previously 
been thresholded, or else it may threshold the video itself. 
 
There are three situations: 
 
Scenario 1: In this situation (the usual case), there is a 
previous Dynamic Threshold (SPxProDynamicThreshold) 
process enabled, but the process is configured to output 
analogue video above the local average. Hence, it is not actually 
thresholding the video, just removing an average signal level to 
leave the above-average signal. This situation allows the 
thresholds for the integrated and non-integrated paths to be set 
separately in the SSI-64 module. Since the Dynamic Threshold 
module is adaptively processing the video to pass through only 
the above average signal, the thresholds used in the SSI-64 
module are relative to the signal average and therefore are 
adaptive to changing signal levels. In most cases, this is the 
preferred configuration. 
 
Scenario 2: In this scenario, there is no preceding threshold 
process so the SSI-64 module is provided with unthresholded 
video. In this case, the SSI-64 module is responsible for 
thresholding the video itself, with different thresholds being used 
for the integrated and non-integrated paths. The limitation of 
this approach is that the thresholds are fixed and do not adapt 
to the local signal average across the return (no CFAR). 
 
Scenario 3: In this scenario, there is a preceding process 
enabled and configured to threshold the video. Since the SSI-64 
module is then supplied with pre-thresholded video, the two 


===== PAGE 199 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 199 
input thresholds in the SSI-64 module are redundant. The same 
thresholded video is passed into the integrated and non-
integrated processing paths. The approach may be useful if 
target video is intermittent and would not be detected in the 
non-integrated path. However, to be effective the video would 
need to be reasonable strong, since weak video (hence a low 
threshold delta in the Threshold module), could present 
excessive video through the non-integrated path and hence 
directly into the plot extractor. In this configuration, any video 
that contributes to the integration process must necessarily 
appear in the output. The more flexible approach is represented 
by Scenario 1 above, which allows a different threshold to be 
used in the integrated and non-integrated paths. 
 
The output of the SSI process is a combination of video that 
exceeds a threshold (non-integrated) and video that has been 
integrated. Where the output is derived from integration, the 
number of hits in the integration is used to compute the output 
amplitude, so that highly correlated video (which may still be 
very weak) is output as 255 and completely uncorrelated video 
as 0. For the non-integrated path, the output video is 255 if the 
input exceeds a threshold, and is otherwise the value derived 
from the integrated path.  
 
A block diagram of the process is shown in Figure 51. 
 
Figure 51 - Block Diagram of the SSI-64 Process 
 
The SSI-64 process is normally used with a preceding Dynamic 
Threshold process (SPxProDynamicThreshold). The typical 
mode is Scenario 1, described previously, in which the Dynamic 
Threshold process is used to remove the local signal average 
Full Window 
Integration 
Window size = FullWindowSize 
Min Hits = FullHitCountThreshold 
Recent Window 
Integration 
Window size = RecentWindowSize 
Min Hits = RecentHitCountThreshold 
 
AND 
MAX 
InstantVideoThreshold 
0xFF or 0 
If True, then  
Output = K.hitCount 
    Where K = 255/FullWindowSize 
IntegrationVideoThreshold 
0xFF or 0 
Input 
video 
Output 
video 
Non-integrated path 
Integrated 
path 
1 = In recent window  
0 = Not in window 
1 = In full window  
0 = Not in window 


===== PAGE 200 =====
Page 200 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
and the thresholding is implemented in SSI-64 using a different 
value for the integrated and non-integrated paths. In this case, 
configure the associated Dynamic Threshold process with: 
 
ThreshDelta = 0 
ThreshDeltaStop = 0 
ThreshOutputValue = -1 
 
This configures the Dynamic Threshold process to output video 
that exceeds the local average of video across a return. The 
window to be used for the thresholding (WindowSizeSample or 
WindowSizeWorld) needs to be set as normal for the process. 
The assignment of ThreshOutputValue = -1, requests the 
threshold module to output video above the local average (i.e. 
video out = video in – local average). 
 
The SSI process examines scan detections over a number of 
scans, up to a limit of 64. A larger number of scans allows 
weaker, more intermittent targets to be considered, but it also 
requires that the targets being considered are moving more 
slowly. Scan correlation means that video is in the same place 
from scan to scan, so the longer the integration period, the 
slower the permitted speed of the target. 
 
In addition to the full size integration window, SSI requires that 
the target is also seen in a smaller, recent window. There must 
be a minimum number of detections in both the long and short 
windows for SSI to emphasise the output video. 
 
The following parameters are used in the SSI process: 
 
FullWindowSize (Default 32) 
This is the size of the main integration window being considered. 
The value is between 1 and 64. Larger window sizes allow 
weaker, but necessarily slower targets to be integrated. 
 
FullHitCountThreshold (Default 0) 
If the number of observed detections across FullWindowSize is 
less than the threshold FullHitCountThreshold then the output 
is set to 0 (unless instant video is exceeded). A typical value for 
this parameter is ½ of the value of FullWindowSize. This means 
that the sample is, on average, larger than the mean noise level. 
 
RecentWindowSize (Default 0) 
This is the size of a smaller integration window. The video must 
be present in both the large window and the small window. The 
requirement for the small window ensures that the video is not 
detected through its history trail. A typical value for this 
parameter is 3-5 scans. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 201 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 201 
RecentHitCountThreshold (Default 0) 
This is the number of recent scans that are considered. There 
must be at least RecentHitCountThreshold hits in the recent 
window. A typical value of this parameter is  
RecentWindowSize – 1. 
 
IntegrationVideoThreshold (Default 0) 
This is the threshold for input video to be considered as 
significant for integration. If the video exceeds this threshold, 
then it is integrated. When used with the Dynamic Threshold 
process configured as described previously, set this threshold to 
0. 
 
InstantVideoThreshold (Default 255) 
Any input video that is greater than this level gives a 
corresponding output value of 0xff. This corresponds to the non-
integrated processing path. Note that the value of this 
parameter is the input level above the local average (when using 
a previous Dynamic Threshold process with the 
ThreshOutputValue set to -1). 
 
RecursiveAverageGain (Default 0.0) 
If this is non-zero, whenever the input video is above the 
IntegrationVideoThreshold level, then a recursive average for 
that polar sample is updated using the following rule: 
 
NewAverage = k * OldAverage + (1 – k) * NewSample 
 
where k is the value of the RecursiveAverageGain parameter.  
For example, a parameter of 0.9 would mean the average 
changes relatively slowly, whereas a parameter of 0.5 would 
mean the average changes relatively quickly.  In this mode, the 
output value of the process is the calculated average if the 
number of detections has exceeded the threshold, or zero if not. 
 
In the example of Figure 52, the original video is shown in the 
left image (along with an underlay map). The right image shows 
the same green input video with a superimposed video in yellow 
after processing with SSI. For this example, the following 
parameters are being used: 
 
FullWindowSize = 22 
RecentWindowSize = 6 
FullHitCountThreshold = 10 
RecentHitCountThreshold = 2 
IntegrationVideoThreshold = 8 
InstantVideoThreshold = 32 
 
This means that any input video exceeding 32 above the local 
average is set to 0xff. Otherwise, video that is at least level 8 for 
10 out of 22 scans AND 2 out of the last 6 scans to set to level 
X, where X is calculated as the number of detections in the last 
22 scans normalised to the range 0 to 255. 
 


===== PAGE 202 =====
Page 202 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
A portion of Figure 52 is shown in greater detail in Figure 53. 
Very weak targets are hardly visible in the original video and are 
not present on all scans. After scan integration, the correlated 
video is significantly enhanced and weak targets become visible. 
 
Figure 52 - Original video (left) appears in green and superimposed SSI  
video in yellow (right). 
 
Figure 53 – Close-up of original video (left) with superimposed SSI video in yellow (right) 
showing weak-intermittent targets enhanced 
 
One of the applications of the SSI is to clean the image by removing low-level 
background noise. If the input video (in green in the previous example) is 
removed from the display leaving only the SSI video (see Figure 54), the result 
is a much cleaner picture. Significantly, the clean-up is achieved without losing 
weak correlated targets, as would have happened with a simple thresholder. 
 


===== PAGE 203 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 203 
Instead of replacing the original video with SSI video, a display solution may 
choose to show both original and SSI-enhanced video, for example using a scan 
converter to display the original video and a separate scan converter to show 
the SSI video. These radar videos may be shown in different colours if desired 
(as in Figure 52 and Figure 53 for example), or the same colour may be used, 
meaning the SSI processing is providing a display enhancement for the original 
video. This is shown in Figure 55, where the top image is the original video and 
the bottom is the video after SSI enhancement (which for this example is 
implemented as a second channel of scan conversion to overlay the SSI video 
on the original video in the same colour). The effect is to greatly enhance the 
correlated video, including very weak intermittent targets that are hardly visible 
in the original video. The highlighted target in the bottom image if Figure 55 is 
hardly visible in the original video. 
 
Figure 54 - Radar picture showing SSI processed video only  
with original video removed. 
 
 


===== PAGE 204 =====
Page 204 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 55 - Showing effect of adding SSI video to original video (top) to get enhanced 
display (bottom). Two scan conversions in the same colour are used to implement this. 
Note the target highlighted in the processed video is not even visible in the original data. 
 
 


===== PAGE 205 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 205 
6.7.32. 
Adaptive Noise Removal 
 
Name 
SPxProAdaptiveNoiseRemoval 
Description 
Removes noise by attenuating video that is below a threshold 
level. Video above the threshold remains unchanged. The 
degree of attenuation for video below the threshold can be 
adjusted. The process helps to remove low frequency noise. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
SuppressionLevel: Real (0.0 .. 1.0) (Default 0.5) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Args 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
The process is designed to improve the appearance of the 
video by attenuating signals below a threshold. The degree of 
attenuation of the video below the threshold is controlled by 
the SuppressionLevel parameter. A value of 1 for this 
parameter removes most video below the threshold. A value of 
0 leaves the video unchanged, and therefore with this value 
the process has no effect on the video (although processing 
resources are still consumed, unless Active = 0). 
 
The video return is first analysed to calculate a smoothed 
signal using a moving window averager with a window set that 
is set to 1/8 of the maximum range. A fixed delta is then 
applied to that average to set a range-dependent threshold, Tr, 
for detection. 
 
A video sample that is above the threshold is passed through 
the process unchanged. A video sample that is less than the 
threshold is attenuated. The attenuation is a combination of 
SuppressionLevel and a taper that attenuates video close to 
the threshold less than video further from the threshold. The 
processing is as follows. 
 
Let Tr be the range-dependent threshold calculated as Ar + 16, 
where Ar is the average signal strength at sample r calculated 
over a window of width Rmax/8, where Rmax is the maximum 
range of the return. 
 
Let Ir be the input sample at range r and let Or be the 
corresponding output value for that sample.  
 
If Ir > Tr then Or = Ir, i.e. the output is just equal to the input. 
Otherwise, let Or = Ir * (1.0-K * e-q(Tr-Ir)) 
 
where K is the SuppressionLevel parameter and q is a built-in 
constant. 
 
An example is shown in Figure 56, which shows the effect of 
increasing the suppression from the original image (top left) to 
maximum suppression (bottom right). 


===== PAGE 206 =====
Page 206 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 56 - Example of Noise Suppression using SPxProAdaptiveNoiseRemoval process. 
Top left is original image. Top right has suppression level = 0.5. Bottom left has 
suppression 0.75 and bottom right has suppression 1.0. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 207 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 207 
6.7.33. 
Gain Offset Adjustment 
 
Name 
SPxProGainOffset 
Description 
Implement a digital adjustment of gain and offset either globally 
or in different areas. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
Gain : Real (Default = 1.0) 
Offset : Integer (Default = 0) 
LocalRegion : String 
LocalGain : Real 
LocalOffset : Real 
RegionNamen : String 
RegionValuen : String 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8 (See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
If active, the process implements a digital gain and offset 
adjustment of radar samples by multiplying by a gain and adding 
an offset. Both gain and offset  may be positive or negative. It 
should be noted that the adjustment is applied to radar samples 
that have already been digitised, hence this processing will not 
change the signal to noise ratio of the captured data. Its function 
is principally as a display enhancement, for example to add an 
offset to make the background visible, or to remove a constant 
background level, or to increase brightness of the radar. The 
process supports a mode where the gain and offset may be set 
to different values in different area, allowing regional adjustment 
of the video display or highlighting of video in selected areas. 
 
The input PIM A may be 8 or 16 bits per sample. The output is 
obtained by multiplying each sample by the Gain parameter and 
adding Offset. The result in then clipped to the range 0 to 255 
(for 8-bit data) or 0 to 65536 (for 16-bit radar samples). 
 
In the simple mode, the Gain and Offset are applied globally. 
However, the process may also be used to implement area-
dependent gain offset adjustments, which are applied in addition 
to and after the global gain and offset. There are two area-
dependent options.  
 
For a single area, the parameters LocalRegion, LocalGain and 
LocalOffset may be used. The LocalRegion parameter defines 
the name of an existing ActiveRegion object. The LocalGain 
and LocalOffset parameters then define the gain and offset 
inside that region (note that a Region is a collection of polygons 
and range/azimuth segments).  
 
If it is desired to have different gain/offset settings in different 
areas (up to 8 different areas), then the RegionNameN and 
RegionValueN parameters may be used. There can be up to 8 


===== PAGE 208 =====
Page 208 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
regions (N = 0..7), each associated with a different gain and 
offset value. The parameters RegionNameN , where N is 0-7, 
define the name of the existing SPxActiveRegion and 
RegionValueN defines the desired gain and offset of each region 
in the form of a string “gain=x offset=y”, where x is a floating 
point number and y is an integer.  
 
For example, with: 
 
RegionName0 = “myregion” 
RegionValue0 = “gain=0.5 offset = -10” 
 
the gain and offset in the SPxActiveRegion having name 
myregion will have values 0.5 and -10 (assuming the named 
active region is active).  
 
Where area-dependent values are used (either from 
LocalRegion or RegionNameN methods), these are applied in 
addition and after the global values. If there are overlapping 
areas, the gains and offsets are applied for each area in 
sequence. 
 
 
6.7.34. 
Video Enhancement 
 
Name 
SPxProEnhance 
Description 
Enhance the appearance of the video for display. 
Parameters 
Active : Integer (0 or 1) 
CapSamples : Integer (Default = 1) 
SmoothSamples : Integer (Default = 0) 
Input Data 
PIM A 
Input Formats 
8, <8 , 12 , 12+1+1+1+1, 16 
(See Section 6.4 for details) 
Output Data 
PIM B (may be the same as PIM A) 
User Arg 
None 
Details 
The process is only active if parameter Active has value 1. 
 
If active, the process enhances the appearance of the video 
depending on the CapSamples and SmoothSamples parameters. 
 
If CapSamples is non-zero, each sample is compared against the 
samples immediately preceding and following it in range.  If 
higher than both, it is reduced to the maximum of the two 
samples.  This has the effect of reducing very short duration 
noise (which is not typical of real target returns). 
 
If SmoothSamples is non-zero, that number of additional 
samples are inserted between each pair of real samples, with 
interpolated values. 
 
For example, if SmoothSamples is set to 1, then one additional 
sample is artificially inserted between each real sample, with a 
value equal to the average of the sample before and sample 


===== PAGE 209 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 209 
after in range. 
 
If set to 2, then two additional samples are inserted between 
each sample, and similarly for 3 or higher.  The values for the 
additional samples are linearly interpolated between the real 
values. 
  
The implementation of smoothing is performed in-situ and 
therefore the maximum number of real samples per spoke is 
reduced when the process is enabled.  The range resolution is 
not changed, so the maximum range is reduced proportionally in 
the output data.  For example, with 1 additional sample between 
each real sample, the number of real samples is halved, and 
therefore the maximum range is halved. For higher numbers 
(N), the range is reduced to “range / (N+1)”. 
 
 
6.8. Supporting Classes and Functions for SPx Processing 
 
This section describes supporting classes and functions that are used by the SPx 
Processing library. 
 
6.8.1. 
SPxAreaSelect for Geometry Specifications 
 
The SPxProAreaSelect process allows video to be processed in different ways 
according to its position. The process uses a SPxAreaSelect object to control this. 
 
The SPxAreaSelect object is constructed from a SPxActiveRegion (see Section 
6.8.6) object, which defines the area of interest, and a SPxLUT8 object (see Section 
6.8.3), which defines a look-up table that converts the video in the area. Video that is 
outside the area of concern is unaffected by the processing. 
 
The look-up table may be used to implement any one-to-one mapping of the affected 
video, including: 
 
 
Blanking video – the LUT is set to identically zero at each location 
 
Highlighting video – the LUT is set to apply a gain of > 1 
 
Dimming video – the LUT us set to apply a gain of < 1 
 
Thresholding video – the LUT sets input samples below a threshold to 0. 
 
See Section 6.8.9 for details of the SPxAreaSelect object. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 210 =====
Page 210 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.2. 
SPxLUT class 
 
The SPxLUT class encapsulates a general purpose LUT.  This class is used as a base 
class from which the classes SPxLUT8, SPxLUT12, SPxLUT16, etc., are derived.  The 
SPxLUT class should not be instantiated; instead use one of the LUT classes described 
in the following sections. 
 
The following configuration functions are supported by all LUT classes derived from 
this class. 
 
 
void SPxLUT::Set(SPxLUTtype type, int arg1=0, int arg2=0,  
                                  int arg3=0, int arg4=0) 
 
Set the LUT to one of a number of predefined LUT types. Refer to the 
documentation for the derived LUT class for details of supported predefined LUT 
types.  The arg1 and arg2 arguments may be used to configure additional 
settings associated with some predefined LUT types. 
 
 
void SPxLUT::Set(unsigned char *data) 
 
Set the contents of the LUT directly. The input data is a pointer to an array of 
values that are programmed into the LUT.  The size of the array should match 
the size of the LUT being programmed. 
 
 
unsigned char *SPxLUT::GetTable(void) 
 
Return the LUT table. The returned value is the address of the first LUT 
location. 
 
 
int SPxLUT::SetFromFile(char *fileName) 
 
This function sets the contents of the LUT from the specified input file. The file 
name may be an absolute path or may reference a file in the current directory. 
If no such file exists in the current directory, the directory $SPX/Config is 
searched ($SPX is the value of the environment variable SPX). 
 
The file contains lines of the form “Input = Output”, where Input is a integer in 
the range 0 to N-1 (where N is the number of LUT entries) and Output in an 
integer in the range 0 to 255. The line defines the LUT value for index Input. 
The file may also contains lines beginning with a ‘#’, which denote comments. 
Prior to reading entries from the file the LUT is initialised to a linear ramp by 
this function. 
 
The function returns an error code or SPX_NO_ERROR if there was no error. 
 
 
SPxLUTtype SPxLUT::GetType(void) 
 
Returns the predefined type that is currently configured.  If the LUT has been 
programmed from an array or a file then SPX_LUT_USER_DEFINED is returned. 
 


===== PAGE 211 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 211 
 
int SPxLUT::GetArg1(void) 
int SPxLUT::GetArg2(void) 
int SPxLUT::GetArg3(void) 
int SPxLUT::GetArg4(void) 
 
Returns the current value of arg1, arg2, arg3 and arg4 used to configure a 
predefined LUT. 
 
The following static look-up functions provide information on the predefined LUTs 
supported in the library. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxLUT::GetNumLuts(void) 
 
Returns the total number of predefined LUTs. 
 
 
const SPxLUTinfo_t *SPxLUT::GetInfoFromIndex(int index) 
 
Returns a pointer to an information structure for the predefined LUT referenced 
by index. The index should be set to a value between 0 and GetNumLuts() – 1. 
If the index is not valid, then NULL is returned. 
 
The returned structure is defined as follows: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
 
SPxLUTtype type;  /* Predefined LUT type. */ 
 
const char *name; /* Pointer to a string containing the LUT type. */ 
 
int sampleSizeBytes; 
/* Number of bytes per LUT entry. */ 
 
int tableSizeSamples; /* Number of entries in the LUT table. */ 
} 
 
 
int SPxLUT::GetIndexFromType(SPxLUTtype type) 
 
Use this function to convert a predefined LUT type to an index in the range 0 to 
GetNumLuts() – 1.  If the LUT type is invalid then -1 is returned. 
 
 
6.8.2.1 
SPxLUT Parameters 
 
int SPxLUT::SetParameter(char *parameterName, char *parameterValue) 
 
Sets a parameter to have a specified value. This function provides an ASCII-
based interface to change parameters of the object. 
 
 
int SPxLUT::GetParameter(char *parameterName, char *valueBuf, int bufLen) 
 
This function retrieves a named parameter’s value into the buffer, valueBuf, 
provided by the calling context. The length of the buffer being provided (in 
bytes) as bufLen.

6.8.3. 
SPxLUT8 class 
 
The SPxLUT8 class encapsulates an 8-bit LUT for mapping input data to output data. 
The LUT may be programmed with an input of 256 values or may be set to one of a 
number of predefined types. 
 
 
SPxLUT8::SPxLUT8(SPxLUTtype type=SPX_LUT_LINEAR,  
 
 
 
int arg1=0, int arg2=0, int arg3=0, int arg4= 0) 
 
Creates a new SPxLUT8 object. The optional type argument may be set to one 
of the following predefined types to set the initial contents of the LUT.  The LUT 
may also be set or changed using the SPxLUT::Set() function described in 
section 6.8.2. 
 
 
SPX_LUT_LINEAR 
The LUT is programmed with a linear ramp 
 
SPX_LUT_NULL 
The LUT is programmed to all 0 
 
SPX_LUT_THRESHOLD 
The LUT outputs 255 if the input video exceeds a 
threshold defined by the input arg, and is 0 
otherwise 
 
SPX_LUT_GAIN 
The LUT multiplies the incoming video by a gain 
defined by arg1/100. For example, a value of arg1 
of 200 implements a gain of 2.0 and a value of 50 
causes the signal to have a gain of 0.5. The video 
is clipped to the interval 0 to 255. 
 
SPX_LUT_OFFSET 
The LUT adds the value of arg1 to each input 
value, clipping the result to the interval 0 to 255. 
 
SPX_LUT_GAIN_OFFSET 
This is a combination of the GAIN and OFFSET 
tables above. The gain is done first using arg1 and 
then the offset defined by arg2 is added. The 
output is therefore arg1/100 + arg2. 
 


===== PAGE 213 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 213 
SPX_LUT_CONSTANT 
The LUT is programmed to a constant value 
defined by the input arg which is in the range 0 to 
255. 
 
SPX_LUT_CLIP 
Video at or less then the level defined by the input 
arg is set to 0. Video greater than arg is left 
unaffected. 
 
SPX_LUT_MAP7 
The LUT is programmed to convert 8-bit input data 
to 7 bit output data by setting the least significant 
bit to 0. 
 
SPX_LUT_MAP6 
The LUT is programmed to convert 8-bit input data 
to 6 bit output data by setting the least 2 
significant bits to 0. 
 
SPX_LUT_MAP5 
The LUT is programmed to convert 8-bit input data 
to 5 bit output data by setting the least 3 
significant bits to 0. 
 
SPX_LUT_MAP4 
The LUT is programmed to convert 8-bit input data 
to 4 bit output data by setting the least 4 
significant bits to 0. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT0 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 0 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 0. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT1 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 1 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 1. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT2 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 2 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 2. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT3 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 3 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 3. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT4 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 4 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 4. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT5 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 5 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 5. 
 
SPX_LUT_BIT6 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 6 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 6. 
 


===== PAGE 214 =====
Page 214 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_LUT_BIT7 
The LUT is programmed to output 0xff if bit 7 is set 
and 0 otherwise. This has the effect of displaying 
one bit video in bit 7. 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP7 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 7 bits 
(b1 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bit 0 is 
ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 7 bits analogue and 1 bit digital 
(7+1). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP6 
The LUT is programmed to convert t top 6 bits (b2 
to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0 and 1 
is ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 6 bits analogue and 2 bit digital 
(6+2). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP5 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 5 bits 
(b3 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0-2 is 
ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 5 bits analogue and 3 bit digital 
(5+3). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP4 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 4 bits 
(b4 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0-3 is 
ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 4 bits analogue and 4 bit digital 
(4+4). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP3 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 3 bits 
(b5 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0-4 is 
ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 3 bits analogue and 5 bit digital 
(3+5). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP2 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 2 bits 
(b6 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0-5 is 
ignored. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue video component when the 8 bits is 
organised as 2 bits analogue and 6 bit digital 
(2+6). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP1 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top bit (b7) 
to the range 0 to 0xff. Data in bits 0-6 is ignored. 
This LUT may be used to display the analogue 
video component when the 8 bits is organised as 1 
bit analogue and 7 bit digital (1+7). 
 


===== PAGE 215 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 215 
SPX_LUT_TOP7_BIT0 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 7 bits 
(b1 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff, and to 
automatically set the output to 0xff if bit 0 is set. 
This LUT may be used to display the analogue and 
digital video components when the 8 bits is 
organised as 7 bits analogue and 1 bit digital 
(7+1). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP6_BIT01 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 6 bits 
(b2 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff, and to 
automatically set the output to 0xff if either bit 0 
or bit 1 is set. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue and digital video components when the 8 
bits is organised as 6 bits analogue and 2 bit digital 
(7+2). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP5_BIT012 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 5 bits 
(b3 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff, and to 
automatically set the output to 0xff if either bit 0,1 
or 2 is set. This LUT may be used to display the 
analogue and digital video components when the 8 
bits is organised as 5 bits analogue and 3 bit digital 
(5+3). 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP4_BIT0123 
The LUT is programmed to convert to top 4 bits 
(b4 to b7) to the range 0 to 0xff, and to 
automatically set the output to 0xff if either bit 
0,1,2 or 3 is set. This LUT may be used to display 
the analogue and digital video components when 
the 8 bits is organised as 4 bits analogue and 4 bit 
digital (4+4). 
 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM7 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 7 
bits (b0 to b6) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bit 7 is ignored. 
 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM6 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 6 
bits (b0 to b5) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bits 6-7 is ignored. 
 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM5 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 5 
bits (b0 to b4) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bits 5-7 is ignored. 
 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM4 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 4 
bits (b0 to b3) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bits 4-7 is ignored 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM3 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 3 
bits (b0 to b2) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bits 3-7 is ignored. 
 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM2 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 2 
bits (b0 to b1) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in 
bits 2-7 is ignored. 
 


===== PAGE 216 =====
Page 216 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_LUT_BOTTOM1 
The LUT is programmed to convert the bottom 1 
bit (b0) to the range 0 to 0xff.  The data in bits 1-7 
is ignored. 
 
SPX_LUT_TOP4_BOTTOM4 
The LUT is programmed show the highest of either 
the bottom 4 bits (b0 to b3) or the top 4 bits (b4 
to b7), converted to the range 0 to 0xff.  This LUT 
may be used to convert two video streams, one 
encoded into the top 4 bits and the other encoded 
into the bottom 4 bits to an 8-bit value for display.  
The value of arg1 may be used to set the gain of 
the bottom 4 bits and value of arg2 used to set the 
gain of the top 4 bits.  The gain value is calculated 
as argN/100.  For example, a value of arg1 of 50 
causes the bottom 4 bits to have a gain of 0.5.  
The top and bottom 4 bits are both clipped to the 
interval 0 to 0xff.  If the gain control is not 
required, arg1 and arg2 must both be set to 100 
for unity gain. If not defined, arg1 and arg2 will 
default to 0, and zero-level video will be output 
from the LUT. 
 
6.8.4. 
SPxLUT12 class 
 
The SPxLUT12 class encapsulates a 12-bit LUT, which is used in the SPxProSSI scan-
to-scan integration process. The LUT may be programmed with an input of 4096 
values or may be set to one of a number of predefined types. 
 
 
SPxLUT12::SPxLUT12(SPxLUTtype type=SPX_LUT12_LINEAR,  
 
 
 
int arg1=0, int arg2=0, int arg3=0, int arg4=0) 
 
Creates a new SPxLUT12 object. The optional type argument may be set to one 
of the following predefined types to set the initial contents of the LUT.  The LUT 
may also be set or changed using the SPxLUT::Set() function described in 
section 6.8.2. 
 
 
SPX_LUT12_LINEAR 
The LUT is programmed with a linear ramp that 
maps a 12-bit input to an 8-bit output. 
 
SPX_LUT12_SSI_DETECT1 
Create a LUT for scan to scan integration. Interpret 
the 12 bits as 4 bits of detection and 8 bits of 
current value. Set the LUT to output 0 if the 
number of detections is less than the arg1, and 
otherwise output the current value. The value of 
arg1 is the M in the M-from-N detector. 
 


===== PAGE 217 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 217 
SPX_LUT12_SSI_DETECT2 
Create a LUT for scan to scan integration. Interpret 
the 12 bits as 4 bits of detections and 8 bits of 
current value. Set the LUT to output the current 
value if that value is greater than arg2, OR if the 
number of detections exceeds arg1. The value of 
arg1 is the M in the M-from-N detector. 
 
SPX_LUT12_SSI_DETECT3 
Create a LUT for scan to scan integration. Interpret 
the 12 bits as 4 bits of detections and 8 bits of 
current value. Set the LUT to output 255 if the 
number of detections exceeds arg1 and 0 
otherwise. The value of arg1 is the M in the M-
from-N detector. 
 
SPX_LUT_CONSTANT 
The LUT is programmed to a constant value 
defined by the input arg which is in the range 0 to 
255. 
 
SPX_LUT_NULL 
The LUT is programmed to all 0. 
 
 
6.8.5. 
SPxLUT16 class 
 
The SPxLUT16 class encapsulates a 16-bit LUT.  The LUT may be programmed with an 
input of 65536 values or may be set to one of a number of predefined types. 
 
SPxLUT16::SPxLUT16(SPxLUTtype type = SPX_LUT16_LINEAR,  
 
 
 
int arg1=0, int arg2=0, int arg3=0, int arg4=0) 
 
Creates a new SPxLUT16 object. The optional type argument may be set to one 
of the following predefined types to set the initial contents of the LUT.  The LUT 
may also be set or changed using the SPxLUT::Set() function described in 
section 6.8.2. 
 
 
SPX_LUT16_LINEAR 
The LUT is programmed with a linear ramp 
that maps a 16-bit input to an 8-bit output. 
 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8 
The LUT is programmed to convert the top 
8 bits (b8 to b15) to the range 0 to 0xff.  
The data in the bottom 8 bits (b0 to b7) is 
ignored.   
 
This LUT is identical to SPX_LUT16_LINEAR. 
 
SPX_LUT16_BOTTOM8 
The LUT is programmed to convert the 
bottom 8 bits (b0 to b7) to the range 0 to 
0xff.  The data in top 8 bits (b8 to b15) is 
ignored. 
 


===== PAGE 218 =====
Page 218 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8_BOTTOM8 
The LUT is programmed show the highest 
of either the bottom 8 bits (b0 to b7) or the 
top 8 bits (b8 to b15), converted to the 
range 0 to 0xff.  This LUT may be used to 
convert two video streams, one encoded 
into the top 8 bits and the other encoded 
into the bottom 8 bits to an 8-bit value for 
display.  The value of arg1 may be used to 
set the gain of the bottom 8 bits and value 
of arg2 used to set the gain of the top 8 
bits.  The gain value is calculated as 
argN/100.  For example, a value of arg1 of 
50 causes the bottom 8 bits to have a gain 
of 0.5.  The top and bottom 8 bits are both 
clipped to the interval 0 to 0xff.  If the gain 
control is not required, arg1 and arg2 must 
both be set to 100 for unity gain.  If not 
defined, arg1 and arg2 will default to 0, 
and zero-level video will be output from the 
LUT. 
 
SPX_LUT_CONSTANT 
The LUT is programmed to a constant value 
defined by the input arg1 which is in the 
range 0 to 255. 
 
SPX_LUT_NULL 
The LUT is programmed to all 0. 
 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8_MID7_BIT0 
Converts 16-bit input to 8-bit output. Input 
is assumed to be 8_7_1 format.  Top 
channel is multiplied by arg1/100. Mid 
channel is multiplied by arg2/100. If bit 0 is 
high, add arg3. 
 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8_MID6_BIT01 
Converts 16-bit input to 8-bit output. Input 
is assumed to be 8_6_11 format. Top 
channel is multiplied by arg1/100. Mid 
channel is multiplied by arg2/100. If bit 0 is 
high, add arg3. If bit 1 is high, add arg4. 
 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8_MID5_BIT012 
Converts 16-bit input to 8-bit output. Input 
is assumed to be 8_5_111 format. Top 
channel is multiplied by arg1/100. Mid 
channel is multiplied by arg2/100. If bit 0 
or bit 1 is high, add arg3. If bit 2 is high, 
add arg4. 
 
SPX_LUT16_TOP8_MID4_BIT0123 Converts 16-bit input to 8-bit output. Input 
is assumed to be 8_4_111 format. Top 
channel is multiplied by arg1/100. Mid 
channel is multiplied by arg2/100. If bit 0 
or bit 1 is high, add arg3. If bit 2 or bit 3 is 
high, add arg4. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 219 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 219 
6.8.6. 
SPxActiveRegion Class 
 
An SPxActiveRegion object supports any reasonable number of complex polygons or 
range-azimuth segments to define an area. Each shape is defined by a set of x,y 
points defined in metres from the origin of the object.  
 
The origin and azimuth offset of the SPxActiveRegion can be changed, allowing the 
polygons to be moved relative to the radar centre This allows polygons to represent 
static features observed from a moving platform. 
 
The class definition of SPxActiveRegion is as follows: 
 
6.8.6.1 
Construction and Deleting 
 
SPxActiveRegion::SPxActiveRegion(SPxPIM *sourcePIM, int mode,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
const char *name = NULL) 
SPxActiveRegion::SPxActiveRegion(int numAzis, int mode, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
const char *name = NULL) 
 
Create a new SPxActiveRegion object. In the first version of the constructor, 
the sourcePIM is used to set the azimuth resolution of the active region to the 
number of azimuths in the incoming data. In the second form, the number of 
azimuths is provided explicitly. In this version, the azimuth resolution of the 
active region can be selected. A value of 360, for example, means that the 
region will have a 1 degree resolution. The azimuth resolution need be no larger 
than the azimuth resolution of the PIM providing the data, although in some 
situation it may be desirable to work with a lower resolution region (reduced 
memory and speed of updates following changes). 
 
The mode defines the default processing of all samples in the area as either 
SPX_REGION_THRO (include area) or SPX_REGION_LUT (exclude area), meaning 
that a sample is passed through without change (THRO) or is passed through a 
LUT (LUT). If it is desired to leave most of the video unaffected, but just 
process a small area, then it is preferable to have an initial state of 
SPX_REGION_THRO and then use SPX_REGION_LUT for the area that is to be 
changed. Conversely, if most of the video is being processed, with just a small 
area left unchanged then use an initial state of SPX_REGION_LUT with 
SPX_REGION_THRO being used for the small area. 
 
An optional name (case insensitive) can be provided for the instance. This 
allows the instance to be looked-up using the GetByName() static class function. 
Note that these names are global with other SPx objects. 
 
 
SPxActiveRegion::~SPxActiveRegion() 
 
This is the destructor for an SPxActiveRegion object. All polygons and 
segments created for this active region are deleted. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 220 =====
Page 220 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.6.2 
Activation 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetActive(int state) 
int SPxActiveRegion::GetActive(int state) 
 
An SPxActiveRegion object is initially active. The SetActive function may be 
used to change the activation state. The behaviour of an object that is inactive 
depends on the use of the SPxActiveRegion object. When it is being used for 
area-based video suppression with SPxProAreaSelect, for example, then an 
inactive object has no effect in the video. The GetActive function returns the 
activity state as either 0 or 1. 
 
 
6.8.6.3 
Read and Write to File 
 
int SPxActiveRegion::ReadFromFile(char *filename) 
 
The definition of a set of polygons or range-azimuth segments comes from a 
file. The filename is provided as an absolute or relative pathname to the 
ReadFromFile function. If a relative filename is given, the file is searched for in 
the current directory and if not found there in the directory $(SPX)/Config, 
where $(SPX) denotes the value of the environment variable SPX. The file is an 
ASCII text file containing commands of the form described in the SPx Data 
Reference Manual (CP-25-110-32). 
 
 
int SPxActiveRegion::SaveToFile(char *filename) 
 
Saves the active region object to a file in the format defined for 
ReadFromFile(). 
 
6.8.6.4 
Naming 
 
char * SPxActiveRegion::GetName(void) 
 
Return the name of the SPxActiveRegion object. 
 
 
char * SPxActiveRegion::SetName(char *name) 
 
Set or change the name of the SPxActiveRegion object. Note that the names 
used for all active area objects (polygons and segments) is from a global name 
space. 
 
6.8.6.5 
Azimuth Offset 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetAzimuthOffset(double azimuthOffset) 
 
Set an azimuth offset in degrees. A value of 0.0 is North and positive values 
increase clockwise. The azimuth offset allows the existing polygons (not range-


===== PAGE 221 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 221 
azimuth segments) to be rotated. After changing the value, the existing 
polygons are converted when the Recalculate() function is next called. 
 
 
double SPxActiveRegion::GetAzimuthOffset(void) 
 
Return the current azimuth offset in degrees. The value returned is in the 
interval 0.0 to 360.0. 
 
6.8.6.6 
Scale Factor 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetScaleFactor(double scaleFactor) 
 
Set a scale factor, which multiplies all the polygon coordinates. The default 
value is 1.0.  The data points are scaled after the effects of any origin shift. 
 
 
double SPxActiveRegion::GetScaleFactor(void) 
 
Returns the current scale factor, as set from SetScaleFactor(). 
 
6.8.6.7 
Radar Location 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetOrigin(double x, double y) 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetRadar(double x, double y) 
(These two functions are equivalent.) 
 
This function sets the position of the radar. For a static radar installation, if the 
polygons are defined in coordinates that are relative to the radar, then leave 
the origin at the default (0,0). If the polygons are defined in a different 
reference system, then use SetOrigin() to position the radar in that 
coordinate system. For moving platforms, the polygon may represent static 
objects (for example a coastline) and SetOrigin() is used to position the radar 
relative to the origin of the polygons. After changing the value, the existing 
polygons are converted when the Recalculate() function is next called. If 
using a scale factor, the origin shift is made before the scaling.  
 
If polygons of the active region are defined in lat/longs, use the 
SetOriginLatLong function below instead of this one. 
 
 
double SPxActiveRegion::GetOriginX(void) 
double SPxActiveRegion::GetOriginY(void) 
 
Returns the currently set x or y component of the active region’s origin, as set 
with SetOrigin(). 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 222 =====
Page 222 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetOriginLatLong(double lat, double long) 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetRadarLatLong(double lat, double long) 
(These two functions are equivalent) 
 
This function sets the position of the radar as a latitude and longitude. It is 
necessary to call this function if any polygons of the active region are defined 
by lat/long. 
 
After changing the value, the existing polygons are converted when the 
Recalculate() function is next called. 
 
 
double GetRadarLat(void) 
double GetRadarLong(void) 
 
Return the latitude and longitude component of the active region’s origin. The 
active region’s origin may have been set explicitly with a previous call to 
SetOriginLatLong. However, for moving platform situations, the origin may be 
set automatically (by an SPxProAreaSelect process if the active region is being 
used as part of an area processing process, or by SPx Server if the active 
region is being used as an area for an area-dependent parameter). 
 
6.8.6.8 
Polygons and Segments 
 
SPxPolyArea * SPxActiveRegion::AddPoly 
                                    (int numPts, SPxPoint *pts, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 int mode,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 int active = 1, char *name = 0) 
 
SPxPolyArea * SPxActiveRegion::AddPolyLatLong 
                                    (int numPts, SPxPoint *pts, 
 
 
 
 
 
       int mode,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 int active = 1, char *name = 0) 
 
These functions add a new polygon into the class. The geometry of the polygon 
is defined by an array of SPxPoint objects of size numPts. If necessary, the 
polygon will be closed automatically. The SPxPoint data type is defined by: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
    double x; 
/* X coordinate in metres or latitude */ 
    double y; 
/* Y coordinate in metres or longitide */ 
} SPxPoint; 
 
The points are in units of metres for the AddPoly function, and are 
latitude/longitudes if AddPolyLatLong is used. For lat/long coordinates, note 
that the x component of the SPxPoint object holds the latitude and the y 
component holds the longitude.  
 
The geometry of the polygon relative to the radar centre is derived by 
subtracting the active region’s origin from the points of the polygon. For 
example, a coordinate (100,200) and an offset of (10,20) would correspond to 
a point that is (90, 180) from the radar centre. 
 


===== PAGE 223 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 223 
When the polygon is defined by lat/longs the position of the radar is defined by 
the SetOriginLatLong (or SetRadarLatLong) function. 
 
The mode is either SPX_REGION_THRO or SPX_REGION_LUT to define whether this 
polygon is defining an area to be passed through or processed. Set the mode to 
SPX_REGION_LUT if this polygon is part of an area definition of tracking 
parameters. If defining a video processing area, a mode of SPX_REGION_LUT is 
used if the polygon defines a cut-out (an exclusion area), and SPX_REGION_THRO 
is used if this polygon defines a region of interest. 
 
The active argument defines the activity state of the polygon. If a polygon is 
active (value = 1) it contributes to the area definition. If it is inactive (value = 
0) it does not. The activity state of a SPxPolyArea object returned from 
AddPoly/AddPolyLatLong can be changed with SetActive(int). Note that 
when the activity state is changed, Recalculate() must be called to let the 
change take effect. 
 
The name argument allows the polygon to have a name that may be used to 
reference it. 
 
The class allocates memory for the polygon internally, so the caller may reuse 
the same memory for a number of polygons. 
 
To remove a polygon, use RemovePoly(). Do not use delete on the 
SPxPolyArea object. 
 
 
SPxSegment * SPxActiveRegion::AddSegment 
                           (double rangeStart, double rangeEnd, 
 
 
 
 
      double azimuthStart, double azimuthEnd, 
 
 
 
 
      int mode, int active = 1, char *name = NULL) 
 
This function adds a new range-azimuth segment into the class. A range-
azimuth segment is a region that is bounded by a lower and upper range and 
azimuth. The input rangeStart and rangeEnd are in metres. The azimuthStart 
and azimuthEnd are in degrees. The mode is either SPX_REGION_THRO or 
SPX_REGION_LUT to define whether this segment is defining an area to be 
passed through or processed by the LUT. 
 
The active argument defines the activity state of the segment. If a segment is 
active (value = 1) it contributes to the area definition. If it is inactive (value = 
0) it does not. The activity state of a SPxSegment object returned from 
AddSegment can be changed with SetActive(int). Note that when the activity 
state is changed, Recalculate() must be called to allow it to take effect. 
 
The name argument allows the polygon to have a name that may be used to 
reference it. 
 
To remove a segment, use RemoveSegment(). Do not use delete on the 
SPxSegment object. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 224 =====
Page 224 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxActiveRegion::RemovePoly(SPxPolyArea* polyToRemove) 
 
Removes a polygon from the SPxActiveRegion object. Ensure that this function 
is used to delete a polygon from an active area. Do not use the delete operator 
directly on the SPxPolyArea object. 
 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::RemoveSegment(SPxSegment *segmentToRemove) 
 
Removes a segment from the SPxPolyArea object. 
 
 
int SPxActiveRegion::GetNumPolys(void) 
 
Returns the number of polygons associated with this ActiveRegion. 
 
 
int SPxActiveRegion::GetNumSegments(void) 
 
Returns the number of segments associated with this ActiveRegion. 
 
 
SPxPolyArea *SPxActiveRegion::GetFirstPoly(void) 
 
Returns the SPxPolyArea object that is the first polygon associated with the 
ActiveRegion. To get the subsequent polygon objects, call GetNext() on the 
SPxPolyArea. When GetNext() returns NULL the item is the last one in the list. 
 
 
SPxSegment *SPxActiveRegion::GetFirstSegment(void) 
 
Returns the SPxSegment object that is the first segment associated with the 
ActiveRegion. To get the subsequent segment objects, call GetNext() on the 
SPxSegment. When GetNext() returns NULL the item is the last one in the list. 
 
 
SPxPolyArea *SPxActiveRegion::GetPolyByName(const char *name) 
 
Returns the SPxPolyArea object having the specified name. The name is the 
(optional) string provided to the AddPoly() function or supplied in the definition 
file. If no such named polygon can be found, the function returns NULL. 
 
 
SPxSegment *SPxActiveRegion::GetSegmentByName(const char *name) 
 
Returns the SPxSegment object having the specified name. The name is the 
(optional) string provided to the AddSegment() function, or supplied in the 
definition file. If no such named segment can be found, the function returns 
NULL. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 225 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 225 
6.8.6.9 
Updating the Active Region 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::Recalculate(void) 
 
This forces all previously defined polygons and range-azimuth segments to be 
reprocessed after a change in the offset, origin, geometry or activity status of 
one of the shapes. The recalculation time can be non-trivial for complex 
geometries with lots of polygons. This function automatically calls the Lock() 
and Unlock() functions around the recalculation call (from 1.23 of the 
software). 
6.8.6.10 Access to Interval List 
 
SPxPolarInterval *SPxActiveRegion::GetInterval(int azimuth) 
 
This function allows access to the interval list maintained by the active region. 
The input azimuth must be in the range 0 to NumAzis, where NumAzis is the 
number of azimuths in the PIM that was used in the original object constructor. 
The returned SPxPolarInterval object is the first interval for the specified 
azimuth and may be accessed using GetRange(), GetInterval() to get the 
range and interval width in metres, and GetNext() to get the next interval for 
the azimuth. When GetNext() returns 0 on a SPxPolarInterval, there are no 
more intervals for that azimuth. When accessing the interval state of the object 
with GetInterval(), the application should use Lock and Unlock functions to 
protect the object from changes in another thread.  
 
6.8.6.11 Lock and UnLock 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::Lock(void) 
void SPxActiveRegion::Unlock(void) 
 
The Lock and Unlock functions provide a locking mechanism that protects the 
active region object from being read by one thread whilst it is being changed in 
another. When using GetInterval to read the state of the active region, the 
object should be locked to prevent another thread from changing the state of 
the object. Call Lock(), then access the object with GetInterval() as needed, 
then call Unlock() to release the lock. 
 
6.8.6.12 Reset 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::Reset(void) 
 
This function deletes all previously defined polygons and range-azimuth 
segments and resets the area definition to the initial configuration, which is the 
configuration after the SPxActiveRegion class was first constructed. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 226 =====
Page 226 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.6.13 Access Functions 
 
static SPxActiveRegion* SPxActiveRegion::GetFirstObject(void) 
 
This function, which is static and can therefore be invoked without a class 
instance, returns the first SPxActiveRegion object that is allocated. From this 
object, use GetNext() to get the next object in the link. When GetNext() 
returns NULL this is the end of the list. 
 
 
SPxActiveRegion* SPxActiveRegion::GetNext(void) 
 
This function returns the next object in the list of allocated objects. The function 
returns NULL if called on the last item in the list of allocated objects. 
 
 
static SPxActiveRegion* SPxActiveRegion::GetLastObject(void) 
 
This function, which is static and can therefore be invoked without a class 
instance, returns the last SPxActiveRegion object that is allocated. 
 
 
static SPxActiveRegion * SPxActiveRegion::GetByName(const char*name) 
 
Return the SPxActiveRegion instance having the specified name (which is case 
insensitive). The name was provided when the SPxActiveRegion object was 
constructed. Return NULL if no such named instance exists. 
 
 
void SPxActiveRegion::SetUserData(void *userData) 
 
Allows a user-specified pointer to be associated with the object. The data is 
unused by the class and it may be accessed with GetUserData(). 
 
 
void * SPxActiveRegion::GetUserData(void) 
 
Returns the user data for the class instance.

6.8.6.15 File Format for SPxActiveRegion 
 
The ReadFromFile function for SPxActiveRegion reads a complete definition of an 
active region from an ASCII file. 
Refer to the SPx Data Reference Manual (CP-25-110-32) for details of the file format. 
 
6.8.6.16 Origin for SPxActiveRegion 
 
The value provided for the origin is the position of the radar when calculating the 
effects of the active area. In the example shown below, the polygon has coordinates 
(10,10), (12,10), (12,8), (10,8). The origin is set to be (10,3) which is the radar 
position in the same coordinate system as the polygon. So when the effects of the 
active area are calculated, it is the relative position of the coordinates with respect to 
the origin that are important. For this example, the active area appears North and 
slightly East of the radar position. 
 
 
Radar position 
origin = (10, 3) 
Active area polygon 
 (10, 10) 
 (10, 8) 
 (12, 8) 
 (12, 10) 


===== PAGE 229 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 229 
In a simple, single radar application it is sensible to set the origin to (0,0) and set the 
coordinates of the polygon areas to be relative to the radar. So the above situation is 
equivalent to the situation below. 
 
Example Area Definitions 
 
Example 1 
 
# Sample definition of area for video suppression in a polygon 
name ‘my active region’ 
default thro 
offset 0.0 
origin 0.0 0.0 
scale 1.0 
# Define a polygon 
poly ‘blank1’ lut 
5000 5000 
8000 5000 
8000 0 
5000 0 
endpoly 
segment ‘blank2’ 0 100000 190.0 200.0 lut 
# End of file. 
 
Example 2 
 
# Area Test 
scale 1.0 
# Pass video thro, except where we define not 
default thro 
# Cut out video in a square north east of the radar  
poly 'area-square' lut 
1000 1000 
2000 1000 
2000 2000 
1000 2000 
1000 1000 
endpoly 
# Cut out a triangle north west of the radar 
poly 'area-triangle' lut 
-1000, 1000 
-4000, 4000 
Radar position 
origin = (0, 0) 
Active area polygon 
 (0, 7) 
 (0, 5) 
 (2, 5) 
 (2, 7) 


===== PAGE 230 =====
Page 230 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
-4000, 2000, 
-1000, 1000 
endpoly 
#Cut out a sector of video from 180 to 200 degrees 
segment 'blank-1' 0 10000.0 180.0 200.0 lut 
# End of file. 
 
The video observed when using the definition shown in Example 2 is shown in Figure 
57. 
 
Figure 57 - Example video display for area blanking 
6.8.7. 
SPxPolyArea class 
 
The SPxPolyArea class is returned from the AddPoly member of SPxActiveRegion. 
The class provides a handle for the polygon added and support the following access 
functions: 
 
int SPxPolyArea::GetNumPoints(void) 
 
Returns the number of points in the polygon. 
 
 
SPxPoint * SPxPolyArea::GetPoints(void) 
 
Returns the array of points comprising the polygon. The geometry of points can 
be modified using the x and y fields of the point. If the SPxPolyArea is 
currently being used in an active area, it is necessary to call Recalculate() on 
the active area to see the effect of the change. The points returned from this 
call will be either lat/longs or metres depending on how the polygon was 
created. If UsingLatLong() returns 1, the coordinates are lat/long, with the x 
component of the point being the latitude and the y component being the 
longitude. 
 
 
void SPxPolyArea::SetName(const char *name) 
 
Sets a new name for the object. Note that the names used for all active area 
objects (polygons and segments) is from a global name space. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 231 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 231 
SPxErrorCode SPxPolyArea::InsertPointAt(int position, SPxPoint point) 
 
Insert a new point into the polygon. The position defines where to insert the 
point, with 0 indicating at the beginning and 1 indicating after the first point 
etc. If the original polygon has N points, the permissible values for position are 
0..N-1. An error code is returned or SPX_NO_ERROR if there was no error. If the 
SPxPolyArea is currently being used in an active area, it is necessary to call 
Recalculate() on the active area to see the effect of the point insertion. The 
point should be a lat/long coordinate (with x as the latitude and y as the 
longitude) if the polygon was originally created as lat/long, and a coordinate in 
metres otherwise. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPolyArea::DeletePointAt(int position) 
 
Delete a point from the polygon. The position defines where to delete the point 
from, with 0 indicating at the beginning and 1 indicating after the first point etc. 
If the original polygon has N points, the permissible values for position are 
0..N-1. An error code is returned or SPX_NO_ERROR if there was no error. If the 
SPxPolyArea is currently being used in an active area, it is necessary to call 
Recalculate() on the active area to see the effect of the point deletion. 
 
 
SPxPolyArea *SPxPolyArea::GetNext(void) 
 
Returns the next SPxPolyArea object in the chain. This function returns NULL at 
the end of the chain. 
 
 
char * SPxPolyArea::GetName(void) 
 
Returns the name of the SPxPolyArea object. The name may have been set in 
the AddPoly call of the SPxActiveRegion, or else may appear in the file read 
from ReadFromFile() of the SPxActiveRegion. The name may be returned as 
NULL if no name was set. 
 
 
void SPxPolyArea::SetActive(int active) 
 
Make the polygon active (active = 1) or inactive (active = 0). Note that when 
the activity state of one or more polygons has changed, the function 
Recalculate() must be called on the SPxActionRegion object before the state 
change is effective. If the SPxPolyArea is currently being used in an active 
area, it is necessary to call Recalculate() on the active area to see the effect 
of the change. 
 
 
int SPxPolyArea::GetActive(void) const 
 
Return the activity state of the SPxPolyArea object. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 232 =====
Page 232 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxPolyArea::GetMode(void) 
 
Return the mode of the polygon object. This is either SPX_REGION_THRO or 
SPX_REGION_LUT. 
 
 
void SPxPolyArea::SetMode(int mode) 
 
Set the mode of the polygon object. This is either SPX_REGION_THRO (value=0) 
or SPX_REGION_LUT (value=1). Use SPX_REGION_LUT when using this polygon as 
part of an active area to define tracking parameters. For specification of video 
processing areas, use SPX_REGION_THRO to pass the video through and 
SPX_REGION_LUT to pass the video through the LUT (e.g. to remove it).   
 
Note that when the mode of one or more polygons has changed, the function 
Recalculate() must be called on the SPxActionRegion object before the state 
change is effective. 
 
 
void SPxPolyArea::SetUserData(void *userData) 
 
Allows a user-specified pointer to be associated with the object. The data is 
unused by the class and it may be accessed with GetUserData(). 
 
 
void * SPxPolyArea::GetUserData(void) 
 
Returns the user data for the class instance. 
 
 
int SPxPolyArea::UsingLatLong(void) 
 
Returns 1 if this polygon is defined with lat/long coordinates and 0 if the 
polygon is defined using coordinates in metres. 
 
6.8.7.1 
Parameters for SPxPolyArea 
 
The following parameters are available for the SPxPolyArea object. These parameters 
permit remote control of a named SPxPolyArea object through the 
SetRemoteParameter() class (see “SPx Server Client Interfacing User Manual”, CP-
25-110-31).

6.8.8. 
SPxSegment class 
 
The SPxSegment class is returned from the AddSegment member of SPxActiveRegion. 
The class provides a handle for the segment added and supports the following access 
functions: 
 
double SPxSegment::GetStartRange(void) 
double SPxSegment::GetEndRange(void) 
double SPxSegment::GetStartAzimuth(void) 
double SPxSegment::GetEndAzimuth(void) 
 
This set of functions retrieves the start range, end range start azimuth and end 
azimuth associated with the range/azimuth segment. The range values are 
measured in metres and the azimuths are measured in degrees. 
 
 
SPxSegment *SPxSegment::GetNext(void) 
 
Returns the next SPxSegment object in the chain. This function returns NULL at 
the end of the chain. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 234 =====
Page 234 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
char * SPxSegment::GetName(void) 
 
Returns the name of the SPxSegment object. The name may have been set in 
the AddSegment call of the SPxActiveRegion, or else may appear in the file 
read from ReadFromFile() of the SPxActiveRegion. The name may be 
returned as NULL if no name was set. 
 
 
void SPxSegment::SetActive(int active) 
 
Make the segment active (active = 1) or inactive (active = 0). Note that when 
the activity state of one or more polygons has changed, the function 
Recalculate() must be called on the SPxActionRegion object before the state 
change is effective. 
 
 
int SPxSegment::GetActive(void) const 
 
Return the activity state of the SPxSegment object. 
 
 
int SPxSegment::GetMode(void) 
 
Return the mode of the polygon object. This is either SPX_REGION_THRO or 
SPX_REGION_LUT. 
 
 
void SPxSegment::SetMode(int mode) 
 
Set the mode of the polygon object. This is either SPX_REGION_THRO or 
SPX_REGION_LUT. Note that when the mode of one or more polygons has 
changed, the function Recalculate() must be called on the SPxActionRegion 
object before the state change is effective. 
 
 
void SPxSegment::SetUserData(void *userData) 
 
Allows a user-specified pointer to be associated with the object. The data is 
unused by the class and it may be accessed with GetUserData(). 
 
 
void * SPxSegment::GetUserData(void) 
 
Returns the user data for the class instance.
