6.8.9. 
SPxAreaSelect class 
 
The activeRegion and the processing LUT are combined in the SPxAreaSelect object 
with an optional NavData object for moving platform applications. 
 
 
SPxAreaSelect::SPxAreaSelect(SPxActiveRegion *activeRegion,  
                                SPxLUT8 *lut=0, SPxNavData *navData) 
 
The object is constructed by providing an SPxActiveRegion object, an SPxLUT8 
object and, optionally, a SPxNavData object. The lut may be omitted if the 
desired effect is to eliminate video in the specified area. In this case, the effect 
is the same as providing a lut of type SPX_LUT_NULL. The combination of the 
active region and the LUT define an area and a video mapping table.  
 
If a navData object is provided, the lat/long origin of activeRegion can be 
periodically set to the radar location provided by navData. This is the 
responsibility of the user of the SPxAreaSelect class (for example, this is done 
by SPxProAreaSelect and SPxServer). 
 
 
SPxActiveRegion * SPxAreaSelect::GetActiveRegion(void) 
 
Returns the active region associated with the object. 
 
 
SPxLUT8 * SPxAreaSelect::GetLUT(void) 
 
Returns the LUT associated with the object. 
 
The same activeRegion and lut could appear in multiple SPxAreaSelect 
objects. This would allow the same geometry specification to be used in 
different ways within different processors. For example, an area might be used 
to suppress video for tracking and attenuate video for display. 
 
 
SPxNavData *SPxAreaSelect::GetNavData(void) 
 
Returns the SPxNavData instance associated with the active region or NULL if 
no nav data object is associated. 
 


===== PAGE 236 =====
Page 236 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.9.1 
SPxAreaSelect Example - Simple Shapes 
 
Consider a situation where it is desired to eliminate the display of video in a selected 
area of the display, as shown in the example of Figure 58. There are three areas to be 
eliminated - the polygons P1 and P2 and the range-azimuth segment S1. The polygon 
P2 has an area inside where video should be visible. 
 
The first step is to create the SPxActiveRegion object and to add to it the four 
shapes. As the object is being used to eliminate video in the selected area, leaving 
most of the video unaffected, the initial mode is SPX_REGION_THRO: 
 
SPxActiveRegion *activeRegion = new SPxActiveRegion(pim, 
SPX_REGION_THRO); 
 
For this example, the PIM is assumed to have been previously created. 
 
Now the polygons and range-azimuth segments can be added: 
 
 
SPxPoint polyP1[] = {{10000,10000}, {17070, 17070},  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
{24140,10000}, {17070, 2930}}; 
 
activeRegion->AddPoly(4, polyP1, SPX_REGION_LUT); 
 
SPxPoint polyP2[] = {{5000,-5000}, {15000, -5000},  
 
 
 
 
 
 
{15000,-10000}, {5000, -10000}}; 
 
activeRegion->AddPoly(4, polyP2, SPX_REGION_LUT); 
 
SPxPoint polyP2insert[] = {{7000,-6000}, {13000, -6000},  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
{13000,-9000}, {7000, -9000}}; 
 
activeRegion->AddPoly(4, polyP2insert, SPX_REGION_THRO); 
 
activeRegion->AddSegment(0.0, 60000.0,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
210.0, 220.0, SPX_REGION_LUT); 
 
Note that polyP2insert is added with mode SPX_REGION_THRO to disable the LUT 
processing of video in the insert. 
 
Figure 58 - Sample shapes for SPxAreaSelect example with resultant scan conversion 
(radar source is scenario generator) 
 
Now construct a LUT to process the data in the affected area: 
 
SPxLUT8 *suppressLUT = new SPxLUT8(SPX_LUT_NULL); 
 
P
1 
P
2 
S
1 


===== PAGE 237 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 237 
Now construct the SPxAreaSelect object: 
 
SPxAreaSelect areaSelect = new SPxAreaSelect(activeRegion, suppressLUT); 
 
Finally, the SPxAreaSelect object can be used in an SPxProAreaSelect Process, for 
example: 
 
SPxRunProcess *areaProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProAreaSelect,  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   NULL, pim, areaSelect); 
 
6.8.9.2 
SPxAreaSelect Example – Airport Map 
 
It is possible to define a complex map, for example the outline of an airport, which 
can be used to enhance the display or eliminate video from areas that are not of 
interest. In the example below, the code creates an activeRegion and then reads a 
polygon definition from a file called “airport.txt”. 
 
SPxActiveRegion *activeRegion = new SPxActiveRegion(pim, 
SPX_REGION_THRO); 
activeRegion->ReadFromFile(“airport.txt”); 
 
The file “airport.txt” is too large to reproduce here in full, so the data points are 
abbreviated below. 
 
# File “airport.txt - Airport Outline 
 
poly 'airportoutline' thro 
3400 4400 
… 
endpoly 
 
poly 'airportlake' lut 
5400 -6200 
… 
endpoly 
 
poly 'airportcentre' lut 
3600  900 
… 
endpoly 
 
 
Now create a LUT to suppress the display of video: 
 
SPxLUT8 *suppressLUT = new SPxLUT8(SPX_LUT_NULL); 
 
and construct the SPxAreaSelect object to combine the region and the LUT: 
 
SPxAreaSelect areaSelect = new SPxAreaSelect(activeRegion, suppressLUT); 
 
Finally, the SPxAreaSelect object can be used in a SPxProAreaSelect Process, for 
example: 
 
SPxRunProcess *areaProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProAreaSelect,   
 
 
 
 
 
 
NULL, pim, areaSelect); 


===== PAGE 238 =====
Page 238 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 59 - Scan Conversion after removal (left) and reduction (right) of video outside 
main area of interest 
 
This process will suppress the video outside the defined map area. If the process is 
attached to a scan conversion process, the resultant display is as shown in Figure 59. 
Now instead of suppressing the display of video outside the main area of interest, it 
might be desirable to reduce its intensity. This is easily achieved by modifying the LUT 
to be of type GAIN and setting the gain to 50%: 
 
suppressLUT->Set(SPX_LUT_GAIN, 50); 
 
The result is to show a low-level of video outside the main area of interest – see 
Figure 59. 
 
6.8.10. 
SPxSyncCombineSrc 
 
The SPxSyncCombineSrc object is used in the SPxProSyncCombine process (see 
Section 6.7.24) to combine the input channels that are providing data at the same 
rate, but with a time difference. 
 
 
SPxSyncCombineSrc::SPxSyncCombineSrc(SPxRIB *buffer) 
 
The object is constructed by providing an SPxRIB, which is used to receive the 
combined data channels. The two input channels are defined as the input to the 
RunProcess objects that reference this object in their User Arg. 
 
 
SPxSyncCombineSrc::~SPxSyncCombineSrc(void) 
 
Delete a SPxSyncCombineSrc object. 
 
6.8.11. 
Blob Detector 
 
The Blob Detector is used in SPxProSpanDetector to identify target blobs (sometimes 
called plots) from the input video. 
 
These plots are characterised by the following: 
 


===== PAGE 239 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 239 
 
Range centroid – this is the weighted range centroid of the plot in metres. 
 
 
Azimuth centroid – this is the weighted azimuth centroid of the plot in 
metres. 
 
 
Minimum azimuth of the plot’s bounding box 
 
 
Maximum azimuth of the plot’s bounding box 
 
 
Minimum range of the plot’s bounding box 
 
 
Maximum range of the plot’s bounding box 
 
 
Weight – the total number of samples that contribute video to the plot 
 
 
Strength – the total amplitude of samples that contribute to the plot. The 
significance of this parameter depends on the configuration of the 
thresholding process that proceeds the plot extraction. The strength is 
obtained by summing the numerical values of samples that are used by the 
plot extractor. The most sensible use of this field in when the plot extractor 
is preceded with a dynamic threshold process (SPxProDynamicThreshold) 
configured with parameter ThreshOutputValue set to 0 (meaning the video 
seen by the plot extractor has strength that is proportional to the input 
video’s strength)  or a value of a -1 (meaning that the video seen by the plot 
extractor has a strength value that is relative to the local signal average). 
 
 
Time stamp – the time the plot was extracted. 
 
The Blob Detector is created as an instance of class SPxBlobDetector, defined as 
follows: 
 
SPxBlobDetector::SPxBlobDetector (SPxTargetDB blobDatabase) 
 
Constructs a new blob detector object that may subsequently be passed to an 
SPxProSpanDetect process. The detector uses a blob database to store its data. 
The blob database must have been created by the caller before creating the 
blob database. Refer to Section 6.8.12 for details of the database. 
 
 
SPxBlobDetector::~SPxBlobDetector() 
 
Deletes a blob detection object. 
 
 
SPxBlobDetector::SetWorldMap(SPxWorldMap *worldMap) 
 
By associating a world map object with the blob detector, it becomes possible 
to reject blobs based on their proximity to the coastline. For example, blobs can 
be rejected over land, or within a threshold distance of the coastline. See 
Section 6.8.17 for details of the SPxWorldMap object. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 240 =====
Page 240 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.12. 
Target DataBase 
 
The class SPxTargetDB provides a database storage facility for plots and targets. The 
database organises entries by sectors, where the number of sectors per 360 degrees 
is set when the database is created. 
 
 
SPxTargetDB::SPxTargetDB(int numSectors, int sizeItem, int numItems) 
 
This function creates a new target database. The items in the database are 
organised in numSectors sectors. A typical value for numSectors is 32. 
 
The database holds items of size sizeItem bytes. When creating the database 
to hold blobs, use sizeof(SPxBlob_t) as the value for this argument, for 
example. The numItems argument is the maximum number of items that the 
database can hold. 
 
SPxTargetDB::~SPxTargetDB() 
 
Deletes a target database object. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::ClearSector(int sector) 
 
This function clears all entries out of the specified sector. The sector number is 
in the range 0 to numSectors-1, where numSectors was the argument provided 
to the SPxTargetDB constructor. An error code is returned. 
 
This function will cause any notifications on event type 
SPX_TDB_EVENT_CLEAR_SECTOR to be invoked. See AddNotify() for the function that 
adds a notification function. 
 
 
SPxTargetRecordLink * SPxTargetDB::AddTarget(void *target,  
                                                 int sector) 
 
This function adds a new record into the database. The record is identified by 
target, which should be of size sizeItem bytes, which was the argument 
provided to the class constructor. The specified sector, which should be in the 
range 0.. numSectors-1, defines the sector number where the target should 
be stored. The sector number should be calculated from the azimuth of the 
target. The function returns a link object that may be used to reference the 
target in future calls. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::GetTarget(int sector, char *target) 
 
This function gets (returns and deletes) the next target from the database 
sector. The specified sector is in the range 0.. numSectors-1. If there is a 
target in the sector, the target’s data is copied to the memory pointed to by the 
input target, the target is removed from the database and the function returns 
1. The next call of the function returns the next target in the sector. This 
continues until there are no more targets in the database, when there is no 
copy to target and the function returns 0. The value of target must be a pointer 


===== PAGE 241 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 241 
to memory whose size is equal to the size of the object in the database. For 
example, if the database is holding blobs, then the caller should pass the 
address of an SPxBlob_t object. 
 
 
SPxTargetRecordLink * SPxTargetDB::GetTargetRef(int sector,  
                                                    void **targetPtr) 
 
This function is used to check out a pointer to a database object, leaving the 
object in the database. Call the function with targetPtr set to NULL to initialise 
the iteration for a sector. Then call the function with the address of a target 
pointer. The function will assign the pointer to be the next target in the 
database. Repeated calls to the function will iterate through the targets in the 
sector until the function returns NULL, which indicates that no target pointer 
was copied because there were no more targets in the database. 
 
A target reference checked out with this function may be returned with 
ReturnTargetRef(). It is only necessary to return the reference if the sector 
has changed. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::ReturnTargetRef(SPxTargetRecordLink *targetLink,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
int newSector) 
 
This function returns a target reference back to the database into the specified 
sector. The targetLink pointer is the value returned from GetTargetRef when 
the reference was checked out. 
 
 
void SPxTargetDB::RemoveTarget(SPxTargetRecordLink *targetRef) 
 
Removes an entry from the database. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::GetTargetCount(int sector) 
 
This function returns the number of targets in the specified sector of the 
database. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::GetNumSectors(void) 
 
This function returns the number of sectors in the database. 
 
 
int SPxTargetDB::GetTotalTargetCount(void) 
 
This function returns the total number of targets maintained by the database. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 242 =====
Page 242 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxTargetDBnotify * SPxTargetDB::AddNotify(UINT32 event,  
 
 
 
 void (*fn)(SPxTargetDB *db, UINT32 event, 
 
 
 
 void *eventData1, int eventData2, void *userData), 
 
 
 
 void *userData = 0); 
 
This function registers a user-defined function to be invoked on a specific event 
type. The user-defined function, fn, is called with the instance of SPxTargetDB, 
the event type that caused the notification, two event-specific parameters 
eventData1 and eventData2 and a user-supplied pointer userData. If no user-
supplied data is required by the notification handler, the userData value may 
be omitted from the call to AddNotify(), and then fn gets called with a value 
of NULL for the userData value. 
 
The currently supported event types are as follows: 
 
SPX_TDB_EVENT_CLEAR_SECTOR – A sector clear has just occurred. The 
user-defined function is called with eventData1 = NULL and eventData2 
equal to the sector that has just been cleared. The sector number is in the 
range 0 to N-1, where N is the number of sectors used in the construction 
of the target database. When accessing the target database to retrieve 
plot data, this notification may be used to indicate that a new sector has 
been cleared ready for new plots and therefore the previous sector (sector 
-1) has been completed (note that the previous sector will be N-1 if sector 
is 0). 
 
The function returns a SPxTargetDBnotify object, which may be used in a 
RemoveNotify function to remove the notification. 
 
 
void SPxTargetDB::RemoveNotify(SPxTargetDBnotify *notify) 
 
This function removes a notification handler added with AddNotify(). 
 
6.8.13. 
Handling Navigation Data with SPxNavData 
 
The class SPxNavData provides capabilities to receive, interpret and manage 
navigation data relating to platform motion. A number of SPx classes and processes 
use the SPxNavData class to read navigation information. 
 
An instance of SPxNavData may be used to receive navigation data from the following 
sources: 
 
 
NMEA serial input 
 
NMEA format sentences over a network  
 
The SPxScenarioGenerator can also use the SPxNavData class to maintain state 
information relating to platform motion. The scenario generator can simulate the 
motion of the platform and uses the SPxNavData object to share this information with 
other SPx objects and processes that need it. 
 
The class may be used to resend navigation data over a network interface. This 
means that a single instance of the class may be receiving NMEA navigation data over 
a serial interface, making that data available to local applications, and simultaneously 


===== PAGE 243 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 243 
resending the data over a network interface to other systems. Those other systems 
would typically be using another instance of SPxNavData to receive the network 
navigation data. 
 
6.8.13.1 NMEA-0183 Sentences 
 
When used with a serial channel, the class expects to receive a sequence of 
characters that confirm to NMEA RMC sentences. An example of an RMC sentence is 
as follows: 
 
$GPRMC,143519,A,5214.088,N,0009.214,E,015.2,135.0,230309,001.3,W*XX 
 
Where: 
GPRMC 
 
Recommended minimum GPS sentence 
143519 
 
Fix taken at 14:35:19 UTC 
A 
 
 
Active fix (or V if Void) 
5214.088,N  
Latitude 52 degrees, 14.088 minutes North 
0009.214,E  
Longitude, 0 degrees, 9.214 minutes East 
015.2  
 
Speed in knots, 
135.0  
 
Course in degrees true, 
230309 
 
Fix taken on 23rd March 2009 
001.3,W 
 
Magnetic variation 
XX 
 
 
Checksum (XOR of all chars between but excluding $ and *) 
 
The checksum is not mandatory for the SPx server and the field may be omitted if it is 
not available. If the checksum field is present, it is assumed to be correct. 
 
The heading information is received via one of three NMEA sentences, “HDG”, “HDT” 
or “HDM”. 
6.8.13.2 NMEA Network Packets 
 
In the network input mode, the UDP messages should be formatted as standard SPx 
network packets, with each message containing a header indicating that the message 
is an NMEA packet and a payload containing a single null-terminated NMEA sentence. 
 
The header is defined by the SPxPacketHeaderB structure in SPxPackets.h and is as 
follows: 
 
typedef struct SPxPacketHeaderB_tag { 
    UINT16 magic;  
/* SPX_PACKET_MAGIC_B */ 
    UINT16 packetType; /* One of SPX_PACKET_TYPEB_... */ 
    UINT32 totalSizeBytes; 
/* Total size of header and payload */ 
    UINT32 timeSecs; 
/* Whole seconds since unix epoch */ 
    UINT32 timeUsecs; 
/* Microseconds to be added to timeSecs */ 
} SPxPacketHeaderB 
 
For an NMEA packet, the header fields should have the following values: 
 
magic  
 
= 0x4342 
(i.e. SPX_PACKET_MAGIC_B) 
packetType  = 0x0131 
(i.e. SPX_PACKET_TYPEB_NMEA) 
totalSizeBytes = length of NMEA sentence, including null termination 
timeSecs/Usecs 
= time of NMEA sentence (since Jan 1 1970) 
 


===== PAGE 244 =====
Page 244 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The payload should immediately follow the header and contain the ASCII characters 
for the NMEA sentence, followed by a zero to null-terminate it. 
 
Note: All multi-byte fields in the header should be stored using network byte-
ordering, i.e. big-endian, most significant byte first. 
 
6.8.14. 
SPxNavData Class Interface 
 
6.8.14.1 Construction and Deletion of Class 
 
SPxNavData::SPxNavData() 
 
Constructs a new SPxNavData object. 
 
 
SPxNavData::~SPxNavData() 
 
Deletes an SPxNavData object. 
 
6.8.14.2 Associating an input source 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetSourceSerial(char *name=NULL,  
unsigned int baud=4800) 
 
Configures the SPxNavData object to listen for serial input on a specified named 
serial device. The given name should be the name of a valid serial device, for 
example “COM1” under Windows or /dev/ttyS0 under Linux (these are the 
default values if NULL is specified as a name). An optional baud rate may be 
specified and this must match the sending equipment. The standard 
configuration is 8 data bits, no parity and 1 stop bit. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetSourceNetwork(char *addr=NULL,  
                                          int port=0, 
                                          const char *ifAddr=NULL) 
 
Configures the object to listen for network messages comprising NMEA data 
streams. If the network address and port are omitted, the default values of port 
5079 at multicast address 239.192.50.79 are assumed. If the data is being 
received on a multicast address, the interface to use may be specified in the 
ifAddr parameter. If omitted, data from any interface will be used. 
 
6.8.14.3 Destination Network 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetDestNetwork(char *addr=NULL, int port=0,  
    const char* ifAddr=NULL) 
 
This function allows the class to resend navigation information out to a different 
network address and port. The format of the data sent across the network is 
consistent with the format expected when the class is used with 


===== PAGE 245 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 245 
SetSourceNetwork. This function may be used to configure an instance of the 
class to receive navigation information from one source, for example serial, and 
resend as network messages to other applications. If the data is being sent 
using a multicast address, the interface to send the data with can be specified 
using the ifAddr parameter. If omitted, data will be sent using the default 
multicast interface. 
 
 
void SPxNavData::SetDestNetworkEnabled(int isEnabled) 
 
Enable (isEnabled = 1) or disable(isEnabled = 0) the network output. 
 
 
void SPxNavData::SetOutputOnly(int isOutputOnly) 
 
If the navigation data object should be receiving data only from a replay 
source, this function can be used to disable serial or network input. 
 
6.8.14.4 Decoding NMEA Strings 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetNMEA(const char *nmeaString,  
                                          int live=TRUE,  
                                          int decode=TRUE) 
 
This function process a NMEA input string, which is passed in the nmeaString 
argument to the function. When the class is used to receive NMEA input from a 
serial or network source then this function is used internally to process each 
received sentence. It may be called by an application which creates a NMEA 
sentence and wishes to set the internal state of the nav data object. For 
example, the SPxScenarioGenerator object can update the state of an 
SPxNavData object using the SetNMEA interface. 
 
6.8.14.5 Retrieval of NMEA State Information 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetVelocity(double *speedMpsPtr,  
                                     double *courseDegsPtr, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 SPxTime_t *timestampPtr=NULL) 
 
This function retrieves the current speed and heading of the platform as 
reported by the last NMEA message. The speed is provided in metres per 
second and the source is provided in degrees. An optional SPxTime_t pointer 
may be provided to receive the time stamp of the last recorded update. The 
function returns error code SPX_ERR_NAV_DATA_EXPIRED if there has not been a 
NMEA update in the last 5 seconds, although the most recent values are still 
returned. Refer to Section 11.11 for details of the Time functions. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 246 =====
Page 246 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetVelocityOverWater(double *speedMpsPtr,  
                                              double *courseDegsPtr,  
                                              SPxTime_t 
*timestampPtr=NULL)  
                                              const 
 
This function retrieves the current speed and heading of the platform relative to 
the water as reported by the last NMEA message. The speed is provided in 
metres per second and the course is provided in degrees. An optional 
SPxTime_t pointer may be provided to receive the time stamp of the last 
recorded update. The function returns error code SPX_ERR_NAV_DATA_EXPIRED if 
there has not been a NMEA update in the last 5 seconds, although the most 
recent values are still returned. Refer to Section 11.11 for details of the Time 
functions. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetLatLong(double *latDegsPtr,  
                                    double *longDegsPtr, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxTime_t *timestampPtr=NULL) 
 
This function retrieves the current latitude and longitude of the platform as 
reported by the last NMEA message. An optional SPxTime_t pointer may be 
provided to receive the time stamp of the last recorded update. The function 
returns error code SPX_ERR_NAV_DATA_EXPIRED if there has not been a NMEA 
update in the last 5 seconds, although the most recent values are still returned. 
Refer to Section 11.11 for details of the Time functions. See also 
GetRadarLatLong() below, which retrieves the lat/long of the radar rather than 
the lat/long of a reference point on the platform. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetMotion(double secs,  
                                   double *xMetresPtr, 
                                   double *yMetresPtr) 
 
This function retrieves the motion of the platform in x and y over a time 
interval. If secs if provided as a negative number, the returned x and y position 
change has occurred over the past secs seconds. If secs is provided as a 
positive number, the returned x and y values are the predicted change in 
position over the next secs seconds. The function returns error code 
SPX_ERR_NAV_DATA_EXPIRED if there has not been a NMEA update in the last 5 
seconds, although the most recent values are still returned. 
 
double SPxNavData::GetSecsSinceLastNMEA(void) 
 
This function returns the time interval in seconds since the last received NMEA 
messsage. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 247 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 247 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetHeading(double *headingTrueDegsPtr,  
                         int *courseNotHeadingFlagPtr=NULL,  
                         SPxTime_t *timestampPtr=NULL) 
 
This returns the requested heading value in the location given by the first 
argument, together with an error code to indicate whether the value is valid or 
not (e.g. expired). Optionally, the time of the value can be returned via the last 
argument. 
 
Additionally though, this function will provide the course instead of the heading 
if the heading is not available. This is to make it easier for applications to get a 
sensible value where they need a heading, even if only course is known.  If the 
"courseNotHeadingFlagPtr" is not NULL, the flag will be set to TRUE on return 
if the function is providing a course rather than a heading. 
  
 
static SPxNavData * SPxNavData::GetNavDataObject(void)  
 
In many applications, only a single SPxNavData object is created. This static 
class function returns the first created object. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetAltitude(double *altitudeMetresPtr, 
                                     double *geoidSepMetresPtr=NULL, 
                                     SPxTime_t *timestampPtr=NULL) 
 
This function retrieves the current altitude of the platform as reported by the 
last GGA or GNS NMEA message. The altitude is provided in metres above 
mean sea level. The geoid separation may also be retrieved. The geoid 
separation is provided in metres as the difference between the WGS-84 earth 
ellipsoid and mean sea level. An optional SPxTime_t pointer may be provided to 
receive the time stamp of the last recorded update. The function returns error 
code SPX_ERR_NAV_DATA_EXPIRED if there has not been an NMEA update to the 
altitude in the last 5 seconds, although the most recent values are still 
returned. 
 
6.8.14.6 Radar Offset from Platform Reference 
 
Although the platform has a reference point (accessed by the GetLatLong() member 
function), the radar (or radars) may be located at different points on the platform. 
The functions below allow an offset to be specified between the platform reference 
point and the radar location. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetRadarOffsetMetres(double xMetres, 
                                          double yMetres, 
                                          unsigned int radarIndex=0) 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetRadarOffsetMetres(double *xMetres, 
                                          double *yMetres, 
                                          unsigned int radarIndex=0); 
 
These functions sets/gets the offset of a radar from the own-ship position on a 
platform.  The offset is defined in metres relative to the own-ship position.  


===== PAGE 248 =====
Page 248 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Positive X is towards starboard, positive Y is towards the bow.  The radar index 
is a value between 0 and 3, used to support multiple radars on the same 
platform.  If not specified, it defaults to zero.   
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetRadarOffsetMetresNorth(double *xMetres,  
                                             double *yMetres, 
                                             unsigned int radarIndex) 
 
This function retrieves the true north referenced offset from the own-ship 
position, which is calculated using the current platform heading (or course if 
heading is not available).  Positive X is east, positive Y is north.  The radar 
index is a value between 0 and 3, used to support multiple radars on the same 
platform.  If not specified, it defaults to zero. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::GetRadarLatLong(double *latDegsPtr,  
                                       double *longDegsPtr, 
                                       SPxTime_t *timestampPtr=NULL, 
                                       unsigned int radarIndex=0) 
 
This function should be used in place of GetLatLong() if the radar lat/long is 
required, as opposed to the platform own-ship lat/long.  The radar index is a 
value between 0 and 3, used to support multiple radars on the same platform.  
If not specified it defaults to zero. 
 
6.8.14.7 Convenience Functions for Building NMEA Sentences 
 
static SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::BuildRMC 
                             (char *sentence, size_t sizeBytes, 
 
 
 
 
 
   double latDegs, double longDegs, 
 
 
 
 
 
   double speedMps, double courseDegs, 
 
 
 
 
 
   double magVarDegs = 0.0, 
 
 
 
 
 
   const SPxTime_t *timestamp = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
   int isVoid = FALSE) 
 
This function builds on RMC sentence. The sentence is formatted in the location 
sentence, which is of size sizeBytes bytes. The sentence is built from the 
provided latitude (positive north), longitude (positive east), speed 
(metres/sec), course (degrees clockwise from true north), magnetic variance 
(degrees clockwise from true north to magnetic north) and optional time stamp 
(current time will be used if this is provided as NULL). The sentence is marked 
as void if isVoid is TRUE, and is marked as active otherwise. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 249 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 249 
static SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::BuildHDx 
                                   (char *sentence, size_t sizeBytes, 
   double headingDegrees, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   int headingIsMagnetic) 
 
This function builds an HDM or HDT sentence. The sentence is formatted in the 
location sentence, which is of size sizeBytes bytes. The sentence is built from 
headingDegrees and a flag that says whether the heading is magnetic 
(headingIsMagnetic is non-zero), or true (headingIsMagnetic = 0). If the 
heading is magnetic, an HDM sentence is generated; if the heading is true, an 
HDT sentence is generated. 
 
6.8.15. 
Debugging  
 
When developing applications with the SPxNavData class, it can be useful to log the 
messages received by the class. Enabling debug and then observing the contents of 
the log file can help to identify errors such as incorrect network addresses (nothing 
gets received, so nothing is in the log file), or incorrectly formatted messages 
(message and errors are in the log file). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxNavData::SetLogFile(const char *filename) 
 
This function sets the file that will be used to log the debug output. If a file is 
currently selected, the function will close that file and open a new one. The 
input filename is assumed to be a fully qualified filename or relative to the 
current directory. The filename argument is passed to fopen() to open the file. 
 
 
void SPxNavData::SetDebug(UINT32 flags) 
 
This function enables debug. The value of flags is a combination of the 
components shown in the table below. To enable full debugging, use flags = 
0xffffffff. To turn off all debug, use flags = 0. The debug output goes to a 
file, which is specified through the SetLogFile() function. 
 
Name 
Value 
Description 
SPX_NAVDATA_DEBUG_RECV 
0x1 
Output a message echoing any message 
received, even if it is not recognised. 
SPX_NAVDATA_DEBUG_MSGS 
0x2 
Output a message for whole messages 
SPX_NAVDATA_DEBUG_DECODES 
0x4 
Output a message for decoded messages 
SPX_NAVDATA_DEBUG_UPDATES 
0x8 
Output a message for navigation data 
updates 
SPX_NAVDATA_DEBUG_VERBOSE 
0x80000000 Enable verbose mode – more output. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 250 =====
Page 250 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.16. 
Testing Nav Data Clients 
 
When developing an application that expects to receive Navigation Data as NMEA 
input, a useful test program, SPxNavGen, is provided in the SPx Development release. 
This is a Windows application that allows a simple moving platform situation to be 
simulated. The ship’s latitude, longitude, speed, heading and course can be entered 
and the program generates NMEA messages, which it outputs on a specified IP port 
and address. An application using the SPxNavData object to receive navigation data 
can receive the messages from this test source. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 251 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 251 
6.8.17. 
World Map 
 
The SPxWorldMap object supports a high-resolution (40m) vector shoreline map of the 
world. This map is based on the NOAA high-resolution shoreline database.  
 
This has a number of applications in SPx software: 
 
1. It can be used as part of a process that eliminates (or more generally affects) 
video in land areas. The world map implicitly defines the polygons that an 
SPxActiveRegion object will use to control the areas that video is displayed. 
This can be used to automatically remove video from a display over land areas 
(static radar applications only). 
 
2. It can be used by plot extraction and tracking applications that need to query 
the distance of a point from land, for example with the objective of eliminating 
plots over land areas (moving platforms and static radars). 
 
3. It can be used to provide a simple map display for a client or server 
applications, for example as shown in Figure 60. The core SPxWorldMap class 
members are described in the following section, but the functions concerned 
with the rendering of the map are described in the document “SPx Library 
Extensions” – CP-25-110-85. 
 
4. Although the map database has a resolution of 40m, it has been observed that 
the accuracy of the data in certain areas of the world is not better than a few 
hundred metres. The map can still provide useful capabilities for display and 
processing, but care must be taken. Certainly, the map should not be used for 
navigation. 
 
Figure 60 - The World Map may be displayed as an underlay map to the radar. There is 
worldwide coverage with a resolution of approximately 40m  
(inset window shows zoom view with detail) 
 


===== PAGE 252 =====
Page 252 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
6.8.17.1 SPxWorldMap Class 
 
SPxWorldMap::SPxWorldMap(void) 
 
This constructor loads and initialises the world map. The construction process 
will read the map data files from a well-known location ($SPX/Config/Maps) in 
the SPx release tree.   
 
 
SPxWorldMap::~SPxWorldMap() 
 
The destructor for the world map will remove the previously created 
SPxWorldMap instance. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::SetNavDataLink(SPxNavData *navData) 
 
This function can be used to link the world map object to an SPxNavData object. 
When the SPxNavData object is receiving navigation information from a moving 
platform, the lat/long of the radar (as would be set by SetRadarLatLong for a 
static platform) is automatically set from the received navigation data. The 
SPxWorldMap class automatically caches an area around the moving radar 
location with radius set from SetRadarRadius(). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::SetRadarRadius(double radiusMetres) 
 
This function sets the radius of the area from the radar position that will be 
considered. The default value is 100km. The function LoadActionRegion() 
must be called after a new radius has been set.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::SetRadarMagneticVariation(double degs) 
 
This function sets the magnetic variation at the specified radar position. The 
value provided is in degrees. Irrespective of whether the magnetic variation to 
set from this function, it is only used if SetUseMagVar() (see below) is called 
with a non-zero value. The magnetic variation should be set to match that of 
the radar’s north reference (true north or magnetic north). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::SetUseMagVar(int use) 
 
This function defines whether magnetic variation is to be used in calculations. If 
the input value is non zero, magnetic variation is used. The default setting is 
that it is used. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::LoadActiveRegion(SPxActiveRegion *region) 
 
The LoadActiveRegion loads the World Map into an Active Region for the 
current radar location. This allows the active region to be used as part of a 
blanking process to remove video over land. The location of the radar in the 
world must be set previously using SetRadarLatLong. Note that this function 


===== PAGE 253 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 253 
implements the conversion of the world map polygons to the active region 
object for the current radar location. It is an expensive function, taking 1-2 
seconds for a mid-range processor. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxWorldMap::SetRadarLatLong 
                                    (double latDegs, double longDegs) 
 
This function sets the static location of the radar as a lat/long. A change in the 
lat/long of the radar is only effective after LoadActiveRegion() is called. 
 
 
int SPxWorldMap::IsOnLand(const struct  
                          SPxWorldMapLatLong_tag *latLong, 
 
 
 
 
    double *distanceMetresPtr=NULL) 
 
This function tests to see if a location is on land and if so returns the distance to 
the coast. The input location is provided in the latLong structure, which is 
defined as: 
 
typedef struct SPxWorldMapLatLong_tag { 
    double latDegs; 
    double longDegs; 
} SPxWorldMapLatLong_t; 
 
The distanceMetresPtr pointer is used to return the distance in metres of the 
specified location from the coast, assuming the point is on the land. The pointer 
can be provided as NULL if it is desired to know only if the test point is on land 
or in the sea (as provided by the return value from the function). 
 
The function returns TRUE if the test point is on land and FALSE otherwise. 
 
 
double SPxWorldMap::GetDistanceToCoast 
                                (const SPxWorldMapLatLong_t *latLong) 
 
This function returns the distance from a specified location to the coast. The 
input location may be on land or sea. The returned distance is always positive, 
so it is not possible to tell whether the input test point is over land or sea (use 
IsOnLand() for that purpose). 
 
6.8.17.2 SPxWorldMap Example for Video Blanking 
 
One use of the SPxWorldMap object is to blank video over land areas for static radar 
applications. This can be used to simplify a display by eliminating land video to show 
only on video over non-land. Additionally, it could be used in a server to eliminate 
video over land prior to distribution of the video. The eliminated land video could 
significantly reduce the bandwidth required to distribute the video (a variant of this is 
that the processing could reduce the resolution of the video over land rather than 
eliminating it all together. In this way, there would still be a worthwhile reduction in 
the bandwidth for distribution). 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 254 =====
Page 254 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
To use the SPxWorldMap object for video blanking, the following steps are necessary: 
 
1. Create the SPxWorldMap object. 
2. Create the SPxActiveRegion object. 
3. Set the lat/long of the radar in the world map object (note that this must occur 
before the step below). 
4. Load the world map into the active region. 
5. Create a LUT to process the video in the land (for example a SPX_LUT_NULL will 
simply blank the video, but a different LUT could attenuate or reduce the 
resolution). 
6. Create the SPxAreaSelect object which combines the SPxActiveRegion and 
the SPxLUT8 objects. 
7. Create the SPxProAreaSelect process which implements the blanking. 
 
For example: 
 
/* Create the WorldMap object */ 
SPxWorldMap *worldMap = new SPxWorldMap(); 
 
/* Create the active region. */ 
SPxActiveRegion *worldMapActiveRegion = new SPxActiveRegion(spxPim,  
SPX_REGION_THRO); 
 
/* Set the lat/long of the radar in the world. */ 
worldMap->SetRadarLatLong(51.533059, 0.805897); 
 
/* Load the world map into the active region */ 
worldMap->LoadActiveRegion(worldMapActiveRegion); 
 
/* Create the LUT that gets applied to video samples that are over land. 
In this example we are using a NULL lut so that the video is blanked. */ 
SPxLUT8 *blankingLut = new SPxLUT8(SPX_LUT_NULL); 
 
/* Create the SPxAreaSelect object which connects the ActiveRegion and the 
LUT. */ 
SPxAreaSelect *blankingAreaSelect = new SPxAreaSelect 
                                          (worldMapActiveRegion, 
                                           blankingLut); 
 
/* Create the SPxRunProcess. In this case the process is the first in the 
chain so the ‘before’ process is NULL. */ 
 
SPxRunProcess *spxWorldBlankingProcess = new SPxRunProcess( 
                                             SPxProAreaSelect, 
                                             NULL, spxPim, 
                                             blankingAreaSelect); 
 
The effect of the above processing is shown by the screen shots in Figure 61. The left 
display shows scan conversion of the full radar coverage. In the middle display, the 
land is blanked using the code fragment shown previously. In the right display, the 
land blanking is used to remove the land video and replace with a constant (this 
means that the land areas are still visible) using SPxLUT8(SPX_LUT_CONSTANT, 30) in 
the blankingLut in the previous code. 


===== PAGE 255 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 255 
 
Figure 61 - Use of the World Map to control video blanking 
 
6.9. Example of Using SPx Processes 
6.9.1. 
Simple Processing Chain 
 
A processing chain starts at a PIM and completes with a process that has no outputs. 
In this example, we process data from a PIM in the following way: 
 
1. Eliminate a sector of video from 170 to 190 degrees using the SectorBlank 
process. 
 
2. Apply a Remap process to eliminate video below 10% of peak intensity. 
 
3. Scan convert the video to a window. 
 
The following code fragment defines three processes that implement this processing. 
The first process, pro1, takes data from the PIM and implements the sector blanking. 
The second process, pro2, takes the output from pro1 and implements the remapping 
of levels. The final process, pro3, is the scan conversion that takes the output from 
pro2 and scan converts to a window. The sequence of processes is shown in Figure 
62. 
 
/* Assume … 
 
pim exists as an instance of SPxPIM  
 
sc exists as an instance of SPxScSourceLocal 
*/ 
 
SPxRunProcess * pro1 = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProSectorBlank, 0, pim); 
pro1->SetParamValueI(“StartAzimuth”, 170.0) 
pro1->SetParamValueI(“EndAzimuth”, 190.0); 
 
SPxRunProcess * pro2 = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProRemap, pro1, pim, pim); 
Pro2->SetParamValueI(“Level1”, 10 * 255 / 100); 
Pro2->SetParamValueI(“Level2”, 255); 
Pro2->SetParamValueI(“Bits”, 8); 
 
SPxRunProcess * pro3 = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv, pro2, pim, 
spxSc); 
 


===== PAGE 256 =====
Page 256 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 62 - Process Sequence for the Example

7. 
Radar Scan Conversion 
 
The SPx library supports radar scan conversion in PPI and B-Scan display formats. 
There is considerable flexibility in the architecture of a scan conversion solution, 
allowing the scan converter to run as part of the application process, as a different 
process or as a remote server. 
 
The SPx scan conversion process occurs in two stages. 
Firstly, the scan converter processes new video and 
creates a bitmap image of the correct size, type (PPI 
or B-Scan) and view. The second stage of the process, 
which is the subject of Section 8, is the display of this 
scan conversion data within the graphics environment 
of the host computer. In the case where the 
application just wants a bitmap of scan converted 
radar then the second stage is not even required. 
 
One feature of this two-stage architecture is that the 
scan converter and the display processing may occur 
on different machines with a network connection. SPx 
can simply provide the scan converted video to a 
custom application that does the graphics display, or 
SPx offers a number of display modules that render 
the radar video on a Windows or X11 graphics display. 
 
In the general case, there are three software 
components and two interfaces. The diagrams in 
Figure 63, Figure 64 and Figure 65 show various 
configurations for application, scan conversion and 
display for X Window and Microsoft Window 
applications. The configurations show which SPx 
classes are involved, and the configuration of software 
and servers. 
 
One supported SPx architecture is based on the Radar 
Display Coprocess (RDC). This is fully described in a 
separate manual (“Radar Display Coprocess User manual, Document CP-25-110-50 
for Linux or CP-25-110-60 for Windows). The RDC implements the scan conversion is 
a separate process – see Figure 66. The application program communicates to the 
scan converter over a simple socket-based interface. The output of the RDC process is 
“inserted” into one or more Windows of the application program. The RDC is a 
preferred solution for many radar display applications, due to the decoupling of the 
application from the scan conversion. In this configuration, SPx libraries are linked 
with the application only to provide the lightweight communication interface to the 
RDC. The complexities of the radar receipt, scan conversion and display integration 
are all handled by a separate Linux/Windows process.  
 


===== PAGE 258 =====
Page 258 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 63 - Integrated Solution. The application, scan conversion and display all run on 
the same machine as part of a single Linux/Windows process 
 
 
Figure 64 - RDC solution. The application and scan converter  
run in different processes.

7.1. Scan Conversion Display Types 
 
The SPx library supports the presentation of radar video in PPI, B-Scan and A-Scan 
formats. 
 
The PPI (Plan Position Indicator) view displays the radar as if looking at a plan view of 
its coverage. It shows a rotating sweep that corresponds to the movement of the 
antenna.  
 
The B-Scan mode is typically used in fire-control situations. The display shows 
azimuth across the window and range up and down. As the radar rotates, the display 
updates across the display from left to right. 
 
The A-Scan mode shows a trace of signal amplitude against time (equivalent to 
range). This type of display is similar to that seen on an oscilloscope, with video 
shown against time. 
 
Figure 67 shows views of a test picture (test picture 33 from the SPx Test Generator) 
in A-Scan, PPI and B-Scan views. 
 
 
 
 
RDC 
 
Client Application 
 
Graphics Subsystem 
SPx 
Command 
Protocol 
X11 
Protocol 
X11  
Protocol 
Display Host  
Client Host 
S/C 
Radar Video 
Radar  
Inputs

Process 


===== PAGE 261 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 261 
 
Figure 67 - Display of Test Picture 33 in A-Scan (top), PPI (left) and  
B-Scan (right) Display Modes 
 
In SPx, the presentation of PPI and B-Scan is handled by the SPxScSourceLocal (local 
scan converter), SPxScRemote (remote scan converter) or SPxRemoteServer (RDC 
architecture) classes. The A-Scan display is handled by the class SPxAScanWindow (see 
Section 9) under Windows and Linux. 
 
7.2. Overview of SPxScSourceLocal Scan Conversion class 
 
The radar scan converter class SPxScSourceLocal is responsible for scan converting 
the radar video into a bitmap in either PPI or B-Scan format. This bitmap may be 
accessed by a user application directly, or else an SPx display module may be used. 
All suitable display processes are derived from the base class SPxScDest.  
 
A number of sub-classes of SPxScDest are identified as follows: 
 
 
SPxScDestBitmap provides a generic, graphics independent method of receiving scan 
converted video into an application. The application software can use this class to 
receive video prior to display processing with Windows, X11, OpenGL or any other 
graphics library. Alternatively, the client may process the video in other ways, such as 
compressing or saving to file. 
 


===== PAGE 262 =====
Page 262 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxScDestDisplayWin is for Windows (MFC applications) and provides a display 
mechanism that allows the scan converted radar video to be displayed along with 
graphics for a user application. 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw is for Windows and provides a display mechanism that 
allows the scan converted radar video to be displayed along with graphics for a native 
Win32 (non-MFC) user application. 
 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer is for Windows (MFC or non-MFC applications) and provides 
a display class that combines scan converted radar video with an application’s 
graphics from a bitmap. The class provides considerable flexibility for interpreting the 
bitmap data as underlay or overlays relative to the radar, allowing the application to 
use alpha or a single bit of colour as a control switch. 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 provides a display capability for Window Manager Compositing 
with X Windows. The class allows radar video to be displayed along with underlay and 
overlay graphics using a compositing window manager to combine the data layers. In 
most situations for X Windows, the preferred interface is through SPxScDestBitmap 
class (see above) used with SPxPPIwindow class. 
 
SPxScDestNet is a module that transfers the scan converted video across a network to 
a remote display. The other side of the connection uses a SPxScSourceNet to receive 
the network video. 
 
SPxScDestTexture is an extension of the SPxScDestBitmap class and makes the scan 
converted radar video available in an OpenGL texture that can be used in both 
Windows (MFC or non-MFC) and X11 applications.   
 
SPxScDestBitmapD2D is an extension of the SPxScDestBitmap class and makes the 
scan converted radar video in a Direct2D bitmap that can be used in Windows 
applications (MFC or non-MFC). 
 
The display modules receive their data from a class derived from SPxScSource, such 
as SPxScSourceLocal and SPxScSourceNet. SPxScSourceLocal is the scan converter 
running on the same machine as the display and SPxScSourceNet is the class that 
provides an interface to a remote scan converter. 
 
7.3. Scan Conversion Operation 
 
There are two phases to the scan conversion process. The first phase is the 
conversion from polar coordinates into the appropriate display format (PPI or B-Scan). 
The second is the display of that image on the graphics display. SPx provides the 
class, SPxScSourceLocal, that handles the generation of a bitmap from the polar-
format input. The display of those bitmaps can then be handled either by the 
application program itself, or else with another SPx display rendering class. 
 
In the situation where the scan converter runs on a different machine the application 
software, the application uses the class SPxScRemote as the API to control the remote 
scan converter. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 263 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 263 
An instance of class SPxScSourceLocal is supplied with data from the PIM at regular 
intervals. The output of the scan conversion is the PPI-format scan converted data. 
 
The size of the scan conversion window and the view represented in the window are 
controlled by API calls into the class. The window can be no larger than the bitmap 
that is used to hold the scan converted image. There are efficiency reasons for 
wanting to keep this bitmap as small as possible. The default size is 1024 x 1024, 
although larger bitmaps are permitted. 
 
Calls to API functions Resize() and SetView() are used to affect the window size and 
the view in the window. Note that there is no concept of a window position at this 
point. The scan converter creates a bitmap which is subsequently positioned on the 
screen by the display class. 
 
The scan converter processes data as it arrives from the input PIM through the 
process SPxProScanConv (see Section 6.7.11). The scan converter outputs process 
data at nominal intervals of 20ms. At each update the output data comprises a set of 
“patches” of newly scan converted data. 
 
7.3.1. 
Scan Conversion and Display on same machine 
 
In the normal situation the scan converter and the display are running on the same 
machine, and the sequence of calls to create a scan converter and display is simply 
(for Windows): 
 
SPxSCDestDisplayWin *spxDisplay = new SPxScDestDisplayWin(); 
                            spxDisplay->Create(0, 512, 512); 
SPxScSourceLocal *spxSc = new SPxScSourceLocal(spxDisplay,   
 
 
 
 
 
 
   SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI); 
 
The first call makes the display object which is initialised by the Create call to have a 
maximum size of 512 x 512.  Then a scan conversion object is created and attached 
to the display window. 
 
To provide data for the scan converter, create a RIB: 
 
 
SPxRIB *spxRib = new SPxRIB(1024*1024, NULL); 
 
Then create a PIM that will take data from the RIB and maintain a polar-store: 
 
 
SPxPIM * spxPim = new SPxPIM(spxRib, 2048, 2048,  
 
 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK, SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, 1); 
 
The PIM was configured for a resolution of 2048 x 2048 and will peak-combine 
additional azimuths or samples to fill its space.  Now connect the PIM to the scan 
converter with a process.  
 
 
 


===== PAGE 264 =====
Page 264 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxRunProcess *spxScProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
          NULL, spxPim,      
                                                spxSc); 
 
Finally add a radar source, for example a test generator, to provide data to the RIB: 
 
SPxTestGen *TestGen = new SPxTestGenerator(spxRib, 2048, 4.0,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1000, 35, 10); 
 
The TestGen test generator outputs 1000 returns per second over a 4 second scan-
period into the Radar Input buffer, generating test pattern 35 with 2048 samples per 
return.  
 
7.3.2. 
Scan Conversion and Display on Different Machines 
 
When the scan converter and the display module are split across a network 
connection, two additional objects are used to provide the network interface. 
Functionally, this provides exactly the same capabilities as the SPxScSourceLocal and 
SPxScDestDisplay objects when they are used on a single machine. The two 
additional objects are responsible for the transfer of the data across the network. As 
real-time fading is handled on the display side, the data transferred across the 
network is the scan converted bitmap data at the average rate of one bitmap per 
rotation of the radar. 
 
7.3.3. 
Application and Scan Conversion running on different machines 
 
In one configuration, the application software runs on one machine and the scan 
converter runs on another. The display may be running on the same machine as the 
application or the scan converter, or may be running on a third machine. 
 
In the configuration where the application runs on a different machine to the scan 
converter, there is a scan conversion server that handles a connection to the 
application. The server is an executable program that presents a well-defined 
interface across the network for control commands. The application connects to the 
server over a TCP interface and sends it commands. The class SPxScRemote is used by 
the application to get access to a remote scan converter. 
 
If the radar display is on the same machine as the scan converter then the scan 
conversion server can include the capabilities of the display. Otherwise, an additional 
display server is used to handle the interface between the scan converter and the 
display. The display server must run on the same machine as the X Server (for X 
Windows configurations) driving the display. 
 
7.4. Radar Presentation 
7.4.1. 
Radar Colour and Emphasis 
 
The colour to be used for the radar video is programmable. The colour is programmed 
as the RGB value of the maximum radar intensity. Radar samples of lower levels are 
drawn in proportionally lower intensities.  
 


===== PAGE 265 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 265 
In addition to the colour, a property called emphasis may be set. The emphasis of the 
radar video affects the display mixing of the video with other layers. With an 
emphasis of 0, the radar video is added into the display. For example, a bright green 
radar sample displayed on top of a bright blue map would appear as cyan, being the 
colour addition of the radar and underlay graphics colour. With an emphasis of 1, the 
underlay graphics are attenuated according to the brightness of the radar. So in the 
same example of a green radar above a blue map, the result would be green. In this 
mode, the radar is given greater emphasis above the map. 
 
7.4.2. 
Fading 
 
Fading is the process by which radar video eventually disappears from the display. 
There are three options: 
 
Sweep fade This form of fade adjusts the video intensity only with the update of the 
sweep. Each update replaces the contents of the sector with the highest of the new 
video and the old video that is reduced in amplitude. 
 
Real-time fade This form of fade attempts to simulate the decay of video in a 
smooth continuous manner that approximates the type of fade seen on the original 
phosphor displays. To implement real-time fade the video data must be adjusted in 
amplitude at regular and frequent intervals. This update frequency is configurable in 
SPx, and needs to be set to reflect the available hardware processing capability. Real-
time fading is more computationally expensive and may not always be available. In 
most applications, Sweep fade is recommended. 
 
No fade: In this mode, new data replaces old data and no history is preserved. The 
display shows data that is no older than one sweep. This mode is otherwise called 
replacement mode, as new video simply replaces existing data. 
 
In terms of the SPx scan conversion objects, real-time fade is implemented in the 
display-side objects, whereas sweep fade (and no fade) is implemented in the scan 
conversion object. Both real-time and sweep fade present history trails, which are 
programmable in duration.  The preferred option for implementing fading is sweep-
based, because it provides a display of long-term persistence without incurring the 
processing load of real-time fade. 
 
For real-time fade, consideration must be given to the total duration of the fade 
versus the rate of fade. Given that there are only 256 levels to fade through, and each 
fade update must modify the data by at least one level, there are at most 256 fade 
updates before video is removed. A fast fade that attempts to show rapidly decaying 
video will therefore have a limit on the total duration of the fade. For example, fading 
at a rate of 50 ms will allow a maximum of about 12.5 seconds for history trails. For 
longer trail history the fade rate can be reduced, but better results (and lower CPU 
load) will be obtained by using sweep fade. In sweep fade the maximum duration of 
the fading is 255 scans of the radar.  
 
 
 


===== PAGE 266 =====
Page 266 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
7.4.3. 
Sweep Line 
 
A sweep line is a visual indication of the position of the antenna as it rotates. When 
using real-time fade, a sweep line is generally visible automatically as a consequence 
of the fading radar video. When using sweep fade, there is no change in the video 
intensity inside one sweep so it can be difficult to see the position of the antenna in 
the rotation. In this situation it is possible to display a sweep line. 
 
7.4.4. 
Understanding Fade Modes and Sweep Lines 
 
To provide a graphical demonstration of the fade modes and use of the sweep line, 
the SPx Development release is provided with a sample application called 
SPxFadeDemo – see Figure 68 (see SPxExamples/SPxWinFadeDemo for source code). 
 
Figure 68 - Application to demonstrate fade modes and sweep lines 
 
Although this demonstration is written for Windows, it is also a useful visual 
demonstration tool for Linux developers, since it shows the effects of the different 
fade modes and use of the sweep line. The executable Windows application is 
provided in the Applications directory in the SPx Development Release 
 
The demonstration shows the scan conversion of a test pattern comprising either a 
static pattern – as shown in the figure – or a set of moving targets which provides an 
opportunity to see trail history if either Sweep or Time fade are selected.   
 
The software can be used to enable the display of the sweep line to see the effect on 
the display. The combination mode can be changed between Offset, Highest and 
Replace and the colour of the sweep line can be adjusted. The API function 


===== PAGE 267 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 267 
SetSweepLine() is available for the SPxScSourceLocal and SPxScRemote classes (see 
Section 7.10.1.10 for SPxScSourceLocal and Sector 7.11.2 for SPxScRemote). 
 
Recommended configurations of fade type and sweep lines for different radar 
configurations are as follows: 
 
Radar Scanning 
Fade type 
Sweep Line 
Random scan 
Real-time 
Possible, but generally not needed. 
Rotating scan 
Sweep 
If required to see sweep position. Recommend 
OFFSET mode with low colour, e.g. 0x00303030 
 
7.4.5. 
Scan to Scan Correlation 
  
The SPxScSourceLocal class supports a window-based scan-to-scan correlation 
option, where the intensity of each pixel can be a function of the radar data for the 
newest scan as well as up to eight previous scans. This can be used to improve the 
appearance of the radar image, remove uncorrelated noise and make real targets 
clearer. 
 
By operating at a pixel-level, the results are dependent on the zoom and off-centre 
position of the current radar view, but can still be used on moving platforms. The 
correlation is performed in the bitmap to be displayed, so any radar returns that are in 
the same position in the window from scan to scan will be deemed to correlate even if 
the radar has moved over that time. For example, in a true motion display, where the 
radar centre is moving within the window, targets that are in the same geographic 
position will correlate in the window and therefore be displayed when uncorrelated 
noise is removed. 
 
Note that this feature differs from the SSI processes (described in section 6.7.31) 
which operate on the polar video and assume a stationary radar centre. 
 
When enabled, or in standby mode, the class constantly maintains a rolling history 
window of up to 8 values for each location, with a 16-bit history per pixel.  This means 
the bit resolution of the stored data is dependent on the window length, allowing 2 
scans of 8-bit data, 3 scans of 5-bit and so on up to 8 scans of 2-bit data. The length 
of the window (and therefore the resolution of the samples) is defined by a 
parameter. A configurable gain can be applied to the data before it is reduced to the 
bit depth, to allow a non-linear mapping between the radar intensity and the sample 
stored for each pixel. The length of the window is an important choice - it should be 
set large enough for sufficient correlation to occur, but not so large that it takes a long 
time for the history to build up initially.  Typically, a value of 3 scans is often used. 
 
The data output to the display is a combination of the live input data and the 
correlated data. The live data can be let through unchanged (so anything in the latest 
scan is visible whether it is correlated or not), or it can be thresholded so that only 
returns exceeding a particular level get through.  The result is highest-wins combined 
with the correlated data, which can have an optional gain applied if required. 
 
A typical usage of SSC is to remove uncorrelated noise, but display returns that are 
consistent over time, or anything that is particularly strong in the instant video. In 
this case, the instant threshold will be set to an appropriate level, e.g. 50%, and the 
correlation will be set to 3 from 3 scans, for example. The output video will therefore 


===== PAGE 268 =====
Page 268 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
show anything that correlates from scan to scan (such as stationary or slow-moving 
weak targets), or anything that doesn’t correlate but is sufficiently strong to exceed 
the threshold (such as fast-moving strong targets). 
  
 
Figure 69 – Before and after examples 
 
An alternative usage can be to make intermittent returns more persistent. For 
example, by setting the correlation to 2 from 3 scans, any target that is visible in the 
data for two scans would still be painted on the third even if missed. Note that this 
can increase the level of displayed noise too though. 
 
A third usage is to emphasise persistent but weak targets. For example, setting the 
correlation to 3 from 3, but applying a gain to the correlated data will ensure that 
anything that correlates is boosted on the display. However, this will only work well on 
data sets that do not have a lot of correlated noise, or else the noise would be 
similarly boosted. Note that since this is done at the pixel resolution, not the polar 
resolution, the results will depend greatly on the zoom factor. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 269 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 269 
7.4.6. 
Scaling of Scan Conversion 
 
The SPxScSourceLocal class will auto-detect when the scan converted image is being 
displayed on a screen with a very high physical resolution. By default, when the 
screen has a physical resolution greater than 200 dpi, the resolution of the scan 
conversion will be scaled down by a factor of 2. The scan converted image will then be 
scaled back up by the destination class. This results in a reduction of processor usage. 
 
Alternatively, the required scan conversion scaling may be controlled explicitly using 
the SetScaleFactor() function and associated "ScaleFactor" parameter. See 
sections 7.10.1.14 and 7.10.1.15 for further details. 
 
7.5. View Control 
 
The scan conversion window has a geometry, which is the size of the window in 
screen pixels, and a view, which defines the contents of the window in terms of scale 
and off-centring. The view is specified in metres or lat/long for PPI and 
metres/degrees for B-Scan.  
The radar has a position in the world coordinate system, which is either set from a 
SetRadarPosition call, or else set indirectly by associating the scan conversion 
window with an entry in a Radar Database. 
 
The view in a scan conversion window may be defined from the radar position, range 
and x, y, width and height of the view geometry – see Figure 70. The centre of the 
orange circle in the left diagram is the radar position. The green rectangle defines the 
view, which is positioned to show a portion of radar video. The resultant scan 
conversion window is shown in the right hand side. 
 
The top left corner of the scan conversion window represents radar at ViewCentreX – 
ViewWidth/2. If the radar position is set to (0,0) then the view centre is simply an 
offset in metres from the radar position to the radar at the centre of the window. 
 
Figure 70 - View Definition for a Scan Conversion Window 
 
For most applications, the control of a view in a PPI window can be best provided by a 
SPxViewControl object, which is attached to the scan converter. The SPxViewControl 
object allows the radar view to be managed in lat/long coordinates. 
R 
Window 
World view of radar 
coverage and view 


===== PAGE 270 =====
Page 270 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
7.5.1. 
View Control with SetView API – Example for PPI Windows 
 
Suppose the returns have a range of 10,000 metres and the radar has a position 
(0,0), as set by SetRadarPosition() in the scan conversion object. Then a 
SetView() call specifying a value of (0,0) for the centre and (20000,20000) for the 
size will cause the radar to be seen as centred and just filling a square window – see 
(a) of Figure 71. Changing the view width and height to be 40,000, keeping the x and 
y position as 0, will achieve a “zoom out” effect, as shown in (b) of Figure 71. Setting 
the view centre to be (5000, -5000) and size to be (5000, 5000) will show a “zoomed-
in” quadrant as shown in (c) of Figure 71. 
 
Figure 71 - Sample View Geometries 
 
Although in the above examples the view geometry has a width and height, in practice 
the scaling of the scan conversion uses an x and y scale factor computed from the 
view width. If, in case (c), the window is not square, then for the same view geometry 
the scaling is correct for the x dimension and more or less of the scan conversion is 
seen in the y direction – see (d) and (e). Significantly, in (d) and (e), the centre point 
of the window is the radar location (5,000 , 5,000), as requested in the view, and the 
scaling in the x direction is to 10,000 metres. The scaling in the y direction is the 
same as the x, so the amount of radar visible varies as the size of the window 
changes. 
 
7.5.2. 
View Changes from SetView() 
 
The view in a window may be changed with SetView(). By default, the scan converter 
immediately repaints the radar video at the new view using available data in the PIM. 
This means that a the response to a SetView() command is a near real-time update 
of the radar at the new view. However, the time taken to repaint the radar depends 
on the window size, hardware configuration and other system activities. If a sequence 
of SetView commands are sent in quick succession the display might not keep up. 
Also, for a very fast scan update it may be sufficient to wait until the radar sweeps 
around to update the display. 
 
The immediate update of a view following a SetView is affected using 
EnableFastRedraws() on the Scan converter object. Setting fast redraws on causes 
(a) 
(b) 
(c) 
(d) 
(e) 


===== PAGE 271 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 271 
the radar view to be updated immediately on a SetView. Setting fast redraws to off 
means that the radar will only be updated with the rotating sweep. 
 
7.5.3. 
B-Scan Views 
 
B-Scan windows are drawn relative to a 0,0 reference position (often called Own 
Ship’s Reference Point, OSRP, on a naval system). If the radar is located at a different 
location, the position relative to OSRP can be defined by the SetRadarPosition 
function.  
The scan conversion automatically performs parallax compensation of the radar data 
to allow for the different range and aspect of the targets seen by the radar compared 
to OSRP. Without this parallax compensation, a target observed by two different 
radars at two locations on the ship would have a different range and azimuth. This 
would be especially problematic if the two radar videos were being overlaid into a 
single composite display. Cambridge Pixel provides a demonstration example of this in 
the SPx Developer library as SPxExamples/SPxX11bscan2. 
 
7.5.4. 
Upside-down radars 
 
In the normal orientation of the radar the rotation is clockwise. In the situation where 
the radar is mounted upside down, the clockwise rotation of the mechanics appears as 
an anti-clockwise rotation when viewed from above. This mode of operation is 
supported in the scan conversion. When properly configured, the radar sweep will be 
anti-clockwise, corresponding to the apparent movement of the radar when viewed 
from above. 
 
For upside down radars, the radar return needs to have the 
SPX_RIB_DATA_FLAG_UPSIDE_DOWN bit set in the Data Flags field of the header - refer 
to Section 3.1.1 for details of this. This bit is automatically set when using the HPx 
series cards where the radar configuration is indicated as upside-down. 
 
7.6. History Trail Retention 
 
History trails are normally lost when the view of the radar is changed. This means a 
zoom or pan on the radar video will lose any accumulated history. This can be 
changed by linking the scan conversion object with a SPxProHistory process, which 
provides retained trail history. Refer to details for SPxProHistory in Section 6.7.28 for 
details. 
 
7.7. Multiple scan conversions in a window 
 
It is possible to configure multiple scan conversions in a single window, although 
hardware performance will provide the practical limitation. In SPx, this can be done by 
having separate Radar Interface Buffers, PIMs and scan conversion processes, with 
the common link being the shared window that both scan conversion processes output 
to. This allows multiple scan conversion processes to provide their windows onto 
different locations of the final display window, with radars overlapped as appropriate. 
See Figure 72 for an example configuration, which has two independent radar inputs 
being presented in a single window, and Figure 73 for a situation in which a single 


===== PAGE 272 =====
Page 272 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
source of radar is shown as original and processed versions in a single window. 
Additional performance savings can be made by sharing the scan converter 
destination between multiple scan converters. However, sharing the scan converter 
destination means that all scan converters must be displayed in the same colour and 
brightness, with real-time fading and the same fade rate. See Figure 74 for an 
example configuration, which has eight independent radar inputs being presented in a 
single window with a single scan converter destination. 
 
Figure 72 - Two scan conversions in a single window with radar  
from separate sources 
 
 
Figure 73 - Two scan conversions in a single window with radar from an  
original and processed source

7.8. Live and Trails Video 
 
Using a single radar scan converter, new radar video appears in the same colour as 
faded radar video from previous scans (trails video). Alternatively, two radar scan 
converters may be used, allowing the new radar video to appear in one colour on top 
of faded radar video from the previous scan in a different colour. The scan converter 
showing live radar video is configured with replace mode fading, while the scan 
converter showing trails video is configured with sweep fading or real-time fading. The 
methods described in section 7.7 are used for display. 
 
The scan converter also supports using a single object for generating scan converted 
live and trails video in different colours. The scan converter allows a second 
destination object to be associated, which will be used for the output of scan 
converter trails video. The two destination objects may then be combined in a single 
window in the usual manner. When the scan converter is used in this way, the fade 
rate, colour and brightness of the two radar videos are configured independently using 
the radar index argument - 0 for live video, and 1 for trails video. 
 
If a history process is attached to the scan converter, it will automatically be applied 
to the trails video, but not the live video.
