7.9. Moving Platform Displays – True and Relative Motion 
 
The PPI display of radar video from a moving platform can be either true motion or 
relative motion. These are explained in the following sub-sections. 
 
In addition, the SPx library provides a class named SPxViewControl which may be 
used to implement lat/long based view control and assist with the display of radar on 
a moving platform.  Please refer to section 7.12 for further details. 
 
7.9.1. 
True Motion Displays 
 
In a true motion display, the position of the own ship moves about the screen, as do 
the position of moving targets. Radar returns associated with static targets appear in 
the same position. Usually, the true motion display is stabilised with North pointing 
upwards (sometimes called stabilised), but the true motion display can also appear as 
heading upwards. 
 
To implement a true motion display in SPx, the position of the radar on the screen 
must be adjusted in position and possibly orientation.  
 
Where the display features a map, the characteristic of a true motion display is that, 
for the same view, the map remains fixed and the ship moves across the map. 
  
Consider the North referenced, true motion display. If the radar is generating North 
referenced data (azimuth 0 corresponds to North) directly, then no reorientation of 
the video is needed. However, if the radar is generating ship-relative video orientation 
(azimuth 0 corresponds to ship heading) then the video will need to be rotated. The 
rotation correction happens in the PIM and is activated using the NorthReference 
function of the PIM (see Section 5.5). Use NorthReference when: 
 
The radar is generating North referenced video and the display calls for ship-
relative video 
 
or 
 
The radar is generating ship referenced video and the display calls for North 
referenced video. 
 
The NorthReference function of the PIM (see Section 5.5) uses a SPxNavData object 
(see Section 6.8.13) to handle the management of the navigation data for the ship. 
The SPxNavData object handles either an RS232 NMEA or network message format for 
the navigation information. This information is decoded and held in the state of the 
SPxNavData object, which is queried by the PIM. 
 
It should be noted that if a heading up display is desired, the map data must be 
oriented to a similar presentation. For a complex map, the need to redraw it at regular 
intervals in response to the ship’s heading change may impose a significant 
computational load. In many situations where a complex map is presented as an 
underlay to the radar, the North up display is preferred. 
 
In addition to the rotation, the x, y position of the ship must be adjusted to effect the 
change of position of the radar centre on the screen. The scan converter function 
SetRadarPosition must be used by the application to shift the centre of the radar in 


===== PAGE 275 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 275 
sympathy with the motion of the radar. This is described in more detail in Section 
7.9.1.1. 
 
The use of NorthReference on the PIM and SetRadarPosition on the scan converter 
is sufficient to implement a true motion radar display.  
 
A static target, say a landmass, will be displayed at the same position on the screen 
and therefore will remain aligned with a map feature of the same land area. If the 
radar is moving, the position of the static landmass will change with respect to the 
radar, but this is compensated for with the adjustments to the radar centre that occur 
through the SetRadarPosition function. 
 
In the example shown in Figure 75, the top picture shows the position of the ship and 
a moving and stationary target. For the true motion, North up display, the ship and 
target move on the map, which remains in a fixed North up orientation. The ship’s 
speed is 10 knts with course of 330 degrees and the moving target has speed 10 knts 
and course 210 degrees. The radar video display in the lower picture shows a single 
echo for the stationary target (i.e. the motion of the radar has been removed) and a 
trail history for the moving target in the direction of motion of the target. 
 
Figure 75 - True Motion, North Up display 
 
The true motion, North up display shown in Figure 75 may be adjusted to a true 
motion heading up display by: 
 
 
Using a different mode value in NorthReference option in the PIM associated 
with the radar display. 
 
 
Rotating the map to ensure the up direction corresponds with ship’s heading. 
 
7.9.1.1 Moving the Radar Position 
 
The SetRadarPosition function on the scan converter object moves the centre of the 
radar into the window. The function takes a new x, y position and a clear argument. 
The clear argument set to 0 to ensure that history trails are not cleared when the 
position shift occurs. 
 
Ship 
Moving 
Target 
Stationary 
Target 
Ship 
Moving 
Target 
Stationary 
Target 


===== PAGE 276 =====
Page 276 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
There are two important considerations when moving the radar position. The first is 
the frequency of calling the function and the second is the computation of the required 
x and y position. 
 
Each time SetRadarPosition is called, there is a computational overhead associated 
with recalculation of internal tables. This computation is done intelligently so that only 
newly visible pixels in the window need recalculating. This means that the 
computation required in the library depends on the extent of the movement in screen 
pixels. The number of screen pixels associated with a position change depends on 
three factors: 
 
 
The rate at which the SetRadarPosition is called 
 
The speed of the platform 
 
The scale factor of the display 
 
It is desirable to aim for a frequency of calling SetRadarPosition that results in a 
position movement of between 1 and 2 pixels on the screen. 
 
For example, suppose the platform is moving at 20 knts (10.29 m/s), the displayed 
window shows 50 NM (92.6km), and the window width is 1000 pixels. This means that 
each pixel is 92.6/1000 = 9.26 metres. At the stated speed, the platform moves one 
pixel in 0.9 seconds and two pixels in 1.8 seconds. Therefore, the SetRadarPosition 
should be called at an interval of between 0.9 and 1.8 seconds. If the window was 
showing a scale of 5 NM (9.26km), the size of a pixel would be 0.926 metres. For this 
scale, the platform moves one pixel in 90 ms and two pixels in 180 ms. Therefore, the 
SetRadarPosition should be called at an interval somewhere between these values. 
 
If SetRadarPosition is called faster than the recommended speed, there may be 
more computation overhead for little display benefit. If the scan converter object 
calculates that the change is less than one pixel, no work is done and the processing 
is efficient. If SetRadarPosition is called slower than the recommended speed, the 
actual number of screen pixels between updates will appear as an error in the true 
position of targets. For example, if the time between calls causes a movement of 10 
screen pixels of the radar centre, then that will be the magnitude of the error in the 
appearance of targets. 
 
Whatever the rate of calling SetRadarPosition, the caller is responsible for 
calculating the required x, y position for each call. Obviously, the more frequently the 
function is called, the smaller will be the step size between consecutive calls. The 
specified x,y position represents the new centre point for the radar in the view 
geometry of the window. This x,y position must be derived from the current position 
of the platform to ensure that the platform always appears over the correct location 
on the display, and, if appropriate, the map. An SPxNavData object may be used to 
handle NMEA message reporting of the platform position, and this object may be 
periodically queried to retrieve the position. Since the SPxNavData object retrieves the 
position as a lat/long coordinate, a conversion process is required to convert the 
lat/long value to an x,y position in metres. Once a reference point is established, the 
exact x,y values are not important, as the values are only used to set the position of 
the radar in the view. For example, the first retrieved position of the radar can be 
designated at (0,0) and a view constructed with (0,0) at the centre to show the 
platform at the centre of the window. Subsequent SetRadarPosition calls would then 
move the platform by an amount in metres computed from the relative change in 
lat/long position. The conversion of the platform lat/long position to an x,y coordinate 
in metres is the responsibility of the application software. 


===== PAGE 277 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 277 
7.9.1.2 True Motion Display Reset 
 
Because the radar moves across the screen in true motion display, it will eventually 
reach the edge of the window. The position of the radar therefore needs to be 
periodically reset to bring the radar back towards the centre of the display. This is the 
responsibility of the SPx application software. The application can use the SetView 
command on the scan converter object to shift the display view so that the current 
position of the radar becomes the centre point of the display. The application software 
may decide to implement this function in response to a manual Recentre operation 
from the user interface, or else may implement it automatically by initiating the 
recentre when the radar centre becomes within a certain distance to the edge of the 
window. For example, one approach is to trigger an automatic recentre when the 
radar position is within 25% of the displayed range scale from the window edge. 
 
7.9.2. 
Relative Motion Display 
 
In a relative motion display, the radar remains at a fixed location on the screen and 
the world moves around it. A stationary target or landmass appears at a different 
location on the screen as the platform moves. Therefore, the observed history trails 
show the relative motion of the targets with respect to the radar. The relative motion 
display can be oriented as North up or heading up. 
 
As far as the display of radar video is concerned, the relative motion display is easy to 
achieve. The only consideration is the orientation of the radar in North or heading up 
orientation. This is achieved using the NorthReference function of the PIM associated 
with the scan conversion (see Section 5.5). 
 
Consider again the example depicted in Figure 75. For the same motion of targets and 
ship consider the representation in a relative motion, North up display, which is shown 
in Figure 76. In this situation the ship position remains stationary on the screen. The 
stationary target shows a history trail that represents the relative motion of the ship 
with respect to the target. Since the ship is moving on a course of 330 degrees, the 
relative motion of the stationary target is 150 degrees. The moving target has a 
course of 210 degrees, so its relative motion is 180 degrees. The longer history trail of 
the moving target compared to the stationary target reflects the relative speed in the 
two situations. Although not depicted in Figure 76, the map itself must move at 
regular intervals to ensure that the ship and targets are correctly located. Although 
the stationary target appears to have a history trail with respect to the radar, it must 
always appear in the same position with respect to the map. 
 


===== PAGE 278 =====
Page 278 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 76 - Relative Motion, North Up display 
 
7.10. SPxScSourceLocal Scan Conversion Class  
 
The SPxScSourceLocal class implements the radar scan converter function. Use the 
SPxScSourceLocal class if you want the scan converter to run on the same machine 
as your application. If you want the scan converter to run on a remote server use the 
SPxScRemote class (see Section 7.11). 
 
When using SPxScSourceLocal, the polar input data comes from a PIM through one 
or more processing modules and data is provided at regular intervals, for example a 
sector at a time for a search radar. The output of the process is an 8-bit deep bitmap 
that contains the scan converted image at the correct view and scale. 
 
The rendering of this scan converted bitmap into a display is the responsibility of an 
application program or another SPx class, which accepts the 8-bit input (directly or 
across a network) and converts to a full-colour output in the format of the native 
graphics system.  
 
The SPxScSourceLocal scan converter class is completely independent of any 
graphics environment. Using the class, data may be scan converted and supplied into 
a user application program for rendering there, or it may be distributed over a 
network, or recorded to a file etc.

7.10.1. 
SPxScSourceLocal Class Interface  
 
7.10.1.1 Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxScSourceLocal::SPxScSourceLocal(SPxScDest *dest,  
                                   int scType = SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI,  
                                   SPxScDest *destTrails = NULL) 
 
Allocates a new scan conversion object, which will scan-convert a source of 
data and provide output to the dest object. The destination object can be a 
graphics class, network interface or a generic bitmap class. Refer to Section 8 
to see the permissible subclasses of SPxScDest. The type of the scan 
conversion may be SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI for PPI scan conversion or 
SPX_SC_TYPE_BSCAN for B-Scan. When multiple scan converters are writing to a 
shared destination, the type must be SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI_MASTER for the first 
scan converter writing to the destination, and SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI_SLAVE for all 
the other scan converters writing to the same destination. Refer to Section 7.1 
for details of the display types. Other types may be supported for your 
configuration. Refer to any system-specific documentation for details. The 
destTrails object may be supplied to allow a single scan converter to generate 
live and trails data, with live data written to the dest object and trails data 
written to the destTrails object. See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
 
SPxScSourceLocal::~SPxScSourceLocal() 
 
Destroy an SPxScSourceLocal instance. 
 
7.10.1.2 Fading Control 
 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 fadeRate) 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetFade(UCHAR rid, UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 
fadeRate) 
 
This function sets the fade type and rate for the window. The fadeType is 
SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME for real-time fade, SPX_RADAR_FADE_SWEEP for 
sweep-based fade and SPX_RADAR_FADE_REPLACE for replacement mode. 
The fadeRate parameter defines the duration of the fade as: 
 
For REAL_TIME fade the value of fadeRate is the fade time in units of 10s of 
milliseconds. For example, a value of fadeRate of 500 indicates a fade time of 
5 seconds (500 x 10ms = 5000ms). 
 
For SWEEP fade the value of fadeRate is the number of sweeps of fade. 
 
For REPLACE mode, the fadeRate parameter is not used. 
 
The optional rid parameter controls whether the fade type and rate are being 
set for live video or trails video, when live and trails video are being generated 
by a single scan converter. See section 7.8 for further details. 


===== PAGE 280 =====
Page 280 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetFadeType(UCHAR rid = 0) 
 
Return the current fade type for the scan conversion object. The optional rid 
parameter controls whether the returned fade type applies to live video or trails 
video when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan converter. 
See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetFadeRate(UCHAR rid = 0) 
 
Return the current fade rate for the scan conversion object. The optional rid 
parameter controls whether the returned fade rate applies to live video or trails 
video when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan converter. 
See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
7.10.1.3 Window Position 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetWinPos(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY) 
 
Set the position of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. Although 
an application may call this function to explicitly set the position of the radar 
window, it is often the case that the class SPxScFollowWin (for Windows) or 
SPxScFollowX11 (for X11 with Window Manager Compositing) is used to 
automatically set the position of the radar window to be aligned with another 
window, which typically will contain graphics. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetWinGeom(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY,  
 
 
 
 
 
   UINT16 screenW, UINT16 screenH) 
 
Set the position and size of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. 
See comments for SetWinPos previously regarding the use of the Follow 
classes. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetRadarScreenX(void) 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetRadarScreenY(void) 
 
These functions return the x and y coordinates of the screen pixel that 
represent the radar centre. The coordinates may be negative if the radar centre 
is outside the window. 
 
 
double SPxScSourceLocal::GetRadarEndRangeInPixels(void) 
 
This function returns the end range of the radar in screen pixels. This function 
is only applicable for PPI type scan conversion. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 281 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 281 
7.10.1.4 View Control 
 
There are two approaches to controlling the radar view in a window. The first 
approach is to set SetView() to define the centre point and width of the window 
relative to the radar position. The units of this are metres. The second approach is to 
attach the SPxScSourceLocal to a SPxViewControl object, and use the 
SPxViewControl to manage the view. The SPxViewControl object provides a lat/long 
interface which is often easier to integrate into a client application. If the 
SPxViewControl object is usd to manage the view then do not use SetView() or 
PanView() on the scan converter class, but rather control the view through the 
SPxViewControl object using its view control functions. 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vx, 
 
 
 
 
 
 REAL32 vw, REAL32 vh) 
 
This function is called to make a view change in the radar window. Refer to 
Section 7.5 for an explanation of view control. For PPI scan conversion, vx, vy 
is the view coordinate at the centre of the window, and vw and vh are the view 
dimensions in metres (in this case only vw is used since the scale factor has to 
be the same in x and y). For B-Scan scan conversion, vx, vy is the view 
coordinate at the centre of the window in degrees and metres, and vw is the 
view’s width in degrees and vh is the view’s height in metres. 
 
When the scan converter receives a SetView command it will consider the 
setting of FastRedraws to decide whether to do an immediate refresh of the 
radar view. See EnableFastRedraws() for the API to control this. If 
FastRedraws is off, the radar only repaints with the rotating sweep. If 
FastRedraws is on, there is an immediate repaint of available data at the new 
view. Note that the repaint will take time (depends on window size), so sending 
SetView commands very quickly with FastRedraws on is not advisable. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetView(REAL32 *vx, REAL32 *vy,  
                              REAL32 *vw,  
                              REAL32 *vh) 
 
This function returns the current x, y, w, and h of the view through the input 
pointers. The view geometry is interpreted according to the type of the scan 
conversion. See SetView() for an explanation of the geometry. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::PanView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vx, 
 
 
 
  
       UCHAR clearTrails = 0) 
 
This variant of the function changes the centre point of the view only, leaving 
the width and height unchanged. The clearTrails parameter may be used to 
remove old history trails during the view shift, although the default behaviour if 
the clearTrails parameter is omitted is to leave history trails in place. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 282 =====
Page 282 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetViewControl(SPxViewControl *viewCtrl,  
                                      unsigned int radarIndex=0) 
 
Install an SPxViewControl object that will be used to manage the view of the 
radar video in a PPI window.  Refer to section 7.12 for further details. 
 
The radar index is a value between 0 and 3, used to identify the radar when 
there are multiple radars on the platform.  If not specified it defaults to zero.  
The radar index is used to obtain the correct offset of the radar from the 
platform own-ship position, as defined for the SPxNavData object.  Refer to 
section 6.8.14.6 for further details. 
 
7.10.1.5 Radar Colour and Brightness 
 
The set of functions below are concerned with the brightness or colour of the radar 
video. These functions are processed by the SPxScSourceLocal class by simply 
passing them on to the associated display class attached to the scan converter. 
Therefore, the effect of the functions below is defined by the behaviour of the 
associated function for the display class. For example, if the scan converter is being 
used with a SPxScDestDisplayWin display class (for display under MS Windows) then 
the SetRadarColour function below simply invokes 
SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetRadarColour(). In some cases, for example, if the 
display class is an 8-bit bitmap object (SPxScDestBitmap with type = 
SPX_BITMAP_8BITS) then the colour and brightness functions will have no effect. 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid, UCHAR red,  
                                      UCHAR green, UCHAR blue) 
 
This function is called to set the radar colour. The radar colour is given by the 
red, green and blue components, each in the range 0..255. The specified colour 
represents the colour of maximum intensity radar with brightness = 100 (see 
SetRadarBright). 
 
The rid parameter controls whether the colour is being set for live video or 
trails video, when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan 
converter (otherwise set rid to 0). See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR brightness) 
 
This function sets the radar brightness. The brightness value is provided as a 
percentage in the range 0 to 255. The default value is 100, which gives a 1 to 1 
conversion from input intensity to display intensity. 
 
The rid parameter controls whether the brightness is being set for live video or 
trails video, when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan 
converter (otherwise set rid to 0). See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
UCHAR SPxScSourceLocal::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness. 
 


===== PAGE 283 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 283 
The rid parameter controls whether the brightness is being retrieved for live 
video or trails video, when live and trails video are being generated by a single 
scan converter (otherwise set rid to 0). See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR rid, unsigned char *lutData) 
 
Set the look-up table that converts radar pixel values into display colours. This 
function is complementary to SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright. Use 
SetRadarLUT to define an arbitrary mapping from 8-bit radar intensity 
values to colours (could be false-colour for example). To set the radar colour to 
a linear scale from background to peak use the SetRadarColour and 
SetRadarBright functions. The lutData is an array of ARGB colour values of 
size 256*4 = 1024 bytes. The data is organised as an array of 256 x 32-bit 
words, with each word holding a colour value in the format alpha (most 
significant 8-bits), red, green, blue (least significant 8 bits). Note that the use 
of this function is incompatible with the use of a sweep line using 
SetSweepLine. 
 
The rid parameter controls whether the LUT is being set for live video or trails 
video, when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan converter 
(otherwise set rid to 0). See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
7.10.1.6 Radar Video Visibility 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::ShowRadar(UCHAR rid, UCHAR state) 
 
Enable or disable the display of radar video. The state is 0 to stop the radar and 
clear the display, 1 for normal updates and 2 to stop and let the display fade (if 
real-time fade enabled). 
 
The rid parameter controls whether to show/hide live video or trails video, 
when live and trails video are being generated by a single scan converter 
(otherwise set rid to 0). See section 7.8 for further details. 
 
7.10.1.7 Radar Position 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetRadarPosition(REAL32 a, REAL32 b,  
                                       UCHAR clear = 0) 
 
Sets the position of the radar in the view. In PPI views, a is the X position in 
metres and b is the Y position. In B-Scan views,  a is the azimuth in degrees 
and b is the range in metres. 
 
The clear parameter is used to determine if the old radar trails should be 
cleared (clear = 1) or not (clear = 0). See Section 7.9.1.1 for a discussion of 
the use of this function in achieving a true motion radar display. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 284 =====
Page 284 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxScSourceLocal::GetRadarPosition(double *a, double *b) 
 
This function returns the current radar position through the input arguments. 
Refer to SetRadarPosition for a description of the interpretation of the 
arguments. 
 
7.10.1.8 Fast Redraws 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::EnableFastRedraws(int enable) 
 
This function may be used to enable (enable = 1) or disable (enable = 0) the 
immediate refresh of a radar display after a view change. The default condition 
is that fast draws are enabled, and on a view change the scan converter will 
immediately do a full scan conversion of the window contents at the new view 
scale. It is sometimes desirable to turn fast redraws off if, for example, the 
radar is rotating quickly and the time to scan convert and display the existing 
data is comparable to the time for new data to arrive. 
 
7.10.1.9 History Process 
 
void SPxScSourceLocal::SetRedrawHistoryProcess(SPxScProcess *history) 
 
When using the scan converter with trail history retention on static radars or 
moving platforms in Relative Trails mode, this function sets the process that 
handles the trail history.  Use SetRedrawHistoryProcess() to either set the 
history process, or else to disable trail history retention by using NULL as the 
history process argument. 
 
Refer to Section 6.7.28 for details of the history retention process, and sections 
7.10.1.11 and 7.10.1.12 for control of the different Moving Platform trails 
modes. 
 
7.10.1.10 
Sweep Line Display 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetSweepLine(SPxScSweepLineType_t type, 
  SPxScSweepLineColMode_t colorMode, 
  UINT32 rgb, 
  UINT32 reserved1, 
  UINT32 reserved2) 
 
This function controls the presentation of a sweep line. The sweep line is a line 
drawn from the radar centre at the current azimuth of the radar. It can be used 
to provide a visual indication of the radar rotation. The type argument is either 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_OFF to disable the sweep line, or 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_AUTO to enable the best type of sweep line for the 
current fading mode. For replace and sweep fading, the 8 bits are split as 7 bits 
for radar and 1 bit for the sweep line, giving a single width line at the leading 
edge of the sweep. For real-time fading, the 8-bits are split as 5 bits for radar 
and 3 bits for the sweep line, giving a wider sweep across all updated azimuths, 
which fades faster than the normal radar. 


===== PAGE 285 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 285 
The colorMode value controls how the sweep line colour is combined with the 
scan converted radar colour for each pixel. The options are: 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_OFFSET implies the sweep line is added on top of 
the radar colour. 
 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_HIGHEST means the sweep line pixels will only be 
shown if they are higher than the radar level. 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_REPLACE means the sweep line temporarily 
replaces the radar image regardless of the colour. 
 
The RGB colour provided is the colour/offset of the sweep line in the format 
0x00RRGGBB, with the blue components in the least significant byte and the 
most significant byte unused. The colour is completely independent of the radar 
colour. The reserved1 and reserved2 fields are unused and should be set to 0. 
 
The return value is an SPX error code. 
 
Refer to Section 7.4.4 for an explanation of the sweep line and recommended 
settings. 
 
7.10.1.11 
Moving Platform Trails 
 
On Moving Platforms, trail histories can be selected between one of two modes. 
 
Relative Trails show a history of how objects have been moving compared to the 
radar, so trails can build up for land and targets that are not actually moving if the 
radar platform itself is moving instead. 
 
True Trails show a history of how objects have been moving compared to the world, 
so trails will only build up for targets that have non-zero velocities. 
 
By default, the trails mode automatically matches the Display Motion Mode, as 
described in section 7.12.1.7.  If the view is configured for Relative Motion (i.e. the 
radar is at a fixed position within the window, with the world moving around it) then 
Relative Trails will be used.  If the view is configured for True Motion (i.e. the view in 
the window is fixed and the radar moves around the window as the platform moves) 
then True Trails will be used.  However, the following functions can be used to change 
this behaviour. 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetMovingPlatformTrailsMode 
                                          (SPxScMpTrailsMode_t mode) 
 
If the scan converter view is being controlled by an SPxViewControl object on 
a moving platform, the view may be in True Motion or Relative Motion display 
mode.  By default, the history trails are rendered in the matching mode, but 
this function can be used to switch the mode if required. The options are: 
 
SPX_SC_MP_TRAILS_NORMAL 
Trails should match the motion mode, i.e. true trails in true motion, or relative 
trails in relative motion. 
 


===== PAGE 286 =====
Page 286 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_SC_MP_TRAILS_RELATIVE_FROM_TRUE 
Relative trails should be used, but true motion is selected for display. 
 
SPX_SC_MP_TRAILS_TRUE_FROM_RELATIVE 
True trails should be used, but relative motion is selected for display. 
 
The current setting can be retrieved with: 
 
SPxScMpTrailsMode_t 
SPxScSourceLocal::GetMovingPlatformTrailsMode(void) const 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetMovingPlatformRefMode (SPxScMpRefMode_t mode) 
 
If the scan converter is being controlled by an SPxViewControl object on a 
moving platform, the display is heading or course-up, and true trails are being 
shown, the scan conversion must happen North-up, with a suitable adjustment 
applied by the scan conversion destination. By default, this combination of 
modes is not supported, because only some scan conversion destinations 
support the required adjustment, and the required adjustment may be 
computationally intensive. This function may be used to opt-in to heading or 
course-up true trails display. See SPxScDestBitmap::GetDisplayMatrix() and 
SPxScDestTexture::GetDisplayMatrix() for further details of how this mode 
may be supported by the scan conversion destination. 
 
The options for the reference mode are: 
 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_NORMAL 
Do not enable support for heading or course-up, true trails display – this is the 
default. 
 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH 
Opt-in to support for heading-up, true trails display. 
 
The current setting can be retrieved with: 
 
SPxScMpRefMode_t 
SPxScSourceLocal::GetMovingPlatformRefMode(void) const 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetMovingPlatformStabiliseMode 
                                              (SPxScMpStabiliseMode_t 
mode) 
 
By default, trails are stabilised relative to the ground - this requires platform 
position or platform speed/course information being supplied to the associated 
SPxViewControl object, either directly or from navigation data. Alternatively, 
trails may be stabilised with reference to the sea – this requires platform 
speed/course over water information being supplied to the associated 
SPxViewControl object. The platform speed and course over water information 
will be derived from platform speed/course over ground and set and drift if 
required. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 287 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 287 
The options for the stabilisation mode are: 
 
SPX_SC_MP_STABILISE_MODE_GROUND 
Stabilise trails relative to the ground. 
 
SPX_SC_MP_STABILISE_MODE_SEA 
Stabilise trails relative to the sea. 
 
The current setting can be retrieved with: 
 
SPxScMpStabiliseMode_t  
SPxScSourceLocal::GetMovingPlatformStabiliseMode(void) const 
 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::SetMPTrailsCallback( 
void (*fn)(SPxScSourceLocal *sc, void *userArg, int x, int y), 
 
 
void *userArg) 
 
Set a handler function (fn), to be invoked whenever the moving platform trails 
are about to be shifted due to the motion of the platform. The function will be 
called before the shift occurs, with the following four arguments: 
 
sc 
 
Handle of the SPxScSourceLocal object invoking the function. 
userArg 
The userArg parameter specified when the function was installed, 
if any. 
X 
 
The X shift in pixels that is about to happen. 
Y 
 
The Y shift in pixels that is about to happen. 
 
Only one handler function is supported per object. Setting a NULL function will remove 
a previously installed handler. 
 
7.10.1.12 
True Trails History Retention 
 
As described in section 7.10.1.11 above, history trails can be in Relative Trails or True 
Trails modes, but by default are lost when the view in the window changes (e.g. by 
panning or zooming). Histories can be preserved across these events, but at a cost of 
slightly increased memory and processor usage, so this needs to be explicitly enabled 
if required. 
 
Relative Trail histories can be retained across view changes by giving the scan 
converter a History Process and additional PIM, as described in section 7.10.1.9. 
 
True Trail histories can be retained across view changes by enabling the maintenance 
of one or more background bitmaps in the scan converter object. In the “live window”, 
trails are built up as normal at the same resolution as the window for the current 
view, but in the background a number of fixed-resolution images are also maintained 
with trails even if the targets are currently off-screen. When a view change occurs, 
the best off-screen image is used to initialise the trails in the new live view, which are 
then updated as normal when new radar data is received. 
 
The following functions can be used to enable and configure this feature. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 288 =====
Page 288 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetTrueTrailsDimension( 
unsigned int pixels); 
 
Set the size of the off-screen bitmap(s) used for preserving True Trails histories 
across view changes. Use a value of 0 to disable the feature, or a non-zero 
dimension to enable it. The default value when the object is created is 0. 
 
Dimensions are typically similar to the window or screen size being used to 
display the radar images, so a value of 1024 would be a common option. Larger 
values will give higher resolutions for the preserved history trails, but at a cost 
of increased memory and processor usage. Use GetTrueTrailsDimension() to 
retrieve the current setting. 
 
Note that the SetTrueTrailsRangeList() function must also be called if 
enabling True Trail history retention. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxScSourceLocal::GetTrueTrailsDimension(void); 
 
Retrieve the current dimension of the off-screen bitmap(s) used for True Trails 
history preservation, as set by SetTrueTrailsDimension(). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetTrueTrailsRangeList( 
 
 
 
 
 
 
const char *rangeMetresList) 
 
Set a white-space separated list of ranges to use for preserving True Trail 
histories, if the feature has been enabled via the SetTrueTrailsDimension() 
function. For each entry in the list, an off-screen bitmap is used to maintain a 
copy of the True Trails history, centred on the radar location with a width 
specified by the entry in the list. 
 
For example, using a list of “10000.0 50000.0 200000.0” will cause three 
bitmaps to be maintained, one with a width equal to 10km, one of 50km and a 
third of 200km. 
 
When the view in the live window is changed, the trails are initialised from the 
bitmap with the smallest range that encloses the new view, i.e. the bitmap with 
the best resolution covering the new area. 
 
Note that memory and processor usage increases with the number of bitmaps 
being maintained, so care should be taken when setting this parameter. There 
is a trade-off between system loading and increased resolution of trails at all 
zoom factors. 
 
 
const char *SPxScSourceLocal::GetTrueTrailsRangeList(void) 
 
Retrieve the current list of ranges for the off-screen True Trails bitmaps. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::TrueTrailsClearBitmaps(void) 
 
Forcibly clear the histories in the True Trails off-screen bitmaps, e.g. because 
the user wants to delete the old trails. 


===== PAGE 289 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 289 
Note that the above functions only affect the True Trails shown immediately 
after a view change, i.e. when the window is redrawn after a pan or zoom.  
Trails in the steady state are not affected by these functions, but are 
automatically maintained at the same resolution as the live window. 
 
7.10.1.13 
Scan-to-Scan Correlation Functions 
 
The following functions control the scan-to-scan correlation feature described in 
section 7.4.5. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCEnabled(int enabled); 
 
Enables or disables the use of per-pixel Scan to Scan Correlation in the display 
video. SSC is disabled by default. 
  
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCEnabled(void) const; 
 
Get the current SSC state. 
  
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCStandbyEnabled(int enabled); 
 
Enables or disables the maintenance of the SSC history window even if SSC is 
disabled. If standby mode is enabled, the history window is still maintained 
even when SSC display is disabled. This means that the effects of SSC can be 
seen sooner when it is later enabled, without waiting for the history to build up 
from scratch. However, there is a small processing overhead in maintaining the 
history, so if SSC is never required then the standby mode should be disabled 
too (the default value). 
  
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCStandbyEnabled(void) const; 
 
Get the current SSC standby state. 
  
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCWindowLength(unsigned int numScans); 
 
Set the length of the SSC history window (and therefore the resolution of the 
correlated data) to between 2 and 8 scans. The default value is 3 scans. 
  
unsigned int SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCWindowLength(void) const; 
 
Get the length of the SSC history window. 
  
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCWindowThreshold(unsigned int  
     numHits); 
 
Set the number of scans within the history window that are required for 
correlated data. For example, if the window length is 3, typical thresholds would 
be either 2 or 3, i.e. data must be seen either 2 or 3 times in the 3 scan 
window before it is displayed. A special value of 0 (the default) automatically 
keeps the threshold equal to the window length itself. 
  
unsigned int SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCWindowThreshold(void) const; 
 
Get the history window threshold setting. 


===== PAGE 290 =====
Page 290 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCInstantThreshold(unsigned char  
sampleLevel); 
 
Set the level above which radar samples are immediately displayed even if 
uncorrelated.  For example, a value of 0 would mean all live radar data is 
displayed.  A value of 255 would mean that no live radar data is displayed 
(unless it correlates).  A value of 127 (the default) would mean that only live 
radar data above 50% intensity would be displayed, in addition to any 
correlated data. 
  
unsigned char SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCInstantThreshold(void) const; 
 
Get the current threshold for live data. 
  
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCQuantisationGain(double gain); 
 
Set the gain applied to the radar data before it is stored in the quantised-
resolution history window. The default value of 1.0 means no gain is applied. 
  
double SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCQuantisationGain(void) const; 
 
Get the gain applied to the quantised history data. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetSSCCorrelationGain(double gain); 
 
Set the gain applied to the correlated data used for display. The default value of 
1.0 means no gain is applied. 
  
double SPxScSourceLocal::GetSSCCorrelationGain(void) const; 
 
Get the gain applied to the correlated data. 
 
7.10.1.14 
Scale Factor Functions 
 
The following functions control the scaling of the resolution at which scan conversion 
is performed, described in section 7.4.6. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScSourceLocal::SetScaleFactor(int scaleFactor); 
 
Set the required scaling for scan conversion. Zero indicates scaling should be 
automatic based on the physical resolution of the screen - with a screen 
resolution of 200 dpi or greater, the scan conversion will be scaled down by a 
factor of 2, otherwise no scaling will be applied. Any positive integer will scale 
the scan conversion down by that multiple, e.g. a scale factor of 3, with a 
window size of 900 by 900 pixels, will perform scan conversion at a resolution 
of 300 by 300 pixels. In all cases, the destination class will scale the scan 
converted image up to the size of the window. 
 
int SPxScSourceLocal::GetScaleFactor(void) const; 
 
Retrieve the configured scale factor. 
 
 


===== PAGE 291 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 291 
7.10.1.15 
Parameter Set/Get

7.11. Controlling a Remote Scan Conversion Server 
 
The class SPxScRemote is used when the application software runs on one machine 
and controls a dedicated scan converter on another machine (note that this is not 
applicable to the Radar Image Server (RIS) ). It should be noted that a usual client-
server architecture for radar distribution has the server distributing polar-format data 
to the client, where it gets scan converted. That configuration is not what is being 
described here. Instead, the SPxScRemote class permits the scan converter to run on 
a server machine and deliver image data over the network to a client. There is a one-
to-one correspondence between the server-side scan converter and the client-side 


===== PAGE 295 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 295 
display. This architecture is appropriate in some situations, but is not the normal 
approach. Please consult with Cambridge Pixel if you believe the server-side scan 
converter is the approach you need. 
 
The SPxScRemote class provides an API to connect to the scan conversion server and 
sends commands to create and maintain scan conversion windows. The scan 
conversion server is an executable program running on the remote machine. 
 
When using a remote scan converter, the client application does not create the objects 
associated with the radar acquisition and processing (that is the RIB, PIM and 
processing objects). The creation of these objects is the responsibility of the remote 
scan conversion server. The server exposes a high-level interface to the application to 
manipulate some of the parameters of the acquisition. It is the responsibility of the 
scan conversion server to decide what to expose, that is what the client can control. 
The scan conversion server can be written as a customised SPx application or else the 
standard SPx Scan Conversion Server may be used. Refer to “Scan Conversion Server 
Refence Manual” for full details of the scan conversion server. 
 
A sample configuration is shown in Figure 77, where the application uses a remote 
scan conversion server, which delivers the bitmap data back to the application for 
compositing and display under X Windows. In this example, the display processing 
occurs on the same machine as the application (the standard example program 
spxcomposite implements this configuration). 
 
An alternate configuration is shown in Figure 78, which features a scan conversion 
server and a display server. Unlike the example in Figure 77, the output of the scan 
conversion server does not return the bitmap data into the application. Instead, it 
provides it to a display server, which converts the bitmap data into a radar window 
that is combined with underlay and overlay graphics symbology (using window-
manager compositing). (The standard example program spxdemox implements this 
configuration). For most requirements, the example shown in Figure 78 would not be 
the preferred solution. 
 
Figure 77 - Application Software Controlling a Remote Scan Conversion Server 
Application Software 
SPxScRemote 
(API to remote SC) 
SPxScSourceNet 
SPxScDestBitmap 
Radar 
Bitmap 
SPxRadarSource 
SPxRIB 
SPxPIM 
SPxScSourceLocal 
SPxScDestNet 
Scan Conversion Server 
(This is a standard Cambridge Pixel-
supplied server application) 
X Client 
Libraries 
X Server 
Application and Display Processor 
Radar Data 


===== PAGE 296 =====
Page 296 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 78 - Application Software Controlling a Remote Scan Conversion Server  
and Display Server 
 
7.11.1. 
Using SPxScRemote 
 
The SPxScRemote class is used by a client application in the following way: 
 
1. Call the static function SPxScRemote::MakeNetwork() to define the networking 
interface for the scan converter connection. 
 
2. Make a connection to the scan conversion server using the 
SPxScRemote::Connect() function. 
 
3. Create an instance of SPxScRemote for each scan conversion window. 
 
4. Call Create() on the new instance to initialise it. 
 
5. Use the access functions to set the window geometry, view etc. 
 
For example: 
 
    SPxScRemote::MakeNetwork(“192.168.2.1”); 
 
    if (SPxScRemote::Connect() != SPX_NO_ERROR) 
    { 
        printf("Could not connect to the remote scan converter.\n"); 
        exit(-1); 
    } 
    // Connected ok     
    SPxScRemote *ScanConverter= new SPxScRemote(); 
    ScanConverter->Create(0,1000,1000, 0, 0); 
    ScanConverter->SetWinGeom(0,0, WinW, WinH); 
Application Software 
SPxScRemote 
SPxScSourceNet 
Scan Conversion Server 
X Client 
Libraries 
Radar Data 
 
 
 
 
SPxRadarSource 
SPxRIB 
SPxPIM 
SPxScSourceLocal 
SPxScDestNet 
X Server 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 
Display Server 


===== PAGE 297 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 297 
Note that SPxScRemote only provides the API for controlling the scan converter. It 
does not provide any display-related functions. 
 
7.11.2. 
SPxScRemote Class Interface 
 
SPxScRemote::SPxScRemote() 
 
Allocates a new scan conversion interface object. 
 
 
SPxScRemote::~SPxScRemote() 
 
Destroy a SPxScRemote instance. 
 
 
static int SPxScRemote::MakeNetwork(char *serverAddress = NULL,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
int serverPort = 0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
int heartbeatPort = 0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *heartbeatAddress=NULL) 
 
Create the network interface to the remote scan converter object. The 
serverAddress is the IP address or name of the remote machine running the 
scan converter. If no value is provided or NULL is used, then “127.0.0.1” is 
assumed, i.e. connect to a server on the local machine. An optional port 
number can be specified, although the default value is generally sufficient. 
Optionally, the constructor can define the heartbeat port and address that the 
server should use.  
 
 
static int SPxScRemote::PollHeartbeat 
                               (SPxScNetHeartbeatStruct *heartbeat, 
 
 
 
 
 
  UINT32 *sendingAddress = 0) 
 
This is a static class function which polls the server for a heartbeat and returns 
0 if there is no heartbeat and non-zero if there is one. In this case the input 
heartbeat structure is filled out and sendingAddress is set to the IP address of 
the server sending the heartbeat. 
 
The heartbeat structure of type SPxScNetHeartbeatStruct is defined as: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
    UINT32 magic;  
/* Magic number, SPX_SCNET_HEARTBEAT_MAGIC */ 
    UINT16 appId;  
/* ID of server - see SPX_SCNET_HB_ID defs */ 
    UINT8 health;  
/* Health of server (see SPxSystemHealth.h) */ 
    UINT8 state;  
/* State of server, see SPX_SCNET_HB_STATE...*/ 
    UINT32 clientAddr;  
/* Address of the client (0 if no connect) 
*/ 
    UINT16 listeningPort; 
/* Port that server accepts connections on 
*/ 
    UINT16 sourceStatus; 
/* Source status information */ 
    UINT32 reserved2; 
} SPxScNetHeartbeatStruct; 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 298 =====
Page 298 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxScRemote::Create(UCHAR screenNumber,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 maxWidth, UINT16 maxHeight, 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 ulayWindow, UINT32 olayWindow, 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 channelInfo = 0, 
 
 
 
 
 
UINt32 scType = SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI) 
 
The function is called after constructing an instance of SPxScRemote. It 
initialises the class with a screen number (which is 0 for a single screen 
system), the maximum window width and height, and the identifiers of the 
underlay and overlay window, if they are known. The channelInfo parameter 
defines the channel number in the remote server. Refer to the documentation 
for the scan conversion server for information about this parameter. The 
scType parameter defines the type of scan conversion that the remote server 
should implement, being one of SPX_SC_TYPE_PPI or SPX_SC_TYPE_BSCAN 
(other types be supported for your configuration – consult any system-specific 
documentation for details). 
 
 
static int SPxScRemote::Connect(unsigned int timeoutMsec = 0) 
 
A TCP connection is made on the port and IP address configured in a previous 
MakeNetwork() call.  
 
The default value of timeoutMsec is 0 which means the call is blocking and will 
only return when the connection is made. Specifying a non-zero timeout means 
wait for up to that time (in milliseconds) for the connection to succeed before 
returning. In practice, it might be a little longer due to the thread scheduling 
times in the operating system. To allow for network transmission times and 
latency in the server accepting the connection and sending the first handshake 
message, it is recommended that 1000 msecs is the smallest time used to 
reliably get a connection if one is possible. 
If the function times out (timeoutMsec was greater than 0 and no connection 
was made), the function returns SPX_ERR_TIMEOUT. At this point, the client 
application can call MakeNetwork() again to change the server address/port it is 
trying to connect to then call Connect() again. This can be repeated for 
different servers until one of them succeeds. 
 
 
static int SPxScRemote::Disconnect(void) 
 
The current TCP connection is disconnected. Another Connect call is needed 
before any commands can be sent. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetWinPos(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY) 
 
Set the position of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. Although 
an application may call this function to explicitly set the position of the radar 
window, it is often the case that the class SPxScFollowWin (for Windows) or 
SPxScFollowX11 (for X11) is used to automatically set the position of the radar 
window to be aligned with another window, which typically will contain 
graphics. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 299 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 299 
int SPxScRemote::SetWinGeom(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      UINT16 screenW,  
                                            UINT16 screenH) 
 
Set the position and size of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. 
See comments for SetWinPos previously regarding the use of the Follow 
classes. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetWinParam(UINT16 param, UINT32 arg1,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT32 arg2, UINT32 arg3) 
 
This function is used to set a number of window parameters as follows: 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_TEST_PATTERN  (value 10): Create a test 
picture in the window. The value of arg1 defines the test picture according to 
Table 9. (test patterns 0-6, 10 and 12 are supported). 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_UNDERLAY  (value = 11): Set the ID of a 
Window, provided in arg1, to be the underlay to the radar window. This 
parameter is often supplied at the time the window is created, but it if is not 
known at that time, or changes it can be reset. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_OVERLAY (value = 12): Set the ID of a 
Window, provided in arg1, to be the overlay to the radar window. This 
parameter is often supplied at the time the window is created, but it if is not 
known at that time, or changes it can be reset. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_UPDATE_INTERVAL (value 14): Set the update interval 
for the radar in the window. The value is provided in arg1 as the update tiime 
in milliseconds. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PRIORITY (value 15): Set the priority of the radar 
window. This is only relevant when multiple radar scan conversions are to be 
displayed in a window. By default, the priority is set when a 
SxScDestDisplayX11 object is created. The most recently created object has 
the highest priority and appears top-most of the radar windows. The priority 
was be changed with this parameter. The number is a integer with a larger 
number denoting a higher priority. 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 fadeRate) 
 
This function sets the fade mode for the window. The fadeType is 
SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME for real-time fade, SPX_RADAR_FADE_SWEEP for 
sweep-based fade and SPX_RADAR_FADE_REPLACE for replacement mode.  
 
The fadeRate parameter defines the duration of the fade as: 
 
For REAL_TIME fade the value of fadeRate is the fade time in units of 10s of 
milliseconds. For example, a value of fadeRate of 500 indicates a fade time of 
5 seconds (500 x 10ms = 5000ms). 
 
For SWEEP fade the value of fadeRate is the number of sweeps of fade.  


===== PAGE 300 =====
Page 300 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
For REPLACE mode, the fadeRate parameter is not used. 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::SetView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vy, REAL32 vw, REAL32 vh) 
 
This function is called to make a view change in the radar window. Refer to 
Section 7.5 for an explanation of view control. For PPI scan conversion vx, vy is 
the view coordinate at the centre of the window, and vw and vh are the view 
dimensions in metres (in the case only vw is used since the scale factor has to 
be the same in x and y). For B-Scan scan conversion, vx, vy is the view 
coordinate at the centre of the window in degrees and metres, and vw is the 
view’s width in degrees and vh is the view’s height in metres. 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::PanView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vx, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UCHAR clearTrails = 0) 
 
This variant of the function changes the centre point of the view only, leaving 
the width and height unchanged. The clearTrails parameter may be used to 
remove old history trails during the view shift, although the default behaviour if 
the clearTrails parameter is omitted is to leave history trails in place. 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid,  
 
 
 
 
 
   UCHAR red, UCHAR green, UCHAR blue) 
 
This function is called to set the radar colour. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The radar colour is given by the red, green and blue components, 
each in the range 0..255. The specified colour represents the colour of 
maximum intensity radar with brightness = 100 (see SetRadarBright). 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR bright) 
 
This function sets the radar brightness. The rid argument is intended to 
support futire expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused (set 
to 0). The brightness is in the range 0 to 100.  
 
 
UCHAR SPxScRemote::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness. The rid argument is 
intended to support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently 
unused (set to 0). 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR lutId, unsigned char *lutData) 
 
Set the look-up table that converts radar pixel values into display colours. This 
function is complementary to SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright. Use 
SetRadarLUT to define an arbitrary mapping from 8-bit radar intensity values to 
colours (could be false-colour for example). To set the radar colour to a linear 
scale from background to peak use the SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright 


===== PAGE 301 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 301 
functions. The lutID argument define a LUT identifier and is currently unused – 
set to 0. 
The lutData is an array of ARGB colour values of size 256*4 = 1024 bytes. 
The data is organised as an array of 256 x 32-bit words, with each word holding 
a colour value in the format alpha (most significant 8-bits), red, green, blue 
(least significant 8 bits). 
 
 
void SPxScRemote::ShowRadar(UCHAR rid, UCHAR state) 
 
Enable or disable the display of radar video. The rid argument is currently 
unused (set to 0). The state is 0 to stop the radar and clear the display, 1 for 
normal updates and 2 to stop and let the display fade (if real-time fade 
enabled).  
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetRadarPosition(REAL32 x, REAL32 y, UCHAR clear) 
 
Sets the position of the radar in the view. In PPI views, a is the X position in 
metres and b is the Y position. In B-Scan views, a is the azimuth in degrees and 
b is the range in metres. 
 
The clear parameter is used to determine if the old radar trails should be 
cleared (clear = 1) or not (clear = 0). See Section 7.9.1.1 for a discussion of 
the use of this function in achieving a true motion radar display. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetSweepLine(SPxScSweepLineType_type, 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxScSweepLineColMode_t colorMode, 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 rgb, UINT32 reserved1,  
                              UINT32 reserved2) 
 
This function controls the presentation of a sweep line. The sweep line is a line 
drawn from the radar centre at the current azimuth of the radar. It can be used 
to provide a visual indication of the radar rotation. The type argument is either 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_OFF to disable the sweep line, or 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_AUTO to enable the best type of sweep line for the 
current fading mode. For replace and sweep fading, the 8 bits are split as 7 bits 
for radar and 1 bit for the sweep line, giving a single width line at the leading 
edge of the sweep. For real-time fading, the 8-bits are plit as 5 bits for radar 
and 3 bits for the sweep line, giving a wider sweep across all updated azimuths, 
which fades faster than the normal radar.  
 
The colorMode value controls how the sweep line colour is combined with the 
scan converted radar colour for each pixel. A value of 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_OFFSET implies the sweep line is added on top of 
the radar colour. The value of SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_HIGHEST means the 
sweep line pixels will only be shown if they are higher than the radar level, and 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_REPLACE means the sweep line temporarily 
replaces the radar image regardless of the colour. 
 
The RGB colour provided is the colour/offset of the sweep line in the format 
0x00RRGGBB, with the blue components in the least significant byte and the 


===== PAGE 302 =====
Page 302 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
most significant byte unused. The colour is completely independent of the radar 
colour. The reserved1 and reserved2 fields are unused and should be set to 0. 
 
The return value is an SPX error code. 
 
Refer to Section 7.4.4 for an explanation of the sweep line and recommended 
settings. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetSource(UINT32 sourceId, UINT32 arg1, UINT32 arg2) 
 
This function provides a means of switching the source of radar video being 
scan converted by the server. Refer to the User Manual for the Radar Scan 
Conversion Server for details on the source options. In general terms, sourceID 
indicates a source identifier and arg1 and arg2 are arguments for that source. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::SetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterValue) 
int SPxScRemote::SetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
int parameterValue) 
int SPxScRemote::SetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
unsigned int parameterValue) 
int SPxScRemote::SetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
REAL32 parameterValue) 
 
These functions set a parameter on a remote object to a specified value. The 
remote scan converter can create named objects, which are then accessible to 
the remote API. Refer to the user documentation for the remote scan converter 
to see what named objects it exports. The parameterName is a parameter name 
for the object, and the parameterValue is either a string representation of the 
parameter value or an integer or real. 
 
 
int SPxScRemote::GetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
int *valuePtr) 
int SPxScRemote::GetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
unsigned int *valuePtr) 
int SPxScRemote::GetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *parameterName, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
REAL32 *valuePtr) 
int SPxScRemote::GetRemoteParameter(char *objectName,  
                                    char *parameterName, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *valueBuf, int bufLen) 
 
These functions retrieve the value of a named parameter on a named object. 
The value is returned through the provided pointer. For a string value, the 
caller provides a buffer in valueBuf and a length of the buffer in bytes. 
 


===== PAGE 303 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 303 
Refer to the user documentation for the remote scan converter to see what 
named objects it exports. The functions return an error code. 
 
7.12. Lat/Long View Control with SPxViewControl 
 
The SPxViewControl class may be used to implement lat/long based view control in a 
PPI window.  It provides features to simplify the implementation of view control on a 
moving platform, including support for true motion and platform relative display with 
both north-up and heading-up display orientation. 
 
An SPxNavData object may be connected to a SPxViewControl object and used to 
provide navigation data that defines the lat/long position of the platform together with 
speed, course and optionally heading information. 
 
Typically, an SPxViewControl object is used to automatically control the view 
displayed by a scan converter (SPxScSourceLocal or SPxPPIwindow).  In addition, the 
SPxViewControl object may be used to control the view used by certain SPx graphics 
rendering classes.  An application may also install a callback function to receive 
notification of changes to the view. 
 
7.12.1. 
SPxViewControl Class 
 
This section describes the public interface provided by the SPxViewControl class.  
Many of the functions make use of the following structure to define a point in terms of 
degrees latitude and longitude: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
 
double latDegs; 
/* Latitude in degrees. */ 
 
double longDegs; /* Longitude in degrees. */ 
 
} SPxLatLong_t; 
 
7.12.1.1 Creation and Destruction 
 
The SPxViewControl class provides the following constructor and destructor functions.  
 
SPxViewControl::SPxViewControl(SPxNavData *navData=NULL) 
 
Constructor used to create a new SPxViewControl object.  A separate 
SPxViewControl object should be created for each PPI window in an 
application.  If a PPI window will be used to display radar from a moving 
platform, then the handle of an SPxNavData object should be provided.  The 
same SPxNavData object may be shared between multiple SPxViewControl 
objects.  If the PPI window will be used to display radar from a static 
installation, then the navData argument may be omitted or set to NULL.  
 
 
SPxViewControl::~SPxViewControl(void) 
 
Destructor called when an SPxViewControl object is destroyed. 


===== PAGE 304 =====
Page 304 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
7.12.1.2 PPI Window Size 
 
An SPxViewControl object needs to know the size of the PPI window used to display 
radar video and graphics so that the scaling between real-world units and pixels can 
be calculated.  The following functions are used to provide this information. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetWindowSizeAuto(SPxScFollow *follow) 
 
If an SPxScFollow (or derived) object is used to manage the display of radar 
video, the same object may also be used by SPxViewControl to obtain the 
window size automatically.  If an SPxScFollow object is not available, then 
SetWindowSizePixels() must be called to set the current window size. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetWindowSizePixels 
                                          (unsigned int widthPixels,  
                                           unsigned int heightPixels) 
 
This function is used to notify SPxViewControl of changes to the size of a PPI 
window.  It should not be called if an SPxScFollow object has been installed 
using SetWindowSizeAuto(). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetWindowSizePixels 
                                         (unsigned int *widthPixels,  
                                          unsigned int *heightPixels) 
 
Get the current window size in pixels. 
 
7.12.1.3 View Centre and Size 
 
The following functions are used to configure the view using lat/long coordinates.  Any 
changes made to the view will be buffered internally until FlushChanges() is called. 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::SetViewCentreLatLong(const SPxLatLong_t *centreLL) 
 
This function is used to set the latitude and longitude of the view centre. 
 
Increasing the view centre latitude will move the view in a northerly direction; 
decreasing the view centre latitude will move the view in a southerly direction.  
Increasing the view centre longitude will move the view in an easterly direction; 
decreasing the view centre longitude will move the view in a westerly direction.   
 
The example in Figure 79 shows three north-referenced views. 


===== PAGE 305 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 305 
 
Figure 79 – View Centre Example 
 
In each view, the width is fixed at 0.05 degrees. In the left-hand image, the 
view centre is set to 53.58 degrees latitude and 0.11 degrees longitude, as 
shown by the red cross. 
 
In the centre image, the view centre latitude is increased to 53.59 degrees.  
The new view centre is shown by the blue cross. The view centre has moved 
north by 0.01 degrees. The previous view centre is shown by the red cross. 
In the right-hand image, the view centre longitude is increased to 0.13 
degrees. The new view centre is shown by the blue cross. The view centre has 
moved east by 0.02 degrees. The previous view centre is shown by the red 
cross. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewCentreLatLong(SPxLatLong_t *centreLL) 
 
Get the current view centre lat/long position. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewCentrePixels(int xPixels,  
                                                 int yPixels) 
 
This function is used to set the view centre by specifying a pixel position within 
the window. That latitude and longitude of the specified pixel position will 
become the new view centre. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewCentrePixels(int *xPixels,  
                                                 int *yPixels) const 
 
Get the pixel position of the current view centre. This will always be half the 
view width in pixels and half the view height in pixels. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewCentreMetres(double cXMetres,  
                                                 double cYMetres) 
 
 
Set the current view centre position in metres from the platform own ship 
position. The X and Y values are true north referenced (X is positive east and Y 
is positive north). 
 
 


===== PAGE 306 =====
Page 306 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewCentreMetres(double *cXMetres,  
                                                 double *cYMetres) 
 
Get the current view centre position in metres from the platform own ship 
position. The returned X and Y values are true north referenced (X is positive 
east and Y is positive north).   
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewCentreMetresDisplay(double cXMetres,  
                                                        double cYMetres) 
 
Set the current view centre position in metres from the platform own ship 
position. The X and Y values are display referenced (X is positive right and Y is 
positive up). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewCentreMetresDisplay(double *cXMetres,  
                                                        double *cYMetres)  
                                                        const 
 
Get the current view centre position in metres from the platform own ship 
position. The X and Y values display referenced (X is positive right and Y is 
positive up). 
 
  
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewAutoRecentreEnabled(int isEnabled) 
 
Enable or disable automatically re-centring the view on the primary platform 
position if it moves off-screen. By default, this feature is disabled. 
 
  
int SPxViewControl::IsViewAutoRecentreEnabled(void) const 
 
Query if automatic view re-centring is enabled or disabled. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewWidthDegs(double degs) 
 
This function is used to set the view width defined in degrees longitude.  
Increasing the view width will increase the visible coverage and zoom out from 
the view centre position. Decreasing the view width will decrease the visible 
coverage and zoom in to the view centre position. The view height is set 
automatically to maintain the correct aspect ratio in the PPI window. 
 
The example in Figure 80 shows three north-referenced views. 
 
 


===== PAGE 307 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 307 
 
Figure 80 – View Width Example 
 
In each view, the centre position is fixed. The view width in the left-hand image 
is 0.05 degrees. In the centre image, the view width is increased to 0.1 degrees 
and therefore the coverage is increased. In the right-hand image, the view 
width is decreased to 0.01 degrees and therefore the coverage is decreased. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewWidthDegs(double *degs) 
 
Get the current view width in degrees longitude. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewMinWidthDegs(double widthDegs) 
 
Set a minimum view width defined in degrees longitude. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl:GetViewMinWidthDegs(double *widthDegs) const 
 
Get the current minimum view width defined in degrees longitude. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewMaxWidthDegs(double widthDegs) 
 
Set a maximum view width defined in degrees longitude. A value of zero (the 
default) implies no limit on the view width. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl:GetViewMaxWidthDegs(double *widthDegs) const 
 
Get the current maximum view width defined in degrees longitude. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewWidthMetres(double widthMetres) 
 
This function is used to set the view width defined in metres. Increasing the 
view width will increase the visible coverage and zoom out from the view centre 
position. Decreasing the view width will decrease the visible coverage and zoom 
in to the view centre position. The view height is set automatically to maintain 
the correct aspect ratio in the PPI window.
