===== PAGE 308 =====
Page 308 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewWidthMetres(double *metres) 
 
Get the current view width in metres. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetViewWidthMode(ViewWidthMode_t mode) 
 
The SPxViewControl class internally maintains a view width in either degrees 
of longitude or metres. This width is kept constant as the view is panned. The 
following modes are supported: 
 
SPxViewControl::VIEW_WIDTH_MODE_CONST_DEGS (default) 
 
In this mode, the view width in degrees of longitude is kept constant as the 
view is panned. This will cause the view width in metres to change as the view 
centre shifts north or south. 
 
SPxViewControl::VIEW_WIDTH_MODE_CONST_METRES 
 
In this mode, the view width in metres is kept constant as the view is panned. 
This will cause the view width in degrees of longitude to change as the view 
centre shifts north or south. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewWidthMode(ViewWidthMode_t *mode) 
const 
 
Get the current view width mode. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetViewPixelsPerMetre(double *ppm) 
 
Get the current scaling between window pixels and metres, defined as pixels 
per metre. 
 
7.12.1.4 Panning and Zooming the View 
 
The SPxViewControl class provides some additional functions to assist with panning 
and zooming the view.  It is important to note that these functions are effectively 
wrappers around SetViewCentreLatLong() and SetViewWidthDegs().  Any changes 
made to the view by panning or zooming will modify the view centre position and/or 
the view width.  Any changes made to the view will be buffered internally until 
FlushChanges() is called. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PanView(double deltaLatDegs,  
                                     double deltaLongDegs) 
 
This function is used to pan the view by applying a delta to the current view 
centre lat/long.  A positive latitude delta will pan the view in a northerly 
direction by the specified number of degrees.  A positive longitude delta will pan 
the view in an easterly direction by the specified number of degrees. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 309 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 309 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PanViewPixels(int xDeltaPixels,  
                                           int yDeltaPixels) 
 
This function is used to pan the view in terms of window pixels.  A positive X 
delta will pan the view towards the right-hand side of the window by the 
specified number of pixels.  A positive Y delta will pan the view down towards 
the bottom of the window by the specified number of pixels.  Note that when 
panning the view centre position using pixel values, the direction of the view 
change in the window is the same, even if the display is not north referenced.  
Refer to section 7.12.1.7 for further details.   
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PanViewMetres(double xDeltaMetres,  
                                           double yDeltaMetres) 
 
This function is used to pan the view in terms of metres, north referenced. A 
positive X delta will pan the view in an easterly direction by the specified 
number of metres. A positive Y delta will pan the view in a northerly direction 
by the specified number of metres. For display referenced metres panning, see 
SPxViewControl::PanViewMetresDisplay(). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PanViewMetresDisplay(double xDeltaMetres,  
                                                  double yDeltaMetres) 
 
This function is used to pan the view in terms of metres, display referenced. A 
positive X delta will pan the view to the right by the specified number of 
metres. A positive Y delta will pan the view upwards by the specified number of 
metres. For north referenced metres panning, see 
SPxViewControl::PanViewMetres(). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::ZoomView(double factor) 
 
This function is used to zoom the view by applying a zoom factor to the current 
view width.  The current view width is divided by the zoom factor.  The applied 
zoom is therefore relative to the current view width.  A zoom factor of less than 
1.0 will zoom out.  A zoom factor greater than 1.0 will zoom in.  A zoom factor 
of 1.0 has no effect.  The zoom is applied relative to the centre of the window 
and therefore the view centre position remains unchanged. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::ZoomViewAtLatLong(double factor,  
                                  const SPxLatLong_t *latLong) 
 
This function provides support for offset zooming.  It works in the same way as 
ZoomView(), however a specified lat/long point is maintained as the zoom 
centre. 
The example in Figure 81 shows two north-referenced views. 
 


===== PAGE 310 =====
Page 310 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 81 - Offset Zoom Example 
 
A zoom factor of 4.0 is applied to the left-hand view, offset to the position 
indicated by the red cross.  The right-hand image shows the view after the 
offset zoom has been applied.  Note that the position of the red cross in the 
window remains the same.  The view centre position has been adjusted 
automatically to maintain the position of the zoom centre. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::ZoomViewAtPixel(double factor,  
                                             int xPixels, int yPixels) 
 
This function is similar to ZoomViewAtLatLong(), however the view centre is 
defined as a window position in pixels as opposed to a lat/long position.  The 
pixel position is referenced from the top-left corner of the window and may be 
off-screen.    
 
7.12.1.5 Platform Position 
 
The platform position functions should only be called if SPxViewControl is being used 
without navigation data (navData argument set to NULL in constructor).  With an 
SPxNavData object installed, the platform position is set automatically. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetPlatformLatLong(const  
                                               SPxLatLong_t *latLong) 
 
Sets the latitude and longitude of the platform own-ship position. This function 
is typically used to set the position of a static radar installation. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetPlatformLatLong(SPxLatLong_t  
                                                            *latLong) 
 
Get the latitude and longitude of the platform own-ship position. 
 
 


===== PAGE 311 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 311 
7.12.1.6 Radar Position 
 
When used in conjunction with an SPxNavData object to provide navigation data, the 
SPxNavData object may be configured to define an offset from the platform own-ship 
position to the position of each radar (see section 6.8.14.6).  The SPxViewControl 
class provides some convenience functions for retrieving the position of each radar on 
the platform. 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::GetRadarPositionMetres(double *xMetres,  
                                       double *yMetres, 
                                       unsigned int index=0) 
 
This function is used to obtain the position of a radar in metres relative to the 
platform own-ship position.  The returned X and Y values are true north 
referenced (X is positive east and Y is positive north).  Therefore if the radar 
offset is non-zero, the position in metres will change when the platform heading 
changes. 
 
The SPxNavData object supports up to 4 radars and therefore the index value 
may be set to a value between 0 and 3.  If the platform only supports a single 
radar then the index may be omitted or set to 0.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::GetRadarLatLong(SPxLatLong_t *latLong,  
                                unsigned int index=0) 
 
This function is used to obtain the position of a radar in degrees latitude and 
longitude.  If the radar offset is non-zero, then the radar position will change 
when the platform heading changes. 
 
The SPxNavData object supports up to 4 radars and therefore the index value 
may be set to a value between 0 and 3.  If the platform only supports a single 
radar, then the index may be omitted or set to 0.  
 
 
 


===== PAGE 312 =====
Page 312 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
7.12.1.7 Moving Platform Configuration 
 
The following functions are used to control the display of moving platform updates.  
This includes that way in which platform motion is displayed and the orientation of the 
display, either north-up or heading-up. See also the trails configuration option on the 
scan-converter (7.10.1.11). 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetDisplayMotionMode(MotionMode_t mode) 
 
SPxViewControl supports automatic updating of the platform position and 
heading from navigation data.  The way in which platform motion is displayed is 
controlled by this function. The following modes are supported: 
 
SPxViewControl::MOTION_MODE_RELATIVE (default) 
 
In this mode, the platform remains at a fixed location in a PPI window and the 
world moves around it.  SPxViewControl maintains the platform position in the 
window by periodically adjusting the view centre.  Where the PPI window 
features a chart, the characteristic of platform relative motion display is that 
the platform position remains fixed and the chart scrolls in the window. 
 
 
SPxViewControl::MOTION_MODE_TRUE 
 
In this mode, the platform moves about a PPI window, as do the position of 
platform relative features.  Radar returns from static targets appear in the 
same position.  Where the PPI window features a chart, the characteristic of 
true motion display is that, for the same view, the chart remains fixed and the 
platform moves across the chart. 
 
The effect of the motion mode is shown in the following two examples. 
 
 
Figure 82 - Platform Relative Motion Example 
 


===== PAGE 313 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 313 
 
Figure 83 - True Motion Example 
 
In both examples, the platform moves with a course of 90 degrees in an 
easterly direction. Each example shows three snapshots captured at the same 
three platform positions. In Figure 82, the display is configured for platform-
relative motion. In Figure 83, the display is configured for true motion. 
 
It should be noted that in the true motion example, the platform will eventually 
disappear from view.  An application may wish to re-centre the view on the 
platform position as the platform approaches the edge of the window. 
 
 
MotionMode_t SPxViewControl::GetDisplayMotionMode(void) 
 
Get the current display motion mode. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetDisplayRefMode(RefMode_t mode) 
 
SPxViewControl supports the automatic update of the platform position and 
heading from navigation data.  The orientation of the display is controlled by 
this function.  The following modes are supported: 
 
SPxViewControl::REF_MODE_NORTH 
 
The display in a PPI window is oriented with true north at the top of the 
window.  
 
 
SPxViewControl::REF_MODE_HEADING 
 
The display is oriented with the true platform heading at the top of the window.  
With a heading-referenced display the radar video and graphics, including the 
map, are rotated as the platform's heading changes. 
 
 
SPxViewControl::REF_MODE_COURSE 
 
The display is oriented with the true platform course at the top of the window.  
With a course-referenced display the radar video and graphics, including the 
map, are rotated as the platform’s course changes. 
 
The effect of the reference mode is shown in Figure 84.  In both images, the 
platform has a heading of 90 degrees and is moving in an easterly direction.  In 


===== PAGE 314 =====
Page 314 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
the left-hand image, the display is north referenced.  In the right-hand image, 
the display is heading referenced. 
 
 
Figure 84 - Display Reference Mode Example 
 
When panning a display that is not north referenced, a different result will be 
obtained depending on whether the change in the view centre position is 
defined in terms of lat/long or window pixels.  Using Figure 84 as an example, 
panning the view in a northerly direction by increasing the view centre latitude 
will move the view towards the top of the window in the north-up display and 
towards the left of the window in the heading-up display.  However, moving the 
view up by decreasing the view centre Y position in pixels will move the view 
centre towards the top of the window irrespective of the reference mode. 
 
 
RefMode_t SPxViewControl::GetDisplayRefMode(void) 
 
Get the current display reference mode. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::GetDisplayRotationDegs(double *degs) 
 
This function may be used to retrieve the current display rotation in degrees 
(positive clockwise).  If the display reference mode is set to north-up, then the 
display rotation will always be zero.  If the display reference mode is set to 
heading-up or course-up then the display rotation will depend on the platform 
heading or course.

Heading

SPxLib Programming Manual

7.12.1.8 Conversion Functions 
 
The following functions are used to convert between lat/long, metres and pixels.  It is 
important to note that all values in metres are referenced from the platform own-ship 
position.  X in metres is positive to the east and Y in metres is positive to the north.  
The platform own-ship position in metres is always (0, 0). 
 
Values in pixels represent window pixels referenced from the top-left corner of the 
window.  X in pixels increases towards the right-hand side of the window.  Y in pixels 
increases towards the bottom of the window.  The top-left corner of the window is (0, 
0). 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::MetresToLatLong(double xMetres,  
                                             double yMetres,  
 
                                             SPxLatLong_t *latLong) 
 
Convert a position in metres relative to the platform own-ship position to a 
lat/long position.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::LatLongToMetres(const  
                                             SPxLatLong_t *latLong, 
                                             double *xMetres,  
                                             double *yMetres) 
 
Convert a lat/long position to a value in metres relative to the platform own-
ship position. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::MetresToPixels(double xMetres,  
                                            double yMetres,  
                                            int *xPixels,  
                                            int *yPixels) 
 
Convert a position in metres relative to the platform own-ship position to a 
pixel position in the window.  Note that the pixel position may be off-screen. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PixelsToMetres(int xPixels,  
                                            int yPixels, 
                                            double *xMetres,  
                                            double *yMetres) 
 
Convert a pixel position in a window to a position in metres relative to the 
platform own-ship position.  The pixel position may be off-screen. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::NorthToDisplay(double xNR, double yNR, 
                                            double *xDR, double *yDR) 
 
Convert a true north referenced position in either metres or pixels to a display 
referenced position. 
 
For example, if the display is in heading-up mode and the platform has a 
heading of 90 degrees, then a north referenced position of (10, 10) will be 


===== PAGE 316 =====
Page 316 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
converted to a display referenced position of (-10, 10).  A rotation of -90 
degrees is applied about the platform own-ship position. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::DisplayToNorth(double xDR,  
                                            double yDR, 
                                            double *xNR,  
                                            double *yNR); 
 
Convert a display referenced position in either metres or pixels to a true north 
referenced position. 
 
For example, if the display is in heading-up mode and the platform has a 
heading of 90 degrees then a display referenced position of (10, 10) will be 
converted to a north referenced position of (10, -10).  A rotation of 90 degrees 
is applied about the platform own-ship position. 
 
7.12.1.9 View Update Control 
 
The following functions are used to control the notification of view changes to any 
connected objects such as scan converters and graphics renderers.  An application 
must call FlushChanges() periodically (usually from a timer) to apply any pending 
view changes.  Typically, it will then call GetChangeFlags(TRUE) to determine if 
anything has changed and perform any actions required, such as rescaling and 
redrawing custom graphics.  More advanced applications may wish to control the 
frequency of view change updates using the provided threshold and buffer interval 
functions. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::FlushChanges(void) 
 
An application must periodically call this function to flush changes to the view 
made by the application and notify any connected objects (e.g. scan converters 
and graphics renderers) of changes to the view.  Typically this function is called 
periodically from a timer. 
 
The use of this function enables an application to control how often changes are 
applied to connected objects and enables updates to be buffered and 
synchronised.  This is particularly useful if view changes and display updates 
are performed in different threads.  
 
On calling this function, SPxViewControl object will only notify connected 
objects of a view change and set change flags if the change exceeds the 
thresholds set by SetUpdateThreshPixels() and/or SetUpdateThreshDegs(), 
and the time since the last notification exceeds the buffer interval set by 
SetBufferIntervalMs().  
 
In addition to flushing any changes made by the application, this function will 
also call PollNavData() to check for platform updates if an SPxNavData object 
is installed and call PollWindowSize() to check for changes to the window size 
if an SPxScFollow object is installed. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 317 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 317 
UINT32 SPxViewControl::GetChangeFlags(int clearFlags=FALSE) 
 
An application may call this function to determine which aspects of the view (if 
any) have changed.  Setting clearFlags to TRUE will clear the current change 
flags to zero.  Please note that change flags will only be set once buffered 
changes to the view have been flushed by calling FlushChanges(). 
For many applications, the presence of any flags is sufficient to indicate that the 
view has changed and, for example, a graphics update is required.  More 
advanced applications may wish to check for the presence of individual flags 
and process the associated changes accordingly. 
 
This function should not be used to check for changes inside a notification 
callback function as described in section 7.12.1.10.  Instead, use the change 
flags passed to the callback function. 
 
The following change flags are supported: 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_VIEW_CENTRE_LATLONG 
 
The view centre lat/long has changed. 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_VIEW_CENTRE_METRES 
 
The view centre position in the PPI window defined in metres from the platform 
has changed. As the view centre in metres is referenced from the platform own-
ship position, a change in platform position may cause a change to the view 
centre defined in metres but not to the view centre defined in lat/long. 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_VIEW_SCALE 
 
The view scale (width) has changed. 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_DISPLAY_ROTATION 
 
The display rotation has changed. 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_RADAR 
 
The position and/or rotation of any radar on the platform has changed. 
 
 
SPX_VC_CHANGE_REQUEST_REDRAW 
 
This flag may be set by a connected object to request a redraw of the display 
even though the view and other parameters may be unchanged.  See 
RequestRedraw(). 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 318 =====
Page 318 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPX_VC_USER_ACTION 
 
This flag is set in conjunction with other change flags if the change to the view 
or radar position was a result of a user action as opposed to an automatic 
moving platform update based on received navigation data.   
 
 
void SPxViewControl::ClearChangeFlags(void) 
 
Clear the current change flags to zero. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetUpdateThreshPixels(unsigned int pixels) 
 
This function defines the number of pixels by which either the view or platform 
position must change before a change is reported.  The default is 1 pixel. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxViewControl::GetUpdateThreshPixels(void) 
 
Get the current update threshold in pixels. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetUpdateThreshDegs(double degs) 
 
This function defines the number of degrees by which either the display 
orientation or platform heading must change before a change is reported.  The 
default is 0.1 degrees. 
 
 
double SPxViewControl::GetUpdateThreshDegs(void) 
 
Get the current update threshold in degrees.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::SetBufferIntervalMs(UINT32 ms) 
 
Set the period over which updates are buffered before a change is reported.  
The buffer period, ms,  is defined in milliseconds.  The default is 0 and therefore 
the buffer period is defined solely by the frequency at which FlushChanges() is 
invoked. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxViewControl::GetBufferIntervalMs(void) 
 
Get the current buffer period in milliseconds.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PollNavData(void) 
 
Poll the SPxNavData object to obtain the latest navigation information (if 
installed).  This function is called automatically by FlushChanges() and does 
not normally need to be invoked by an application. 
 
 


===== PAGE 319 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 319 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::PollWindowSize(void) 
 
Poll the SPxScFollow object to obtain the latest window size.  This function is 
called automatically by FlushChanges() and does not normally need to be 
invoked by an application. 
 
7.12.1.10 
View Update Notification 
 
The functions in this section are provided to enable an advanced application to install 
a callback that will provide notification of changes to the view.  The callback function 
must match the following function pointer type: 
 
 
typedef int (* viewControlCallback)(void *viewCtrlObj, 
                                    void *contextPtr, 
                                    void *changeFlagsPtr) 
 
The viewCtrlObj argument is the handle of the SPxViewControl object that 
invoked the callback.  This argument should be cast to a pointer to 
SPxViewControl. 
 
The contextPtr argument is a pointer to a read-only context structure 
provided by the SPxViewControl object.  This structure contains the following 
fields: 
 
 
typedef struct _CallbackInfo_t 
     { 
 
 
_CallbackInfo_t *next; 
 /* Reserved for internal use. */ 
         
void *targetObj;         /* The object to control. */ 
         
unsigned int radarIndex; /* Assigned radar index. */ 
     
     } CallbackInfo_t; 
 
The contextPtr argument should be cast to a pointer to CallbackInfo_t.  The 
context structure provides access to the additional data defined when the 
callback function was installed by AddUpdateCallback(). 
 
The changeFlagsPtr argument defines the change flags associated with an 
update.  This argument should be cast to a UINT32 (not a pointer to UINT32 – 
this argument is passed as a void pointer for compatibility reasons).  Details of 
the change flags can be found by referring to GetChangeFlags().  Please note, 
however, that GetChangeFlags() must not be invoked from within a callback 
function. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::AddUpdateCallback(SPxCallbackListFn_t fn,  
                                  void *targetObj, 
                                  unsigned int radarIndex=0) 
 
This function is used to install an update callback function into an 
SPxViewControl object.  The callback will be invoked if any changes are 
pending when the application calls FlushChanges().  The targetObj argument 
is user defined.  It is typically used to pass the handle of the object that is 


===== PAGE 320 =====
Page 320 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
being updated.  The radar index is a value between 0 and 3 and is optionally 
used to specify the index of a radar on the platform. 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxViewControl::RemoveUpdateCallback(SPxCallbackListFn_t fn,  
                                     void *targetObj, 
                                     unsigned int radarIndex=0) 
 
This function may be used to uninstall a callback function. The targetObj and 
radarIndex arguments must match the values set when the function was 
installed otherwise an error will occur. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxViewControl::RequestRedraw(void) 
 
This function is provided to enable an application or connected object to request 
a redraw/update of the PPI window.  A redraw may be required if, for example, 
the graphics displayed by a graphics renderering object have been enabled or 
disabled.  Calling this function will assert the SPX_VC_CHANGE_REQUEST_REDRAW 
change flag. An application should check for this flag and redraw graphics 
accordingly.

7.12.2. 
SPxViewControl Example 
 
The follow code extract shows how the SPxViewControl class may be used to provide 
a simple lat/long based interface to an SPxScSourceLocal scan converter object in a 
Windows application. The creation of the RIB, PIM and source objects and all error 
handling has been omitted. Full source code and be found in the SPxWinLatLongPPI 
example program. In addition, the SPxX11LatLongPPI example program shows a 
similar example for Linux applications. 
 
BOOL MyDialog::OnInitDialog(void) 
{ 
    /* The PPI underlay window (typically a child of the dialog). */ 
    CWnd *m_ulayWin; 
 
 
. . . 
 
    /* Create the window used to display radar video. */ 
    m_spxDispWin = new SPxScDestDisplayWin(); 
    m_spxDispWin->Create(0, 1024, 1024, m_ulayWin); 
 
 
. . . 
 
 
/* Create the scan converter. */ 
    m_spxSc = new SPxScSourceLocal(m_spxDispWin); 
 
    /* Create the scan conversion process. */ 
    m_spxScProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv, NULL, 
 
 
                         
 m_spxPim, m_spxSc); 
 
    /* Add the radar window to a follow object so that the  
     * radar window tracks the size, position and stacking order 
     * of the underlay window. 
     */ 
    m_winFollow = new SPxScFollowWin(NULL, m_ulayWin); 
    m_winFollow->AddSC(m_spxSc); 
 
 
. . . 
    /* Create a SPxViewControl object that will provide a  
     * lat/long based view control interface for the radar video 


===== PAGE 323 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 323 
     * and graphics displayed in the PPI window. 
     */ 
    m_viewCtrl = new SPxViewControl(); 
 
    /* Connect the view control to the follow object. 
     * This will be used by the view control to track any 
     * changes to the window size. 
     */ 
    m_viewCtrl->SetWindowSizeAuto(m_winFollow); 
 
 
 
/* Connect scan converter to view control. The view control 
     * object will update the scan converter so that the  
     * displayed radar video automatically matches the current view. 
     */ 
    m_spxSc->SetViewControl(m_viewCtrl); 
 
    /* Set the static platform position to Thames Estuary, UK. */ 
    SPxLatLong_t platformLL = {51.533485, 0.805722}; 
    m_viewCtrl->SetPlatformLatLong(&platformLL); 
 
    /* Set the default view to be centred on the platform. */ 
    SPxLatLong_t viewCentreLL = platformLL; 
    m_viewCtrl->SetViewCentreLatLong(&viewCentreLL); 
    m_viewCtrl->SetViewWidthDegs(0.5); 
 
    . . . 
 
} /* MyDialog::OnInitDialog() */ 
 
 
void MyDialog::OnTimer(UINT_PTR nIDEvent) 
{ 
    /* Flush any pending view changes. */ 
    m_viewCtrl->FlushChanges(); 
 
    /* Get and clear view control change flags. The change flags will  
     * show what has been updated since this function was last 
     * called. It is used to determine if there are any changes  
     * to the view that requires PPI graphics to be redrawn. 
     */ 
    UINT32 changeFlags = m_viewCtrl->GetChangeFlags(TRUE); 
 
    /* Any change flags set? */ 
    if (changeFlags != 0) 
    { 
 
/*  
       * Yes, so redraw PPI graphics at the new scale.  
 
 */ 
    } 
 
} /* MyDialog::OnTimer()*/

SPxLib Programming Manual

8. 
Scan Conversion Display 
 
The class family SPxScDest provides a processing capability for the scan conversion 
performed by an instance of SPxScSource. Whereas the SPxScSourceLocal class 
processes data without knowledge of the graphics environment for the final display, 
SPxScDest handles the display-specific aspects of converting the scan converted data 
onto the display.  
 
In most situations the programmer will not need to work with the interface to 
SPxScDest and its sub-classes. An instance of this class is attached to the scan 
converter class and thereafter it is the scan converter that manipulates the display 
class. 
 
There are a number of sub-classes of SPxScDest.  
 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWin provides the functions to display radar video with a 
Microsoft Windows MFC application. As Windows provides high-performance 
compositing functions as standard, this class is almost always the preferred 
solution for display of radar video under Windows. 
 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw provides the same functions as 
SPxScDestDisplayWin, but without requiring MFC. 
 
 
SPxScDestBitmap provides a generic graphics-independent capability to 
receive radar bitmaps. This class makes no assumptions about any graphics 
environment. It provides data is a simple bitmap format that may be 
manipulated by the application software. This may be used to interface to an 
existing display application, for example to support application compositing, 
or to a non-standard graphics display library. The class may be used to put 
data into a graphics-dependent bitmap, such as a Windows HBITMAP or an 
X11 Pixmap. 
 
 
SPxScDestTexture is an extension of the SPxScDestBitmap class and makes 
the scan converted radar video available in an OpenGL texture that can be 
used in both Windows and X11 applications.   
 
 
SPxScDestNet provides a mechanism to move scan converted video from 
one system to another across a network. The network interface is 
encapsulated by SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet. On the other size of 
the network, SPxScSourceNet provides data to another SPxScDest subclass. 
 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 provides the functions to display radar video inside an 
X Window using Window Manager Compositing. The class creates its own X 
Window and relies on a compositing window manager (such as Compiz) to 
combine the radar video with underlay and overlay graphics. 
 


===== PAGE 326 =====
Page 326 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
SPxScDestBitmapD2D is an extension of the SPxScDestBitmap class and 
makes the scan converted radar video in a Direct2D bitmap that can be used 
in Windows applications (MFC or non-MFC). 
 
In addition to these classes, for X Windows radar display the class SPxPPIwindow 
provides the functions to display radar. This class supports Application Window and 
Application Pixmap methods of compositing and is described in Section 8.4.7. 
 
8.1. Using SPxScDestBitmap 
 
The class SPxScDestBitmap provides a simple, graphics-independent interface to 
receive scan converted radar bitmaps from a remote or local scan converter. 
 
The class makes no assumptions about the graphics environment, if indeed there is 
one, and provides data in a simple bitmap format that may be further manipulated by 
the application software. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmap class is used as the basis of Application Window and Application 
Pixmap compositing under X11. For window manager compositing under X11 use 
SPxScDestDisplayX11. For most situations in Windows, the SPxScDestDisplayWin 
(MFC) or SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw (non MFC) class is recommended. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmap class receives data from either a scan converter running on the 
same machine, using SPxScSourceLocal, or else from a scan converter running on a 
remote machine using the class SPxScSourceNet. In the case of the network 
interface, the combination of SPxScDestBitmap and SPxScSourceNet provides all the 
functions to connect to the remote scan converter, decode the network messages, 
update the local bitmap and provide timely notifications to the application software. 
 
8.1.1. 
Accessing data from SPxScDestBitmap 
 
The bitmap memory associated with SPxScDestBitmap is updated by the scan 
converter that it is connected to. A client can read the bitmap by one of two methods. 
 
The synchronous method is to register a function that SPxScDestBitmap class will call 
when it has received a logical group of updates (for example 1 sector of data). Call 
SetNotifyUpdate on the instance of SPxScDestBitmap to register the function. The 
function will get called at rates of the scan conversion update – nominally every 20 
ms. The implementation of the user’s notification function can decide whether to 
process the bitmap data on each update or less frequently. The user’s function gets 
called in the thread of the scan converter. 
 
The asynchronous method is to have another thread reading the SPxScDestBitmap 
instance at regular intervals, for example within a timer loop. Under this timer, the 
client can simply read the content of the bitmap. Even though this reading of the 
bitmap is not synchronised with the scan converter’s updates to the bitmap (from a 
different thread), the results obtained are generally acceptable (it is possible that the 
data is read out mid-way through a sector update. This would be immediately 
corrected for on the next update, so the worst-case situation is that some data in a 
sector would be handled one update period after the rest – it would hardly be 
noticeable). 


===== PAGE 327 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 327 
The function ReadAndClearDirtyBox() can be used to read the geometry of the 
bitmap data that has changed since the last update. The ‘DirtyBox’ is a rectangular 
area within the bitmap that contains changed radar data. Reading the geometry of 
this box and handling only that area of data can substantially reduce the amount of 
processing needed. 
 
8.1.2. 
SPxScDestBitmap Class Definition 
 
8.1.2.1 Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestBitmap::SPxScDestBitmap(void) 
 
Constructs a new SPxScDestBitmap object. Call Create() on the new object to 
initialise it. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::Create(UINT16 maxScreenW, UINT16 maxScreenH, 
 
 
 
 
 
 int bitmapType = SPX_BITMAP_8BITS, 
 
 
 
 
 
 unsigned char *bitmapMemory = 0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 int bitmapStride = 0) 
 
Initialise a new SPxScDestBitmap object. The maxScreenW and maxScreenH 
parameters are the maximum dimensions of the window. The value of 
bitmapType can be either SPX_BITMAP_8BITS or SPX_BITMAP_32BITS. The video 
data delivered from the scan converter is normally 8 bits per pixel (options are 
under development for expanded modes, including 16 bits per pixel). Creating a 
bitmap of type SPX_BITMAP_8BITS means that the local bitmap is a copy of the 
bitmap data generated by the scan converter. Significantly, it contains only 
intensity information with no colour. For a bitmap type of SPX_BITMAP_32BITS, 
the incoming 8-bit values are converted into a 32-bit ARGB colour using the 
currently set radar colour or LUT. See functions SetRadarColour(), 
SetRadarBright() and SetRadarLUT() for functions that control the 8-to-32-
bit conversion. 
 
If bitmapMemory is NULL, the Create function allocates memory for the bitmap. 
The size of memory allocated is maxScreenW x maxScreenH x depth bytes, 
where depth = 1 for a bitmap type of SPX_BITMAP_8BITS and 4 for a bitmap 
type of SPX_BITMAP_32BITS. 
 
If bitmapMemory is provided as non-NULL, i.e. the memory is pre-allocated by 
the caller, then the bitmapStride must be provided as the width of one line of 
the bitmap in bytes. In most situations, the stride is maxScreenW x depth, 
where depth = 1 for a bitmap type of SPX_BITMAP_8BITS and 4 for a bitmap 
type of SPX_BITMAP_32BITS. In this situation, the bitmapStride can be 
omitted. 
 
If it is desired to write data into a Windows HBITMAP, then the memory for that 
bitmap may should provided as bitmapMemory. This is further explained in 
Section 8.1.6. 
 


===== PAGE 328 =====
Page 328 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.1.2.2 Destruction 
 
SPxScDestBitmap::~SPxScDestBitmap(void) 
 
Delete a SPxScDestBitmap. 
 
8.1.2.3 Notification Update 
 
static void SPxScDestBitmap::SetNotifyUpdate(void (*fn)( 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxScDestBitmap* bitmap,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 changes,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 firstAzimuth,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 lastAzimuth, DirtyBox box))  
 
Sets a notification procedure to be called when the class receives updates to 
the radar video. The function is called at the update rate of scan converted 
data, which is nominally 20ms, and execution occurs in the thread of the scan 
converter object (i.e. the thread of the RIB providing the input source). The 
function is static meaning that it can be called without reference to a class 
instance. The notification handler is called with: 
 
bitmap - an instance of a SPxScDestBitmap 
 
changes - a word that indicates what, if any, attributes of the radar window 
have changed since the last update. The word is a bitwise or of the following 
flags: 
 
SPX_BITMAP_CHANGE_POSITION  - the window has changed position 
SPX_BITMAP_CHANGE_SIZE – the window has changed size 
SPX_BITMAP_CHANGE_VISIBLE – the window’s visibilty has changed 
SPX_BITMAP_CHANGE_VIEW – the view has changed. 
 
firstAzimuth – the position of the radar antenna at the start of the update (the 
azimuth value is in the range 0..65536 to representing 0 to 360 degrees). 
 
lastAzimuth – the position of the radar antenna at the end of the update. 
 
box – A structure of type DirtyBox having fields x, y, w and h that define the x, 
y position and width and height of the minimum enclosing area of the bitmap 
that has recently changed. The dirty box should be cleared by calling 
ClearDirtyBox() on the bitmap object when the data is processed. If it isn’t 
cleared then the next call of the notification handler will update the dirty box to 
include any additional area updated.  
 
 


===== PAGE 329 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 329 
void SPxScDestBitmap::SetUpdateCallback( void (*fn)( 
    
 
 
 
 
 
 
 SPxScDestBitmap* bitmap, 
      
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT16 changes, 
      
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT16 firstAzimuth, 
      
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT16 lastAzimuth,  
      
 
 
 
 
 
 DirtyBox box, 
      
 
 
 
 
 
 void *userPtr) 
      
 
 
 
 
Set a notification handler function to be called when an SPxScDestBitmap 
object receives updates to the radar video. The handler is called at the update 
rate of scan converted data, which is nominally 20ms, and execution occurs in 
the thread of the scan converter object (i.e. the thread of the RIB providing the 
input source). This handler differs from the static handler installed by 
SetNotifyUpdate(). This is a per-object update handler - a unique handler 
may be installed for each SPxScDestBitmap object. In addition, this handler 
supports the use of a user pointer to pass a user-defined argument to the 
handler. The handler is called with the same core arguments as the static 
version, with the addition of: 
 
userPtr - User argument set when the handler is installed, and passed to the 
handler function when it is invoked. 
 
 
8.1.2.4 Fading 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::FadeBitmap(int *fadesDone = 0) 
 
The contents of the bitmap, which can be an 8-bit or a 32-bit type, are faded by 
replacing each pixel value by one that is smaller. This function is only relevant  
for real-time fading of the bitmap. The reduction in intensity value is dependent 
on the programmed fade interval. Call SetMinFadeInterval() to tell the class 
the minimum period between fades. 
 
This function does nothing if the current fade type is not real time. 
 
This function may be called as frequently as desired. The decision whether or 
not to actually perform a fade is taken by the function itself using an internal 
timer. (Note that this is a change in V1.19 of the software. Prior to this version, 
the caller of this function was responsible for calling this function at the correct 
frequency.) 
 
If fadesDone is provided as a non-null pointer, the number of fades performed  
by the class is returned through the pointer.  
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetFadeType(void) 
 
Return the current fade type. The scan converter implements replacement or 
sweep-based fading itself and delivers pre-faded data. For real-time fade, the 
scan converter delivers new data which is combined with existing data in the 
bitmap using highest-wins combination. If real-time fading is desired, the 
contents of the bitmap must be faded by reading the data, subtracting a fade 
level and writing back the answer (or writing back zero if the fade value would 


===== PAGE 330 =====
Page 330 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
take it below zero). The SPxScDestBitmap class provides the FadeBitmap() 
function to implement the pixel fading on 32-bit bitmaps. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetFadeInterval(void) 
 
Returns the current fade interval in milliseconds. This is the time between fade 
intervals to ensure the bitmap is faded at the correct rate (set using SetFade on 
the scan converter). The actual value calculated for the fade interval will be 
dependent on the requested fade time and the provided minimum fade interval 
(set through SetMinFadeInterval). The calculated fade interval will not be less 
than the value set from SetMinFadeInterval. The client must call FadeBitmap() 
at the rate implied by the value returned from this function. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::SetMinFadeInterval(int intervalMS) 
 
Tells the bitmap object how often the display is being faded (real-time fade 
only) so it may compute the correct level of fading. The provided argument,  
intervalMS, is the update interval in milliseconds. Suppose for example the 
bitmap is required to fade from peak to background in 2 seconds. This would be 
requested with a call to SetFade on the scan converter object. Since there are 
256 levels in the image, fading in 2 seconds requires 128 updates per second, 
or one update every 7.8ms. Now, it is very unlikely that the bitmap is being 
faded at that rate. The actual rate of fade of the bitmap is reported to the class 
with SetMinFadeInterval(). If this rate was 100ms, for example, then the 
class would decide that the number of levels to fade on each update would be 
12.8, meaning that the full fade occurs in the requested 2 seconds. Note that 
the consequence of setting the fade update rate to be larger than the preferred 
value is that the step size of the fading is increased (fewer fade levels). If this 
function is not called, a value of 100ms is assumed as a default. The client is 
responsible for actually fading the bitmap using FadeBitmap() and the interval 
between successive calls of that function is found by calling 
GetFadeInterval() in the bitmap. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetMinFadeInterval(void) 
 
Returns the minimum fade interval in milliseconds.  
 
8.1.2.5 Data Retrieval 
 
unsigned char * SPxScDestBitmap::GetBitmap(void) 
 
Get the start address of the bitmap that the class is managing. The bitmap’s 
width (the stride) is found from GetWidth(). Note that the width of the bitmap 
does not change, but the size of the window may change. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetWidth(void) 
 
Returns the bitmap width as defined in the Create() function. The scan 
converter will update a portion of the bitmap starting at (0,0) to correspond 


===== PAGE 331 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 331 
with the window size. Use the GetW() member function to get the current size 
being updated. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetShowState(void) 
 
Return the current show state of the radar video. The value is 0 if radar is not 
visible and 1 if it is visible. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetHeight(void) 
 
Returns the bitmap height as defined in the Create() function. The scan 
converter will update a portion of the bitmap starting at (0,0) to correspond 
with the window size. Use the GetH() member function to get the current size 
being updated. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetView(REAL32 *vx, REAL32 *vy,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
     REAL32 *vw, REAL32 *vh)  
 
Get the current view of the radar video represented in the bitmap. The view 
returned here is the one reported in the most recent SetView command sent to 
the scan converter. If the view has not changed since the last call of the 
function, the return value is 0, otherwise it is 1. Irrespective of the view 
change, the geometry is always returned through the input pointers, vx, vy, vw, 
vh. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetW(void) 
 
Returns the current width of the radar window being maintained in the bitmap. 
Note that the bitmap width is found from GetWidth() and this is fixed after the 
bitmap has been created. The actively updated data always starts at (0,0) in 
the bitmap and extends to the limit of the window size, clipped by the size of 
the bitmap. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetH(void) 
 
Returns the current height of the radar window being maintained in the bitmap. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 332 =====
Page 332 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.1.2.6 
Heading-Up or Course-Up with True Trails 
 
When the radar video is displayed heading or course-up with true trails, the radar 
video bitmap must be rotated and translated by the user of this class before display. 
To determine if this rotation and translation is required, check if the moving platform 
reference mode is set to SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH. When rotation 
and translation is required, the necessary transformation may be queried using the 
GetDisplayMatrix() function. When in this mode, the width and height of the area of 
the bitmap to display is specified by GetDisplayW() and GetDisplayH() rather than 
the usual GetW() and GetH() functions. 
 
/* Normal area of bitmap to render. */ 
int w = bitmap->GetW(); 
int h = bitmap->GetH(); 
 
/* Check if rotation and translation is required. */ 
if(bitmap->GetMovingPlatformRefMode() 
== SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH) 
{ 
 
/* Get the required 3 by 3 transformation matrix. */ 
 
float matrix[9]; 
 
bitmap->GetDisplayMatrix(matrix); 
 
 
/* Apply matrix to rendering of bitmap here. */ 
 
 
 
/* The area of the bitmap to render is larger 
  
 * when performing a transformation. 
 
 */ 
 
w = MIN(bitmap->GetDisplayW(), bitmap->GetWidth()); 
 
h = MIN(bitmap->GetDisplayH(), bitmap->GetHeight()); 
} 
 
/* Render w by h pixels from the corner of the bitmap here. */ 
 
 
 
SPxScMpRefMode_t 
SPxScDestBitmap::GetMovingPlatformRefMode(void) const 
 
Query the current moving platform reference mode. If this mode is 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_NORMAL then the bitmap can be rendered as normal. If 
this mode is set to SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH then the bitmap 
must be transformed by the matrix returned by GetDisplayMatrix() so that it 
is correctly positioned. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetDisplayW(void) 
int SPxScDestBitmap::GetDisplayH(void) 
 
Get the width and height of the area of the bitmap to render, when in 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH moving platform reference mode. 
 
 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::GetDisplayMatrix(float (&matrix)[9]) 
 
Get the 3 by 3 transformation matrix that must be applied while rendering the 
bitmap to correctly position it within the window when in 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH moving platform reference mode. 


===== PAGE 333 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 333 
8.1.2.7 Colour, Brightness and Emphasis 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::SetRadarEmphasis(double emphasis) 
 
This function sets the emphasis of the radar video in the range 0.0 to 1.0. The 
default is 1.0. The value of the emphasis is used in the calculation of the radar’s 
alpha value, which is significant when the radar is combined with other 
components. A value of 1.0 means that increasingly brighter radar causes a 
corresponding reduction in the brightness of the underlay for each pixel – 
effectively the radar is emphasised over the underlay. A value of 0.0 means 
that there is no emphasis and the combination of radar and underlay is 
effectively a summation. 
 
 
double SPxScDestBitmap::GetRadarEmphasis(void) 
 
Returns the current value of the radar emphasis, which is in the interval 0.0 to 
1.0. 
 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid,  
UCHAR red,  
 UCHAR green, 
 UCHAR blue) 
 
This function is called to set the radar colour. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The radar colour is given by the red, green and blue components, 
each in the range 0..255. The specified colour represents the colour of 
maximum intensity radar with brightness = 100 (see SetRadarBright). Note 
that this function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. The function has no effect if the 
bitmap depth is 8 bits. 
 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR bright) 
 
This function sets the radar brightness. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The brightness is in the range 0 to 100. Note that this function is 
called automatically by the scan converter and should not normally be called 
from a client application. 
 
 
UCHAR SPxScDestBitmap::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness value. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 334 =====
Page 334 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxScDestBitmap::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR lutId, unsigned char *lutData) 
 
Set the look-up table that converts radar pixel values into display colours for a 
32-bit bitmap. This function is complementary to SetRadarColour and 
SetRadarBright. Use SetRadarLUT to define an arbitrary mapping from 8-bit 
radar intensity values to colours (could be false-colour for example). To set the 
radar colour to a linear scale from background to peak use the SetRadarColour 
and SetRadarBright functions. The lutID argument define a LUT identifier and 
is currently unused – set to 0. The lutData is an array of ARGB colour values of 
size 256*4 = 1024 bytes. The data is organised as an array of 256 x 32-bit 
words, with each word holding a colour value in the format alpha (most 
significant 8-bits), red, green, blue (least significant 8 bits). Note that this 
function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not normally 
be called from a client application. 
 
8.1.2.8 The Dirty Box 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::LockReadAndClearDirtyBox 
                                      (int *x, int *y, int *w, int*h) 
 
This function returns the geometry of the current dirty box associated with 
recent radar updates. Calling this function causes the current geometry of the 
dirty box to be returned in the x, y, w and h arguments. The dirty box is then 
cleared. 
 
 
void SPxScDestBitmap::ClearDirtyBox(void) 
 
This function clears the dirty box that defines the recent area of updated radar 
video. Call this function in the notification handler when the contents of the 
dirty box have been processed.

interface, for example from a remote application. The supported operations are:

8.1.3. 
Using SPxScDestBitmap with SPxScSourceLocal 
 
If the scan converter is running on the same machine as the display, the 
SPxScSourceLocal class can use the SPxScDestBitmap class as its output. An 
example of this configuration is shown in Figure 85. 
 
Figure 85 - Use of SPxScSourceLocal with SPxScDestBitmap class 
 
The SPxScSourceLocal scan converter is provided with data from a source, RIB, PIM 
and process chain in the normal way. 
 
The output of the scan converter is connected to the SPxScDestBitmap class. Scan 
converted video is passed to the SPxScDestBitmap and stored in the 8-bit or 32-bit 
bitmap that it manages. 
 
The application makes calls to the SPxScSourceLocal object to control the scan 
conversion, including changing views, window size etc. 
 
In the example of Figure 85, the application will composite the underlay, overlay and 
radar bitmaps into a single bitmap that will be used to update the screen. The 
application reads data from the bitmap of the SPxScDestBitmap class to get the scan 
converted radar video (this data may be 8-bit or 32-bit).

SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 337 
In a simple configuration, the update of the SPxScDestBitmap by the scan converter 
may not be synchronised to the reading of the bitmap by the application. The bitmap 
is written by one and read by the other. However, the bitmap class provides a 
notification option through the member function SetNotifyUpdate(), which allows a 
user-defined function to be called at significant update points. These updates occur at 
a nominal rate of 20ms and are synchronised with the update of sectors in the bitmap. 
The notification function registered with SetNotifyUpdate() may signal an action in 
the application processing. Note that the notification function runs in the thread of the 
scan converter (same as the thread of the RIB, PIM and processing chain that proceed 
it). The dashed boxes in Figure 85 show the threads for this example. 
 
8.1.4. 
Example use of SPxScDestBitmap with a local scan converter using 
SPxScSourceLocal 
 
The following sample code shows the use of SPxScDestBitmap to receive scan 
converted radar video from a local scan converter. 
 
The sample code uses a SPxTestGenerator as a source of radar video. This source is 
connected to a RIB, a PIM and to a scan conversion process. The output of the scan 
conversion is the bitmap class. The function SetNotifyUpdate is used on the bitmap 
to register a function to be called when a new sector of radar video has been scan 
converted. The sample code is easily changed to replace the TestGenerator with 
another source object (for example, to receive video from a network or a hardware 
card). 
 
The main loop of this example does nothing. The scan conversion occurs in a separate 
thread created and managed by the RIB, and the supplied notification function is 
called in the context of that thread. 
 
The code, which is supplied in an expanded version in the SPx Development release as 
SPxExamples/SPxBitmap,  is written for Linux, although the changes for Windows are 
minimal (different include files). 
 
 
/* Sample (Linux) application for receiving scan converted radar video 
into  
A SPxScDestBitmap class. See SPxExamples/SPxBitmap */ 
 
/* Standard headers. (Linux in this case) */ 
#include <stdio.h> 
#include <stdlib.h> 
 
/* SPx headers. */ 
#include "SPx.h" 
 
/* Callback for when the bitmap has been updated. */ 
static void bitmapCallback(SPxScDestBitmap *bitmap, UINT16 changes,  
 
 
 
 
UINT16 startAzi, UINT16 endAzi, DirtyBox box); 
 
static int MaxWinWidth  = 512; 
static int MaxWinHeight = 512; 
     
int main(int argc, char **argv)  
{ 
    /* Initialise the SPx libraries. */ 
    SPxInit(); 
 


===== PAGE 338 =====
Page 338 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
/* Create the bitmap, which is the destination for the scan 
converter */ 
    SPxScDestBitmap *spxBitmap = new SPxScDestBitmap(); 
    spxBitmap->Create(MaxWinWidth, MaxWinHeight); 
     
    /* Register the function that will be called when the bitmap is 
updated. */ 
    spxBitmap->SetNotifyUpdate(bitmapCallback); 
     
 
/* Create the scan converter */ 
 
SPxScSourceLocal *spxSc = new SPxScSourceLocal(spxBitmap);  
  
spxSc->SetWinGeom(0,0,MaxWinWidth, MaxWinHeight); 
  
 
  
/* Create the RIB */ 
  
SPxRIB * spxRib = new SPxRIB(1024*1024); 
  
 
  
/* Create a PIM */ 
  
SPxPIM * spxPIM = new SPxPIM(spxRib, 1024, 1024,  
  
 
 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK, SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPX_PIM_OUTPUT(1)); 
  
 
  
/* Create a process that connects the scan converter to the PIM */ 
  
new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv, NULL, spxPIM, spxSc); 
  
 
  
/* Create a test generator input */ 
  
SPxTestGenerator *spxTest = new SPxTestGenerator(spxRib, 1024, 4.0, 
200, 5, 0); 
  
spxTest->Enable(1); 
  
 
    /* Run the main loop.  In this demo, we don't do anything. */ 
 
    while(1) 
    { 
 
 
/* Don't busy loop. */ 
        SPxTimeSleepMsecs(1000); 
    } 
}  
 
 
static void bitmapCallback(SPxScDestBitmap *bitmap, UINT16 changes, 
                             UINT16 startAzi, UINT16 endAzi, DirtyBox box) 
{ 
    /* This function gets called as a notification that new data is 
     * available in the bitmap and/or the bitmap configuration has 
changed. 
     */ 
 
 
printf("New data: pos = (%d,%d), sz = (%d,%d), for %.1f to %.1f 
deg\n", 
 
 
 
box.x, box.y, box.w, box.h, 
 
 
 
((double)startAzi) * 360.0 / 65536.0, 
 
 
 
((double)endAzi) * 360.0 / 65536.0); 
 
 
/* Clear the dirty box. */ 
 
bitmap->ClearDirtyBox();   
} 
 
 


===== PAGE 339 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual
