8.1.5. 
Using SPxScDestBitmap with a remote scan converter using 
SPxScSourceNet  
 
The class SPxScSourceNet allows an application to receive scan converted video from 
a remote scan converter. The class takes responsibility for setting up the network 
connection with the scan converter and receiving commands and network video 
packets. The SPxScDestBitmap class works with SPxScSourceNet to update a local 
bitmap image of the scan converted radar video.  
 
In the code fragment below, the main thread function 
ReceiveVideoThreadFunction() creates a SPxScDestBitmap and a SPxScSourceNet 
object. The CommandLoop function of the SPxScSourceNet handles incoming 
commands and video data from the remote scan converter, despatching these to the 
display class, which is associated in the SPxScCreateDisplay() function. The 
SPxScCreateDisplay() function is called by the SPx software when the 
SPxScSourceNet object receives a request to create a new display window. The 
implementation of the function is the responsibility of the application, and it should 
either create a display object or return a display that was created previously. In the 
case of the example below, the function just returns a display that was previously 
created.  
 
Note that this code fragment provides a receiving interface to process commands and 
video data from the remote scan converter. The API that delivers the commands to 
the scan converter would be part of a different thread and likely provided by 
SPxScRemote() class (see Section 7.11.2). 
 
 
SPxScDestBitmap *DestBitmap; 
 
static void ReceiveVideoThreadFunction() 
 
{ 
 
    /* Create the Bitmap class to handle received video */ 
      SPxScDestBitmap *DestBitmap = new SPxScDestBitmap()    
 
 
    /* Maximum window size is 1000 x 1000. We’ll handle a 
 
      32-bit bitmap, which we have already created. */  
      DestBitmap->Create(1000, 1000,  
                         SPX_BITMAP_32BITS,  
                         RadarVideodata);         
    
 
    SPxScSourceNet * SourceNet = new SPxScSourceNet(  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SPX_SCNET_DEFAULT_PORT_DISPLAY); 
      while (1) 
      { 
        // Wait for a connection from the scan conversion server. 
        if (SourceNet->TryAccept() != -1)  
        { 
            /* Got a connection – run the command loop. */ 
            SourceNet->CommandLoop(); 
        }    
                    
        // Wait 1 second. 
        sleep(1); 
      } 
  } 
  
  SPxScDest *SPxScCreateDisplay(UCHAR screen, UINT16 w,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 h, UINT32 w1,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 w2,  UINT32 channelInfo,  


===== PAGE 340 =====
Page 340 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 scType, UINT32 *err) 
 { 
 
  /* This function gets called by the SPx framework to get the Dest 
 
  Object for a SourceNet. We simply return the Bitmap. */ 
 
    return DestBitmap; 
} 
 
See sample code in SPxExamples/SPxBitmapClient (single radar video) and 
SPxExamples/SPxBitmapClient2 (multiple radar videos). 
 
8.1.6. 
Using a Windows HBITMAP with SPxScDestBitmap 
 
The SPxScDestBitmap class can be used to update a Windows HBITMAP object.  
 
To achieve this, the memory for the Windows bitmap object should be provided to the 
SPxScDestBitmap Create() function.  This is most easily achieved using the class 
SPxBitmapWin to wrap the HBITMAP. For example: 
 
1. Create a SPxBitmapWin object (which is a wrapper around a Windows 
HBITMAP): 
 
SPxBitmapWin *bitmap = new SPxBitmapWin(); 
bitmap->CreateLocal(1280, 1024); 
 
2. Then create a SPxScDestBitmap, specifying the memory of the SPxBitmapWin: 
 
SPxScDestBitmap *destBitmap = new SPxScDestBitmap(); 
destBitmap->Create(1280, 1024, SPX_BITMAP_32BITS, 
              (unsigned char *)bitmap->GetBufferPtr()); 
 
3. The HBITMAP can then be obtained from the SPxBitmapWin class: 
 
HBITMAP hBitmap = bitmap->GetHBitmap() 
 
 
In the above example, the maximum size of the bitmap is assumed to be 1280 x 
1024, as an example. 
 
Note that this process creates the HBITMAP from a DIB-Section Bitmap which is 
created in the CreateLocal() call for the SPxBitmapWin class. In principle, it is 
possible to create the DIB Section directly and pass the memory for this into the 
SPxScDestBitmap Create() function. This is effectively what is happening inside the 
convenience class SPxBitmapWin. 
 
Refer to Section 8.3.11.1 for details of the SPxBitmapWin class. 
 
8.1.7. 
Display Radar with X11 using SPxScDestBitmap 
 
For an X Windows application, the SPxScDestBitmap class provides a general-purpose 
receiver for the scan converted data. From SPxScDestBitmap, the application may 
take complete control over the compositing process (so-called User Compositing), or 
the class can be used with SPxPPIwindow for Window or Pixmap Compositing. Refer to 


===== PAGE 341 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 341 
Section 8.4 for details of these compositing approaches. When using Window Manager 
Compositing, the class SPxScDestBitmap is not used. 
 
8.2. Radar Display using OpenGL 
 
The SPxScDestTexture class is derived from SPxScDestBitmap and supports all of the 
functions provided by SPxScDestBitmap. The SPxScDestTexture class makes the scan 
converted radar video contained in the bitmap available as an OpenGL texture. 
 
The SPxScDestTexture class requires OpenGL V2.1 or later to be installed. A recent 
PCIe Nvidia or AMD graphics card is required together with an up-to-date driver. 
 
8.2.1. 
SPxScDestTexture Class Definition 
 
The SPxScDestTexture class provides the following public functions: 
 
8.2.1.1 Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestTexture::SPxScDestTexture(void) 
 
Constructs a new SPxScDestTexture object. Call Create() on the new object 
to initialise it. 
 
 
int SPxScDestTexture::Create(UINT16 maxWidth,  
 
 
 
 
     UINT16 maxHeight, 
 
 
 
           texInternalFormat=GL_RGBA8, 
                             texFormat=GL_BGRA, 
                             texType=GL_UNSIGNED_INT_8_8_8_8_REV) 
 
Initialise a new SPxScDestTexture object. The maxWidth and maxHeight 
parameters are the maximum dimensions of the window in which the image will 
be displayed and are used to set the maximum size of both the internal bitmap 
and OpenGL texture.  The values for texInternalFormat, texFormat and 
texType are used to configure the 2D image associated with the OpenGL 
texture using glImage2D().  For most applications, the default values should be 
used. 
 
A valid OpenGL context must be bound before Create() is called, otherwise 
this function will fail and return an error. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 342 =====
Page 342 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.2.1.2 Destruction 
 
SPxScDestTexture::~SPxScDestTexture(void) 
 
Delete an SPxScDestTexture. 
 
8.2.1.3 Texture Access 
 
GLint SPxScDestTexture::GetTexture(void) 
 
Retrieve the ID of the OpenGL texture created by the class. The texture 
contains a single image that is sized to match maxWidth and maxHeight. 
 
8.2.1.4 Texture Update 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestTexture::UpdateTexture(void) 
 
Updating the OpenGL texture from the object’s internal bitmap requires a copy 
of data from CPU to GPU memory and should therefore only be performed when 
required.  Calling UpdateTexture() will perform the copy and update the 
texture’s image.  Typically, this function should be called just before the texture 
is rendered to an off-screen buffer or window. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 343 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 343 
8.2.1.5 Heading-Up or Course-Up with True Trails 
 
When the radar video is displayed heading or course-up with true trails, the radar 
video texture must be rotated and translated by the user of this class before display. 
To determine if this rotation and translation is required, check if the moving platform 
reference mode is set to SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH. When rotation 
and translation is required, the necessary transformation may be queried using the 
GetDisplayMatrix() function. When in this mode, the width and height of the area of 
the texture to display is specified by GetDisplayW() and GetDisplayH() rather than 
the usual GetW() and GetH() functions. 
 
/* Normal area of texture to render. */ 
int w = texture->GetW(); 
int h = texture->GetH(); 
 
/* Check if rotation and translation is required. */ 
if(texture->GetMovingPlatformRefMode() 
== SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH) 
{ 
 
/* Get the required 3 by 3 transformation matrix. */ 
 
float matrix[9]; 
 
texture->GetDisplayMatrix(matrix); 
 
 
 
/* OpenGL has represenst transformation matrices 
 
 * differently to SPx. 
 
 * Convert from SPx 3 by 3 matrix to OpenGL 4 by 4 matrix. 
 
 */ 
 
 
float fullMatrix[16] = {}; 
 
 
fullMatrix[0] = matrix[0]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[4] = matrix[1]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[12] = matrix[2]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[1] = matrix[3]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[5] = matrix[4]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[13] = matrix[5]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[2] = matrix[6]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[6] = matrix[7]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[14] = matrix[8]; 
 
 
fullMatrix[15] = 1.0f; 
 
 
/* Apply matrix to rendering of texture here. */ 
 
glMultMatrixf(fullMatrix); 
 
 
 
/* The area of the texture to render is larger 
  
 * when performing a transformation. 
 
 */ 
 
w = MIN(texture->GetDisplayW(), texture->GetWidth()); 
 
h = MIN(texture->GetDisplayH(), texture->GetHeight()); 
} 
 
/* Render w by h pixels from the corner of the texture here. */ 
 
 
 
SPxScMpRefMode_t 
SPxScDestTexture::GetMovingPlatformRefMode(void) const 
 
Query the current moving platform reference mode. If this mode is 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_NORMAL then the texture can be rendered as normal. If 
this mode is set to SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH then the texture 


===== PAGE 344 =====
Page 344 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
must be transformed by the matrix returned by GetDisplayMatrix() so that it 
is correctly positioned. 
 
 
int SPxScDestTexture::GetDisplayW(void) 
int SPxScDestTexture::GetDisplayH(void) 
 
Get the width and height of the area of the texture to render, when in 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH moving platform reference mode. 
 
 
 
void SPxScDestTexture::GetDisplayMatrix(float (&matrix)[9]) 
 
Get the 3 by 3 transformation matrix that must be applied while rendering the 
texture to correctly position it within the window when in 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH moving platform reference mode. 
 
8.3. Radar Display with Microsoft Windows 
 
When SPx is used to add radar display into a Windows application the classes 
SPxScDestDisplayWin, SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw or SPxScDestDisplayMixer handle 
the radar display. The key features and differences between these classes are as 
follows: 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWin – This is used for most applications. The class provides a 
radar window which is driven by a scan converter object. The radar window 
may be presented with underlay graphics (with which the radar is alpha 
blended) and overlay graphics (which appear as an overlay to the radar) by the 
client application. This display class supports a “Two Layer” or “Three Layer” 
dispay model. 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw – This is functionally similar to 
SPxScDestDisplayWin but is intended for non-MFC applications. 
 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer - This class takes an application bitmap to define the 
underlay and overlay graphics. This display method is called the Bitmap Model. 
The contents of the bitmap are combined with the radar display to give a 
composite window with underlays, radar and overlays. The class supports a 
number of rules that define how the graphics in the bitmap are to be 
interpreted as underlays or overlays. 
 
SPxScDestTexture – This class can be used to create an Open GL texture. 
Refer to Section 8.2. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmapD2D class is derived from SPxScDestBitmap and supports 
all of the functions provided by SPxScDestBitmap. The SPxScDestBitmapD2D 
class makes the scan converted radar video contained in the bitmap available 
as a Direct2D bitmap. Direct2D is Microsoft's replacement to GDI+ for 2D 
drawing and vector graphics on Windows. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmapD2D class requires Windows 7 or later to run, and Visual 
Studio 2010 or later to compile the application. The SPxWinD2DIsSupported() 
function can be used to check for Direct2D support at runtime. The 


===== PAGE 345 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 345 
SPX_SUPPORT_D2D define can be used to check for Direct2D support at compile 
time. This means that when Direct2D is not available applications can fall back 
to a non-Direct2D technique or display a useful error message. 
 
An example display showing radar being presented with underlay and overlay graphics 
under Windows is shown in Figure 86. 
 
Figure 86 - Displaying radar video with underlay and overlay  
graphics with Windows 
 
Many of the functions of the display classes are accessed through the scan converter 
object that is providing data to the display class. For example, although the 
SPxScDestDisplayWin class provides a SetView() API function, the application 
software will generally never call this function directly. Instead, it will call SetView() 
on the scan conversion object, which will then pass the message to its display class 
automatically. 
 
8.3.1. 
Window Layers 
 
There are three approaches to combining radar with graphics: the two-layer model, 
the three-layer model, and the bitmap model. The two-layer and three-layer models 
use the SPxScDestDisplayWin or SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw classes to handle the 
display of radar video, with the graphics provided either in a single window (two-layer 
model) or in two separate windows (the three-layer model). The bitmap model uses 
the SPxScDestDisplayMixer class to handle the display of radar video with graphics 
provided in a bitmap. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 346 =====
Page 346 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.3.1.1 Windows Layers – The Two Layer Model 
 
In the two-layer model, the graphics are all handled within a single Microsoft Window, 
which is part of a Win32, MFC, GDI or GDI+ application. The radar data (there may be 
one or more radar window) is combined with the graphics to give a composite display. 
There are two variants of the Two Layer Model. In the first, the graphics are an 
overlay to the radar. The graphics picture has a transparent background, which 
enables the display of radar video where there are no graphics. The transparent 
background is achieved by defining a specific RGB colour to represent background 
(chromakey). During the combination of the graphics windows and the radar, pixels 
displayed in the chromakey colour will become transparent.  In this mode, graphics 
are always an overlay to the radar. These graphics can include lines, vectors and text, 
but probably would not include filled polygon areas, which would mask the display of 
radar video (although that would be valid if that were the intent). 
 
The window structure for the Two Layer model with graphics as an overlay is shown in 
Figure 87. 
 
Figure 87 – Overlays and Radar in a Two Layer Model 
 
In the second variant of the Two Layer Model, the graphics are mixed with the radar. 
This allows solid filled polygons and raster maps to be used to provide an underlay to 
the radar, but it does not allow true overlays to the radar. 
 
The window structure for the Two Layer model with graphics as an underlay is shown 
in Figure 88. This mode can demonstrated with the SPx example program 
spxtransparentwin in the SPxExamples directory. This example creates a radar 
window with a scan converted test picture, which is combined with graphics in another 
window. In the example shown in Figure 89, a Google Earth window has been created 
and positioned at the same screen location as the radar window. It can be seen that 
the radar is combined with the graphics window.  
 
The two layer model allows radar video to be presented in any existing graphics 
application with very little effort. 
 
Radar Window 
Overlays 
Scan 
Converter 
updates this 
window 
Both windows are the same size 
and are maintained at the same 
position on the screen. 
Application software 
updates this 
window 


===== PAGE 347 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 347 
 
Figure 88 – Radars and Underlays in a Two Layer Model 
 
 
Figure 89 - Two layer presentation using the sample application spxtransparentwin to 
show the addition of radar into an unrelated Windows graphics application (Google 
Earth) 
 
8.3.2. 
Windows Layers – The Three Layer Model 
 
In the three layer model the graphics are split into separate windows for underlay and 
overlay. These windows are of the same size and located at the same position. The 
radar window (or windows) appear in the middle of the stack.  
To implement the three-layer mode, the application constructs its underlay graphics in 
a normal Window. There are very few restrictions on this window and how the 
graphics are generated (for example, see the presentation shown in Figure 89, which 
shows radar mixed with graphics application). 
Underlay graphics 
Radar 
Scan Converter 
updates this 
window 
Both windows are the same size 
and are maintained at the same 
position on the screen. 
Application 
software updates 
this window 


===== PAGE 348 =====
Page 348 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 90 - Overlays, Underlays and Radar in a Three Layer Model 
 
The three window stack is shown in Figure 90. 
 
The overlay window has a transparent background, so that the combination of 
underlay and radar are visible where the overlay graphics are absent. 
The overlay graphics need to be created in a window that is of style WS_EX_LAYERED, 
which is a layered window in Microsoft Windows. The window can be created with the 
layered style, or have its style changed to include the WX_EX_LAYERED option. There 
are then two options for handling the display of layered windows.  
 
One option is to call SetLayeredWindowAttributes (see standard Microsoft 
documentation) to set the attributes of the layered window and use the normal 
WM_PAINT mechanism for handling repaints of the window. One of the attributes is the 
colour key, i.e. the background colour in the overlay that represents transparency. 
Although in principle you can have any colour, the simple approach is to define black 
as the colour key. (Unlike old analogue keying process, which required a colour band 
for keying, the digital keying process requires a precise RGB value. By using 
R=G=B=0 for this purpose, it is still possible to have a ‘black’ overlay with, for 
example, R=0, G=0, B=1.) By filling the overlay window with black (R=G=B=0), the 
window will be transparent to the radar and the underlay. Any pixels in the overlay 
that are not black will appear in their exact colour.  
 
The procedure for handling a window in this way is summarised as follows: 
 
 
Create the overlay window as a normal Microsoft Window 
 
 
Set the WX_EX_LAYERED style flag on the window to enable Windows to 
handle it as a layered window. For example, use: 
 
SetWindowLong(hwnd, GWL_EXSTYLE, 
 
 
 GetWindowLong(hwnd, GWL_EXSTYLE) | WS_EX_LAYERED); 
 
 
Call SetLayeredWindowAttributes to set the colour key. For example: 
 
SetLayeredWindowAttributes(hwnd, RGB(0,0,0), 
 
 
 
                    255,LWA_COLORKEY); 
 
 
Handle the WM_PAINT messages to display the contents of the overlay 
window, ensuring that the background of the window is R=G=B=0 to get 
transparency. 
 
 
Underlay Window 
Radar Window 
Overlay Window 
Scan Converter 
updates this 
window 
All three windows are the same size and 
are maintained at the same position on 
the screen. 
Application 
software updates 
these windows 


===== PAGE 349 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 349 
A worked example of this method is provided in the document “A tutorial guide to 
using SPx with Microsoft Windows”, document number CP-25-110-06. 
 
The second approach for handling layered windows is using the 
UpdateLayeredWindow() function. This requires that the window is of style 
WS_EX_LAYERED, as before, but the SetLayeredWindowAttributes() function is not 
used. Instead, UpdateLayeredWindow() is used to manage the update process. This 
takes over from the WM_PAINT mechanism for window update. A bitmap is created to 
hold the overlay graphics and the process of calling UpdateLayeredWindow is the point 
where the bitmap contents is used to update the display. A transparency colour is 
used to fill the bitmap’s background and that same transparency colour appears as an 
argument in the UpdateLayeredWindow() function. Refer to standard Microsoft 
documentation for UpdateLayeredWindow. 
 
8.3.3. 
Window Layers - The Bitmap Model 
 
In the Bitmap model, the application’s graphics are drawn into an off-screen bitmap. 
The graphics and radar are mixed together using a customisable mixing function, 
allowing parts of the bitmap to appear as underlay, and other parts as overlay. The 
resultant combined image is then rendered into a visible window. The operation is 
summarised in Figure 91. 
 
This Bitmap model is especially suitable where existing graphics are rendered into a 
24-bit off-screen bitmap, and the single bitmap includes items which should appear in 
the underlay and items which should appear in the overlay. The mixing function allows 
a number of modes for determining if graphics are for the underlay or for the overlay. 
The available modes are alpha-blending, level of alpha, any single bit or multiple bits, 
and the peak intensity of each pixel of the graphics.  
 
Figure 91 - Overlays, Underlays and Radar the Bitmap Model 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayMixer class supporting the Bitmap model is described in 
Section 8.3.6. 
 
Scan Converter 
updates this bitmap 
Application software 
updates this bitmap 
 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer 
class reads application 
bitmap and radar images 
and blends together 


===== PAGE 350 =====
Page 350 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.3.4. 
SPxScDestDisplayWin Class 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayWin class provides a display rendering capability for PPI and B-
Scan radar windows. The class receives scan converted radar video and displays it in a 
Microsoft window under Windows 7, 8 or 10. The radar window may be blended with 
an underlay and combined with one or more overlays. Multiple radar windows may be 
stacked. 
In the description of the class functions below, it is stated that many of the functions 
are not normally called directly by the client application. This is because the client 
sends the request to the scan converter object, which then sends it on the request to 
the display class. For example, to set the radar colour, use SetRadarColour on the 
scan converter (e.g. SPxScSourceLocal). The scan converter then calls 
SetRadarColour automatically on the display class.  
 
The SPxScDestDisplayWin class references windows using the MFC CWnd class. For a 
native Win32 (non MFC) application, the SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class should be 
used instead (which refers to windows using HWND window handles). See Section 
8.3.5 for further details of this class. 
 
8.3.4.1 Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWin::SPxScDestDisplayWin(void) 
 
Create a new SPxScDestDisplayWin object. Call Create() after the 
construction to build and initialise the window. 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::Create(UCHAR screenID, UINT16 maxScreenW,  
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
      UINT16 maxScreenH, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      CWnd *underlay = 0,  
                                                CWnd *overlay = 0) 
 
Having created a SPxScDestDisplayWin object with the constructor, this call 
initialises the object with a screenID (0 for single screen systems), a maximum 
window size in pixels, and the identifiers of the underlay and overlay windows. 
The underlay window, if specified, is the parent for the radar window, thus 
ensuring that the radar is always above the underlay. The application can 
create an overlay window that is a child of the radar window to ensure correct 
window priorities. Use the GetRadarWindow() function on the 
SPxScDestDisplayWin instance to get the CDialog object that is used to 
display the radar video. If the overlay window is provided in this constructor, a 
subsequent creation of an SPxScFollowWin object can ensure that the overlay 
window is always maintained above the radar in the stacking order (this 
happens anyway if the overlay window is created as a child of the radar 
window). 
 
This function uses CWnds to refer to the underlay and overlay Windows, which 
are a part of MFC. If your application is not or cannot use MFC, then instead of 
SPxScDestDisplayWin use the class SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw. 
 
The Create function creates a new thread to handle the display updates. 
 
The function returns an error code. 
 


===== PAGE 351 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 351 
8.3.4.2 Destructor 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWin::~SPxScDestDisplayWin(void) 
 
Delete a radar display window. 
 
8.3.4.3 Update Interval 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetUpdateInterval(int updateIntervalMS) 
 
Set the update interval for the window. The value is in milliseconds and defines 
the period between window refreshes. The value provided for the update 
interval affects the smoothness of the radar video update. A value of 30ms, for 
example, corresponds to an upate of about 35 times per seond and this is 
generally the target figure for good appearance. Although the update interval 
can be reduced further to give an impression of smoother updates, this is at the 
expense of more processing and graphics activity. For situations in which the 
video updates are less critical, a longer update time is desirable. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetUpdateInterval(void)  
 
Return the current update interval in milliseconds. 
 
 
8.3.4.4 Fading 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 fadeRate) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. To set the fade type and fade rate 
call the SetFade function on the Scan converter object. 
 
This function sets the fade mode for the window. The fadeType is 0 for real-
time fade, 1 for sweep-based fade and 2 for replacement mode.  
 
The fadeRate parameter defines the duration of the fade as: 
 
For REAL_TIME fade the value of fadeRate is the fade time in units of 10s of 
milliseconds. For example, a value of fadeRate of 500 indicates a fade time of 
5 seconds (500 x 10ms = 5000ms). 
 
For SWEEP fade the value of fadeRate is the number of sweeps of fade.  
 
For REPLACE mode, the fadeRate parameter is not used. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetFadeType(void) 
 
Returns the current fade type as set from SetFade. 
 
 


===== PAGE 352 =====
Page 352 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetFadeRate(void) 
 
Returns the current fade rate as set from SetFade. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetMinFadeInterval(int minFadeIntervalMS) 
 
Set the minimum interval between fades for real-time fading mode. If the value 
is provided as zero (the default), it is interpreted as meaning that the fading 
should occur at the same interval as the display updates. If the value is > 0 
then the value defines the interval in milliseconds between fades. If the value is 
< 0 then it defines a fading interval that is a multiple of the update interval. For 
example: 
 
SetMinFadeInterval(50) 
- Fading is no more often than 50ms 
SetMinFadeInterval(0)  
- Fading is no more often than the current update 
interval. 
SetMinFadeInterval(-2) 
- Fading is no more often than twice the current 
update interval. 
 
Note that the value being set here is the minimum fade interval. The actual 
fade interval is calculated from the requested fade time, with the restriction 
that the value used can be no smaller than the minimum being set here.  
 
For example, for a fade time of 10 seconds, the duration between fades is 
about 40ms (10,000 / 255 fade levels). So setting a minimum fade interval that 
is less than 40 ms will have no effect. If the minimum fade interval is set to 
80ms, for example, then the fade processing will recalculate the per-update 
fade amount to ensure fading over 10 seconds (for this example). The 
consequence of increasing the minimum fade time, therefore, is to effectively 
reduce the number of fade levels in this example to 128. 
The benefit of increasing the fade time is a reduction in display processing, 
although for most modern computing platforms the saving in CPU load is 
minimal. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetFadeInterval(void) 
 
Return the current time interval between fades in milliseconds. 
 
8.3.4.5 Window Geometry 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetWinPos(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. To set the window geometry, call 
the SetWinPos function on the Scan Converter object. The Scan Converter will 
then report the display geometry to the display class. 
 
Set the position of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates.

SPxLib Programming Manual

UINT16 screenW, UINT16 screenH)

normally be called from a client application. 
 
Set the position and size of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. 
 
8.3.4.6 
Information Retrieval 
 
CDialog * SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetRadarWindow(void) 
 
Get the radar window. Generally the application will not need to know the 
identifier of the radar window, but if creating an overlay window it may be 
desirable to make the overlay a child of the radar window to ensure correct 
priority stacking. 
 
This function returns the window as a CWnd object, which is an MFC class. When 
dealing with windows as HWND window handles use the 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class which does not depend on MFC. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetWinParam(UINT16 param,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT32 arg1, UINT32 arg2,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT32 arg3) 
 
This function is used to set a number of window parameters as follows: 
 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_TEST_PATTERN  (value = 10): 
Create a test picture in the window. The value of arg1 defines the test picture 
according to Table 9. 
 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_UPDATE_INTERVAL  (value = 14): 
Set the update interval (in milliseconds) for the window refresh. This is the 
same as calling SetUpdateInterval(). 
 
8.3.4.7 View Control 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vx, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   REAL32 vw, REAL32 vh) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. To set the view, call the SetView 
function on the Scan Converter object. 
 
This function is called to report a view change in the radar window. The display 
typically clears the radar video in readiness for new data arriving.  
 
 


===== PAGE 354 =====
Page 354 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.3.4.8 Radar Colour, Brightness and Emphasis 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid,  
UCHAR red,  
UCHAR green,  
UCHAR blue) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. 
 
This function is called to set the radar colour. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The radar colour is given by the red, green and blue components, 
each in the range 0..255. The specified colour represents the colour of 
maximum intensity radar with brightness = 100 (see SetRadarBright). 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR bright) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. 
 
This function sets the radar brightness. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The brightness is in the range 0 to 100.  
 
 
UCHAR SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness. The rid argument is 
intended to support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently 
unused (set to 0). 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR lutId,  
                                      unsigned char *lutData) 
 
Set the look-up table that converts radar pixel values into display colours. This 
function is complementary to SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright. Use 
SetRadarLUT to define an arbitrary mapping from 8-bit radar intensity values to 
colours (could be false-colour for example). To set the radar colour to a linear 
scale from background to peak use the SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright 
functions. The lutID argument define a LUT identifier and is currently unused – 
set to 0. The lutData is an array of ARGB colour values of size 256*4 = 1024 
bytes. The data is organised as an array of 256 x 32-bit words, with each word 
holding a colour value in the format alpha (most significant 8-bits), red, green, 
blue (least significant 8 bits). Note that this function is called automatically by 
the scan converter and should not normally be called from a client application. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetRadarEmphasis(double emphasis) 
 
This function sets the emphasis of the radar video in the range 0.0 to 1.0. The 
default is 1.0. The value of the emphasis is used in the calculation of the radar’s 
alpha value, which is significant when the radar is combined with other 


===== PAGE 355 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 355 
components. A value of 1.0 means that increasingly brighter radar causes a 
corresponding reduction in the brightness of the underlay for each pixel – 
effectively the radar is emphasised over the underlay. A value of 0.0 means 
that there is no emphasis and the combination of radar and underlay is 
effectively a summation. 
 
 
double SPxScDestDisplayWin::GetRadarEmphasis(void) 
 
Returns the current value of the radar emphasis, which is in the interval 0.0 to 
1.0.  
 
8.3.4.9 Enable Radar Video 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayWin::ShowRadar(UCHAR rid, UCHAR state) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. To enable the display of radar 
video, call ShowRadar() on the Scan Converter object. 
 
Enable or disable the display of radar video. The rid argument is currently 
unused (set to 0). The state is 0 to stop the radar and clear the display, 1 for 
normal updates and 2 to stop and let the display fade (if real-time fade 
enabled).  
 
8.3.4.10 Patch Reporting 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWin::PatchSet(UCHAR format,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 numPatches, UINT16 *patchData) 
 
This function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. It is an internal function used to 
communicate the delivery of patches (newly scan converted radar video) from 
the scan converter to the display window. 
 
This is the function used to reports radar patches to the display. The patchData 
value is a pointer to a sequential list of numPatches patches. Refer to Table 12 
for the details of the patch format.  
 
8.3.4.11 Debug 
 
static void SPxScDestDisplayWin::SetDebugFlags(UINT32 flags) 
 
Sets the debug flags. The flag is a bitwise combination of the following 
components: 
 
 
D_COMMANDS – Show information on commands sent to the class 
 
D_PATCH_UPDATES – Show patch update information 
 
D_FADE – Show detailed fade information 
 
D_SETLUT – Show details of the LUT being programmed.

8.3.5. 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw Class 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class is a native Win32 non-MFC variant of the 
SPxScDestDisplayWin class. All the member functions of the SPxScDestDisplayWin 
class are supported by the SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class, with the exception of 
variants for the Create() and GetRadarWindow() functions described below. 
 
When using the SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class instead of the SPxScDestDisplayWin 
class, the SPxScFollowWinRaw class must be used where the SPxScFollowWin class 
would have been used (see section 8.2.5 for further details). 
 
8.3.5.1 Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw::SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw(void) 
 
Create a new SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw object. Call Create() after 
construction to build and initialise the window. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw::Create(UCHAR screenID, UINT16 maxScreenW, 
                                                   UINT16 maxScreenH, 
                                                   HWND underlay = 0,  
                                                   HWND overlay = 0) 
 
Having created a SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw object with the constructor, this 
call initialises the object with a screenID (0 for single screen systems), a 
maximum window size in pixels, and the identifiers of the underlay and overlay 
windows. The underlay window, if specified, is the parent for the radar window, 
thus ensuring that the radar is always above the underlay. The application can 
create an overlay window that is a child of the radar window to ensure correct 
window priorities. Use the GetRadarHwnd() function on the 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw instance to get the HWND window handle that is used 
to display the radar video. If the overlay window is provided in this constructor, 
a subsequent creation of an SPxScFollowWinRaw object can ensure that the 
overlay window is always maintained above the radar in the stacking order (this 
happens anyway if the overlay window is created as a child of the radar 
window).  
  
The Create function creates a new thread to handle the display updates. 
 
The function returns an error code. 
 
8.3.5.2 Destructor

Delete a radar display window.

SPxLib Programming Manual

Get the radar window. Generally, the application will not need to know the 
identifier of the radar window, but if creating an overlay window it may be 
desirable to make the overlay a child of the radar window to ensure correct 
priority stacking.

those supported by the SPxScDestDisplayWin class (see section 8.3.4). 
 
8.3.6. 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer Class 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayMixer class provides a flexible display rendering capability for 
PPI and B-Scan radar windows under Microsoft Windows. The class receives scan 
converted radar video and displays it in a window under Windows. The radar is 
blended with graphics from a single off-screen bitmap that is created and managed by 
the application. Multiple SPxScDestDisplayMixer classes may be chained together to 
display multiple radars (see SetMixerBitmap() for further details). The output of the 
class, comprising the original graphics with included radar images, is written into a 
user-supplied output Window. 
 
This class requires either an Nvidia or AMD (previously ATI) graphics card to be 
installed in the current system. The latest graphics card drivers must be installed. The 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer::IsSupported() function should be used to verify correct 
configuration of the system. 
 
The example application SPxWinExampleMixer (supplied with the SPx Development 
package) demonstrates the use of this class to display radar video blended with 24-bit 
graphics, where graphics are displayed as both underlay and overlay graphics. 
 
Limitations imposed by graphics card vendors may restrict the number of 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer objects that may be used in a single system at any one 
time. This limit could be as low as four. Once the limit is reached, the next call to the 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer::Create() function will return SPX_ERR_NOT_SUPPORTED. 
 
8.3.6.1 
Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SPxScDestDisplayMixer(void) 
 
Create a new SPxScDestDisplayMixer object. Call IsSupported() after 
construction to verify that the class is supported on the current system, then 
Create() to initialise the object. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 359 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 359 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer::~SPxScDestDisplayMixer(void) 
 
Destroy an SPxScDestDisplayMixer object. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayMixer::IsSupported(void) 
 
Call this function after construction to verify that the class is supported on the 
current system. The function returns TRUE if the class is supported and FALSE 
otherwise. This class requires either an Nvidia or AMD (previously ATI) graphics 
card to be installed in the current system (see section 8.2.4). If this function 
returns FALSE, then the object must not be used, and should be destroyed. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::Create(UINT16 maxScreenW,  
                                           UINT16 maxScreenH,  
                                           HWND outputWindow = 0) 
 
Having constructed an SPxScDestDisplayMixer object, this call initialises the 
object with a maximum window size in pixels, and an optional output window. 
The output window, if specified, is a window into which the mixed radar video 
and graphics will be rendered. The mixed radar video and graphics is always 
rendered into a bitmap before being rendered to the screen – this bitmap may 
be retrieved with the GetOutputBitmap() function. 
 
If an output window is specified, a subsequent creation of an SPxScFollowWin 
object can ensure that the scan converter size always matches the output 
window size. 
 
This function creates a thread to perform output bitmap and window updates. 
 
8.3.6.2 
Information Retrieval 
 
HWND SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetOutputWindow(void) const 
 
Retrieve the window handle passed to the Create() function. This function 
returns NULL if no output window was specified. 
 
 
HBITMAP SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetOutputBitmap(void) const 
 
Retrieve the output bitmap. This function returns NULL if the Create() function 
has not been called. The output bitmap is a 32-bit DIB-section bitmap of size 
maxScreenW pixels wide by maxScreenH pixels high (as specified to the 
Create() function). 
 
8.3.6.3 
Update Interval 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetUpdateInterval(int updateIntervalMS) 
 
Set the update interval for the output bitmap and output window. The value is 
in milliseconds and defines the interval between window refreshes. The value 
provided for the update interval affects the smoothness of the radar video 


===== PAGE 360 =====
Page 360 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
update. A value of 30ms, for example, corresponds to an update of about 35 
times per second and this is generally the target figure for good appearance. 
Although the update interval can be reduced further to give an impression of 
smoother updates, this is at the expense of more processing and graphics 
activity. For situations in which the video updates are less critical, a longer 
interval is desirable. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetUpdateInterval(void) const 
 
Return the current update interval in milliseconds. 
 
 
8.3.6.4 
Graphics Bitmaps 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetGraphicsBitmap 
                                           (HBITMAP hbitmap,  
                                            int isDibSectionTopDown) 
 
Set the bitmap from which graphics are retrieved. There are two variants to this 
function – this variant requires the bitmap to be a DIB-section bitmap (created 
using the Windows function CreateDIBSection()). The alternative function 
accepts a non-DIB section, but is less efficient because the bitmap must be 
copied. The isDibSectionTopDown flag specifies whether the DIB-section 
bitmap is top-down, or bottom-up. If the DIB-section was created with a 
positive height then it is bottom-up (and the flag should be set to FALSE). If the 
DIB-section was created with a negative height then it is top-down (and the 
flag should be set to TRUE). 
 
1. To remove a bitmap from the mixer, call this function with a NULL hbitmap 
argument. 
 
2. Graphics are retrieved from the bitmap every time the window is refreshed if 
UseLatestGraphics() is set to TRUE, otherwise the graphics are only retrieved 
each time the GraphicsChanged() function is called. 
 
Note: If graphics are provided by another mixer, see SetMixerBitmap() instead. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetGraphicsBitmap 
                                                 (HBITMAP hbitmap,  
                                                  HDC bitmapDC) 
 
Set the bitmap from which graphics are retrieved. There are two variants to this 
function – this variant accepts a non DIB-section bitmap. The alternative 
function requires a DIB-section bitmap. This variant is less efficient because the 
bitmap must be copied. Use the alternative function if possible. The bitmap 
must be selected into the supplied device context before calling this function, 
otherwise an error will be returned. 
 
To remove a bitmap from the mixer, call this function with a NULL hbitmap 
argument. 
 


===== PAGE 361 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 361 
Graphics are retrieved from the bitmap every time the window is refreshed if 
UseLatestGraphics() is set to TRUE, otherwise the graphics are only retrieved 
each time the GraphicsChanged() function is called. 
 
Note: If graphics are provided by another mixer, see SetMixerBitmap() instead. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetMixerBitmap 
                                       (SPxScDestDisplayMixer *mixer) 
 
Set the input mixer from which graphics are retrieved. Use this function in 
preference to connecting the output bitmap from another mixer using 
SetGraphicsBitmap(). 
 
The following example shows how two radars can be displayed in one window 
using two mixer objects (error checking and scan conversion configuration has 
been omitted in the example below for brevity). The blending operation used in 
the first mixer must preserve the underlay/overlay information for the blending 
of the second radar to behave as expected. 
 
/* First display for radar that is underneath second radar. */ 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer *mixer1 = new SPxScDestDisplayMixer(); 
/* Create 1st mixer without output to window. */ 
mixer1->Create(512, 512);            
mixer1->SetGraphicsBitmap(bitmap, TRUE); 
 
/* Set bit-based blending for first mixer. */ 
mixer1->SetBlendMode(SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS); 
mixer1->SetBlendOperators(SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER, SPX_BLEND_OP_DST); 
mixer1->SetBlendMask(RGB(0, 0, 1)); 
                 
/* Second display for radar that is on top of first radar. */ 
SPxScDestDisplayMixer *mixer2 = new SPxScDestDisplayMixer(); 
/* Create 2nd mixer with output to window. */ 
mixer2->Create(512, 512, GetDlgItem(IDC_RADAR_AREA)->GetSafeHwnd());  
 
/* Connect 1st mixer to 2nd mixer. */ 
mixer2->SetMixerBitmap(mixer1); 
 
/* Use the same blend mode for second mixer. */ 
mixer2->SetBlendMode(SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS); 
mixer2->SetBlendOperators(SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER, SPX_BLEND_OP_DST); 
mixer2->SetBlendMask(RGB(0, 0, 1)); 
 
The first mixer correctly calls GraphicsChanged() on the second mixer, so the 
UseLatestGraphics() setting can be set to TRUE or FALSE as required. 
 
 
HBITMAP SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetGraphicsBitmap(void) const 
 
Return the current graphics bitmap. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayMixer::UseLatestGraphics(int useLatestGraphics) 
 
Set whether graphics are retrieved from the graphics bitmap every time the 
window is refreshed or only after each call to the GraphicsChanged() function. 
Set to TRUE to retrieve the graphics every frame, and FALSE to retrieve the 


===== PAGE 362 =====
Page 362 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
graphics only after each call to GraphicsChanged(). The default setting is to 
retrieve the graphics every time the window is refreshed. 
 
Retrieving the graphics from the graphics bitmap every time the window is 
refreshed may be an expensive operation. If the graphics are unlikely to change 
frequently or at all, using the latest graphics for every window refresh is 
unnecessary. It is recommended to set this setting to FALSE to improve 
performance in this scenario. 
 
If the graphics bitmap is being updated when the mixer captures the graphics, 
then the graphics captured may be only partially updated. This is likely to cause 
artefacts and tearing in the graphics display. To prevent these artefacts, either 
use double buffering for the graphics (as demonstrated in the 
SPxWinExampleMixer example program), or set the UseLatestGraphics setting 
to FALSE and call the GraphicsChanged() function after each update to the 
graphics. It is preferable to use double buffering when the update rate of the 
graphics is close to the update interval of the mixer. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayMixer::IsUsingLatestGraphics(void) const 
 
Return the current latest graphics setting as set with the UseLatestGraphics() 
function. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GraphicsChanged(void); 
 
When the UseLatestGraphics() setting is set to FALSE, use this function to 
trigger an update from the graphics bitmap. The update will occur the next time 
the window is refreshed by this class. 
 
8.3.6.5 
Blending Configuration 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetBlendMode 
                                             (SPxBlendMode blendMode) 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayMixer class supports a number of modes to select 
between two blending operators. The blending operators can be any of the 
standard alpha compositing modes listed below and are set with the 
SetBlendOperators() function. The blending is performed by blending the 
radar video onto the graphics, so the radar video is the source and the graphics 
are the destination.  
 
Blend Operator 
Output Colour Component 
SPX_BLEND_OP_CLEAR 
0 
SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC 
Src 
SPX_BLEND_OP_DST 
Dst 
SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER 
Src + (1.0 – SrcAlpha) * Dst 
SPX_BLEND_OP_DST_OVER 
Dst + (1.0 – DstAlpha) * Src 
SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_IN 
Src * DstAlpha 


===== PAGE 363 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 363 
Blend Operator 
Output Colour Component 
SPX_BLEND_OP_DST_IN 
Dst * SrcAlpha 
SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OUT 
Src * (1.0 – DstAlpha) 
SPX_BLEND_OP_DST_OUT 
Dst * (1.0 – SrcAlpha) 
SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_ATOP 
Src * (1.0 – DstAlpha) + Dst * SrcAlpha 
SPX_BLEND_OP_DST_ATOP 
Dst * (1.0 – SrcAlpha) + Src * DstAlpha 
SPX_BLEND_OP_XOR 
Src * (1.0 – DstAlpha) + Dst * (1.0 – SrcAlpha)  
 
In this table, SrcAlpha and DstAlpha are the normalised alpha components of 
the source and destination pixels. The Output Colour Component equation is 
applied to each of the Red, Green and Blue colour components individually, as 
well as to the Alpha component (if applicable). The source image is always the 
radar video and the destination image is the graphics bitmap. 
 
The following table describes when the two blending operators are used with 
the available blend modes. 
 
Blend Mode 
When is blend 
operator 1 used? 
When is blend operator 2 
used? 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_ALPHA_BLEND 
Always 
Never 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_ALPHA 
Alpha component less 
than threshold set with 
SetBlendIntensity() 
Alpha component greater 
than or equal to threshold 
set with 
SetBlendIntensity() 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_INTENSITY 
Maximum RGB 
component less than 
threshold set with 
SetBlendIntensity() 
Maximum RGB component 
greater than or equal to 
threshold set with 
SetBlendIntensity() 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS 
None or not all the bits 
within mask set with 
SetBlendMask() are set 
All the bits within mask set 
with SetBlendMask() are 
set 
 
For example, to display only the graphics when the maximum RGB component 
intensity is above 100, and otherwise alpha blend the radar video on top of the 
graphics: 
 
destDisplay->SetBlendMode(SPX_BLEND_MODE_INTENSITY); 
destDisplay->SetBlendOperators(SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER, 
  
 
 
 
    SPX_BLEND_OP_DST); 
destDisplay->SetBlendIntensity(100); 
 
For example, to display only the graphics when the bottom bit of blue is set, 
and otherwise alpha blend the radar video on top of the graphics: 
 
destDisplay->SetBlendMode(SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS); 
destDisplay->SetBlendOperators(SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER, 
                               SPX_BLEND_OP_DST); 
destDisplay->SetBlendMask(RGB(0, 0, 1)); 
 
The default blend mode is SPX_BLEND_MODE_ALPHA_BLEND. The default 
operators are SPX_BLEND_OP_SRC_OVER for operator 1 and SPX_BLEND_OP_DST 
for operator 2. The default blend mask is 0, and the default blend intensity is 0. 


===== PAGE 364 =====
Page 364 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Windows bitmaps always use pre-multiplied alpha values. This means that if a 
pixel is drawn with red: 255, green:  0, blue: 0, and alpha: 127, then the 
stored values are red: 127, green: 0, blue: 0, and alpha: 127. For this reason, 
when using the SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS blend mode, either a 24-bit graphics 
bitmap should be used, or all alpha values should be set to 255. 
 
 
SPxBlendMode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetBlendMode(void) const 
 
Return the currently selected blend mode. 
 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetBlendOperators 
                                            (SPxBlendOp blendOp1,  
                                             SPxBlendOp blendOp2) 
 
Set the operators to use for blending the radar video and the graphics. See 
SetBlendMode() for further details. 
 
 
SPxBlendOp SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetBlendOperator1(void) const 
SPxBlendOp SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetBlendOperator2(void) const 
 
Return the currently selected blend operators. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetBlendMask(UINT32 blendMask) 
 
Set the bit mask to use to select between blend operator 1 and blend operator 
2 when using blend mode SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS. See SetBlendMode() for 
further details. 
 
The 32-bit blend mask value is a combination of red, green, and blue values, 
each in the range 0 to 255.  The position of the RGB components within the 32-
bit value is red in the least significant byte, green in the next byte, and blue in 
the next byte, e.g. 0xBBGGRR in hexadecimal notation. It is simplest to use the 
Microsoft macro RGB() to create the 32-bit value from the red, green and blue 
components. 
 
The masked bits within the graphics bitmap are always preserved in the output 
bitmap when using the SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS blend mode. This guarantees 
that chaining multiple mixers together (see SetMixerBitmap()) with the 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_BITS blend mode will correctly preserve underlay and overlay 
graphics in the final output bitmap. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetBlendMask(void) const 
 
Return the currently selected blend mask. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 365 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 365 
SPxErroCode SPxScDestDisplayMixer::SetBlendIntensity(UINT8 intensity) 
 
Set the threshold to select between blend operator 1 and blend operator 2 
when using either blend mode SPX_BLEND_MODE_ALPHA or 
SPX_BLEND_MODE_INTENSITY. When the blend mode is SPX_BLEND_MODE_ALPHA, 
the alpha component of the graphics is used to select between the blend 
operators. When the blend mode is SPX_BLEND_MODE_INTENSITY, the maximum 
of the red, green and blue components of the graphics is used to select 
between the blend operators. 
The intensity value is in the range 0 to 255. 
 
See SetBlendMode() for further details. 
 
 
UINT8 SPxScDestDisplayMixer::GetBlendIntensity(UINT8 intensity) const 
 
Return the currently selected blend intensity. 
 
 
8.3.7. 
Radar Display with SPxScDestBitmapD2D 
 
The SPxScDestBitmapD2D class is derived from SPxScDestBitmap and supports all of 
the functions provided by SPxScDestBitmap. The SPxScDestBitmapD2D class makes 
the scan converted radar video contained in the bitmap available as a Direct2D 
bitmap. Direct2D is Microsoft's replacement to GDI+ for 2D drawing and vector 
graphics on Windows. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmapD2D class requires Windows 7 or later to run, and Visual Studio 
2010 or later to compile the application. The SPxWinD2DIsSupported() function can 
be used to check for Direct2D support at runtime. The SPX_SUPPORT_D2D define can be 
used to check for Direct2D support at compile time. This means that when Direct2D is 
not available, applications can fall back to a non-Direct2D technique or display a 
useful error message. 
 
The SPxScDestBitmapD2D provides the following public functions.  
 
8.3.7.1 Construction and Creation 
 
SPxScDestBitmapD2D::SPxScDestBitmapD2D(void) 
 
Constructs a new SPxScDestBitmapD2D object. Call Create() on the new object 
to initialise it. 
 
 
int SPxScDestBitmapD2D::Create(UINT16 maxWidth, 
                                           UINT16 maxHeight, 
                                           ID2D1RenderTarget *target) 
 
Initialise a new SPxScDestBitmapD2D object. The maxWidth and maxHeight 
parameters are the maximum dimensions of the window in which the image will 
be displayed and are used to set the maximum size of the internal bitmap. The 
target must be a valid Direct2D render target – the Direct2D bitmap will be 


===== PAGE 366 =====
Page 366 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
created using this render target, which makes it suitable for rendering the 
bitmap into the render target. There is no restriction on the type of render 
target used. 
 
If Direct2D is not supported, then this function will return 
SPX_ERR_NOT_SUPPORTED. 
 
8.3.7.2 Destruction 
 
SPxScDestBitmapD2D::~SPxScDestBitmapD2D(void) 
 
Delete an SPxScDestBitmapD2D. 
 
8.3.7.3 Direct2D Bitmap Access 
 
ID2D1Bitmap* SPxScDestBitmapD2D::GetBitmap(void) 
 
Retrieve the Direct2D bitmap created by the class. The Direct2D bitmap 
contains a single image that is sized to match maxWidth and maxHeight. 
 
8.3.7.4 Direct2D Bitmap Update 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxScDestBitmapD2D::UpdateBitmap(void) 
 
Updating the Direct2D bitmap from the internal non-Direct2D bitmap requires a 
copy of the data and should therefore only be performed when required. Calling 
UpdateBitmap() will perform the copy and update the Direct2D bitmap's 
image. Typically, this function should be called just before the Direct2D bitmap 
is rendered to a Direct2D render target. 
 
8.3.7.5 Direct2D Bitmap Draw 
 
SPxErrorCode  
SPxScDestBitmapD2D::DrawBitmap(ID2D1RenderTarget *target,  
                               const D2D_RECT_F& rect) 
 
The Direct2D bitmap may be drawn manually using the standard DrawBitmap() 
functions provided by the ID2D1RenderTarget class. Alternatively, this 
convenience function may be used. This function additionally applies the 
required display transform when rendering heading or course-up radar video 
with true trails. The rect parameter should be the size of the window into 
which the bitmap is being rendered. 
 
When displaying heading or course-up, true trails radar video, the scan 
converter platform reference mode must be set to 
SPX_SC_MP_REF_MODE_ADJUST_FROM_NORTH – see 
SPxScSourceLocal::SetMovingPlatformRefMode() for further details. 
 


===== PAGE 367 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 367 
8.3.8. 
Window Following with SPxScFollowWin 
 
The radar window created with an instance of SPxScDestDisplayWin or 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw is a Microsoft Window that will contain the radar video. A 
special property of this window is that it is transparent, so that another window 
underneath is visible through the radar. Furthermore, the radar video that appears in 
the window is translucent, so that it blends with the contents of the window 
underneath it.  Finally, the radar window can itself be overlaid with another window 
that display graphics. The combination of these windows in a co-located stack 
supports the display of overlays, radar and underlay charts. 
 
The class SPxScFollowWin allows the position and state of the radar window to be 
automatically managed by the state of another window, for example the overlay or 
the underlay. The radar window is then effectively slaved to the position and state of 
another window, so that as the other window moves the radar window stays with it. 
The SPxScFollowWIn class is intended to be used with MFC windows. For non MFC 
windows use the related class SPxScFollowWinRaw (Section 8.3.9) which uses simple 
HWND Window handlers. 
 
 
SPxScFollowWin::SPxScFollowWin (SPxSc *radarWindow, CWnd *win, 
 
  
 
 
 
  SPxScDestDisplayWin *destDisplay = 0) 
 
Connect the radar window radarWindow to the graphics window win. The 
radarWindow can be NULL if it is not known at the time of the object creation. 
The screen position and visbility state of win are monitored by SPx in a thread 
that is created by the class. The screen location of the radar window is 
automatically set to match the state of win. It is possible for an instance of 
SPxScFollowWin to manage the position of a number of radar windows to allow 
multiple radars to be presented inside a single window. To add additional radar 
windows use AddSC(). Furthermore, a number of slave CWnds may be 
managed using subsequent SetSlaveWindow() calls so that the position and 
state of those windows match the state of win. 
 
If the destDisplay object is specified it is assumed to be the display window 
associated with the SPxSc object and will be used by the FollowWin class to 
assist in the correct stacking of the display windows.  
 
 
SPxScFollowWin::~SPxScFollowWin(void) 
 
Delete an instance of a SPxScFollowWin object. 
 
 
static void SPxScFollowWin::SetPollPeriod(int pollPeriod) 
 
This function sets the poll period for geometry measurements. The value is set 
for all instances of the class. The default value is 250ms. 
 
 
static int SPxScFollowWin::GetPollPeriod(void) 
 
Return the current window polling period in milliseconds. 
 


===== PAGE 368 =====
Page 368 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxScFollowWin::GetX(void) 
int SPxScFollowWin::GetY(void) 
int SPxScFollowWin::GetW(void) 
int SPxScFollowWin::GetH(void) 
 
This set of functions retrieves the current x, y, w and h of the Window being 
followed. Note that the x and y are in screen coordinates. 
 
 
int SPxScFollowWin::AddSC(SPxSc *radarWindow, SPxScDestDisplayWin  
                                                 *destDisplay = 0) 
 
Add an additional SPxSC radar window to be managed by the CWnd specified in 
the original constructor. Optionally, a destDisplay can be provided as well. 
This is assumed to be the display class associated with the radarWindow object. 
If provided, the destDisplay value assists the FollowWin class in managing 
the correct priority stacking of the windows. It is not needed if the client 
application is managing the stacking itself, for example using parent-child 
relationships between the windows.  
 
A maximum of SPX_FOLLOW_WIN_MAX radar windows may be managed by an 
SPxScFollowWin. The function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the radar source was 
added correctly, otherwise returns SPX_ERR_TOO_MANY_SC_WINS if there are too 
many windows. Note that there is currently no method to remove a scan 
conversion window from being managed after this function has been called. 
 
 
CWnd *SPxScFollowWin::GetWin(void) 
 
Return the CWnd* object that is associated with the controlling window. 
 
 
SPxThread *SPxScFollowWin::GetThread(void) 
 
Returns the SPx thread object associated with the SPxScFollowWin object. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowWin::SetSlaveWindow(int index, CWnd *slaveWin) 
 
This functions sets a slave window. The state and geometry of the slave window 
is automatically set to the state and geometry of the controlling window 
provided in the class constructor. Each slave window is accessed with an index, 
which is in the range 0 to SPX_MAX_NUM_SLAVE_WINS -1. If the slave window is 
being used it is normally expected to have minimal decoration, for example no 
title bar nor border. 
 
 
CWnd * SPxScFollowWin::GetSlaveWindow(int index) 
 
Return the current slave window. May be NULL if no slave window is associated 
with this instance. The slave window is accessed with an index, which is in the 
range 0 to SPX_MAX_NUM_SLAVE_WINS -1.  
 


===== PAGE 369 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 369 
8.3.9. 
Window Following with SPxScFollowWinRaw 
 
The SPxScFollowWinRaw class is a native Win32 non-MFC variant of the 
SPxScFollowWin class. It can also be used with an MFC client (since a HWND can easily 
be provided by an MFC Windows) but it does not require an MFC Window. 
 
All the member functions of the SPxScFollowWin class are supported by the 
SPxScFollowWinRaw class with the exception of variants for the AddSC(), GetWin(), 
SetSlaveWindow() and GetSlaveWindow() functions described below. 
 
The SPxScFollowWinRaw class can be used with either the SPxScDestDisplayWin or 
SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw class. 
 
 
 
SPxScFollowWinRaw::SPxScFollowWinRaw(SPxSc *radarWindow, HWND win, 
                             SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw *destDisplay = 0) 
 
Connect the radar window radarWindow to the graphics window win. The 
radarWindow can be NULL if it is not known at the time of the object creation. 
The screen position and visibility state of win are monitored by SPx in a thread 
that is created by the class. The screen location of the radar window is 
automatically set to match the state of win. It is possible for an instance of 
SPxScFollowWinRaw to manage the position of a number of radar windows to 
allow multiple radars to be presented inside a single window. To add additional 
radar windows, use AddSC(). Furthermore, a number of slave HWNDs may be 
managed using subsequent SetSlaveHwnd() calls so that the position and state 
of those windows match the state of win.  
  
If the destDisplay object is specified, it is assumed to be the display window 
associated with the SPxSc object and will be used by the FollowWinRaw class to 
assist in the correct stacking of the display windows. 
 
   
SPxScFollowWinRaw::~SPxScFollowWinRaw(void)  
   
Delete an instance of a SPxScFollowWinRaw object. 
 
 
int SPxScFollowWinRaw::AddSC(SPxSc *radarWindow, 
                             SPxScDestDisplayWinRaw *destDisplay = 0) 
 
Add an additional SPxSC radar window to be managed by the CWnd specified in 
the original constructor. Optionally, a destDisplay can be provided as well. 
This is assumed to be the display class associated with the radarWindow object. 
If provided, the destDisplay value assists the FolloWin class in managing the 
correct priority stacking of the windows. It is not needed if the client application 
is managing the stacking itself, for example using parent-child relationships 
between the windows.  
 
A maximum of SPX_FOLLOW_WIN_MAX radar windows may be managed by a 
SPxScFollowWin. The function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the radar source was 
added correctly, otherwise returns SPX_ERR_TOO_MANY_SC_WINS if there are too 


===== PAGE 370 =====
Page 370 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
many window. Note that there is currently no method to remove a scan 
conversion window from being managed after this function has been called. 
 
 
HWND SPxScFollowWin::GetHwnd(void) 
  
Return the HWND object that is associated with the controlling window. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowWin::SetSlaveWindow(int index, HWND slaveWin)  
  
This functions sets a slave window. The state and geometry of the slave window 
is automatically set to the state and geometry of the controlling window 
provided in the class constructor. Each slave window is accessed with an index, 
which is in the range 0 to SPX_MAX_NUM_SLAVE_WINS -1. If the slave window is 
being used, it is normally expected to have minimal decoration, for example no 
title bar nor border.

Return the current slave window. May be NULL if no slave window is associated 
with this instance. The slave window is accessed with an index, which is in the 
range 0 to SPX_MAX_NUM_SLAVE_WINS -1.
