8.3.11. 
Windows Utility Classes 
 
The following classes are provided to assist with the display of 
graphics in combination with radar video.  For an example of 
usage, please see the SPxWinLatLongPPI example program. 
 
 
 
8.3.11.1 SPxBitmapWin 
 
The SPxBitmapWin class is provided by the library to assist with the creation of a DIB 
(Device Independent Bitmap), which may be used for off-screen rendering of 
graphics.  The memory allocated by the SPxBitmapWin class may be local to the 
process or shared, in which case the bitmap may be accessed my multiple processes. 
 
 
SPxBitmapWin::SPxBitmapWin(void) 
 
Constructor for SPxBitmapWin class. After creating a new object, the 
CreateLocal() or CreateShared() function must be called to complete 
initialisation. 
 
 
SPxBitmapWin::~SPxBitmapWin(void) 
 
Destructor for SPxBitmapWin class. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxBitmapWin::CreateLocal(unsigned int width, 
                                       unsigned int height, 
                                       unsigned int bitsPerPixel=32) 
 
Create a bitmap that is mapped to local process memory.  This function may be 
used to create a standard bitmap for off-screen rendering. The width and 
height arguments define the size of the bitmap.  As a special case, setting 
width and height to zero will set the bitmap size to match the screen size.  
The bitsPerPixel argument defines the colour depth of the bitmap. The 
default value is 32-bits per pixel. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxBitmapWin::CreateShared(const char *name, 
 
                                  unsigned int width, 
 
                                  unsigned int height, 
 
                                  unsigned int bitsPerPixel=32) 
 
Create a shared memory bitmap.  A shared memory bitmap may be accessed 
by multiple processes on the same host.  Another process may attach to a 
shared memory bitmap by calling Attach(). The assigned name is used to 


===== PAGE 373 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 373 
reference the bitmap and must be unique between all shared memory bitmaps 
on the same host.  To automatically generate and assign a bitmap name, pass 
NULL as the name argument and then retrieve the generated name using 
GetBitmapName().   
The width and height arguments define the size of the bitmap.  As a special 
case, setting width and height to zero will set the bitmap size to match the 
screen size.  The bitsPerPixel argument defines the colour depth of the 
bitmap. The default value is 32-bits per pixel. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxBitmapWin::Attach(const char *name) 
 
Attach to an existing shared memory bitmap on the same host.  The name 
argument defines the unique bitmap name set when the shared memory bitmap 
was created.  Once attached, the bitmap may be accessed in the same way as 
a local memory bitmap. However, it is a good idea to use the Lock() and 
Unlock() functions to control access. 
 
 
const char *BitmapWin::GetBitmapName(void) 
 
Get the name of a shared memory bitmap. 
 
HBITMAP SPxBitmapWin::GetHBitmap(void) 
 
Get a handle to the underlying Win32 bitmap object. 
 
 
HDC SPxBitmapWin::GetHDC(void) 
 
Get a Win32 device context that may be used to draw to the bitmap.  The HDC 
handle may be used with standard windows GDI or GDI+ functions to render 
graphics to the bitmap. 
 
 
void *SPxBitmapWin::GetBufferPtr(void) 
 
Get a pointer to the start of bitmap’s memory buffer.  The pointer may be used 
to read and write directly to the buffer.  The number of bytes per pixel is given 
by (bitsPerPixel / 8).  The total size of the memory buffer is given by (width * 
height * (bitsPerPixel / 8)). 
For a 32-bit bitmap, each pixel contains alpha, red, green and blue stored as 
0xAARRGGBB in little-endian byte order. 
 
 
void SPxBitmapWin::ClearBitmap(void) 
 
This function may be used to zero the bitmap, effectively setting each pixel to 
black with an alpha of zero.  Please note, however that this function is not 
efficient.  A better solution for clearing a bitmap is to render a black rectangle 
using GDI or GDI+. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 374 =====
Page 374 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
unsigned int SPxBitmapWin::GetWidth(void) 
 
Get the width of the bitmap in pixels. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxBitmapWin::GetHeight(void) 
 
Get the height of the bitmap in pixels. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxBitmapWin::GetBitsPerPixel(void) 
 
Get the number of bits per pixel. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxBitmapWin::Lock(int timeoutMs=-1) 
 
This function is used to lock access to a shared memory bitmap. 
This function will block the calling thread until access is granted to the bitmap.  
A timeout may be specified in milliseconds.  If a timeout occurs before access is 
granted, then SPX_ERR_TIMEOUT will be returned and the bitmap will remain 
unlocked.  Setting timeoutMs to 0 will cause the function to return immediately 
if access is not granted.  Setting timeoutMs to -1 (the default) will block 
indefinitely until access is granted. 
 
The bitmap must be unlocked by calling Unlock() once access is no longer 
required. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxBitmapWin::Unlock(void) 
 
This function is used to unlock a shared memory bitmap that was previously 
locked by calling Lock(). 
 
 
static BOOL SPxBitmapWin::IsSharedBitmap(const char *name) 
 
This static function is used to determine if a shared bitmap exists with the 
specified name.  It returns TRUE if a shared bitmap exists with the specified 
name, else it returns FALSE. 
 
8.3.11.2 SPxGraphicsWindow 
 
The SPxGraphicsWindow class is provided by the library to assist with the creation of a 
layered window, typically used to display overlay graphics.  This class is effectively a 
wrapper around a Windows CWnd object.  It simplifies the configuration of the window 
to use either chroma-keying or per-pixel alpha-blending to control transparency. 
 
 
SPxGraphicsWindow::SPxGraphicsWindow(void) 
 
Constructor for SPxGraphicsWindow class. 
 
 


===== PAGE 375 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 375 
SPxGraphicsWindow::~SPxGraphicsWindow(void) 
 
Destructor for SPxGraphicsWindow class.  After creating a new object, the 
Create() function must be called to complete initialisation. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::Create(HWND ownerHWnd) 
 
Create a new window.  The ownerHWnd argument is used to set the owner of the 
window.  If using a SPxGraphicsWindow to display overlay graphics, the 
ownerHWnd is typically set to the handle of the associated underlay window to 
enforce the correct Z-order (stacking order) of the windows. 
 
 
CWnd * SPxGraphicsWindow::GetCWnd(void) 
 
Get the underlying CWnd object. 
 
 
HWND SPxGraphicsWindow::GetHWND(void) 
 
Get the HWND handle of the underlying CWnd object. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::SetAlphaBlending(BOOL enable) 
 
Set to TRUE to make the window layered (WS_EX_LAYERED flag set) and use 
per-pixel alpha values to control transparency.  A pixel with an alpha of 0 will 
be fully transparent and a pixel with an alpha of 255 will be fully opaque.  
Values in between will yield varying degrees of transparency. The default is 
FALSE (disabled). 
 
If both per-pixel alpha blending and chromakey is enabled then chroma-keying 
will take precedence. 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::SetChromaKeying(BOOL enable) 
 
Set to TRUE to make the window layered (WS_EX_LAYERED flag set) and use a 
nominated chromakey colour to control transparency.  Any pixels in the window 
that exactly match the chroma-key colour are displayed as fully transparent.  
All other pixels remain unchanged and are therefore displayed as fully opaque.  
The default is FALSE (disabled). 
If both per-pixel alpha blending and chromakey is enabled then chroma-keying 
will take precedence.   
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::SetChromaKeyColour(UINT32 crKeyRGB) 
 
Set the colour to use for transparency when chromakeying is enabled.  The 
colour is defined as 0xRRGGBB.  The default chroma-key colour is black 
(0x000000). 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 376 =====
Page 376 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::SetTransparency(BOOL enable) 
 
Set to TRUE to make a layered window transparent to mouse events.  The 
default is FALSE (disabled).   
 
Note that this should not be confused with visual transparency, which is 
controlled by SetAlphaBlending() and SetChromaKeying().   
 
If using an SPxGraphicsWindow to display overlay graphics then typically 
mouse event transparency should be enabled.  Therefore, mouse events will 
‘fall through’ the overlay window and may be handled by the application’s 
underlay window.  
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxGraphicsWindow::PaintWindow(HDC srcHdc, srcX,  
int srcY, int width, int height) 
 
This function is provided to efficiently paint the window using graphics provided 
in an offscreen bitmap.  The srcHdc argument defines the source of graphics 
data.  The srcX and srcY arguments define a pixel offset into the source.  The 
width and height argument define the size of the area to update.  Setting 
width and height to -1 will set the update area to match the size of the 
window. 
 
 
8.3.11.3 SPxRadarWindowD2D 
 
The SPxRadarWindowD2D class is provided by the library to assist in displaying radar 
video in a window using Direct2D, with optional underlay and overlay graphics 
provided by SPx renderers. 
 
Various SPx renderers for common radar related graphics are described in detail in the 
“SPx Library Extensions” manual, document number CP-25-110-85. Using Direct2D 
graphics for radar rendering can result in reduced CPU load, particularly for high 
resolution monitors. 
 
The SPxRadarWindowD2D class uses Direct2D V1.1, which requires Windows 7 or later 
to run, and Visual Studio 2013 or later to compile the application. 
The SPxWinD2D1_1IsSupported() function can be used to check for Direct2D V1.1 
support at runtime. 
The SPX_SUPPORT_D2D_1_1 define can be used to check for Direct2D V1.1 support at 
compile time. This means that when Direct2D is not available, applications can fall 
back to a non-Direct2D technique or display a useful error message. 
 
 
SPxRadarWindowD2D::SPxRadarWindowD2D(void) 
 
Constructor for SPxRadarWindowD2D class. After creating a new object, the 
Create() function must be called to complete initialisation. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 377 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 377 
SPxRadarWindowD2D::~SPxRadarWindowD2D(void) 
 
Destructor for SPxRadarWindowD2D class. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::Create(HWND appHWin, 
                                       unsigned int maxWidth, 
                                       unsigned int maxHeight) 
 
Create the window. The appHWin argument is used to set the window or control 
within the application, where the radar window will be displayed. 
The maxWidth and maxHeight arguments set the maximum permissible window 
size. 
 
 
ID2D1RenderTarget *SPxRadarWindowD2D::GetTarget(void) const 
 
Get the underlying Direct2D render target used for rendering. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::SetSPx(SPxSc *sc,  
                                       SPxScDestBitmapD2D *scBitmap) 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::AddSPx(SPxSc *sc,  
                                       SPxScDestBitmapD2D *scBitmap) 
 
Add a new layer of radar video. The sc argument is the scan converter source 
object, and the scBitmap is the Direct2D scan conversion destination object. 
The order of adding the radar video layers controls the stacking order when 
rendering, with the first radar video added being rendered first, and subsequent 
radar video alpha blended on top. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::AddUnderlayRenderer( 
                                           SPxRendererD2D *renderer) 
 
Add a new layer of underlay graphics, rendered by the specified Direct2D 
renderer. Multiple renderers may be added to add multiple alpha blended layers 
of underlay graphics. The order of adding the underlay layers controls the 
stacking order when rendering, with the first underlay layer added being 
rendered first, and subsequent underlay layers alpha blended on top. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::AddOverlayRenderer(SPxRendererD2D 
*renderer) 
 
Add a new layer of overlay graphics, rendered by the specified Direct2D 
renderer. Multiple renderers may be added to add multiple alpha blended layers 
of overlay graphics. The order of adding the overlay layers controls the stacking 
order when rendering, with the first overlay layer added being rendered first, 
and subsequent overlay layers alpha blended on top. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 378 =====
Page 378 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxRadarWindowD2D::IsWorldMapAvailable(void) const 
 
Query if a world map renderer layer has been added and has successfully 
loaded the required world map fails. Returns TRUE if world map rendering is 
available and FALSE otherwise. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::SetUnderlayBrightness(int 
underlayBrightness) 
int SPxRadarWindowD2D::GetUnderlayBrightness(void) const 
 
Set or get the required brightness adjustment to apply to all underlay graphics. 
A positive value will increase the brightness, and a negative value will decrease 
the brightness. Valid values are between -100 and +100. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::SetUnderlayContrast(int underlayContrast) 
int SPxRadarWindowD2D::GetUnderlayContrast(void) const 
 
Set or get the required contrast adjustment to apply to all underlay graphics. A 
positive value will increase the contrast, and a negative value will decrease the 
contrast. Valid values are between -100 and +100. 
 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::SetBackgroundColour(UINT32 colour) 
 
Set the background colour to use underneath all underlay, radar video and 
overlay graphics layers. The colour is a 24-bit RGB colour in the form 
0xRRGGBB. The default background colour is black. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::CompositeDisplay(int updateUlay,  
                                                 int updateOlay) 
 
Redraw the radar window. The updateUlay argument indicates whether the 
underlay graphics need to be updated. The updateOlay argument indicates 
whether the overlay graphics need to be updated. The CompositeDisplay() 
function must be called at regular intervals from the applications GUI thread to 
redraw the window. The rate at which the function is called will control the 
smoothness of the display. The higher the update rate, the smoother the 
display, but the higher the CPU load. When using MFC, the recommended 
method is to call this function from the OnTimer() function of a dialog class. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxRadarWindowD2D::GetUnderlayUpdateTime(void) const 
UINT32 SPxRadarWindowD2D::GetOverlayUpdateTime(void) const 
 
Get the ticker times at which the underlay and overlay graphics were last 
updated. This may be useful in making sure the graphics are updated at a 
minimum rate, which is likely to be slower than the required update rate for 
radar video. For example, to update the underlay graphics every 500ms, the 
updateUlay argument can be set to TRUE when calling CompositeDisplay() 
only if enough time has elapsed: 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 379 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 379 
UINT32 now = SPxTimeGetTickerMsecs(); 
int updateUlay = (SPxTimeGetDiff(radarWin->GetUnderlayUpdateTime(), now) > 
500); 
radarWin->CompositeDisplay(updateUlay, updateOlay); 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarWindowD2D::SetViewControl(SPxViewControl *obj) 
 
Associate a view control object with this radar window. When a view control is 
associated with a radar window, the view control's window size will be 
automatically updated when the radar window size changes. 
 
8.4. Radar Display with X Windows 
 
This section describes the supported options for the display of scan converted video 
with SPx under X Windows with Linux. 
 
A typical requirement in a radar display application is that graphics and radar are 
presented in multiple layers with radar blended with an underlay map and overlaid by 
other graphics, such as symbology and targets. The method of combining a number of 
data layers is called compositing. 
 
There are a number of options for compositing graphics and scan converted radar 
video under X Windows with SPx. Different approaches will be preferred for different 
situations, depending on the graphics applications that the radar will work with. 
 
The approaches are as follows: 
 
Radar Display Coprocess (RDC) 
 
In the RDC solution, the radar scan conversion is implemented by a separate 
Linux process (the RDC). The client application handles all the graphics of the 
application as part of a normal X11 application. The client application 
communicates to the RDC over a socket interface to handle the radar 
processing. The advantage of this method is that all the radar acquisition, 
processing, scan conversion and display processing is outside of the main 
application. The main application simply has to communicate with the RDC over 
a simple socket interface using the SPxRemoteServer class, so programming 
with RDC is much simpler. The only disadvantage of this approach is that there 
is less flexibility for complex applications - using the SPx class library as part of 
the main application means that the full set of capabilities of the library are 
available. The use of the RDC with SPx is the subject of a separate manual 
“Radar Display Coprocess (RDC) for Linux X11”.  
 
Application Window Compositing 
 
In Application Window Compositing, a graphics application creates and 
maintains an X Window that contains graphics. The SPx radar display will be 
created as part of the same application and will be inserted into the graphics 
application. The graphics application is able to control the appearance of 
graphics as underlay or overlay relative to radar by using the alpha value of the 
graphics. The display application must support 32-bit graphics for this 
presentation to be effective. A 24-bit display will work but radar will be blended 
with all graphics. 


===== PAGE 380 =====
Page 380 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
See Section  8.4.3 for details of Application Window Compositing. 
 
Application Pixmap Compositing 
 
In Application Pixmap Compositing, a graphics application constructs the 
components of the graphics application as separate underlay and overlay 
Pixmaps. The SPx compositing process, which is part of the main application, 
takes these bitmaps and combines with the radar to form a composited image 
that is copied to the output. See Section 8.4.4 for details of Application Pixmap 
Compositing. 
 
Window Manager Compositing 
 
In Window Manager Compositing, the combination of the images is done by the 
window manager. The application creates normal windows to handle the 
underlay, radar and overlay and leaves it to the window manager to combine 
the pixels in the windows in the correct way. This approach needs a 
compositing windows manager, such as Compiz. See Section 8.4.5 for details of 
Window Manager Compositing. This approach is not recommended for most 
applications and is likely to become deprecated in a future release of SPx. 
 
User Compositing 
 
In User Compositing, the combining of radar with graphics is the responsibility 
of the user’s application, or perhaps the existing graphics toolkit.  In principle, 
the compositing techniques used by SPx can be implemented by user code, 
although the convenience of using the standard SPx library routines is generally 
the preferred method. The User Compositing process takes the scan converted 
radar data from a bitmap as its input and proceeds to combine it with other 
graphics layers in the display. See Section 8.4.6 for details of User 
Compositing. 
 
In general, either Application Window Compositing or Application Pixmap Compositing 
is the preferred approach. You are strongly advised to review your situation 
with Cambridge Pixel’s engineers to get the best advice. 
 
8.4.1. 
Acceleration using OpenGL 
 
The performance of the SPx scan converter under X Windows is very high when using 
modern graphics boards with up-to-date drivers. Additional performance 
improvements can be achieved for RDC, Application Window Compositing and 
Application Pixmap Compositing using OpenGL capabilities of the graphics card. The 
SPxPPIwindowGlx class may be used to automatically implement accelerated 
composition if support is available from the hardware. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 381 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 381 
8.4.2. 
Terminology Review for X Windows 
 
The sections of this chapter describe the behaviour of the compositing in terms of X 
Windows objects. This section provides a brief summary of the terminology used by X 
Windows. 
 
Server – X Windows is a client-server architecture. Applications are clients that 
communicate to the server. The server provides the display and input 
capabilities.  
 
Client – An X Client application makes calls into X API functions to send 
commands to the server, for example to create and manipulate windows. Many 
clients may connect to the same server (clock, terminal window, text editor 
etc.). A special X client is a window manager, which allows a user to manipulate 
windows by selecting them on the desktop.  
 
Display – A client specifies which server it wants to connect to using a Display. 
In principle, a client could communicate to multiple servers. 
 
Screen – A Server may provide multiple screens, for example a dual-head 
display. If a client wants to create a window it will have to specify the screen 
for that window. The screen number starts at 0 for the first screen. 
 
Window – A Window is a display area on the screen. X Drawing operations 
(draw line, text, polygon etc.) can be applied to a Window. A Window may be 
unmapped, which means that drawing operations on it have no effect. Windows 
have a stacking order or priority so that windows below a higher one may be 
whole or partially invisible. Windows exist in a hierarchy that starts with the 
root window (the desktop). Children of the root window are sometimes called 
top-level windows.  
Pixmap – A Pixmap is a server-side, off-screen bitmap. Graphics operations 
(such as draw line, draw text, fill polygon etc.) can be directed to a Pixmap in 
the same way as a Window. Often, graphics are drawn into a Pixmap then 
copied into a window. Doing updates in this way avoids the flashing that occurs 
if the display window was cleared before being updated. 
 
Image – An Image is a client-side bitmap structure. Data stored in an image 
has to be copied across to the server (into a Pixmap or directly to a Window). 
Normal X drawing primitives do not apply to an Image.  
 
Drawable – A Drawable is the term used in X to mean a Pixmap or Window. 
For example, if an X operation can accept a Drawable, then it can be provided 
with a Pixmap or Window. 
 
8.4.3. 
Application Window Compositing 
 
In Application Window Compositing, the graphics are displayed in a normal X Window 
with no special requirements for SPx. The SPx radar can be inserted into the X 
Window with minimal co-operation from the graphics client. 
 
The graphics application shown on the left in Figure 92 contains graphics components 
that show an underlay map and overlay symbols from targets and range rings. 
 


===== PAGE 382 =====
Page 382 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
Without changing, recompiling or even relinking this graphics application, radar video 
can be inserted into the application as shown in the right-hand screen shot of Figure 
92. 
 
Figure 92 - Application Window Compositing - Inserting radar video  
into a graphics Window. 
 
Sample applications are provided with SPx in SPxExamples/SPxX11graphics for the 
simple graphics application and SPxExamples/SPxX11insertradar for the radar 
insertion example. 
 
As a simple demonstration of Application Window Compositing, you can use the 
insertradar example to insert radar into your existing graphics application without 
any changes to that application. To properly integrate the SPx Scan Converter into a 
client application, the client needs to send a small number of API calls to the scan 
converter, for example to define the radar centre and scale factor.  
 
The Application Window Compositing implementation provided by SPx substantially 
eliminates knowledge of the display presentation from the graphics application. In 
general, however, the graphics application still needs to control the appearance of a 
graphic relative to the radar video, as either an underlay or an overlay. If the graphics 
is an underlay, the colour is required to be mixed with the radar. If the graphics is an 
overlay, then the colour is required to be opaque with respect to the radar. The 
graphics application controls this using the alpha component of the display colour. An 
alpha value of 255 indicates that the RGB value is an overlay. An alpha value of 0 
indicates that the RGB value is an underlay. Alpha values in between the minimum 
and maximum values can be used to affect the “depth” of the radar relative to the 
graphics. 
 
The ability to control the alpha value of display colour requires a 32-bit display buffer. 
This is common on all modern display hardware and most modern graphics display 
toolkits support 32-bit display presentation 
 
If the graphics application can only support 24-bit displays (no alpha channel), then it 
is still possible to insert radar video. However, in this situation all the graphics will be 
interpreted as an underlay and the radar will be mixed with them. This situation is 
shown in Figure 93. The difference from Figure 92 may not be plainly visible, and may 


===== PAGE 383 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 383 
not be significant. Careful examination of the display of the 24-bit graphics window 
will show that the colour of “overlay” components such as targets and range rings is 
affected by the colour of the radar video at the same point. In contrast, in the 32-bit 
situation when the overlays are marked with peak alpha the colour of the targets and 
range rings is unaffected by the colour of radar at the same pixel. 
 
Figure 93 - Radar video inserted into a 24-bit graphics application 
 
The difference in a 24 and 32-bit display can be shown more clearly with the example 
in Figure 94 (this is from the SPx Example SPxX11appWinComp in the Development 
release tree). 
 
In Figure 94 (a) one set of colour ramps (the top ones) are clearly an overlay to the 
radar. They achieve this by setting the alpha value of their 32-bit colour to 255.  The 
lower ramps in this example set the alpha value to 0 so they appear as an underlay to 
the radar video. 
 
In Figure 94 (b) the ramps are drawn in a 24-bit graphics window. This time, there is 
no alpha channel so radar is blended with all the graphics.  In Figure 94 (c), the 
blending of the radar video has been emphasised in the mixing process. There is 
control over the emphasis placed on the radar video using the function 
SetRadarEmphasis on the SPxScDestBitmap class, (minimum in (b) and maximum in 
(c)), but in each case the radar is blended with the graphics. 
 


===== PAGE 384 =====
Page 384 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 94 - Example of Window Compositing with 32 and 24-bit graphics 
(SPxX11appWinComp example) 
 
The use of a 32-bit graphics application provides the greatest flexibility when 
allocating colours and controlling the presentation with the radar. The use of a 24-bit 
display may be restricted by the existing graphics application, and although it 
precludes the display of true overlays it may otherwise provide an acceptable display. 
 
8.4.3.1  Using Application Window Compositing with SPx 
 
The SPx class SPxPPIwindow provides full support for Application Window 
Compositing. The insertradar example is a simple client program that uses 
SPxPPIwindow to add radar video to an existing X Window. The example is stand-
alone and shows that the X Window being affected by the insertion need not even 
belong to the radar client. In many cases, however, it will belong to the client.  
(c) 24-bit display with graphics below 
emphasised radar 
(b) 24-bit display with graphics below 
the radar 
(a) 32-bit display with graphics above 
and below radar 


===== PAGE 385 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 385 
The creation of the SPxPPIwindow requires the X Window containing the graphics to 
be provided as an input. Optionally, pixmaps (which are created and managed by the 
application code or by a separate application) can be used to provide underlay and 
overlays. This enables the class to support a wide variety of compositing methods 
including: 
 
 
Overlay graphics and underlay graphics can come from an X Window, which 
is created and managed by the application code. The difference between an 
underlay and overlay pixel is on the basis of the alpha value (the Window 
must be 32 bits per pixel depth).  
 
 
Overlay graphics can come from an X Window and underlay graphics can 
come from a pixmap. This allows a separate mapping application to output 
into a pixmap, which is then used by the SPxPPIwindow class as the source 
of underlay graphics, with overlays coming from an existing Window.  
 
 
Underlay graphics can come from a Window and overlay graphics can come 
from a pixmap. This allows a mapping application to output directly into a 
Window, with overlays being created in a pixmap. 
 
The SPxPPIwindow class also provides the connection to the SPx scan conversion 
objects. Under control from the application, the class periodically updates the display 
window with a composition of application graphics and scan converted radar. This 
process is shown in Figure 95. 
 
Refer to Section 8.4.7 for details of the SPxPPIwindow class. 
 


===== PAGE 386 =====
Page 386 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 95 - Use of SPxPPIwindow for compositing with Window input

SPxInsertRadar – This is a client application that inserts radar video into an

Pixmap.  
 
8.4.4. 
Application Pixmap Compositing  
 
In Application Pixmap Compositing, the application provides pixmaps to the 
compositing process. These pixmaps get combined with the radar video and the result 
copied to an output drawable (window or pixmap). This compositing method is similar 
to Application Window Compositing, but the inputs are pixmaps instead of the window.   
 
Since this compositing approach requires that all graphics are drawn into one of the 
pixmaps, the graphics application must not draw any graphics direct to the window. 
 
The compositing process can take a single pixmap that contains underlay and overlay 
components, or else two separate pixmaps representing separate underlay and 
overlay components. 
 
The graphics application provides pixmaps A and B to contain all the display graphics. 
The output of the composition is built using the following sequence: 
 
1. Pixmap A, if it exists, is first copied into an intermediate composing Pixmap. 
 
2. The radar videos are then overlaid on the composing pixmap with an alpha-
blend. 
 
3. Pixmap B, if it exists, is copied into the composing Pixmap. 
 
4. The composing Pixmap is copied to the output window or Pixmap. 
 
Graphics appearing in Pixmap A are always alpha blended with the radar. Pixmap A is 
assumed to be 24 bits deep. 
 
Graphics appearing in Pixmap B are combined with the radar according to the alpha 
component of the pixel. Pixmap B must be 32 bits deep. This means that Pixmap B 
may be used to define both underlay and overlay components using the alpha value to 
control the selection. In this situation, all graphics may be created in this pixmap and 
Pixmap A need not be used. 
 
In summary, the following options are available: 
 
1. Write all graphics into Pixmap B (32 bits) using the alpha component of the 
colour to define the underlay or overlay level. Pixmap A is unused. 
 
2. Write underlay graphics into Pixmap A (24 bits) and overlay graphics into 
Pixmap B (32 bits). 
For graphics written to Pixmap B, the colour value must have alpha set to 
0xff for each graphic colour. 
The background colour must be RGBA=(0,0,0,0). 
 


===== PAGE 388 =====
Page 388 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
3. Write all graphics into Pixmap A (24 bits). All graphics get blended with 
radar, so there are no true overlays. 
 
8.4.4.1 Using Application Pixmap Compositing with SPx 
 
The SPx class SPxPPIwindow provides full support for Application Pixmap Compositing. 
 
The creation of the SPxPPIwindow requires the specification of pixmaps A and B (one 
or both or neither). The class also provides the connection to the SPx scan conversion 
objects. Under control from the application, the class periodically updates the display 
window with a composition of application graphics and scan converted radar. This 
process is shown in Figure 96. 
 
Figure 96 - Use of SPxPPIwindow for compositing with Pixmap input 
 
Application Pixmap compositing is provided by the SPxPPIwindow class using the 
Create() option that requires Pixmap inputs. Refer to Section 8.4.7 for details of the 
SPxPPIwindow class.

8.4.5. 
Window Manager Compositing 
 
In most cases, Window Manager Compositing is not the preferred compositing 
method under X Windows. Before using this method, please discuss your needs 
with Cambridge Pixel. 
 
In Window Manager Compositing, the graphics window and the radar window are 
created as separate X Windows. The radar window is sized and positioned directly 
overlay the graphics window. When used with a Compositing Window Manager 
(Compiz), the two windows are combined to allow the radar to mix with the graphics. 
 
An additional window may be created - the overlay window - to form a three layer 
stack of windows. 
 
The class SPxScDestDisplayX11 provides a PPI scan conversion display capability for 
X Windows for Window Manager Compositing, using Compiz. The class can interface 
directly to a SPxScSourceLocal class, when scan conversion and display are running 
on the same machine, or else it can interface to SPxScSourceNet, when the scan 
converter is running elsewhere on the network, or as part of another server process 
on the same machine – see Figure 97. 
 
Figure 97 - Using SPxScDestDisplayX11 for Window Manager Compositing 
 
Most of the interaction with SPxScDestDisplayX11 occurs through the 
SPxScSourceLocal or SPxScSourceNet. In the simplest situation, an application can 
create an instance of SPxScDestDisplayX11 and pass it to the scan converter. All 
manipulation of window position and view occurs through the scan converter. 
SPxScSourceLocal 
SPxScDestNet 
SPxScSourceNet 
Network 
SPxScDestDisplayX11, 
SPxScDestDisplayWin 
SPxScDestBitmap 
 


===== PAGE 390 =====
Page 390 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The application creating SPxScDestDisplayX11 must be running on the same machine 
as the X Server.  
 
Although the SPxScDestDisplayX11 class can be used in an application program to 
display radar in an X Window, another method of achieving a similar result is to use 
the SPx X Windows Display Server. This is a self-contained binary server that a scan 
converter connects to and provides radar video. The server creates and manages an X 
Window that contains the radar, and ensures that the window is correctly positioned 
and stacked with respect to an underlay and overlay window, which remain the 
responsibility of the application developer. Refer to “Display Server Reference 
Manual“, document number CP-25-110-02 for more information. 
 
Refer to Section 8.4.9 for details of the SPxScDestDisplayX11 class. 
 
In Window Manager Compositing, video is presented on the screen in a normal X 
Window. This window is created and managed by the class SPxScDestDisplayX11. 
The window contains only radar video with a transparent background and is intended 
to overlay, and be overlaid, by other windows in the X display. In particular, the radar 
window is intended to be co-located with one or two user-created X Windows that will 
contain the graphics for the application. An X Window can be designated as the 
underlay for radar video, and the radar video is then semi-transparently mixed with 
that video. Another X Window can be designated an overlay to the radar, and its 
contents will be displayed as an overlay to the radar video. 
 
The existing windows that are desired to be underlays and overlay to the radar video 
are provided in the creation of the SPxScDestDisplayX11 class. 
 
The radar window has an associated Pixmap (the server-side off-screen image store) 
and an Image (the client-side image store) associated with it – see Figure 98. 
 
Figure 98 - X Windows Image, Pixmap and Window for an X11 radar window

8.4.5.1 Overlays and Underlay with Window Manager Compositing 
 
An existing X Windows application, written directly with the low-level X toolkit, or built 
with a third-party application toolkit, can be integrated with SPx for the purposes of 
radar scan conversion display.  
 
In its simplest form, the SPx radar window will be semi-transparently mixed with the 
graphics window, allowing radar video to be blended with any graphics that are being 
displayed (and irrespective of how they are displayed).  In this situation, there is no 
distinction between overlays and underlays. The radar video will be alpha-blended 
with everything in the X Window. However, note that other X Windows can still 
overlay that window. 
 
In an extended configuration, an additional X Window can be used as the overlay 
window, allowing a three layer structure, as shown in Figure 98. In this situation, 
anything that is drawn into the overlay window will mask the display of the radar 
video. To support this mode it is necessary to create two X Windows, one for the 
overlay and one for the underlay. The overlay window looks like a normal X Window, 
including the title bar, if that is part of the normal display. However, the underlay 
window is a minimal X Window with no title bar or decoration. The overlay and 
underlay window need to be positioned at the same screen location and be of the 
same size. With a transparent background in the overlay window, the contents of the 
underlay are then visible. The SPx software is responsible for the creation of the radar 
X Window, and will handle the registration of the three X Windows to ensure they 
remain co-located on the screen. 
 
8.4.5.2 X Event Handling in Window Manager Compositing 
 
In the three-layer overlay-radar-underlay window structure, the overlay window is the 
top-most in the X Windows stacking order. Therefore, that window receives the X 
events as normal. 
 
In the two-layer radar+graphics window structure, the radar window is the top-most 
therefore it would receive the X window events. The application providing the graphics 
can get those events by asking the radar window to redirect them. This is the normal 
behaviour when an underlay window is registered underneath the radar. There is a 
window parameter that defines the X Event mask that is used to filter the events. 
Refer to the SetWinParam documentation on page 421. 
 
8.4.6. 
User Compositing 
 
User Compositing means that the application (or third party libraries) is responsible 
for displaying the radar data with whatever method is appropriate. The application 
may use existing compositing code, may implement its own version of one of the 
other SPx compositing methods, or may do something different. 
 
The interface to the scan converted data is through the SPxScDestBitmap class (see 
Section 8.1). This class receives scan converted radar video and makes it available in 
a graphics-independent bitmap structure. The application code can read this data and 
perform its compositing – see Figure 99. 


===== PAGE 392 =====
Page 392 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 99 - User Compositing using SPxScDestBitmap class 
 
The class SPxScDestBitmap provides an event reporting mechanism so that the 
application’s compositing activity can be synchronised with the receipt of radar video 
into the bitmap class. It should be emphasised that the class SPxScDestBitmap is not 
related to Windows or X11 graphics. It provides a simple 2D data store for the 
application to access. 
 
8.4.7. 
Application (Windows and Pixmap) Compositing with SPxPPIwindow 
 
The class SPxPPIwindow provides an implementation of Application Window and 
Application Pixmap compositing for X Windows. This is a very flexible class that 
accommodates many display requirements, including: 
 
 
Use an existing X Window to provide the overlay and underlay graphics. 
 
 
Use an existing X Window to provide the overlay graphics and a Pixmap to 
provide the underlay graphics. 
 
 
Use an existing X Window to provide the underlay graphics and a Pixmap to 
provide the overlay graphics. 
 
 
Use Pixmaps for the underlay and overlay graphics. 
 
Where the use of the class refers to the “use of an existing Window”, this means that 
the Window is created and managed by the client application software. Graphics 
written to that Window are entirely under the control of the client application and may 
use any third party toolkit in the process. 
The SPxPPIwindow class is able to “read” the contents of the Window, modify them to 
include radar, and write back the result without co-operation from the client 
graphics application.  Furthermore, X Windows event management in the client 
application is unaffected. 
SPxScSourceLocal or 
SPxScSourceNet 
(Local or remote scan 
conversion) 
SPxScDestBitmap 
 
Radar bitmap 
(8 or 32-bit) 
Application  
 
User 
Compositing 
Code

Refer to Table 7 for guidance on the selection of how to use the SPxPPIwindow class. 
Read across this table to select the source of the overlay graphics and read down this 
table to select the underlay graphics. The descriptions will reference the method 1 or 
method 2 Create() function of the SPxPPIwindow class. 
 
The class takes user-created input pixmaps or a window that contains the graphics 
layers, and takes one or more channels of radar video. By calling the Composite() 
function of the class, the radar and graphics are combined into the output. 
 
The class is used as follows: 
 
1. Create an instance of the class, e.g. new SPxPPIwindow() 
 
2. Call Create() to initialise the instance, providing the graphics Window 
and/or pixmaps A and B (see above and Table 7 for discussion on how 
these are used). 
 
3. Create the SPx objects for the scan conversion and bitmap receipt. When 
creating the SPxDestBitmap object, give it the memory allocated by the 
PPIwindow class and retrieved from GetRadarImageMemory() and 
GetRadarImageStride member functions. 
 
4. Call SetSPx() and AddSPx() to associate the SPx objects with the class 
instance. 
 
5. Call CompositeDisplay() at regular intervals under an application timer to 
combine the new radar video with overlay and underlay graphics. 
 
Example configurations are shown in Figure 95 and Figure 96 for Application Window 
and Application Pixmap compositing respectively. These examples show scan 
conversion provided by SPxScSourceLocal (local scan converter) or SPxScSourceNet 
(remote scan converter) providing data into a SPxScDestBitmap class. The bitmap 
created by SPxScDestBitmap is then provided as input to the SPxPPIwindow class, 
which composites the layers of the scene. The output of the compositing is either 
another pixmap or a window.

No overlays, no underlays. To demonstrate this, use

be added into the blank spxgraphics window.

the –i option and click in the spxgraphics window to select that window 
for radar insertion. Radar will be inserted into the spxgraphics window, 
with the spxgraphics overlays on top of the radar video. The spxgraphics 
application can also be started with the –r option to create graphics in a 
24-bit (rather than 32-bit) Window. Since this Window then lacks an 
alpha channel, the graphics are mixed with the radar rather than being 
true overlays.

8.4.7.1 Managing Views with SPxPPIwindow 
 
For Application Pixmap Compositing with SPxPPIwindow, the question of managing the 
combined radar and graphics view arises. There are two solutions to this. Normally, 
the application will handle view changes and redraw the pixmaps and then report the 
new view to the SPxPPIwindow class using the SetRadarView() member function. The 
class will then rescale all radar videos that are associated with the window.  The 
SPxViewControl class may be used to assist the application with lat/long based view 
control and moving platform display in a PPI window. 
 
The other approach is to register a set of redraw functions with the SPxPPIwindow 
class and let the class invoke those functions when a view change is required. In this 
situation, calling the SetView() member function on the class causes the registered 
redraw functions to be called, as well as the SetRadarView() function. The application 
must provide implementations of the overlay and underlay functions. 
 
 


===== PAGE 398 =====
Page 398 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.4.7.2 Pixmap Updates 
 
The SPxPPIwindow class accepts pixmaps A and B as input to the compositing. 
Updating the contents of these pixmaps is the responsibility of the application 
software. However, the SPxPPIwindow class should be notified when changes to these 
pixmaps occur. Call PixmapBChanged() to report a change to Pixmap B or 
PixmapAChanged() to report a change to the Pixmap A. If these functions are not 
called, the updates to the screen occur only as the rotation of the radar sweep forces 
an update. 
 
8.4.8. 
SPxPPIwindow and SPxPPIwindowGlx Class Interface 
 
This section describes the SPxPPIwindow and SPxPPIwindowGlx class interfaces.  
 
The SPxPPIwindow class implements the preferred method for composition for radar 
and graphics under Linux. The SPxPPIwindowGlx class is an enhanced version of 
SPxPPIwindow, which optimises the composition when OpenGL is available. There is a 
small to medium size performance improvement when using SPxPPIwindowGlx, 
versus using SPxPPIwindow. 
 
SPxPPIwindowGLx is a sub-class of SPxPPIwindow, and therefore inherits all the public 
functions of the base class. For reasons of brevity, the class documentation below 
documents the access functions for SPxPPiwindow, but it should be noted that the use 
of SPxPPIwindowGlx is to simply change the class name. 
 
8.4.8.1 Using SPxPPIwindowGlx 
 
GLX is an extension to the X Windows system that allows OpenGL to be used within X 
Windows. By using GLX with an accelerated graphics card and suitable driver the 
computation required for compositing is off-loaded to the graphics card, thereby 
reducing the CPU utilisation. 
 
SPxPPIwindowGlx requires a graphics card and a suitable driver that supports OpenGL 
and GLX along with OpenGL and GLX extensions. Graphics cards from Nvidia and 
ATI/AMD are supported with the suitable drivers available direct from the 
manufacturer’s web site. 
 
In the documentation below, the API for the base class SPxPPIwindow is documented. 
The SPxPPIwindowGlx class has exactly the same API (it is a sub-class of 
SPxPPIwindow). 
 
An application using SPxPPIwindowGlx class must link against the libGL library. This 
can be achieved by passing “-lGL” to the linker. If libGL is not linked, undefined 
references to many functions beginning in “gl”, such as “glDeleteTextures” will be 
seen. 
 
A sample application, SPxX11graphicsGlx, is provided in the SPx Development 
release to show how the accelerated SPxPPIwindowGlx may be used. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 399 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 399 
8.4.8.2 Construction and Destruction 
 
SPxPPIwindow::SPxPPIwindow(void) 
 
Construct a new SPxPPIwindow object. Call Create() after this function to 
initialise the object. 
 
 
SPxPPIwindowGlx::SPxPPIwindowGlx(SPxPPIwindowAccel_t  
                                 accelMode=SPX_PPI_WIN_ACCEL_AUTO) 
                 
Construct a new PPI window GLX object. The parameter accelMode is one of: 
 
SPX_PPI_WIN_ACCEL_OFF (0)     
Never use accelerated fading. 
 
SPX_PPI_WIN_ACCEL_AUTO (1)     
Use accelerated fading if the hardware supports it, otherwise use non-
accelerated fading with no warning or error. 
 
SPX_PPI_WIN_ACCEL_GLX (2)     
Use GLX accelerated fading if the hardware supports it, otherwise throw an 
error. 
 
 
SPxPPIwindow::~SPxPPIwindow(void) 
 
Delete a SPxPPIwindow object. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::Create(Display *display, int screen, 
 
 
 
 
   Window appGraphics, 
 
 
 
 
   int maxWidth, int maxHeight, 
 
 
 
 
   int numRadarVideos, 
   Pixmap pixmapA = 0, Pixmap pixmapB = 0) 
 
Elsewhere in this section, this is called the method 1 Create function. Use this 
form of Create() for Application Window Compositing. This functions allows 
radar video to be inserted into the appGraphics window, with graphics in that 
window providing an underlay or an overlay (according to their alpha value) to 
the radar video. Additionally, or as an alternative, the underlay graphics can 
come from pixmapA and the overlays from pixmapB. Refer to Table 7 for options 
on using this function. 
 
The Create function initialises an SPxPPIwindow object. The X Display is 
provided in display and the screen number in screen. The window containing 
the graphics is defined by appGraphics. This may be specified as 0 if the 
Window is not known at the time of this call (it may subsequently be set using 
SetWindow() function).  The maximum size of the window to be composited is 
set with maxWidth and maxHeight in units of pixels. The number of radar videos 
to be displayed in the PPI window is defined by numRadarVideos. 
 
The Pixmaps pixmapA and pixmapB can be used to provide an underlay 
(through pixmapA) and an overlay (through pixmapB). This leads to a variety of 


===== PAGE 400 =====
Page 400 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
display modes to support different graphics configurations. Refer to Table 7 for 
guidance in selecting the right mode. The depth of pixmapA (for the underlay) 
can be 24 or 32 bits. The depth of pixmapB (for overlays or overlays and 
underlays combined) should be 32 bits. 
 
After a successful call to Create() the client should call SetSPx() to associate 
the SPx objects with the SPxPPIwindow. If there is more than one radar video in 
the window, use AddSPx() to add the additional radar channels. 
 
The function returns an error code. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::Create(Display *display, int screen,  
 
 
 
 
Drawable drawable,  
 
 
 
 
int maxWidth, int maxHeight, 
 
 
 
 
Pixmap pixmapA, Pixmap pixmapB,  
 
 
 
 
int numRadarVideos) 
 
Elsewhere in this section, this is called the method 2 Create function. Use this 
form of the Create() call for Application Pixmap Compositing. 
 
The Create function initialises an SPxPPIwindow object. The X Display is 
provided in display and the screen number in screen. The destination for the 
composited image is drawable, which may be an X Windows Pixmap or Window. 
The drawable is assumed to exist on X Screen screen of Display display. The 
drawable may be specified as 0 if it is not known when calling Create. It may 
subsequently be set (not changed) with SetDrawable(). The maximum size of 
the window to be composited is set with maxWidth and maxHeight in units of 
pixels. The pixmaps A and B are provided in pixmapA and pixmapB, both of 
which are optional and may be specified as 0 if not required. If non zero, they 
must have been created by the caller and be of size maxWidth x maxHeight. 
The depth of pixmap B must be 32 bits per pixel and pixmap A can be 24 or 32 
bits. The number of radar videos to be displayed in the PPI window is defined 
by numRadarVideos. See Section 8.4.4 for an explanation of pixmaps A and B. 
Refer to Table 7 for options on using this function. 
 
After a successful call to Create() the client should call SetSPx() to associate 
the SPx objects with the SPxPPIwindow. If there is more than one radar video in 
the window, use AddSPx() to add the additional radar channels. 
 
The function returns an error code. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 401 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 401 
int SPxPPIwindow::SetSPx(SPxSc *scanConverter,  
 
 
 
 
SPxScDestBitmap *destBitmap,  
 
 
 
 
SPxRunProcess *scProcess, 
 
 
 
 
SPxScFollowX11 *followWin = 0) 
 
After a successful call to Create(), the function SetSPx must be called by the 
client to associate a set of SPx objects with the PPI window. The scan converter 
is provided with scanConverter argument, which should be an instance of 
SPxScSourceLocal (for a local scan converter) or SPxScRemote (for a remote 
scan conversion server). The bitmap object defined by destBitmap is the 
memory bitmap that is the display object for the scan converter. This bitmap 
must have been created using the memory provided by the SPxPPIwindow class 
using the GetRadarImageMemory and GetRadarImageStride functions. The scan 
conversion process, scProcess, is an instance of SPxScProcess. This argument 
should be NULL for a remote scan converter. The followWin argument 
(optionally NULL) defines the SPxScFollowX11 object that monitors the position 
and size of the destination drawable and maintains the correct geometry of the 
bitmap. The followWin argument is only needed for Application Pixmap 
Compositing. For Application Window Compositing, just omit the argument (it 
defaults to 0). 
 
The SetSPx function defines the first channel of radar video associated with a 
window. If there are additional channels of radar video to be displayed in the 
same PPI window then use AddSPx() to add them.  
 
The calls to Create(), SetSPx() and AddSPx() must all be from the same 
thread when using SPxPPIwindowGlx. 
 
The function returns an error code. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::AddSPx(SPxSc * scanConverter, 
 
 
 
 
 SPxScDestBitmap *destBitmap,  
                         SPxRunProcess *scProcess=0) 
 
This function may be used to add additional channels of radar video. The scan 
converter is provided with the scanConverter argument, which should be an 
instance of SPxScSourceLocal (for a local scan converter) or SPxScRemote (for 
a remote scan conversion server). The bitmap object defined by destBitmap is 
the memory bitmap that is the display object for the scan converter. This 
bitmap must have been created using the memory provided by the 
SPxPPIwindow class using the GetRadarImageMemory and 
GetRadarImageStride functions. 
 
The scan conversion process scProcess is the instance of SPxScRunProcess. 
This argument should be NULL for a remote scan converter. 
 
Note that each channel of radar video requires its own set of scanConverter, 
destBitmap and scProcess objects. 
 
The calls to Create(), SetSPx() and AddSPx() must all be from the same 
thread when using SPxPPIwindowGlx. 
 
The function returns an error code. 


===== PAGE 402 =====
Page 402 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxPPIwindow::DeparentWindow(void) 
 
This function removes the input window from the object and all links to it, 
restoring the window back to its original parent etc.  This should only be used 
shortly before destroying the SPxPPIwindow object, although it is not normally 
necessary because it will be called internally by the destructor anyway. 
 
8.4.8.3 Acceleration 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetAccelerated(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if accelerated composition (using OpenGL) is in use and FALSE 
otherwise. This function must only be used after the Create() function has 
been called. This function always returns FALSE for an instance of 
SPxPPIwindow, but may return TRUE for an instance of SPxPPIwindowGlx. 
 
 
static int SPxPPIwindowGlx::IsSupported(Display *dpy,  
int screen, char *errReturnBuf, int bufLen) 
 
This function (which is static and can therefore be called without reference to a 
particular class instance) tests whether OpenGL accelerated composition can be 
used on the specified X display and screen number. To determine why 
accelerated compositing is not supported, the errReturnBuf parameter can be 
used. If used, the caller-provided string is assumed to be of size bufLen bytes. 
On return from the function, the string will be filled with the reason or reasons 
why accelerated composition is not supported. The function returns TRUE if 
accelerated compositing is supported and FALSE if not. 
 
8.4.8.4 Information Retrieval 
 
SPxScDestBitmap * SPxPPIwindow::GetBitmap(int index) 
 
Return the SPxScDestBitmap pointer at index. The permissible values of index 
are 0 .. numRadarVideos -1, where  numRadarVideos was the value provided in 
the original Create() call. 
 
 
Pixmap SPxPPIwindow::GetCombinePixmap(void) 
 
 
Return the Pixmap created by the class to hold the composed image prior to 
copy to the display. The application may want to retrieve this Pixmap to help in 
repainting the screen on an expose event. This function returns 0 when called 
on an instance of SPxPPIwindowGlx. 
 
 
unsigned char * SPxPPIwindow::GetRadarImageMemory(int index) 
 
Return the pointer to the image memory at index used to hold the bitmap data. 
The bitmap is 32 bits per pixel and contains the radar video after conversion 
into a colour. The width of the bitmap is found from GetMaxWidth(). This 
function returns NULL when called on an instance of SPxPPIwindowGlx.  


===== PAGE 403 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 403 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetRadarImageStride(int index) 
 
Returns the stride of the Image bitmap. The stride is the number of bytes in 
one line of the image. The stride is often equal to the bitmap’s width 
(GetMaxWidth() x 4), but this need not be so. It is possible for the stride to be 
larger that this with only the first GetMaxWidth() * 4 bytes in each line 
representing valid bitmap data. In computing the memory location of a row, the 
stride must be used as the width of the memory. This function returns 0 when 
called on an instance of SPxPPIwindowGlx. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetMaxWidth(void)   
 
Returns the maximum width of the bitmap. This is the value originally provided 
in the Create() function.  
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetMaxHeight(void) 
 
Returns the maximum height of the bitmap. This is the value originally provided 
in the Create() function.  
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetWidth(void)  
 
 
Returns the current width of the bitmap. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetHeight(void) 
 
Returns the current height of the bitmap. 
 
 
XImage* SPxPPIwindow::GetImage(int index) 
 
Return the Image associated with radar display index. The Image is the X 
Windows client-side bitmap structure that holds the display data. This function 
returns NULL when called on an instance of SPxPPIwindowGlx 
 
 
8.4.8.5 Fading Controls 
 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetFadeTime(int channel) 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetFadeTime(void) 
 
Return the current fade time for the specified channel. If the fade mode is real-
time, the value returned by this function is the fade time in seconds. For 
sweep-based fade the value returned is the fade duration is sweeps. The 
second form of the function, which takes no channel number, is provided for 
legacy only and returns the fade time of channel 0. 
 
 


===== PAGE 404 =====
Page 404 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetFadeTime(double fadeTime) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetFadeTime(int channel, double fadeTime) 
 
Set the current fade time.  For a fade type of SPX_RADAR_FADE_SWEEP, the 
fadeTime is specified in terms of the number of sweeps. For a fade type of  
SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME, the fadeTime argument defines the number of 
seconds of fade. If no channel is specified (first form of function) then the set 
fade is set for all channels. If a channel is specified (seond form of function) 
then the fade time applies to that channel only. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetFadeType(int channel) 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetFadeType(void) 
 
The current fade type for the specified channel is returned as either 
SPX_RADAR_FADE_REPLACE, SPX_RADAR_FADE_SWEEP or 
SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME. The function that takes no arguments is provided 
for backward compatibility only and returns the fade type for channel 0. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetFade(int channel, UCHAR fadeType) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType) 
 
Set the current fade type as either SPX_RADAR_FADE_REPLACE, 
SPX_RADAR_FADE_SWEEP or SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME. If a channel is 
specified (first form of function), then the fade type is set for that channel only. 
If no channel is specified (second form of function) then the specified fade type 
is set for all channels. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetFadingIntervalMS(int fadeInterval) 
 
Set the minimum fading interval in milliseconds. This sets a lower limit to the 
interval between successive fades. Increasing the value of this parameter will 
reduce the number of fades per second, but will also reduce the number of 
levels used for fading and therefore the perceived smoothness of the fade. Note 
that fading can be no more frequently that the rate of calling 
CompositeDisplay(), so that value provided for fadeInterval should be at 
least at large as the interval between successive calls to CompositeDisplay(). 
 
8.4.8.6 
Radar Colour and Brightness 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarColour(UINT32 colour) 
 
Set the colour of all radar videos being displayed in this window. The colour 
argument is provided as a 24-bit RGB value with 8 bits of red, 8 bits of green 
and 8 bits of blue (least significant byte). 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 405 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 405 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarColour(int channelIndex, UINT32 colour) 
 
Set the colour of radar video index being displayed in this window. The 
permissible values of index are 0 .. numRadarVideos -1, where  
numRadarVideos was the value provided in the original Create() call. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarBrightness(int brightness) 
 
Set the brightness of each radar video in the window. The brightness value is 
in the range 0 to 100 for normal cases, although may be set to values larger 
than 100. 
 
When the radar brightness is set to greater than 100, and accelerated 
compositing is used, some components of the radar colour may become 
saturated, causing the displayed colour to change. When using standard 
compositing, the radar colour is preserved in this situation. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarBrightness(int index, int brightness) 
 
Set the brightness of radar video index in this window. The permissible values 
of index are 0 .. numRadarVideos -1, where  numRadarVideos was the value 
provided in the original Create() call. 
 
 
8.4.8.7 Window Assignment 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetWindow(Window appGraphics) 
 
This function sets the window containing the graphics for Application Window 
Compositing. This function can only be used if the Create() call for Application 
Window Compositing specified appGraphics as 0. The SetWindow function 
allows the window to be defined at a later point in the application. Once set 
(either through the Create function or SetWindow), the window for application 
graphics cannot be changed. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetDrawable(Window appGraphics) 
 
This function sets the drawable for Application Pixmap Compositing. This 
function can only be used if the Create() call for Application Pixmap 
Compositing specified the drawable as 0. The SetDrawable function allows the 
drawable to be defined at a later point in the application. Once set (either 
through the Create function or SetDrawable), the drawable for application 
graphics cannot be changed. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 406 =====
Page 406 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.4.8.8 Radar Video Control 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::EnableRadarVideo(int state) 
 
Enable (state = 1) or disable (state = 0) the display of all radar video in the 
window. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::EnableRadarVideo(int index, int state) 
 
Enable (state = 1) or disable (state = 0) the display of radar video index in the 
window. The permissible values of index are 0 .. numRadarVideos -1, where 
numRadarVideos was the value provided in the original Create() call. 
 
 
8.4.8.9 Pixmap Controls and Change Reports 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::EnablePixmapADrawing(int state) 
 
Enable (state = 1) or disable (state = 0) the drawing of Pixmap A in the 
compositing. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::EnablePixmapBDrawing(int state) 
 
Enable (state = 1) or disable (state = 0) the drawing of Pixmap B in the 
compositing. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetPixmapAEnable(void) 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetPixmapBEnable(void) 
 
Return the current state of the Pixmap A or B enable. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::PixmapBChanged(void) 
void SPxPPIwindow::PixmapAChanged(void) 
 
These functions are only relevant for Application Pixmap compositing. The 
functions report to the class that a pixmap has been changed by the 
application. This information is used by the class to force the composition 
process to take into account the full extent of the image, rather than just the 
incremental change associated with the radar video update. The application 
should call these functions when it changes the pixmaps. If it doesn’t call these 
functions, the screen will only show the changes in the pixmaps as the radar 
sweep rotates. This might be desired behaviour in some cases. 
 
When accelerated compositing is used, the full pixmaps will always be used in 
combination with the radar image. Calling these functions will have no effect 
when using accelerated (GLX) compositing. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 407 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 407 
void SPxPPIwindow::RedrawPixmapA(void) 
void SPxPPIwindow::RedrawPixmapB(void) 
 
Request a redraw of Pixmap A (B). This function calls the registered  redraw 
handler for the pixmap A (B) with the current view. There is no action if no 
redraw handler is registered. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::RegisterPixmapARedraw(  
 
void (*)(Pixmap, int, int, double, double, double, double)) 
void SPxPPIwindow::RegisterPixmapBRedraw(  
 
void (*)(Pixmap, int, int, double, double, double, double)) 
 
Register a function that the class calls when it wants to redraw pixmap A or B. 
The use of this callback is optional and depends on how the graphics and radar 
video are to be synchronised. If the redraw of the graphics is handled by the 
application, it is sufficient to call SetRadarView() to set the radar video to 
match the graphics. By registering functions to be called for a redraw, the client 
can call SetView(), which internally calls SetRadarView() and then uses the 
registered pixmap A and B redraw functions to redraw the graphics. 
 
 
8.4.8.10 View Changes and Retrieval 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetView(double cx, double cy, double width,  
                                                 double height) 
 
Set the view of the radar to the specified coordinates, where cx, cy define the 
centre of the view and width and height define the view size in metres. If 
functions are registered for pixmap A and B redraw, then these are called with 
the new view (in normal situations, the view height is not necessary since it is 
automatically calculated from the view width and the geometry of the window). 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarView(double cx, double cy, double w,  
                                                      double h) 
 
Set the view of the radar videos in the window. This function is called 
automatically by SetView(), which also invokes the redraw functions for the 
pixmaps A and B. Calling SetRadarView directly allows the radar video alone to 
be changed. 
 
 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetViewX(void) 
 
Return the x coordinate of the current view. 
 
 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetViewY(void) 
 
Return the y coordinate of the current view. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 408 =====
Page 408 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetViewW(void) 
 
Return the width of the current view in metres. 
 
 
double SPxPPIwindow::GetViewH(void) 
 
Return the height of the current view in metres. 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarPosition(double xMetres, double yMetres,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
              int clearTrails=0) 
 
Set the radar XY position in metres.  The default radar position is (0,0).  Setting 
clearTrails to a non-zero value will clear any trails in the radar video when 
the radar position is changed.  Setting clearTrails to 0 will preserve trails 
when the radar position is changed. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarPosition(int index,  
                                    double xMetres, double yMetres,  
 
 
 
 
 
         int clearTrails=0) 
 
Set the the radar XY position in metres of radar video index. The permissible 
values of index are 0 .. numRadarVideos -1, where  numRadarVideos was the 
value provided in the original Create() call. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::GetRadarPosition(double *xMetresRtn,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   double *yMetresRtn) 
 
Get the current radar position in metres. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::GetRadarPosition(int index,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   double *xMetresRtn,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   double *yMetresRtn) 
 
Get the current radar position in metres of radar video index. 
 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetViewControl(SPxViewControl *viewCtrl) 
 
Install an SPxViewControl object that will be used to manage the view of the 
radar video in a PPI window.  Refer to section 7.12 for further details. 
 
The radar index is a value between 0 and 3, used to identify the radar when 
there are multiple radars on the platform.  If not specified, it defaults to zero.  
The radar index is used to obtain the correct offset of the radar from the 
platform own-ship position, as defined for the SPxNavData object.  Refer to 
section 6.8.14.6 for further details.

8.4.8.11 Geometry Changes 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::CheckWindow(void) 
 
This function can be called to force a check of window geometry (using the 
registered SPxScFollowWin). If the destination window is observed to have 
changed, requests are made to redraw the pixmaps through the registered 
redraw functions. 
 
 
8.4.8.12 Sweep Line 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineColour(UINT32 colour) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineColour(int channel, UINT32 colour) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineType(SPxScSweepLineType_t type) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineType(int channel, SPxScSweepLineType_t 
type) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineColMode(SPxScSweepLineColMode_t mode) 
void SPxPPIwindow::SetSweepLineColMode (int channel,  
SPxScSweepLineColMode_t mode) 
UINT32 SPxPPIwindow::GetSweepLineColour(int channel) 
SPxScSweepLineType_t SPxPPIwindow::GetSweepLineType(int channel) 
SPxScSweepLineColMode_t SPxPPIwindow::GetSweepColMode(int channel) 
 
This function controls the presentation of a sweep line. The sweep line is a line 
drawn from the radar centre at the current azimuth of the radar. It can be used 
to provide a visual indication of the radar rotation. The type argument is either 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_OFF to disable the sweep line, or 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_TYPE_AUTO to enable the best type of sweep line for the 
current fading mode.  
 
For replace and sweep fading with SPxPPIwindow (not SPxPPIwindowGlx) the 8 
bits are split as 7 bits for radar and 1 bit for the sweep line, giving a single 
width line at the leading edge of the sweep. For real-time fading, the 8-bits are 
split as 5 bits for radar and 3 bits for the sweep line, giving a wider sweep 
across all updated azimuths, which fades faster than the normal radar.  
 
The colorMode value controls how the sweep line colour is combined with the 
scan converted radar colour for each pixel. The options are: 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_OFFSET implies the sweep line is added on 
top of the radar colour. 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_HIGHEST means the sweep line pixels will 
only be shown if they are higher than the radar level. 
 
SPX_SC_SWEEPLINE_COLMODE_REPLACE means the sweep line temporarily 
replaces the radar image regardless of the colour. 
 
The RGB colour provided is the colour/offset of the sweep line in the format 
0x00RRGGBB, with the blue components in the least significant byte and the 
most significant byte unused. The colour is completely independent of the radar 
colour.  
 


===== PAGE 410 =====
Page 410 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The SPxPPIwindowGlx class implements a single pixel sweep line in all fade 
modes (when using accelerated composition), whereas the single pixel sweep 
line for standard compositing is only supported in sweep and replace fade 
modes. 
 
8.4.8.13 Display Composition 
 
UINT32 SPxPPIwindow::CompositeDisplay(void) 
 
This is the main compositing function which must be called by the application at 
regular intervals (the composition rate). In a typical application, this function 
should get called from a timer at intervals of 50ms (20 updates per second) or 
faster up to 20ms (50 updates per second).  
 
The CompositeDisplay function  performs the following activities: 
 
(i) If real-time fading is enabled and sufficient time has elapsed since the last 
fade, the bitmaps associated with each radar video are faded. (All radar videos 
are assumed to be fading at the same rate). 
 
(ii) The graphics are combined with the radar according to the compositing 
mode and supplied input window/pixmaps. 
 
(iii) The composited scene is copied to the destination drawable (Window or 
Pixmap) defined in the original Create() function. 
 
The function returns a value that defines the work it did, as follows: 
 
Function 
Value 
Bits 0-3 
A number between 0 and 15 representing the number of radar videos that 
were considered for compositing. 
Bits 4-7 
A number between 0 and 15 representing the number of radar videos that 
are visible. 
Bit 8 
1 if a real-time fade occurred. 
Bit 9 
1 if a copy of the underlay pixmap occurred. 
Bit 10 
1 if a copy of at least 1 radar video occurred. 
Bit 11 
1 if a copy of the overlay pixmap occurred. 
Bit 12 
1 if a copy to the output bitmap or window occurred. 
 
The CompositeDisplay function blocks the caller until the composition is 
complete. The time taken for this will depend on the number of radar video 
channels and the size of the window. The copytest application supplied with 
the SPx Development release will profile the speed of the composition tests. 
This is generally an important first step in verifying the performance of the 
graphics hardware and associated drivers.  
 
When using SPxPPIwindowGlx, the Pixmaps used for underlays and overlays 
will be used every time CompositeDisplay() is called, rather than only after 
PixmapAChanged() or PixmapBChanged() is called. Updates to the Pixmaps 
should only be made in between calls to the CompositeDisplay() function. 


===== PAGE 411 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 411 
8.4.8.14 Sub-Area 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::EnableSubArea(int enable) 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetSubAreaEnable(void) 
 
Enable or disable the use of a sub-area for radar display. When a sub-area is 
enabled, the radar does not fill the window. Instead, the radar is displayed at a 
position and size specified with the SetRadarSubArea() function, within the 
window. 
 
This can be useful where the X11 Window is somewhat larger than the desired 
area of radar video. In the normal situation, an X11 application has a hierarchy 
of windows and one of those windows exactly encloses the area required for 
radar video. In this case, sub-area is not required. A sub-area is most often 
used with the SPxPPIwindow class as part of the Radar Display Coprocess 
application to add radar video to an existing Java application. See the “Radar 
Display Coprocess for Linux User Manual” for further details. 
 
 
int SPxPPIwindow::SetRadarSubArea(int x, int y, int w, int h) 
int SPxPPIwindow::GetRadarSubArea(int *x, int *y, int *w, int *h) 
 
Set or get the position and size of the sub-area for radar video. The positions 
and size values are in pixels. 
 
A sub-area is only supported when the PPI window is using non-accelerated 
composition. 
 
See the “Radar Display Coprocess for Linux User Manual” for further details. 
 
8.4.8.15 32-bit Blend Mode 
 
void SPxPPIwindow::Set32BitBlendMode(int op) 
 
Set the alpha composition mode to use when blending 32-bit graphics from a 
window with radar video. The default blend mode used is “Over” (op value = 
3), which means that graphics drawn into the window with maximum alpha 
appear as overlay graphics, and graphics drawn into the window with no alpha 
appear as underlay graphics. In some circumstances, an existing 32-bit window 
with graphics drawn with maximum alpha may be required to be displayed as 
an underlay. To achieve this the 32-bit blend mode can be set to 
“OverReverse” (op value = 4) which results in graphics drawn with maximum 
alpha appearing as an underlay. 
 
This is an advanced option that is normally not required. A specific case where 
it might be used is if all the graphics in a window are drawn with an alpha value 
of 255. In this case, the default blend mode would interpret all the graphics as 
being overlays, even the background, and therefore no radar would be visible. 
By setting the blend mode to “OverReverse” in this case, the alpha value for 
the graphics is inverted so appears as 0 and the radar is alpha blended with the 
graphics (and there are no overlays). Consult Cambridge Pixel for further 
details. 
 


===== PAGE 412 =====
Page 412 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd.

“1” for highest-wins mode and 
“2” for replace mode. 
GetSweepLineColMode()
