8.4.9. 
Window Manager Compositing with SPxScDestDisplayX11 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 is the class for display of radar video with Window Manager 
Compositing under X Windows and Linux. For most applications, this is not the 
preferred method for display of radar under X Windows. Consult Cambridge Pixel 
before using this class.   
 
The class SPxScDestDisplayX11 is likely to be decome deprecated in a future release 
of SPx. 
 
The preferred solution for most Linux applications is to use SPxPPIwindow class – see 
Section 8.4.7. 
 
The SPxScDestDisplayX11 class receives radar video from the scan converter and 
copies the data into an X Window, handling real-time fading and blending with 
underlay graphics. 
 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11::SPxScDestDisplayX11() 
 
Create a new SPxScDestDisplayX11 object. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayX11::Create (UCHAR screenID, 
 
    
 
 
 
UINT16 maxScreenW, UINT16 maxScreenH, 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 ulayWindow, UINT32 olayWindow, 
 
 
 
 
 
UIN32 flags) 
 
Create an instance of an SPxScDestDisplayX11class to handle the display of 
scan converted video from an SPxSC source.  
 
The screenID specifies the X Windows screen number for a multi-screen 
configuration. 
 
The values of maxScreenW and maxScreenH specify the maximum dimensions of 
the radar window in pixels. The radar window can never be resized larger than 
this dimension. Although it is possible to simply use the screen size for these 
numbers (they should never be larger than the screen size), it is desirable to 
keep the dimensions smaller if the maximum size of the window is known. 
 


===== PAGE 417 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 417 
The values of ulayWindow and olayWindow are the Window identifiers of the 
underlay and overlay X windows that the radar should be stacked with. They 
may be 0 if the window identifiers are not known or not relevant for the 
configuration. 
 
The flags argument supports the following options: 
  
SPX_DISPLAY_OVERRIDE_REDIRECT – Creates the X Window with the 
override redirect flag set, which forces the window manager to ignore the 
window. Set this to true in the situation where the window manager 
prohibits programmatic adjustment of stacking order. 
 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11::~SPxScDestDisplayX11 (void) 
 
Delete an instance of SPxScDestDisplayX11. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetUpdateInterval(int updateIntervalMS) 
 
This function defines the update interval for the window. The specified time is 
given in milliseconds and represents the interval between successive real-time 
updates of the radar video. The optimum value for the update interval is a 
trade-off between the smoothness of the sweep update and the amount of 
display processor resource available. A value of 100ms (the default), for 
example, means that the display is updated 10 times per second. The desired 
update time is used to calculate the level of intensity reduction at each fade 
update. For example, with a desired real-time fade time of 5 seconds (defined 
as the time to fade from peak value to background), and 10 updates per 
second, the intensity change between successive fades will be 255/50 = 5. Note 
that the actual updates to a window happen in the Update() function. 
 
 
static void SPxScDestDisplayX11::Update(void) 
 
This function must be called at regular intervals by something outside of the 
class. It is a static function, so it may be called without regard to a particular 
instance. The actual frequency of calling the function is only important in the 
sense that screen updates can be no more often than the frequency of calling 
this function. For example, if SetUpdateInterval has been used to set updates 
at every 100ms, then the period of calling Update() much be at least every 100 
ms. Calling it less often means that the updates occurs less frequently and the 
duration of the fade will not be consistent with the requested fade time. This 
function will be commonly called under an X timer. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 418 =====
Page 418 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetMinFadeInterval(int minFadeIntervalMS) 
 
Set the minimum interval between fades for real-time fading mode. If the value 
is provided as zero, it is interpreted as meaning that the fading should occur at 
the same interval as the display updates. If the value is > 0 then the value 
defines the interval in milliseconds between fades. If the value is < 0 then it 
defines a fading interval that is a multiple of the update interval. For example: 
 
SetMinFadeInterval(50) 
- Fading is no more often than 50ms 
SetMinFadeInterval(0) 
- Fading is no more often than the current  
  update interval. 
SetMinFadeInterval(-2) 
- Fading is no more often than twice the   
  current update interval. 
 
Note that the value being set here is the minimum fade interval. The actual 
fade interval is calculated from the requested fade time, with the restriction 
that the value used can be no smaller than the minimum being set here. For 
example, for a fade time of 10 seconds, with 255 levels to fade, the duration 
between fades is about 40ms (10,000 / 255). So setting a minimum fade 
interval that is less than 40 ms will have no affect. If the minimum fade interval 
is set to 80ms, for example, then the fade processing will recalculate the per-
update fade amount to ensure fading over 10 seconds (for this example). The 
consequence of increasing the minimum fade time, therefore, is to effectively 
reduce the number of fade levels in this example to 128. The benefit of 
increasing the fade time is a reduction in display processing.  
 
 
static Display * SPxScDestDisplayX11::OpenXDisplay(char *displayString) 
 
This function reports the name of the X Server that the radar display is to use. 
The format of the displayString conforms to the normal rules for naming the 
display, including NULL, which means use the DISPLAY environment. The 
displayString argument is passed to XOpenDisplay to open the connection to 
the X Server. If the application is running on the same machine as the radar 
display, then the application should have opened the connection to the X server 
already. In this case, use UseDisplay() instead of OpenXDisplay(). 
 
 
static int SPxScDestDisplayX11::UseDisplay(Display *display) 
 
This function tells the radar display module to use an existing X Windows 
connection that the calling context has already opened.  
 
 
Pixmap SPxScDestDisplayX11::GetPixmap(void) 
 
Returns the Pixmap identifier of the server-side off-screen pixmap associated 
with the radar video updates. It only makes sense to call this function when the 
application and the display are running on the same machine, therefore this 
function is not supported over the network interface. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 419 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 419 
XImage * SPxScDestDisplayX11::GetImage(void)  
 
Returns the XImage identifier of the client-side off-screen image associated with 
the radar video updates. It only makes sense to call this function when the 
application and the display are running on the same machine, therefore this 
function is not supported over the network interface. 
 
 
Window SPxScDestDisplayX11::GetWindow(void)  
 
Returns the Window identifier of the window used for the radar display. It only 
makes sense to call this function when the application and the display are 
running on the same machine, therefore this function is not supported over the 
network interface. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::UseWindow(int state)  
 
This function can be used to tell the display module not to use a window for 
output of radar video. By calling with function with state = 0, the display 
module will disable any display of video in a window. It will still output data into 
the XImage and the Pixmap (but see UsePixmap). This function allows a local 
application to have more control over the presentation of the radar video, 
optionally handling the display of the pixmap or image itself. The default 
behaviour is to use the window. It only makes sense to call this function when 
the application and the display are running on the same machine, therefore this 
function is not supported over the network interface. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::UsePixmap(int state)  
 
This function can be used to disable the display module from using a pixmap 
and, by implication, a Window. The scan converted video is processed and 
faded as normal in the XImage structure, where it can be retrieved by an 
application. Again, this allows the application to have full control over the 
presentation of the radar video. It only makes sense to call this function when 
the application and the display are running on the same machine, therefore this 
function is not supported over the network interface.

                                              int argument) 
 
This function can be used to generate a test pattern on the display for system 
testing. The supported test patterns are defined in Table 9. Note that there is a 
difference where the test patterns are generated.

Set the position of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates. Note that 
this function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application.

Set the position and size of the scan conversion window in screen coordinates.

int SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetWinParam(UINT16 param,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT32 arg1, UINT32 arg2,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 UINT32 arg3) 
 
This function is used to set a number of window parameters as follows: 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_TEST_PATTERN (value = 10): Create 
a test picture in the window. The value of arg1 defines the test picture 
according to Table 9. 
 
Param = SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_UNDERLAY (value = 11): Set the ID 
of an X Window, provided in arg1, to be the underlay to the radar 
window. This parameter is often supplied at the time the window is 
created, but it if is not known at that time, or changes, it can be reset. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PARAM_OVERLAY (value = 12): Set the ID of 
an X Window, provided in arg1, to be the overlay to the radar window. 
This parameter is often supplied at the time the window is created, but it 
if is not known at that time, or changes, it can be reset. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_XEVENT_MASK (value=13): Set the X Event 
mask to be used in reporting events to the X Underlay window. The mask 
is provided in arg1 and is used as an input to XSelectInput. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_UPDATE_INTERVAL (value=14): Set the update 
interval (in milliseconds) for the window. 
 
Param SPX_DISPLAY_WIN_PRIORITY (value 15): Set the priority of the 
radar window. This is only relevant when multiple radar scan conversions 
are to be displayed in a window. By default, the priority is set when a 
SxScDestDisplayX11 object is created. The most recently created object 
has the highest priority and appears top-most of the radar windows. The 
priority was be changed with this parameter. The number is a integer 
with a larger number denoting a higher priority. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 fadeRate) 
 
This function sets the fade mode for the window. The fadeType is 0 for real-
time fade, 1 for sweep-based fade and 2 for replacement mode. The fadeRate 
is the duration of the real-time fade in units of 10s of milliseconds. Note that 
this function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 422 =====
Page 422 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vx, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
REAL32 vw, REAL32 vh) 
 
This function is called to report a view change in the radar window. The display 
typically clears the radar video in readiness for new data arriving. Note that this 
function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not normally 
be called from a client application. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid,  
 
 
 
 
 
UCHAR red, UCHAR green, UCHAR blue) 
 
This function is called to set the radar colour. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The radar colour is given by the red, green and blue components, 
each in the range 0..255. The specified colour represents the colour of 
maximum intensity radar with brightness = 100 (see SetRadarBright). Note 
that this function is called automatically by the scan converter and should not 
normally be called from a client application. 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR bright) 
 
This function sets the radar brightness. The rid argument is intended to 
support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently unused 
(set to 0). The brightness is in the range 0 to 100. Note that this function is 
called automatically by the scan converter and should not normally be called 
from a client application. 
 
 
UCHAR SPxScDestDisplayX11::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness. The rid argument is 
intended to support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently 
unused (set to 0). 
 
 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR lutId, unsigned char 
*lutData) 
 
Set the look-up table that converts radar pixel valus into display colours. This 
function is complementary to SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright. Use 
SetRadarLUT to define an arbitrary mapping from 8-bit radar intensity values to 
colours (could be false-colour for example). To set the radar colout to a linear 
scale from background to peak use the SetRadarColour and SetRadarBright 
functions. The lutID argument define a LUT identifier and is currently unused – 
set to 0. The lutData is an array of ARGB colour values of size 256*4 = 1024 
bytes. The data is organised as an array of 256 x 32-bit words, with each word 
holding a colour value in the format alpha (most significant 8-bits), red, green, 
blue (least significant 8 bits). Note that this function is called automatically by 
the scan converter and should not normally be called from a client application. 
 
When used with the class SPxPPIwindowGlx, due to the way radar is combiend 
with graphics only the colour and brightness may be configured and therefore 
this function has no effect. 


===== PAGE 423 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 423 
void SPxScDestDisplayX11::ShowRadar(UCHAR rid, UCHAR state) 
 
Enable or disable the display of radar video. The rid argument is currently 
unused (set to 0). The state is 0 to stop the radar and clear the display, 1 for 
normal updates and 2 to stop and let the display fade (if real-time fade 
enabled). Note that this function is called automatically by the scan converter 
and should not normally be called from a client application. 
 
 
int SPxScDestDisplayX11::PatchSet(UCHAR format,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 numPatches, UINT16 *patchData) 
 
This is the function used to reports radar patches to the display. The format is 
specified in Table 12. The patchData value is a pointer to a sequential list of 
numPatches patches. Note that this function is called automatically by the scan 
converter and should not normally be called from a client application. 
static void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetDebugFlags(UINT32 flags) 
 
Sets the debug flags. The flag is a bitwise combination of the following 
components: 
 
 
D_COMMANDS – Show information on commands sent to the class 
 
D_PATCH_UPDATES – Show patch update information 
 
D_FADE – Show detailed fade information 
 
D_SETLUT – Show details of the LUT being programmed. 
 
 
static void SPxScDestDisplayX11::SetDebug(FILE *file) 
 
Set a FILE for debug output. The value of file is a file handler for output of the 
messages.  
 
 
8.4.9.1 Multithreading issues with SPxScDestDisplayX11 
 
If SPxScDestDisplayX11 is used in a display server, then that server is a self-
contained X Windows client and there are no multi-threading issues to consider. 
However, if the class is used in an application that is responsible for other X Windows 
activity then the application will become multi-threaded. Although 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 does not create a new thread, it does run in the thread created 
for a RIB. In this situation, the application needs to use the X Windows calls for thread 
protection. These are: 
 
XInitThreads() 
XLockDisplay() 
XUnlockDisplay() 
 
Refer to standard X Windows programming documentation for use of these functions. 
If these functions are not used correctly, the application may generate errors of the 
form "Xlib: unexpected async reply", which are an indication that multiple threads 
were trying to access common data structures. 
 
 


===== PAGE 424 =====
Page 424 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
8.4.10. 
SPxScFollowX11 Class 
 
The class SPxScFollowX11 allows a radar window to be automatically slaved to an X 
Window, ensuring that changes to the geometry or visibility state of the X Window are 
automatically reflected in the state of the corresponding radar window. 
 
This class may be used with Application Pixmap, Window Manager or User 
Compositing. It is not required for Application Window Compositing. 
 
The class is created with an X Window or Widget, which is the controller. This 
controller is assumed to be the overlay Window/Widget in a three-layer stack 
(overlay-radar-underlay), or the underlay in a two-layer stack (radar-underlay). In 
addition, the class constructor takes the scan conversion object, which is represented 
by an SPxSc object. 
 
When the SPxScFollowX11 object has been created, additional radar windows can be 
added using the AddSC() member function. These additional radar windows are all 
slaved to the controlling X Window or Widget. 
 
It is possible to add another X Window or Widget to be slaved using the AddWindow() 
or AddWidget() member function. In the case of the three layer stack, this will be 
used to add an underlay Window/Widget as a slave of the controlling Window/Widget. 
 
So for the three-layer stack situation, the procedure is: 
 
 
Create an SPxScFollowX11 object specifying the scan conversion object and 
the overlay Window/Widget in the constructor 
 
 
Call AddSC() if there are other scan conversions in the same window 
 
 
Call AddWindow() or AddWidget() to add the underlay Window/Widget 
 
With this completed, the overlay Window/Widget will automatically set the size and 
position of the radar window and the underlay window. 
 
 
SPxScFollowX11::SPxScFollowX11(SPxSc *radarWindow,  
 
 
 
 
 
 Display *display,  
 
 
 
 
 
 Window windowToFollow, int flags = 0) 
 
Create an object to follow the state of the Window windowToFollow. Changes in 
the position, size or visibility of the specified X Window are reported to the 
instance of SPxSc radarWindow. The optional flags SPX_SET_WINDOW_UNDERLAY 
or SPX_SET_WINDOW_OVERLAY may be set to tell the scan converter that the 
specified X Window to be interpreted as the underlay or overlay of the radar 
window.  
 
 
SPxScFollowX11::SPxScFollowX11(SPxSc *radarWindow,  
 
 
 
 
 
 Widget widgetToFollow, int flags=0) 
 
This is similar to the previous function, but instead references an X Widget. The 
radar window is slaved to the X Window associated with the Widget. It is 


===== PAGE 425 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 425 
permissible to call this constructor when the Widget has been created but the X 
Window does not yet exist (that is Widget has not yet been realised).  
 
The optional flags SPX_SET_WIDGET_UNDERLAY or SPX_SET_WIDGET_OVERLAY 
may be set to tell the scan converter that the specified Window of the Widget is 
to be interpreted as the underlay or overlay of the radar window.  
 
 
SPxScFollowX11::~SPxScFollowX11() 
 
Delete an SPxScFollowX11 object. 
 
 
int SPxScFollowX11::CheckWindow(void) 
 
When an SPxScFollowX11 object is created through either the Widget or 
Window constructor, the checks on the X Window state are made by calling this 
function at periodic intervals. The function returns 1 if a change in window 
geometry was observed and returns 0 otherwise. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::CheckUnderTimer(int intervalMS) 
 
Instead of the application initiating the calls to CheckWindow, an automatic poll 
can be implemented using an X timer. The function CheckUnderTimer creates a 
timer that causes an automatic call of CheckWindow every intervalMS 
milliseconds. A typical value for this interval is 250ms. This function can only be 
used if the application is using X Intrinsics as the Widget toolkit (this function 
cannot be used with GTK, for example). 
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::SetWindow(Window window) 
 
Sets or changes the X window being followed. Normally the window being 
followed will not change. However, if the Window is not known at the time the 
Window-based constructor of SPxScFollowX11 is called, then this function can 
subsequently set the Window. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::SetWidget(Widget widget) 
 
Sets or changes the X Widget being followed. The X Window associated with the 
Widget is retrieved and the geometry of that Window is used to set the radar 
window geometry.   
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::AddWindow(Window window,  
 
 
 
 
 
 int flag = SPX_SET_WINDOW_UNDERLAY) 
 
This function defines an X Window that the controlling Window will manage. 
This specified Window has its state and geometry set automatically to match 
the state and geometry of the Window or Widget specified in the class 
constructor (or subsequently changed with a SetWindow, SetWidget call). This 
function is normally used to define an underlay window that is desired to track 
an overlay window, for example when being used with a Display Server. 


===== PAGE 426 =====
Page 426 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The flag is either SPX_SET_WINDOW_UNDERLAY or SPX_SET_WINDOW_OVERLAY. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::AddWidget(Widget widget,  
 
 
 
 
 
int flag = SPX_SET_WINDOW_UNDERLAY) 
 
This function defines an X Widget that the class will manage. This specified 
Window of the Widget has its state and geometry set automatically to match 
the state and geometry of the Window or Widget specified in the class 
constructor (or subsequently changes with a SetWindow, SetWidget call). This 
function is normally used to define an underlay widget that is desired to track 
an overlay window, for example when being used with a Display Server. 
 
The flag is either SPX_SET_WINDOW_UNDERLAY or 
SPX_SET_WINDOW_OVERLAY. 
 
 
void SPxScFollowX11::RemoveTimer(void) 
 
Remove the timer and stop the automatic polling. 
 
 
int SPxScFollowX11::GetX(void) 
int SPxScFollowX11::GetY(void) 
int SPxScFollowX11::GetW(void) 
int SPxScFollowX11::GetH(void) 
 
This set of functions retrieves the current x, y, w and h of the window being 
followed. Note that the x and y are in screen coordinates. 
 
 
int SPxScFollowX11::AddSC(SPxSc *radarWindow) 
 
Add an additional radar window to be managed by the X11 Window/Widget 
specified in the original constructor. A maximum of SPX_FOLLOW_WIN_MAX radar 
windows may be managed by a SPxScFollowWin. The function returns 
SPX_NO_ERROR if the radar source was added correctly, otherwise returns 
SPX_ERR_TOO_MANY_SC_WINS if there are too many windows. Note there is 
currently no way of removing a scan conversion window from being managed. 
 
 
8.4.11. 
SPx X11 Convenience Functions 
 
The following convenience functions are provided by the SPx X11 library: 
 
 
void SPxNoWindowDecorate(Display *display, Window window) 
 
This function instructs the window manager not to decorate the specified 
window with the normal top-level title bar and border. This function can be 
used with an underlay window that is to be displayed directly underneath a 
radar and overlay window. 
 
 


===== PAGE 427 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 427 
Visual *SPxFindArgbVisual(Display * display, int screen) 
 
This function finds and returns a 32-bit ARGB visual for the specified screen of 
the display. A 32-bit ARGB visual is required for application compositing and for 
the overlay window in a window manager composited application. 
 
 
Visual *SPxFindRgbVisual (Display * display, int screen) 
 
This function finds and returns a 24-bit RGB visual for the specified screen of 
the display. 
 
8.5. Networking with SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet 
 
The class SPxScSourceNet is used as part of the network distribution of scan 
converted video (remote scan conversion architecture). 
 
8.5.1. 
Using SPxScSourceNet for receipt of scan converted video 
 
 
 
Radar video scan converted by a remote SPxScSourceLocal may be passed to a 
Windows or X11 display module across a network interface. The pair of classes 
SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet encapsulate the network interface. 
 
From the point of view of the scan converter, there is no difference between supplying 
data to a local display or a network display. The capabilities and interface are the 
same. 
 
The network interfacing between SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet is shown in 
Figure 100: 
SPxSsSourceLocal 
SPxScDestNet 
SPxScSourceNet 
Network 
SPxScDestDisplayX11, 
SPxScDestDisplayWin, 
SPxScDestBitmap 
 


===== PAGE 428 =====
Page 428 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
Figure 100 - Network Interaction between SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet 
 
The sequence of events in establishing a connection is as follows: 
 
 
The SPxScSourceNet sends heartbeat UDP messages (either to a unicast or 
multicast address). The heartbeat indicates whether there is an existing 
connection to the class instance. 
 
 
An instance of SPxScDestNet can detect the heartbeats and, if there is more 
than one server responding, select a server to use. In most situations the 
instance of SPxScDestNet will know which network address it should connect 
to, so the detection of the heartbeat is really just a confirmation that the 
server is alive and well. 
 
 
The instance of SPxScDestNet then does a TCP connection to the server. 
 
 
With the connection established, SPxScDestNet can send commands to 
create new display windows, change colours, move windows etc. These 
commands are sent over the TCP interface, ensuring a guarantee of delivery. 
 
 
In addition to the commands, SPxScDestNet sends regular UDP messages 
containing packages of scan converted radar video. These “patches” are 
small areas of coverage that are generated to approximate the sweep of the 
radar video. 
 
 
The instance of SPxScSourceNet can send message and events back to 
SPxScDestNet over a separate UDP interface. 
 
 
 
 
 
SPxScDestNet 
SPxScSourceNet 
UDP Heartbeat 
TCP 
Connection 
TCP 
Commands 
UDP Data 
UDP Messages 
+ events 
SPxScDestDisplayX11, 
SPxScDestDisplayWin  
or 
SPxScDestBitmap 
Display Module 
SPxScSourceLocal 
Scan Converter 


===== PAGE 429 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 429 
8.5.2. 
SPxScSourceNet Class  
 
The SPxScSourceNet class can be used in two ways. It can be used to handle all 
network interfacing and decoding of commands, or it can be used to just decode the 
commands, leaving the user’s application to handle the networking issues.  
 
The preferred method of use lets the class handle all the networking. In this situation, 
it sends UDP heartbeats to an address and port provided in the constructor to the 
class. It listens on a command port for TCP connection requests from a remote 
network interface. When it receives a connection, it starts listening for commands and 
data, whilst making periodic calls into a user’s background process. The commands 
and data packets are decoded and sent to a display class. 
 
The alternative use of the class is simply to use it to decode message received by 
another means. In this situation, the application code takes full responsibility for 
receiving the commands and data packets. 
 
8.5.2.1 Construction and Destruction 
 
 
SPxScSourceNet::SPxScSourceNet(int serverPort, 
 
 
 
 
 
FILE *log = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
int heartbeatPort = 0,   
 
 
 
 
 
const char *heartbeatAddress = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
const char *serverAddress = NULL) 
 
Create an instance of the SPxScSourceNet class. The object will listen on port 
serverPort for connection requests. The optional log file can be used for 
debugging. The default heartbeat port and heartbeat destination address can be 
overridden with heartbeatPort and heartbeatAddress. 
 
 
SPxScSourceNet::SPxScSourceNet (FILE *log=0) 
 
Create an instance of the SPxScSourceNet, but do not use the networking of 
the class. The optional log argument defines a device for output of debugging 
information. 
 
 
SPxScSourceNet::~SPxScSourceNet() 
 
Delete an instance of SPxScSourceNet, including all of the attached display 
windows. 
 
 
8.5.2.2 Event Handling 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetEventSource(int eventFD) 
 
Add a source of events for processing by the main event loop. The provided 
eventFD is a file descriptor. 
 


===== PAGE 430 =====
Page 430 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetEventHandler(void (*fn)(void)) 
 
Provide a function to be called when there is input on the file descriptor 
provided by SetEventSource(). 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::CommandLoop(void) 
 
This executes the main command loop. It should be called after a connection is 
established, i.e. after TryConnect returns something other than -1. The 
function waits for command and data on the input ports. After a specified time 
of waiting, set with SetTimeout(), the function will call the users’s background 
processing function, set from SetBackgroundProc(). The CommandLoop() 
function returns when the TCP socket that it is listening on is closed by the 
host, or when the user’s background processing function calls 
StopCommandLoop(). 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::StopCommandLoop(void) 
 
This function is typically called in the user’s background processing function. It 
causes the command loop to exit after the next attempt to read a command or 
patch returns (either through a successful read or a timeout). After the 
command loop returns, the application is responsible for accepting a new 
connection as required. 
 
 
8.5.2.3 Background Processing 
 
SPxScSourceNet::SetBackgroundProc(void (*fn)(void*), void* userData) 
 
Define a function that will be called by the class as periodic intervals between 
processing commands. The interval between calls to this function is set from 
SetTimeout(). The function itself is invoked using the provided userData. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetTimeout(int seconds, int microseconds) 
 
 
Set the timeout period in the main processing loop. The period defines the time 
interval that the class will wait for a command or a data request before calling 
the user’s background processing. The value is given in seconds and 
microseconds, although the precision of the timing is system dependent. After 
the user’s processing function returns, the control loop waits for a new 
command or data patches. 
 
8.5.2.4 Network Functions 
 
int SPxScSourceNet::SocketsOK(void) 
 
Return 0 if the sockets are open and ready. Returns non-zero if the open failed. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 431 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 431 
void SPxScSourceNet::EnableHeartbeats(int state) 
 
Enable the transmission of heartbeats. By default, heartbeat transmission is on. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SendHeartbeat(void) 
 
Send a heartbeat. The format of heartbeat is as shown in Table 10. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetID(int id) 
 
Set an identifier that appears in the heartbeat and could be used to identify the 
display server providing the information. The value is not used other than as a 
field in the heartbeat. 
 
int SPxScSourceNet::GetID(void)  
 
Return the current identifier used in the heartbeat. 
 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetState(int state)  
 
Set the state field in the UDP heartbeat. The state field is not interpreted by the 
class and may be used to indicate current activity. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceNet::GetState(void)  
 
Return the current state field in the UDP heartbeat. 
 
 
int SPxScSourceNet::TryAccept(void) 
 
Try to accept a connection for a command interface. This function is non-
blocking. If no connection request has been made, this function returns -1. 
 
8.5.2.5 Debugging 
 
void SPxScSourceNet::SetLogFile(FILE *logFile) 
 
Set a log file to be used for the output of debugging messages.

8.5.2.7 The Command Interface to SPxScSourceNet 
 
Commands can be sent to SPxScSourceNet from a remote client using the class 
SPxRemoteServer (see “SPx Server Client Interfacing” manual). The commands are 
sent over the TCP connect as ASCII strings (with one exception). The list of 
commands is shown in Table 11. 
 
The non-ASCII exception is the setradarlut command. This command is an ASCII 
string with appended binary data to store the contents of the look-up table (256 x 4 
bytes).

8.5.2.8 The Patch Interface to SPxScSourceNet  
 
Radar video may be distributed from a remote scan converter to the display in a set of 
patches, which are rectangular areas of radar coverage. The SPxScSourceNet class 
receives the patch list over a UDP interface.  
 
The patch data is formatted as shown in Table 12.

If the top bit of x is set, this indicates 
“update now”, i.e. this is the logical point 
to update the display (end of sector, full 
window patch, new random spoke etc.). 
Data for patch is of size w*h*d bytes,

If necessary, an extra 0 is added to the end 
of the data to ensure that the length of 
data is even.

8.5.3. 
SPxScDestNet Class Interface 
 
The SPxScDestNet class is relevant if you are writing a server application that wants 
to send radar out from the scan converter across a network interface to a remote 
client. As an example, the SPxScDestNet class is used by the SPx Scan Conversion 
server. 
 
The SPxScDestNet class presents an interface to the scan converter for the delivery of 
scan converted data across the network. The scan converter sees the same interface 
through SPxScDestNet as it would through the display classes SPxScDestDisplayWin 
or SPxScDestDisplayX11. It should be noted that, in most situations, it is the scan 
converter class itself, rather than a user’s application, that will call the functions of the 
SPxScDestNet class. 
 
SPxScDestNet packages commands and patch data for delivery across a network with 
UDP and TCP protocols to an instance of class SPxScSourceNet on the other side of 
the network. 
 
SPxScSourceLocal 
SPxScDestNet 
SPxScSourceNet 
Network 
SPxScDestDisplayX11, 
SPxScDestDisplayWin, 
SPxScDestBitmap 
 


===== PAGE 437 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 437 
SPxScDestNet and SPxScSourceNet can be viewed as implementing a remote 
procedure call so that the scan converter can deliver display commands and radar 
video to a display class on another machine.  
 
The basic sequence of instructions for using SPxScDestNet is as follows: 
 
1. Use MakeNetwork to define the network interfaces: 
 
SPxScDestNet::MakeNetwork("192.168.2.12") 
 
2. Make a connection to the remote server 
 
SPxScDestNet->Connect() 
 
3. Use the constructor to get a new SPxScDestNet display for each scan 
conversion window. 
 
SPxScDestNet *display = new SPxScDestNet(); 
 
4. Call the Create() function on the new instance to initialise it. 
 
display->Create(radarSource, 0, 1000, 1000, 0, 0); 
 
5. Send commands and radar data as required 
 
display->SetWinGeom(0,0,500,500); 
display->PatchSet(0, 1, buffer, len); 
 
8.5.3.1 SPxScDestNet Class Definition 
 
SPxScDestNet::SPxScDestNet() 
 
Constructor for SPxScDestNet. After calling the constructor, initialise the 
instance with the Create() function. 
 
 
SPxScDestNet::~SPxScDestNet(void) 
 
Deletes a SPxScDestNet instance. 
 
 
static int SPxScDestNet::MakeNetwork(char *serverAddress,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
int serverPort=0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
int heartbeatPort=0, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
char *heartbeatAddress=NULL) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This is a static class function, meaning it should get called once, not per 
instance of the class. The function is called to set up the network interfaces to 
the remote server. The serverAddress is the IP address of the remote server 
in the normal IP dot notation (for example “192.168.2.100”). The port on the 
remote server may be specified by serverPort, but only if the server is 
configured to listen on the same por number. The value of heartbeatPort is 
the port number that SPxScDestNet listens on for heartbeats from the server. 


===== PAGE 438 =====
Page 438 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
On a subsequent Connect() call, the call will make a TCP connection to the 
remote server.  
 
 
static int SPxScDestNet::Connect(unsigned int timeoutMsecs) 
 
A TCP connection is made on the port and IP address configured in a previous 
MakeNetwork call. 
 
The default value of timeoutMsec is 0 which means the call is blocking and will 
only return when the connection is made. Specifying a non-zero timeout means 
wait for up to that time (in milliseconds) for the connection to succeed before 
returning. In practice, it might be a little longer due to the thread scheduling 
times in the operating system. To allow for network transmission times and 
latency in the server accepting the connection and sending the first handshake 
message, it is recommended that 1000 msecs is the smallest time used to 
reliably get a connection if one is possible. 
 
If the function times out (timeoutMsec was greater than 0 and no connection 
was made), the function returns SPX_ERR_TIMEOUT. At this point, the client 
application can call MakeNetwork() again to change the server address/port it is 
trying to connect to then call Connect() again. This can be repeated for 
different servers until one of them succeeds. 
 
 
static int SPxScDestNet::Disconnect(void) 
 
The current TCP connection is disconnected. Another Connect call is needed 
before any commands can be sent. 
 
 
static void SPxScDestNet::ShowMessages(void (*fn)(char*, int)) 
 
Defines a function that the class will call to display messages. The function is 
called with a char * and a string length. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::Create(UCHAR screenID,  
    
 
 
 
 UINT16 maxWidth, UINT16 maxHeight, 
 
    
 
 
 
 UINT32 ulayWindow, UINT32 olayWindow, 
  
 
 
 
 int replyBufSz = 0, char *replyBuf = NULL) 
 
For each new scan conversion window, the class constructor is called followed 
by the Create function. The Create function sends a newwin command to the 
network to create a new window in the display object handled by 
SPxScSourceNet. The window identifier returned from newwin is then 
associated with the local SPxScDestNet object. Before calling the constructor 
for the first time a successful call to the static class function MakeNetwork() 
should have been successfully made. This will set up the network connection to 
the display server. 
 
The value of screenId defines the identifier of a screen in a multi-screen 
system. The values of maxWindowW and maxWindowH define the upper bound for 
the size of the radar window. The window can be resized but is always clipped 
to the size of the parameters provided. Although the values can be provided as 


===== PAGE 439 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 439 
the screen resolution (they should never be any larger), it is desirable to 
provide them as smaller numbers is a window is known to be of reduced size, 
for example. The values ulayWindow and olayWindow are the identifiers of 
windows that are respectively the overlay and underlay windows to the radar 
video. 
 
An optional buffer can be supplied, replyBuf, which is of length replyBufSz 
bytes. If provided, the value string returned from the call will be returned in the 
buffer. 
 
 
static int SPxScDestNet::PollHeartbeat 
                                   (SPxSCDisplayHeartbeat *heartbeat,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
   UINT32 *sendingAddress = 0); 
 
This function may be called by the application using SPxScDestNet. It causes a 
non-blocking poll of the heartbeat queue for incoming heartbeats. This function 
is typically called from a timer. If this function is never called, the class still 
functions properly, but heartbeats will not be detected. If sendingAddress is 
non-null, the function stores the IP address of the sending server in network 
endian format. 
 
 
This function formats a heartbeat structure as: 
 
typedef struct 
{ 
 
UINT32 magic; 
 
// Magic Number 
 
UINT16 appId; 
 
// Display Server ID 
 
UINT16 state; 
 
// State of server 
 
UINT32 clientAddr; 
// Address of sending client 
 
UINT16 listeningPort; 
// Port that the server is listening on 
 
UINT32 res1; 
 
 
// Reserved 
 
UINT32 res2; 
 
 
// Reserved 
} SPxScNetHeartbeatStruct; 
 
 
static int SPxScDestNet::PollMessage(char * buf, int len) 
 
This function may be called by the application using SPxScDestNet. It causes a 
non-blocking poll of the message queue for incoming messages. This function is 
typically called from a timer. If this function is never called, the class still 
functions properly, but messages will not be detected. The caller is responsible 
for providing a buffer buf, and defining the buffer’s length len. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetWinPos(UINT16 screenX, UINT16 screenY) 
 
Sets the new window position (top left) to be the specified value. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetWinGeom(UINT16 screenX,UINT16 screenY,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      UINT16 screenW,  
                                            UINT16 screenH)  
 
Sets the new window position and size to be the specified values. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 440 =====
Page 440 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxScDestNet::SetWinParam(UINT16 param, UINT16 arg1,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      UINT16 arg2,  
                                            UINT16 arg3) 
 
Set a parameter for the window. The parameter code is defined by param. Up 
to three arguments may be provided, which are interpreted according to the 
parameter being set and the display server’s capabilities. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetFade(UCHAR fadeType, UINT16 fadeRate)  
 
Set the fade type and fade rate for a window. Set fadeType to 0 for real-time 
fading, 1 for sweep-based fading and 2 for replacement mode.  
 
The fadeRate parameter defines the duration of the fade as: 
 
For REAL_TIME fade the value of fadeRate is the fade time in units of 10s of 
milliseconds. For example, a value of fadeRate of 500 indicates a fade time of 
5 seconds (500 x 10ms = 5000ms). 
 
For SWEEP fade, the value of fadeRate is the number of sweeps of fade.  
 
For REPLACE mode, the fadeRate parameter is not used. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetView(REAL32 vx, REAL32 vy, REAL32 vw, REAL32 vh) 
 
Tell the display that the radar view has changed. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetRadarColour(UCHAR rid, UCHAR mode,  
 
 
 
 
 
   UCHAR red, UCHAR green, UCHAR blue) 
 
Set the radar colour. The peak radar colour is set from the values of red, green 
and blue components, each in the range 0..255. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetRadarBright(UCHAR rid, UCHAR bright)  
 
Set the radar brightness in the range 0..100. 
 
 
UCHAR SPxScDestNet::GetRadarBright(UCHAR rid) 
 
This function returns the current radar brightness. The rid argument is 
intended to support future expansion to multiple radar channels and is currently 
unused (set to 0). 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetRadarLUT(UCHAR lutId, unsigned char *lutData)  
 
Set a look-up table for the radar colours. The size of the LUT is 1024 bytes, 
being 256 entries of 32-bit ARGB. The lutID argument define a LUT identifier 
and is currently unused – set to 0. The lutData is an array of ARGB colour 
values of size 256*4 = 1024 bytes. The data is organised as an array of 256 x 


===== PAGE 441 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 441 
32-bit words, with each word holding a colour value in the format alpha (most 
significant 8-bits), red, green, blue (least significant 8 bits). 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::ShowRadar(UCHAR rid, UCHAR state)  
 
Set the visibility state for the radar. For normal operation, state =1. Set state 
to 0 to stop updating the radar and clear the display. Set state to 2 to stop 
updating the radar and let the existing radar fade away (if real-time fade is 
enabled). 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::SetRadarPosition(REAL32 x, REAL32 y, UCHAR clear) 
 
Sets the centre of the radar in the view. The clear parameter is used to 
determine if the old radar trails should be cleared (clear = 1) or not (clear = 0). 
See Section 7.9.1.1 for a discussion of the use of this function in achieving a 
true motion radar display. 
 
 
int SPxScDestNet::PatchSet(UCHAR format, UINT16 numPatches,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
     UINT16 *patchData, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
     UINT16 patchLen) 
 
Set the new visibility patches for the radar. These commands are sent to the 
display server as UDP messages. The caller is responsible for constructing the 
patch data list, which is of the form defined by Table 12 (in the field name 
PatchData. Note the additional padding byte that is needed if the size of the 
patch is otherwise odd). The PatchSet function provides the format of the 
patches (also defined in Table 12), the number of patches, numPatches,  being 
reported in this command, and the total length, patchLen, of the patch data 
provided by patchData. 
 
8.5.4. 
Building a Radar Display Server with SPxScSourceNet 
 
A display server built with SPxScSourceNet comprises an instance of SPxScSourceNet 
and an instance of a display class, such as SPxScDestBitmap, SPxScDestDisplayX11 
or SPxScDestDisplayWin, which does the drawing to the screen.  
 
When SPxScSourceNet decodes a newwin message on the command interface it must 
create a new destination by instantiating a display object. To define which display type 
the class must create, the application must provide an implementation of the function 
SPxCreateDisplay(). This function is called by SPxScSourceNet. Your 
implementation of this function is simply to construct the display object required. For 
example: 
 
 


===== PAGE 442 =====
Page 442 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxScDest *SPxScCreateDisplay(UCHAR screenID,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 maxW, UINT16 maxH,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 uLay, UINT32 oLay 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 scType, UINT32 *errCode) 
{ 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 *display = new SPxScDestDisplayX11(); 
 
*errCode = display->Create(screenID, maxW, maxH, uLay, oLay); 
 
return display; 
} 
 
For Window Manager (Compiz) compositing in X Windows, or 
 
SPxScDest *SPxScCreateDisplay(UCHAR screenID,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 maxW, UINT16 maxH,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 uLay, UINT32 oLay 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 scType, UINT32 *errCode) 
{ 
SPxScDestDisplayWin *display = new SPxScDestDisplayWin(); 
 
*errCode = display->Create(screenID,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
maxW, maxH, uLay, oLay); 
 
return display; 
} 
 
For Microsoft Windows or for general Bitmap processing 
 
SPxScDest *SPxScCreateDisplay(UCHAR screenID,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT16 maxW, UINT16 maxH,  
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 uLay, UINT32 oLay 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 scType, UINT32 *errCode) 
{ 
 
SPxScDestBitmap *display = new SPxScDestBitmap(); 
 
*errCode = display->Create(maxW, maxH,  
                                 SPX_BITMAP_8BITS); 
 
return display; 
} 
 
When SPxScSourceNet decodes a delwin message on the command interface, it will 
release the SPxDestSc object by calling SPxScReleaseDisplay().  The default 
implementation of this function will delete the SPxDestSc object.  The application may 
override this function, for example:   
 
void SPxScReleaseDisplay(SPxScDest *disp) 
{ 
/* Handle display release here */ 
} 
 
 


===== PAGE 443 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 443 
8.5.5. 
Complete Minimal Display Server 
 
A sample display server is shown by the code segment below. This is shown as a self-
contained main() program, so that the program serves as a simple display server. 
Similar capability could be enclosed in a thread (instead of main) and embedded as a 
display processing function in an application that might also control the scan 
converter. For an example of this, see the spxX11Composite example program 
supplied with SPx. 
 
/*************************************************************************
* 
/ 
/ SPX Display Client Application for Window Manager Compositing with 
Compiz 
/ 
/*************************************************************************
/ 
 
#include “SPxLibUtils/SPxLibUtils.h” 
#include “SPxLibSc/SPxLibSc.h” 
#include “SPxLibX11/SPxLibX11.h” 
 
#define BACKGROUND_POLL_INTERVAL_US 50000  
Display *XDisplay; 
SPxScSourceNet *SourceNet; 
 
main(int argc, char **argv) 
{ 
 
SPxInit(); 
 
XDisplay = SPxScDestDisplayX11::OpenXDisplay(NULL); 
 
SourceNet = new SPxScSourceNet(“192.168.2.100”); 
 
SourceNet->SetBackgroundProc(SPxDisplayServerBackground, 0); 
 
SourceNet->SetTimeout(0,BACKGROUND_POLL_INTERVAL_US); 
 
 
// Wait for connection 
 
while (1) 
 
{ 
 
 
if (SourceNet->TryAccept() != -1)  
 
 
{ 
 
 
 
// Got a connection – start the command loop. 
 
 
 
SourceNet->CommandLoop(); 
 
 
} 
 
 
 
SourceNet->SendHeartbeat(); 
 
 
// Wait 1 second. 
 
 
sleep(1); 
 
} 
} 
 
static void SPxDisplayServerBackground(void *userData) 
{ 
 
// Background function – calls Fading 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11::Update(); 
} 
 
 
SPxScDest *SPxScCreateDisplay(UCHAR screen, UINT16 w, UINT16 h,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 w1, UINT32 w2,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
UINT32 scType, UINT32 *err) 
{ 
 
SPxScDestDisplayX11 *newDest = new SPxScDestDisplayX11(); 
 
*err = newDest->Create(screen, w, h, w1, w2); 
 
return newDest; 
} 


===== PAGE 444 =====

SPxLib Programming Manual

===== PAGE 445 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 445 
9. 
A-Scan Display 
 
The SPx A-Scan functions provide a high-performance implementation of an A-Scan 
window for the display of radar video. 
 
 
A-Scan is normally associated with the display of radar video on an oscilloscope, with 
the video being displayed on one or two traces to show amplitude as a function of 
time. The display shows the video from each return of the video and is commonly 
used with a tracking radar to control the direction of camera or firing device. It is less 
commonly used with search radars, although it still has value in examining the signal, 
in much the same way as connecting an oscilloscope might help to measure and 
monitor. 
 
The SPx A-Scan display can be added into an application with just a few function calls. 
It receives radar video from a PIM or another SPx Process. 
 
Figure 101 - Using the RIB and PIM for an A-Scan application 
 
The SPx A-Scan display is implemented for Windows and X11 under Linux. Most of the 
functionality and A-Scan API is consistent across these window systems, so this 
section is applicable to all environments. Where certain features or APIs are different 
between Windows and X11, that is identified in the section. Sample code is provided 
at the end of this section for both Windows and X11. 
 
9.1. A-Scan Updates 
 
The A-Scan window updates a discrete number of times per second – this is called the 
update rate. In the Windows version of the A-Scan display, the update of the window 
is the responsibility of the class. The client defines the update period and the class 
handles the updating. In the X11 version of the class, the application must call the 
Update() function at regular intervals to implement the window updates. In this case, 
the Update is typically called in a timer from the client’s main loop.  
 
The A-Scan module is used with the SPx A-Scan process (see Section 6.7.12). The 
SPx process provides regular inputs of data into the A Scan module. The module 
stores this data, process it and then updates the display at the update rate. In 
general, data is arriving at the A-Scan input much more frequently than the display is 
being output. 
For example, consider a radar source generating data at 2 kHz to an A-Scan window 
configured to update at 40 ms. With 25 updates per second, there are 50 returns 
PIM 
Radar Source 
RIB 
A-Scan 
Process 
Window 


===== PAGE 446 =====
Page 446 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
received in the update period. The question arises as to how those 50 returns are 
displayed. The A-Scan process can display all returns, or reduce the number with a 
combination process based on highest-wins, average or sub-sample.  
 
The number of data points shown in the A-Scan window is equal to the pixel width of 
the window (less a little for display margins). If more data points are provided by a 
return then these are sub-sampled by the A-Scan processing. If range correlation is 
required, this must be done in the PIM. The displayed range scale of the A-Scan 
window can be changed to zoom-in to the incoming data, thereby revealing the full 
detail 
 
To support mouse interaction under Linux, X11 events relating to the A-Scan X 
window must be forwarded to the A-Scan class – see the ReportXEvent() function for 
further details. 
 
9.2. A-Scan Class Overview 
 
The A-Scan display is shown in a window of user-defined size. A common situation in 
an A-Scan display is to have two traces. One, by convention the upper, shows the 
radar video from minimum to maximum range. The lower window shows a selected 
highlight from the upper portion. Although any number of A-Scan windows can be 
created, the situation of one being an enlarged portion of the first is supported as a 
special case. It is possible to highlight an area from the primary trace and have the 
secondary trace display that area. The highlighted area can be selected with cursors. 
 
Figure 102 - A-Scan Display Components 
 
The size of the window to be used for the A-Scan display is provided on construction 
of the class instance. The left, right, top and bottom margins may be changed after 
creation. 
 
If the display of the cursor is enabled (SetCursor function), then a cursor moves with 
the mouse and shows the current range (Windows version only - in X Windows, it is 
the responsibility of the client to handle events on the window and send commands to 
the class to display the cursor). 
 
A Cursor 
B Cursor 
AB Highlight 
Left 
margin Cursor 
Title of Window 
Title 


===== PAGE 447 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 447 
Left-clicking the mouse marks the A cursor for the current range of the cursor. The 
next click marks the B cursor. With A and B marked, the displays shows the interval 
highlighted. At this point, the secondary trace can be made to display the selected AB 
interval using the function SetHighlightAB on the primary trace. Alternatively, by 
calling SetABselectMode with argument SPX_ASCAN_ABSELECT_AUTO the secondary 
trace is automatically set to the AB interval. 
 
9.3. SPxAScanWindow  A-Scan Class 
 
9.3.1. 
Create and Delete 
 
SPxAScanWindow::SPxAScanWindow() 
 
The constructor of the class takes no arguments. After construction, call 
Create() to initialise the class. By default, the A-Scan window allocates a 
buffer of size 4k to store a return (if longer returns are provided they would get 
truncated). The buffer size can be increased by calling SetBufferLen() after 
the construction but before calling Create(). 
 
Note there is a different prototype for Create() for Windows and X11. 
 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::Create(CWnd *parent, double x, double y, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
     double w, double h, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
     int maxWidth = -1,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
     int maxHeight = -1) 
 
Creates the A-Scan window in Windows. The window’s position and size are 
specified by x, y, w and h and are defined relative to the parent window. If x 
and y are > 0 they define the pixel position of the A-Scan window relative to 
the parent. If x and y are < 0 they define the position as a fraction of the 
parent’s width and height. If w and h are > 0 they define the fixed size of the 
A-Scan window in pixels. If w and h are < 0 they define the size of the window 
as a fraction of the parent’s window size. For example, h = -0.5 means that the 
A-Scan window is half the height of its parent. Where negative values of x, y, w 
and h are used, implying a dependency on the parent, the geometry is updated 
on each Update of the A-Scan window.  
 
The optional arguments maxWidth and maxHeight define the maximum size of 
the window in pixels. If the values are omitted, the maximum is the given w,  if 
x > 0, or otherwise is the width of the screen. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 448 =====
Page 448 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxAScanWindow::Create(Display *display, Drawable destination, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      int x, int y, int w, int h,  
                                       int maxWidth = -1,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
      int maxHeight = -1 
int screen = 0) 
 
Creates the A-Scan Window in X11. The window’s position and size are specified 
by x,y,w and h and are defined relative to the parent window. If x and y are > 
0 they define the pixel position of the A-Scan window relative to the parent. If x 
and y are < 0 they define the position as a fraction of the parent’s width and 
height. If w and h are > 0 they define the fixed size of the A-Scan window in 
pixels. If w and h are < 0 they define the size of the window as a fraction of the 
parent’s window size. For example, h = -0.5 means that the A-Scan window is 
half the height of its parent. Where negative values of x, y, w and h are used, 
implying a dependency on the parent, the geometry is updated on each Update 
of the A-Scan window.  
 
The optional arguments maxWidth and maxHeight define the maximum size of 
the window in pixels. If the values are omitted, the maximum is the given w,  if 
x > 0, or otherwise is the width of the screen. If the screen number is provided, 
the value must match the screen on which the destination drawable was 
created. 
 
The function uses the client-supplied destination Drawable (either a Window or 
a Pixmap) in the specified X11 Display. The destination drawable may be 
provided as 0 if it is not known at the time of calling Create(). It may then be 
reported to the class by calling the member function SetDestination(). 
 
 
SPxAScanWindow::~SPxAScanWindow(void) 
 
Destructor for the A-Scan Window. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetBufferLen(int buflen) 
 
This function can only be called after the constructor and before Create(). It 
defines the size of the buffer that the class will used to hold a return. The 
default size is 4096 samples per return. If a return that is longer than the 
buffer is input, the additional input data will be lost. 
 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::GetBufferLen(void) 
 
This function returns the length of the allocated buffer. 
 
9.3.2. 
Activity State 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetActive(int activeState) 
int SPxAScanWindow::GetActive(void) const 
 
Change or retrieve the current activity state of the A-Scan Window. If 
activeState = 0 no further updates occur. 
 


===== PAGE 449 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual

9.3.3. 
Display Presentation and Colours 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetRangeUnits( double multiplier, char *string) 
 
This function changes the units for labelling. The default labelling is km. The 
value of multiplier is the conversion from metres to the desired unit, and the 
value of string is the units label. For example, calling SetRangeUnits with 
multiplier = 1.0/1852.0 and string – “nm” causes the display to be labelled in 
nm. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetBackgroundColour(UINT red,  
                                         UINT green,  
                                         UINT blue) 
 
This function sets the RGB colour for the background of the A-Scan window. 
The red, green and blue components are in the range 0 to 255. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetMargins(int left, int right, int top, int bottom) 
 
Sets the margins around the window – see Figure 102. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetTitle(char *titleString) 
 
Sets a title that appears on the top of the A-Scan window. 
 
 
char * SPxAScanWindow::GetTitle(void) 
 
Get the title for the A-Scan Window. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetTraceColour(UINT8 red, UINT8 green,  
                                    UINT8 blue) 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetTraceColour(UINT32 rgb) 
 
Set the colour of the primary trace as either red, green and blue components in 
the range 0 to 255, or as an RGB value formatted as 0xRRGGBB. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxAScanWindow::GetTraceColour(void) 
 
Get the primary trace colour as an RGB value formatted as 0xRRGGBB. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAuxColour(UINT32 rgb, unsigned int index) 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAuxColour(UINT8 red, UINT8 green, UINT8 blue,  
                       
 
 
 
    unsigned int index=0) 
 
Set the colour of an auxiliary trace as either red, green and blue components in 
the range 0 to 255, or as an RGB value formatted as 0xRRGGBB.  Index may be 
set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two auxiliary traces supported. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 450 =====

Cambridge Pixel Ltd.
