Get an auxiliary trace colour as an RGB value formatted as 0xRRGGBB.  Index 
may be set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two auxiliary traces supported. 
 
9.3.4. 
Highlight Window and Cursors 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetHighlightAB(void) 
 
Set the slave A-Scan window to show the range defined by the highlighted area 
between the A and B cursors on the primary display. The A and B cursors must 
be displayed for this to work. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetHighlightShift(int shift) 
 
When an AB highlight area is set, a small shift in the y-axis can be applied to 
the displayed signal to emphasise the highlighted area. The parameter defines 
the level of the shift in pixels. The default is 12 (+ve is down). See  Figure 103. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetCursorA(int x) 
 
Sets the position of the A cursor. The value of x is pixels from the left hand 
edge of the window. The A cursor is only visible if ShowCursor() has been 
called with state = 1. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetCursorB(int x) 
 
Sets the position of the B cursor. The value of x is pixels from the left hand 
edge of the window. The A cursor is only visible if ShowCursor() has been 
called with state = 1. 
 
Figure 103 - The shifted portion of the AB highlight region 
 
 
The vertical shift (down) of the highlighted area of the trace 


===== PAGE 451 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 451 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetHighlightSlave(SPxAScanWindow *) 
 
This function allows one A-Scan window to be slaved to another. The first 
window (the one making the call) can define a second window (the one given as 
an argument) to display the highlighted area. Various controls are provided in 
the class to display and manipulate a highlight area. 
 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::GetHighlightShift(void) 
 
Retrieve the current value for the highlight shift. The value is in pixels. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetShowHighlight(int state) 
 
Controls whether the highlight region is displayed. Set state to 1 to enable the 
highlight display and 0 to disable it. 
 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::GetShowHighlight(void) 
 
Returns the current show state of the highlight region, as set from 
SetShowHighlight. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetABselectMode(int mode) 
 
Set the method of selecting the contents of the secondary trace. If mode is 
SPX_ASCAN_ABSELECT_MANUAL, the content of the secondary trace is set from 
the AB interval only when the function SetHighlightAB is called. If the mode is 
SPX_ASCAN_ABSELECT_AUTO, the secondary trace is set automatically when the 
AB interval is defined through the mouse actions in the primary trace. 
 
9.3.5. 
Update Control 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetUpdateInterval(int updateIntervalMS) 
 
This function is only used for Windows. Set the update interval for the display. 
After each update period, the latest set of received video data is displayed. This 
function is only application for Windows. For X11, the client must call Update() 
at regular intervals to update the A-Scan displays. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::Update(void) 
 
This function must be called by an X Windows client to update the A-Scan 
output. The function causes the accumulated set of returns to be composed on 
the display and copied to the currently set destination drawable. 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 452 =====
Page 452 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetDestination(Drawable drawable) 
 
This function sets the destination drawable (either an X Windows Pixmap or 
Window) for the X Windows output. The drawable must have been created with 
a 32-bit ARGB visual. No output occurs until a valid destination drawable has 
been provided.  
 
9.3.6. 
Combine Mode 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetCombineMode(int mode) 
 
This function is now superseded by SetAziCombineMode. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAziCombineMode(int mode) 
 
Set the method of combining new returns received between display updates. 
The permissible values are: 
 
SPX_ASCAN_AZI_COMBINE_ALL – Shows all the returns that are presented to the 
A-Scan window. Every return passed to the NewReturn function is drawn. This 
mode must be used with the display mode of FADE – otherwise data is written 
to the display but will never be removed.  
 
SPX_ASCAN_AZI_COMBINE_HIGHEST – a highest wins operation is performed on 
all traces received in the update interval.  
 
SPX_ASCAN_AZI_COMBINE_AVERAGE – an averaging process is applied to all 
traces received in the update interval. 
 
SPX_ASCAN_AZI_COMBINE_LAST_ONLY – only the last trace received in the 
update interval is displayed. 
 
The default value is SPX_ASCAN_AZI_COMBINE_HIGHEST. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetRanCombineMode(int mode) 
 
Set the method of combining samples in range. Depending on the size of the A-
Scan window, one or more range samples may map onto a single point on the X 
scale.  This function determines how the sample values are combined to 
calculate the Y value of the trace at each X position. The permissible values 
are: 
 
SPX_ASCAN_RAN_COMBINE_LOWEST – a lowest wins operation is performed on all 
sample values that map to a point on the X scale. 
 
SPX_ASCAN_RAN_COMBINE_HIGHEST – a highest wins operation is performed on 
all sample values that map to a point on the X scale. 
 
SPX_ASCAN_RAN_COMBINE_AVERAGE – an averaging process is applied all sample 
values that map to a point on the X scale. 
 


===== PAGE 453 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 453 
SPX_ASCAN_RAN_COMBINE_SUBSAMPLE – the first sample value that maps to a 
point on the X scale is used. 
The default value is SPX_ASCAN_RAN_COMBINE_HIGHEST. 
 
9.3.7. 
Sweep Indicator 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::ShowSweepIndicator(int state) 
 
If enabled (state = 1), a small icon on the top left corner of the A-Scan window 
shows a rotating sweep line to indicate the current azimuth being processed. 
 
9.3.8. 
Data Transfers 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::NewReturn(SPxReturn *return) 
 
This function is used to input a new return of data to the A-Scan window. 
Although an application can call this directly, the preferred method is to use the 
SPx Process SPxProAScan (see Section 6.7.12), which will automatically call 
NewReturn(). Generally, only the SPx library calls this function. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::NewAuxData(unsigned char *data, int numBytes, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
     int index=0) 
 
Load a new auxiliary return into the A-Scan window.  An auxiliary return may 
be used to display up to two additional traces alongside the primary trace.  
Note that no azimuth correlation is performed when multiple auxiliary traces 
are loaded as each trace will overwrite the last.  The auxiliary trace data is 
copied from the buffer provided.  The scaling of data in the buffer must match 
the scaling of the primary trace.  For example, if the primary trace contains 
2048 samples that cover a range of 20480 metres, the same scaling of 10 
metres/sample will be used to scale data read from the auxiliary trace buffer.  
Index may be set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two auxiliary traces supported. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::NewAux(SPxReturn *newReturn,  
                            unsigned int index=0) 
 
This function operates in the way as NewAuxData() except that data is read 
from a return payload.  The return header is not used and the same scaling 
rules outlined for NewAuxData() apply.  Index may be set 0 or 1 to identify one 
of the two auxiliary traces supported. 
 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::IsAuxSet(unsigned int index=0) 
 
This function returns TRUE if an auxiliary trace has been loaded or FALSE if not 
set.  Index may be set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two auxiliary traces 
supported. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 454 =====
Page 454 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAuxFromNextReturn(unsigned int index=0) 
 
This function is used to load an auxiliary trace from the next live return 
received (via NewReturn()).  Index may be set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two 
auxiliary traces supported. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::ClearAux(unsigned int index=0) 
 
Clear an auxiliary trace. Index may be set 0 or 1 to identify one of the two 
auxiliary traces supported. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAuxMode(int mode) 
 
The data loaded for AUX-0 (auxiliary trace at index 0) may be combined with 
live data to modify the primary trace.  The following modes are supported: 
 
SPX_ASCAN_AUX_MODE_NONE – Do not combine AUX-0 with live data (input 
mode). 
 
SPX_ASCAN_AUX_MODE_SUBTRACT – Display the live video with the AUX-0 trace 
amplitude subtracted. The minimum amplitude is limited to zero.  
 
SPX_ASCAN_AUX_MODE_DIFFERENCE - Display the live video with the AUX-0 trace 
amplitude subtracted.  A positive half-scale shift is applied to both the reference 
trace and live video.  Amplitude values are limited to +/- half-scale amplitude. 
 
The default is SPX_ASCAN_AUX_MODE_NONE. An example is shown in the following 
diagram: 
 
Figure 104 - Example showing A-Scan AUX-0 Trace Modes 


===== PAGE 455 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 455 
9.3.9. 
Display Mode 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetDisplayMode(int mode) 
 
This function defines the scanning mode of the display as follows: 
 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_AZIMUTH – Only updates the display if the incoming azimuth 
matches a defined azimuth. The azimuth is set using the function 
SetDisplayAzimuth(). 
 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_RANGE – Only updates the display if the incoming azimuth is 
within a defined interval. The interval is set using the function 
SetDisplayRange(). 
 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_SCAN – Shows every azimuth. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetDisplayAzimuth(int azi) 
 
Set the azimuth to display when the display mode is SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_AZIMUTH.  
Note that the azimuth range is 0 to 65536 to represent 0 to 360 degrees. 
 
int SPxAScanWindow::GetDisplayMode(void) 
 
Returns the current display mode. See SetDisplayMode for possible values. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetHighlight(double range, double rangeWidth) 
 
Set the highlight region to the specified centre range and width. The units are 
metres. 
 
9.3.10. 
Fade Control 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetFadeMode(int fadeMode) 
 
Set the fade mode. The options are SPX_ASCAN_OVERWRITE or SPX_ASCAN_FADE. 
In FADE mode, the existing data is faded (reduced in intensity) on each update. 
In OVERWRITE mode, new data replaces old data. The FADE mode produces a 
display with persistence, so that a number of recent traces can be seen 
together at decaying amplitude 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetFadeRate(double fadeRate) 
 
Set the fade rate. The permissible value is in the range 0..1 and indicates the 
factor by which current data is reduced on each update. Note that the degree of 
fade achieved depends on the value of the fadeRate parameter and also on the 
update interval. For an update interval of 50 ms and a fadeRate of 0.7, for 
example, the data is reduced by a factor of 0.7 on each 50 ms update. So radar 
video arriving at a peak value of 255 will be reduced over each 50 ms update as 
(178, 124, 87, 61, 42, 30, 21, 14, 10, 7), that is fading to near black in 500 


===== PAGE 456 =====
Page 456 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
ms. Conversely, a value of fade time of 0.95 requires around 65 updates to get 
to same value, i.e. 3.5 seconds for the same update interval. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetAveragerTC(int tc) 
 
Sets the time-constant associated with the averaging process used to combine 
returns. The permissible value is in the range 0..100. The default value is 50. 
Returns are combined according to: 
 
Xi = T * Xi / 100 + (100-T)* Di / 100); 
 
Where Xi is the current integrated return at sample I, Di is the new data at that 
sample and T is the time-constant. 
 
9.3.11. 
View Control 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetViewAsSamples(int minSample, int maxSample) 
 
Set the view being displayed in the window. The parameters specify the 
minimum and maximum sample to be displayed. The actual range in metres 
represented by these samples is extracted from the header of the return. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetViewAsWorld(double minRangeWorld,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
double maxRangeWorld) 
 
Set the view being displayed in the window. The parameters specify the 
minimum and maximum range in metres to be displayed.  
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetDisplayRange(int startAzi, int endAzi) 
 
Set the minimum and maximum azimuths to display when the display mode is 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_RANGE. Note that the azimuth range is 0 to 65536 to 
represent 0 to 360 degrees. 
 
9.3.12. 
Cursors 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::ShowCursor (int state) 
 
Sets the visibility state of the cursors. Call with state = 1 to show the cursors.  
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetCursor(int x) 
 
Sets the position of the principal cursor. The value of x is pixels from the left 
hand edge of the window. The A cursor is only visible if ShowCursor() has been 
called with state = 1. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 457 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 457 
void SPxAScanWindow::ShowMovingCursorX(int enable) 
 
Show or hide a moving cursor on the X axis that indicates the current range 
associated with the mouse cursor position in the window. This is enabled by 
default. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::ShowMovingCursorY(int enable) 
 
Show or hide a moving cursor on the Y axis that indicates the current intensity 
associated with the mouse cursor position in the window. This is disabled by 
default. 
 
 
9.3.13. 
Labels 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetLabelFlag(int label) 
 
This function can be used to control the appearance of the A-Scan window. The 
label argument is a bitwise OR of the following label options: 
 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_LABEL_Y – Label to Y axis with numbers 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_LABEL_X – Label the X axis with numbers 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_GRATICULE – Show the graticule grid 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_XAXIS – Show the x axis 
SPX_ASCAN_SHOW_YAXIS – Show the y axis. 
 
By default, all the above items are shown. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::ClearLabelFlag(int label) 
 
This function may be used to clear one or more display options on the A-Scan. 
Refer to the SetLabelFlag for a list of flag options. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetXAxis(unsigned int numGridLines, 
                              unsigned int numDecimalPlaces=1, 
                                       int relativeLabelling=FALSE) 
 
Set the scaling, precision and labelling method used to create the A-Scan X 
(range) axis. The value of numGridLines defines the number of vertical grid 
lines that are drawn. A value of 0 indicates that the number of lines should be 
automatically calculated based on the window width. The value of 
numDecimalPlaces sets the number of decimal places to display for each label. 
Use SetRangeUnits() to define the range units to be used for labelling. Setting 
relativeLabelling to TRUE indicates that the range label values should be 
relative to the centre range of the A-Scan rather than relative to zero range. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 458 =====
Page 458 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetYAxis(double minVal,  
                              double maxVal, 
                              unsigned int numGridLines, 
                              unsigned int numDecimalPlaces=0, 
                              const char *units=NULL,  
                              int adjustLeftMargin=TRUE) 
 
Set the scaling and units used to create the A-Scan Y (intensity) axis. The axis 
is numbered from minVal at the bottom to maxVal at the top. The value of 
numGridLines defines the number of horizontal grid lines that are drawn. For 
example with minVal set to 0 and maxVal set to 100, setting numGridLines to 
10 will result in 10 horizontal gridlines labelled 0, 10, 20, etc. Setting 
numGridLines to 5 will result in 5 horizontal gridlines labelled 0, 20, 40, etc. 
The value of numDecimalPlaces sets the number of decimal places to display 
for each label. Units may define a string that is appended to each label, e.g. 
“%” or “Volts”. Typically the adjustLeftMargin flag should be set to TRUE (or 
omitted) to allow the left margin to be resized to accommodate the longest 
label that will be displayed. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxAScanWindow::SetXAxisType(int type) 
 
This function is currently only supported in X11. 
 
Call this function to set the X (range) axis type. Set type to 
SPX_ASCAN_AXIS_TYPE_LINEAR (the default) to set a linear X axis or 
SPX_ASCAN_AXIS_TYPE_LOG to set a logarithmic axis.  
When using a logarithmic axis, it is recommended that the axis is configured 
using SetXAxisWorld(). This function allows the spacing between grid lines to 
be defined in metres, as opposed to SetXAxis(), which defines the spacing in 
pixels. 
 
 
void SPxAScanWindow::SetXAxisWorld(double gridLineMetres, 
                                   unsigned int numDecimalPlaces, 
                                   int relativeLabelling) 
 
This function is currently only supported in X11. 
 
Set the scaling, precision and labelling method used to create the A-Scan X 
(range) axis. The value of gridLineMetres defines the spacing between vertical 
grid lines in metres. The value of numDecimalPlaces sets the number of 
decimal places to display for each label. Use SetRangeUnits() to define the 
range units to be used for labelling. Setting relativeLabelling to TRUE 
indicates that the range label values should be relative to the centre range of 
the A-Scan rather than relative to zero range. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 459 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 459 
9.3.14. 
X Windows Events 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxAScanWindow::ReportXEvent(XEvent *event) 
 
To support mouse interaction under X Windows with Linux, X events must be 
forwarded to the A-Scan window. All X events may be forwarded and this 
function will filter those of interest. Alternatively, only those events triggered by 
the SPX_ASCAN_XEVENT_MASK for the A-Scan drawable (as set in the Create() 
or SetDestination() functions) can be forwarded. 
 
If native X is being used, then the application’s X event loop can simply pass all 
events received to this function for processing. 
 
If Xt Intrinsics is being used, then the XtAddEventHandler() function should be 
used with the SPX_ASCAN_XEVENT_MASK mask to install a function that forwards 
events of interest to the SPxAScanWindow object – see the SPxX11ascan 
example application for further details. 
 
 
If GTK is being used, a handler should be installed for the “realise” event 
associated with the widget that is used for A-Scan display. Within this handler, 
the gdk_window_add_filter() function should be called to add a handler that 
is called for all X Events associated with the A-Scan window before GTK 
processes the events. Within this second handler function, each GdkXEvent 
should be cast to an XEvent and passed to the A-Scan window’s 
ReportXEvent() function. 
 
Alternatively, when using GTK, the standard GTK callback mechanism can be 
used to install handlers for the “button-press-event”, “button-release-event”, 
and “motion-notify-event” events, and from within each handler, call the 
relevant OnButtonPress(), OnButtonRelease(), or OnMouseMotion() 
SPxAScanWindow function. ReportXEvent() calls these functions internally. The 
ReportXEvent() function should be the preferred method, because this allows 
any additional functionality added to the SPxAScanWindow class to be 
automatically available without adding extra GTK handlers to the application. 
 
9.4. A-Scan Sample Code – Windows 
 
The following code fragment creates two A-Scan windows, where the second one is a 
slave of the first. A RIB is created to receive radar video from a Test Generator and a 
PIM connects the RIB to the A-Scan display process. 
 
 
 
AScan = new SPxAScanWindow(); 
 
AScan->Create(ParentWindow, 0, 0, 768, 180); 
 
AScan->ShowCursor(TRUE); 
 
AScan->SetABselectMode(SPX_ASCAN_ABSELECT_AUTO); 
 
 
AScan2 = new SPxAScanWindow(); 
 
AScan2->Create(ParentWindow, 0, 190, 768, 180); 
 
AScan->SetHighlightSlave(AScan2); 
 
 
SPxRIB *buffer = new SPxRIB(4096*16, NULL); 
 


===== PAGE 460 =====
Page 460 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
TestGen = new SPxTestGen(buffer, 600, 2.0, 1000, 37); 
 
       SPxPIM *pim = new SPxPIM(buffer, 1024, 1,  
                 SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK, SPX_PIM_AZI_OVERWRITE,  
                 SPX_PIM_INPUT(1) ); 
 
 
/* Create the RunProcess */ 
 
new SPxRunProcess(SPxProAScan, NULL, pim, AScan); 
 
 
/* Enable the test generator */ 
 
TestGen->Enable(1); 
 
The output of this is shown in Figure 105. 
 
Figure 105 - Display of A-Scan from sample code

10. SPx Server and Trackers 
 
The SPx software includes radar video servers, plot extractors and trackers. These are 
typically supplied as executable server applications, which may be controlled and 
accessed through a network interface from a client application. The client application 
may be receiving radar video and/or track data. 
 
Detailed interfacing information for the SPx Server is supplied in the following manual: 
 
SPx Server Client Interfacing 
Document Number: CP-25-110-31 
 
A client application accesses a SPx server application through the SPxRemoteServer 
class. This class is documented in the above manual. 
 
If it required to build a new server application using the SPx classes (for example, a 
custom radar video server instead of the standard SPx Server) then the class 
SPxServerInterface may be used to provide the communication interface that SPx 
client classes can connect to. Note that this class is used in the server. In the situation 
where a standard Cambridge Pixel server such as SPx Server, SPx Scan Conversion 
Server or SPx Radar Image Server is used then SPxServerInterface is not relevant. 
 
10.1. SPxServerInterface Class  
 
The SPxServerInterface class can be used in two ways. It can be used to handle all 
network interfacing and decoding of commands, or it can be used to just decode the 
commands, leaving the user’s application to handle the networking issues.  
 
The preferred method of use lets the class handle all the networking. In this situation, 
it sends UDP heartbeats to an address and port provided in the constructor to the 
class. It listens on a command port for TCP connection requests from a remote 
network interface. When it receives a connection, it starts listening for commands and 
data, whilst making periodic calls into a user’s background process. The commands 
and data packets are decoded and sent to a display class. 
 
The alternative use of the class is simply to use it to decode message received by 
another means. In this situation, the application code takes full responsibility for 
receiving the commands and data packets. 
 
 


===== PAGE 464 =====
Page 464 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
10.1.1.1 Construction and Destruction 
 
 
SPxServerInterface::SPxServerInterface (int serverPort, 
 
 
 
 
 
FILE *log = NULL, 
 
 
 
 
 
int heartbeatPort = 0,   
 
 
 
 
 
const char *heartbeatAddress = NULL, 
const char *serverAddress = NULL ) 
 
Create an instance of the SPxServerInterface class. The object will listen on 
port serverPort for connection requests. The optional log file can be used for 
debugging. The default heartbeat port and heartbeat destination address can be 
overridden with heartbeatPort and heartbeatAddress. 
 
 
SPxServerInterface::SPxServerInterface (FILE *log=0 ) 
 
Create an instance of the SPxServerInterface, but do not use the networking 
of the class. The optional log argument defines a device for output of debugging 
information. 
 
 
SPxServerInterface::~SPxServerInterface() 
 
Delete an instance of SPxServerInterface, including all of the attached display 
windows. 
 
10.1.1.2 Event Handling 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetEventSource(int eventFD) 
 
Add a source of events for processing by the main event loop. The provided 
eventFD is a file descriptor. 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetEventHandler(void (*fn)(void)) 
 
Provide a function to be called when there is input on the file descriptor 
provided by SetEventSource(). 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::CommandLoop(void) 
 
This executes the main command loop. It should be called after a connection is 
established, i.e. after TryConnect returns something other than -1. The 
function waits for command and data on the input ports. After a specified time 
of waiting, set with SetTimeout(), the function will call the users’s background 
processing function, set from SetBackgroundProc(). The CommandLoop() 
function returns when the TCP socket that it is listening on is closed by the 
host, or when the user’s background processing function calls 
StopCommandLoop(). 
 
 


===== PAGE 465 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 465 
void SPxServerInterface::StopCommandLoop(void) 
 
This function is typically called in the user’s background processing function. It 
causes the command loop to exit after the next attempt to read a command or 
patch returns (either through a successful read or a timeout). After the 
command loop returns, the application is responsible for accepting a new 
connection as required. 
 
10.1.1.3 Background Processing 
 
SPxServerInterface::SetBackgroundProc(void (*fn)(void*), void* userData) 
 
Define a function that will be called by the class as periodic intervals between 
processing commands. The interval between calls to this function is set from 
SetTimeout(). The function itself is invoked using the provided userData. 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetTimeout(int seconds, int microseconds) 
 
Set the timeout period in the main processing loop. The period defines the time 
interval that the class will wait for a command or a data request before calling 
the user’s background processing. The value is given in seconds and 
microseconds, although the precision of the timing is system dependent. After 
the user’s processing function returns, the control loop waits for a new 
command or data patches. 
 
10.1.1.4 Network Functions 
 
 
int SPxServerInterface::SocketsOK(void) 
 
Return 0 if the sockets are open and ready. Returns non-zero if the open failed. 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetID(int id) 
 
Set an identifier that appears in the heartbeat and could be used to identify the 
display server providing the information. The value is not used other than as a 
field in the heartbeat. 
 
 
int SPxServerInterface::GetID(void) 
 
Return the current identifier used in the heartbeat. 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetState(int state) 
 
Set the state field in the UDP heartbeat. The state field is not interpreted by the 
class and may be used to indicate current activity. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 466 =====
Page 466 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
int SPxServerInterface::GetState(void)  
 
Return the current state field in the UDP heartbeat. 
 
 
int SPxServerInterface::TryAccept(void) 
 
Try to accept a connection for a command interface. This function is non-
blocking. If no connection request has been made, this function returns -1. 
 
10.1.1.5 Debugging 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SetLogFile(FILE *logFile) 
 
Set a log file to be used for the output of debugging messages. 
 
10.1.1.6 Heartbeat Messages 
 
void SPxServerInterface::EnableHeartbeats(int state) 
 
Enable the transmission of heartbeats. By default, heartbeat transmission is on. 
 
 
void SPxServerInterface::SendHeartbeat(void) 
 
Send a heartbeat. The format of heartbeat is as shown in Table 13.

10.1.1.7 The Command Interface to SPxServerInterface 
 
Commands can be sent to SPxServerInterface from a remote client using the class 
SPxRemoteServer (see “SPx Server Client Interfacing” manual). The commands are 
sent over the TCP connect as ASCII strings. The list of commands is shown in Table 
14.

10.2. Using SPxServerInterface in a server application. 
 
When building a server application (radar video server, tracking server etc.), the main 
loop that the server uses to receive client connections through the SPxRemoteServer 
API (see “SPx Server Client Interfacing” document), is managed by the 
SPxServerInterface object. This class handles the network interfacing, waits for a 
TCP connection then handles commands sent from the connecting client. 
 
A skeleton server application using SPxServerInterface is reproduced below: 
 
    /* Address for sending out heartbeats */ 
    char *heartbeatAddr = “192.168.1.1”; 
 
    /* Port that we will listen on. */ 
    int port = 6501; 
    SPxServerInterface * svrSrcNet = new SPxServerInterface (port,NULL, 
0, heartbeatAddr); 
 
    /* Install a function to be called periodically in CommandLoop.*/ 
    svrSrcNet->SetBackgroundProc(heartbeatSender, svrSrcNet); 
 
    /* Start main server loop */ 
    while (1) 
    { 
/* Send a heartbeat. */ 
 
heartbeatSender(svrSrcNet); 
 
 
/* See if any client is trying to connect to us. */ 
 
if( svrSrcNet->TryAccept() != -1 ) 
 
{                                                                           
 
 
/* Run the command loop to process messages etc. from the 


===== PAGE 468 =====
Page 468 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
 
     * client.  This only returns when the connection is dead. 
 
     */ 
 
    svrSrcNet->CommandLoop(); 
 
 
    /* Once here, the connection is gone/lost/closed. */ 
} 
    } 
 
 


===== PAGE 469 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 469 
11. SPx Programming 
 
11.1. SPx Include Files 
 
The normal SPx include file is: 
 
#include “SPx.h” 
 
This file will in turn include all relevant SPx header files. 
 
If you are developing an application without Windows MFC, then do not use SPx.h. 
Instead use: 
 
#include “SPxNoMFC.h” 
 
Prior to version 1.27.1 of the SPx library, for use of SPx without MFC use the following 
list of include files (in place of #include SPx.h): 
  
#include "SPxLibUtils/SPxLibUtils.h" 
#include "SPxLibData/SPxLibData.h" 
#include "SPxLibSc/SPxLibSc.h" 
#include "SPxLibProc/SPxLibProc.h" 
#include "SPxLibNet/SPxLibNet.h" 
 
11.2. SPx Library 
 
SPx applications can normally be built by including the single top-level library 
SPxLib.lib (for Windows) or libspx.a (for Linux). See the sub-sections below for 
additional libraries that may need to be linked. 
 
11.2.1. 
Windows Linking 
 
The SPx library is SPxLib.lib. There are different version of the SPx library for static 
and dynamic linking with MFC libraries.  
 
If the application is not using MFC, then the library to use is SPxLibNoMFC.lib. It will 
also be necessary to include the Windows libraries winmm.lib, ws2_32.lib and 
shlwapi.lib in this situation. 
 
For ZLIB compression or decompression (for example when using SPxRadarReplay), 
the library zlib.lib (or zlib-static.lib for statically linked builds) is needed. 
 
For licensing, include the library mxst32.lib. 
 
(Refer to “SPx Dongle Installation”, Document number CP-25-110-13 for details of 
licensing.) 
 
 


===== PAGE 470 =====
Page 470 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
11.2.2. 
Linux Linking 
 
The exact libraries required for Linux have some dependency on the Linux build and 
the SPx modules used. The following library set is used for one of the example client 
programs comprising X Windows and SPx scan conversion and display: 
 
-lspx -lc -lstdc++ -lXaw -lXt -lX11 -lrt –lXrender 
 
For ZLIB compression, the library libz.a is needed, therefore add –lz to the above 
linker line. 
 
For licensing, include the libraries libmxlin260.a and libusb.a (refer to “SPx Dongle 
Installation”, document number CP-25-110-13 for details of licensing). 
 
An application using SPxPPIwindowGlx class must link against the libGL library. This 
can be achieved by passing “-lGL” to the linker. If libGL is not linked, undefined 
references to many functions beginning in “gl”, such as “glDeleteTextures” will be 
seen. 
 
11.3. SPx Initialisation 
 
Your application should call the function SPxInit() before any other SPx functions. 
The function is defined as: 
 
int SPxInit(int verbose=0, FILE *logFile=NULL) 
 
The verbose flag may be used to get diagnostic output (which is sent to logFile) 
defining the module versions (verbose = 1) or versions with dates and times (verbose 
= 2). The function returns an error code, which is SPX_NO_ERROR if the initialisation 
completes without error. 
 
Although calling SPxInit() is the preferred initialisation method, this does mean that 
all the SPx modules need to be linked with the application. If this is not possible, the 
initialisation functions of the individual modules may be called directly as: 
 
int SPxLibUtilsInit(int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL) 
 
 
 
// For Utils module 
int SPxLibDataInit( int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL) 
 
 
 
// For Data processing module 
int SPxLibScInit( int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL)  
 
 
 
// For Scan Conversion module 
int SPxLibProcInit( int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL) 
 
 
 
// For Signal processing module 
int SPxLibWinInit( int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL) 
 
 
 
// Only for Windows 
int SPxLibX11Init( int verbose, FILE * logFile = NULL) 
 
 
 
// Only for X Windows 
 
 


===== PAGE 471 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 471 
11.4. SPx Licensing 
 
SPx applications need a run-time licence. The function SPxLicInit should be called 
after SPxInit to initialise the licensing. The function is defined as: 
 
int SPxLicInit(void) 
 
The function returns SPX_NO_ERROR if the dongle or licence key was found correctly. 
Refer to the technical manual “SPx Dongle Installation”, Document number CP-25-
110-13 for details of dongle installation. If the function returns an error, SPx 
capabilities will be limited in time and possibly capabilities. 
 
The following convenience functions are provided to establish if a specific feature is 
licensed in the current software: 
 
int SPxLicIsPpiLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if PPI scan conversion is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsAScanLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if A-Scan is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsBScanLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if B-Scan is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsPlotExtLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if plot extraction is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsTrackingLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if tracking is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsNetSourceLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if receipt of radar video over a network is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsHPxSourceLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if HPx hardware support is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsDistributionLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if distribution over LAN is licensed. 
 
int SPxLicIsRecordingLicensed(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if recording of radar video is licensed. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 472 =====
Page 472 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
11.5. Object Deletion 
 
All SPx objects have a destructor, which takes care of deleting objects. Although 
objects are automatically deleted when the program completes, it may be desirable or 
necessary to delete objects at other times. If this occurs, consideration must be given 
to the order of object deletion, particularly with regard to the RIB, PIM, radar source 
objects and processes. The general order of deletion should be as follows (first listed 
is deleted first): 
 
1. Delete source objects (test generators, scenario generators, network receive, 
radar replay etc.) 
2. Delete RIBs 
3. Delete PIMs 
4. Delete RunProcess objects 
5. Delete FollowWin objects 
6. Delete Scan Conversion objects 
7. Delete Display Objects (A-Scan windows, SPxScDestBitmap, 
SPxScDestDisplayWin etc.) 
 
The global function SPxDeleteAll will delete all SPx objects. A new set of SPx objects 
can be created after calling this function. The function SPxDeleteAllSystem invokes 
SPxDeleteAll and then deletes internally allocated SPx objects. After calling 
SPxDeleteAllSystem, you cannot create any new SPx objects in the current process 
(the only practical application of SPx DeleteAllSystem is to force a clean, but strictly 
unnecessary, tidy-up before existing the application). 
 
 
void SPxDeleteAll(void) 
 
Delete all user SPx objects in the correct sequence. The function also deletes all 
configuration parameters. 
 
 
void SPxDeleteAllSystem(void) 
 
Delete all user and system SPx objects. 
 
 
Note: When developing under Microsoft Windows, there is sometimes confusion 
surrounding memory leaks, reported from Visual Studio, on program exit. 
Visual Studio reports as a “leak” memory that is allocated but not explicitly 
freed on program exit. This memory is automatically reclaimed by the operating 
system, so is not a leak in the normal usage of the term. The application 
programmer can use SPxDeleteAllSystem to explicitly release SPx-allocated 
memory, but this really isn’t necessary and failing to do so does not cause any 
lost system resource. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 473 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 473 
11.6. Error Reporting 
 
SPx functions call an error handler when an error is detected. The function that 
handles the errors can be programmed with SPxSetErrorHandler(): 
 
void SPxSetErrorHandler(SPxErrorHandlerFnV3_t errFn, void *userArg, 
int writeErrorsToFile = FALSE) 
 
The errFn is a pointer to a function that is called when an error is detected. The type 
of the function is defined as: 
 
typedef void (*SPxErrorHandlerFnV3_t)(SPxErrorType errType, 
                                      SPxErrorCode errorCode, 
                                      int arg1, int arg2, 
                                      const char *arg3, 
                                      const char *arg4, 
                                      void *userArg) 
 
The function is called with an error type, an error code, a number of optional 
arguments depending on the context of the error and a user argument that was 
provided when installing the error handler. 
 
Errors are written to the file SPxErrors.txt if no user-defined error handler is 
installed. To continue writing errors to the file after installing an error handler, 
set the writeErrorsToFile parameter to TRUE. 
 
If SPxSetErrorHandler() is called with NULL, the error handler is removed. 
 
By default an error handler, SPxDefaultErrorHandler, is installed, which 
outputs errors to stderr. The standard implementation of this error handler is 
shown below: 
 
void SPxDefaultErrorHandler (SPxErrorType errType, 
                   SPxErrorCode errorCode, 
                   int arg1, int arg2, 
                   const char *arg3, const char *arg4, 
void *userArg) 
{ 
    fprintf(stderr, "SPxError: %s-%d, args %d, %d, '%s', '%s'.\n", 
           SPxGetErrorSeverityString(errType), errorCode, arg1, arg2, 
           (arg3 ? arg3 : "<none>"), 
           (arg4 ? arg4 : "<none>")); 
    return; 
} 
 
The SPxErrorHandlerFnV3_t form of error handler was added to the SPx 
library in version 1.71 to replace the previous SPxErrorHandlerFnV2_t error 
handler. A version of the SPxSetErrorHandler() function taking an 
SPxErrorHandlerFnV2_t is maintained for backwards compatibility. The V3 
error handler supports passing a user argument, whereas the previous error 
handler did not. The SPxErrorHandlerFnV2_t form of error handler was added 
to the SPx library in version 1.43 to replace the original SPxErrorHandlerFn_t 
error handler. A version of the original SPxSetErrorHandler() function taking 
an SPxErrorHandlerFn_t is maintained for backwards compatibility. 
 


===== PAGE 474 =====
Page 474 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
A description of an error can be obtained from an error code using the 
SPxGetErrorString() or SPxGetErrorStringDetail() functions. 
 
 
const char *SPxGetErrorString(SPxErrorCode errorCode) 
 
Convert an error code to a short description of the error. 
 
 
const char *SPxGetErrorStringDetail(SPxErrorCode  
                                    errorCode) 
 
Convert an error code to a detailed description of the error. 
 
11.7. SPx Common Class Functions 
 
Most SPx Objects are derived from the base class SPxObj. The base class provides a 
number of capabilities that are inherited by all derived classes: 
 
 
char * SPxObj::SetName(const char *name) 
 
All SPx objects can be given a name with the SetName() function. The input 
character string is copied and associated with the instance of the class. The 
name pool is global, so the name provided for each object must be unique. If 
the name is not unique, a unique name is automatically created by appending a 
numeric suffix to the provided name. The name actually used is returned. It is 
not necessary to name an object, but with a name, it is possible to manipulate 
the object across a network interface. 
 
 
char *SPxObj::GetName(void) 
 
Returns the current name of an object. Even if an object has not been named, 
GetName() will always return a name, creating one if necessary. 
 
 
int SPxObj::IsNameSet(void) 
 
Returns TRUE if the object has a name assigned and FALSE if there is no name.  
 
 
void SPxObj::SetActive (int activity) 
 
An object can be made inactive by calling this function with 0. The 
implementation of an inactive class is dependent on the class itself. For 
example, an SPx Process will be deactivated if activity = 0. 
 
 
int SPxObj::GetActive (void) 
 
Return the current activity state for an object. 
 
 


===== PAGE 475 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 475 
void SPxObj::AddDeletionCallback (void *fn, void *userData) 
void SPxObj::RemoveDeletionCallbackvoid *fn, void *userData) 
 
These functions register or remove a user-function to be invoked on the 
deletion of the object. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxObj::SetStateFromConfig (void) 
 
This function may be used to update the state of an SPx object using 
parameters defined in the current configuration. The object’s name must have 
been set by calling SetName(), otherwise this function will have no effect. When 
invoked, this function will search the current configuration for any parameters 
prefixed with the object’s name, and will then call the SetParameter() function 
to update the object using the parameter’s value. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxObj::SetStateFromConfigAll  
                                      (const char* incObjList[]=NULL 
                                       const char *excObjList[]=NULL) 
 
This function calls SetStateFromConfig on all SPx objects. If incObjList or 
excObjList is provided, it must be a NULL-terminated list of object names. If 
incObjList is provided, then SetStateFromConfig will only be called on 
objects with names included in the NULL-terminated incObjList list. If 
excObjList is provided, then SetStateFromConfig will NOT be called on 
objects with names included in the NULL-terminated excObjList list. 
 
11.8. Debugging 
 
A useful capability for extracting the objects and relationships from an SPx application 
is SPxShowState(). This is a function that will output currently defined SPx objects, 
including the links to other objects and useful state information. 
 
The function is accessed through one of a number of interfaces: 
 
 
void SPxShowState (void (*fn)(const char*)) 
 
This version takes a pointer to a function that processes a character string. The 
SPxShowState function will then use this function to output its information. 
 
 
void SPxShowState (const char *filename) 
 
This version of the function takes a file name, opens the file for write and then 
outputs the state information to the file. The filename supplied is passed 
directly to the system call fopen(). 
 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 476 =====
Page 476 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
void SPxShowState (FILE *file) 
 
This version of the function takes a FILE pointer, previously created, for 
example through a call to fopen(). The state information is output to the FILE 
object, which remains open when SPxShowState returns. 
 
11.8.1. 
SPxShowState Sample Output 
 
The following SPx code fragment creates a RIB, PIM, scan conversion processes and 
outputs into a Window (for Microsoft Windows) 
 
   // Create the window for radar video, giving it a maximum size of 512 x 
512 
    SPxScDestDisplayWin * spxDisplayWin = new SPxScDestDisplayWin(); 
    spxDisplayWin->Create(0, 512, 512, GetDlgItem(IDC_RADAR_AREA)); 
    spxDisplayWin->SetUpdateInterval(30); 
 
    // Create the RIB. Size is 1 Mb, with memory allocation done by class. 
    SPxRIB * spxRib = new SPxRIB(1024*1024, NULL); 
    // Create the PIM to provide the polar storage. 
    SPxPIM * spxPim = new SPxPIM(spxRib, 2048, 2048, SPX_PIM_RAN_PEAK,  
 
 
 
 
    SPX_PIM_AZI_PEAK, SPX_PIM_OUTPUT(1)); 
 
    // Create the scan converter, telling it which display window to use 
    SPxScSourceLocal *spxSc = new SPxScSourceLocal(spxDisplayWin); 
    // Enable real-time fading with 200*10 = 2000ms fade time. 
    spxSc->SetFade(SPX_RADAR_FADE_REAL_TIME, 200); 
 
    // Initialise the Processing library. 
    SPxLibProcInit(); 
 
    // Create the scan conversion process object to link the pim to the 
scan converter 
    SPxRunProcess *spxScProcess = new SPxRunProcess(SPxProScanConv, NULL,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
spxPim, spxSc); 
 
    // Locate the radar window at the position of the Window defined by 
IDC_RADAR_AREA 
    SPxScFollowWin *winFollow = new SPxScFollowWin(spxSc, 
GetDlgItem(IDC_RADAR_AREA)); 
 
    // Create a source of data and set test picture 33, with a period of 2 
seconds. 
    SPxTestGenerator *spxTestGen = new SPxTestGenerator(spxRib, 2048,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.0, (int)(2048/2.0), 33, 
0); 
 
    // Turn on the test generator. 
    spxTestGen->Enable(1); 
 
    SPxShowState("spxdump.txt"); 
 
The final line of the code is a call to SPxShowState, specifying the name of the file. 
After execution of the above code, the file “spxdump.txt” contains the following 
information:

11.9. Configuration Parameters 
 
A set of functions are available to read parameters from a file. These parameters are 
not used by the SPx class library, but instead may be used by application software or 
servers that need to read initial values from a file. 
 
11.9.1. 
Reading Parameters from Configuration Files 
 
int SPxLoadConfig(char *fileName)  
 
Loads a set of configuration data from the specified fileName. The file name 
may be a fully-qualified name or relative to the current directory of the 
program execution. If the file is not found in that location, the location 
$(SPX)/Config/fileName is searched, where $(SPX) is the value of the 
environment variable SPX. 
 


===== PAGE 478 =====
Page 478 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The file is assumed to contain a set of parameter value assignments, for 
example: 
 
# Parameter File 
DisplayType = 0 
ChartName = “mychart” 
ThresholdLevel = 20 
MaximumRange = 120.5 
# End of file 
 
Lines beginning with a ‘#’ are comments. 
 
Other lines are of the form parameter = value, where value is either an integer, a 
real number or a quote string. 
 
The name of the parameter is not case sensitive. 
 
A line of the form include “filename” may be used to include the contents of 
another file. The specified filename has the same format as supplied to 
SPxLoadConfig(). For example: 
 
# Parameter File 
DisplayType = 1 
ChartName = “mychart2” 
include “extraParameters” 
 
assigns two parameters and then reads the file extraParameters for additional 
parameters. 
 
 
int SPxTestP(const char *parameter)  
 
This function test to see if a value is assigned to the input parameter. It returns 
1 if the parameter has been the subject of an assignment, and 0 otherwise. 
 
 
int SPxGetInt(const char *name)  
 
This function returns the current integer value of a parameter. The caller must 
understand the type of the parameter and only call SPxGetInt if the parameter 
is known to be an integer (or known to be a Real, but the truncation to integer 
is acceptable). Input values may be decimal or hex values if preceeded by 0x 
(for example “0xff”). 
 
 
int SPxGetSafeInt(const char *name, int safeValue)  
 
This function returns the current integer value of a parameter. The caller must 
understand the type of the parameter and only call SPxGetInt if the parameter 
is known to be an integer (or known to be a Real, but the truncation to integer 
is acceptable). If the specified parameter name does not exist, the function 
returns safeValue. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 479 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 479 
double SPxGetReal(const char *name) 
 
This function returns the current real value of a parameter. The caller must 
understand the type of the parameter and only call SPxGetReal if the 
parameter is known to be a real or an integer. If the specified parameter name 
does not exist, the function returns safeValue. Input values may be decimal or 
hex values if preceeded by 0x (for example “0xff”). 
 
 
double SPxGetSafeReal(const char *name, double safeValue) 
 
This function returns the current real value of a parameter. The caller must 
understand the type of the parameter and only call SPxGetReal if the 
parameter is known to be a real or an integer. If the specified parameter name 
does not exist, the function returns safeValue. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxGetUint(const char *name) 
 
This function returns the current value of a parameter as an unsigned integer. 
 
 
unsigned int SPxGetSafeUint(const char *name, unsigned int safeValue) 
 
This function returns the current value of a parameter as an unsigned integer. 
If a parameter of the specified name does not exist, the function returns 
safeValue. 
 
 
char* SPxGetString( const char *name, int replaceCtrlChars=FALSE) 
 
This function returns the current string value of a parameter. A string 
representation can be returned for any type of parameter. The memory for the 
string is managed by the routine and should not be freed by the caller. If the 
specified parameter does not exist, NULL is returned. If replaceCtrlChars is 
TRUE then control character sequences, such as "\t", will be replaced with their 
literal equivalents in the returned string. 
 
 
char* SPxGetSafeString(const char *name, char *safeValue, 
                       const char *prefix=NULL,  
                       const char *suffix=NULL, 
                       int replaceCtrlChars=FALSE) 
 
This function returns the current string value of a parameter. A string 
representation can be returned for any type of parameter. The memory for the 
string is managed by the routine and should not be freed by the caller. If the 
specified parameter does not exist, the function returns safeValue. If prefix 
or suffix is supplied then it will be prepended/appended to name before 
searching for the parameter. If replaceCtrlChars is TRUE then control 
character sequences, such as "\t", will be replaced with their literal equivalents 
in the returned string. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 480 =====
Page 480 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
char* SPxGetSafeString(char *name, char *safeValue)  
 
This function returns the current string value of a parameter. A string 
representation can be returned for any type of parameter. The memory for the 
string is managed by the routine and should not be freed by the caller. If the 
specified parameter name does not exist, the function returns safeValue. 
 
 
int SPxSetInt(char *name, int value) 
 
This function sets the value of the named parameter to be the specified integer.  
 
 
int SPxSetReal(char *name, double value, int precision=-1)  
 
This function sets the value of the named parameter to be the specified real. If 
precision is greater than or equal to zero, then the real value will be stored 
with precision decimal places. 
 
 
int SPxSetUint(char *name, unsigned int value) 
 
This function sets the value of the named parameter to the specified unsigned 
int value. 
 
 
int SPxSetString(char *name, char *value)  
 
This function sets the value of the named parameter to the specified string. 
 
11.10. Radar Database 
 
Information about radars, including position and operating parameters, is contained in 
a configuration file that a number of SPx classes can read through the class 
SPxRadarDatabase. 
 
11.10.1. SPxRadarDatabase class 
 
SPxRadarDatabase::SPxRadarDatabase(void) 
 
 
Creates a new instance of a radar database. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxRadarDatabase::Open(const char *fileName) 
 
Reads the contents of the specified file name into the radar database. The file 
contains lines of the form: 
 
‘name’ x y id period prf range beamwidth 
 
Where  
‘name’ is a string defining the name of the radar,  


===== PAGE 481 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 481 
x is the world x location, 
y is the world y location, 
id is an integer identification code, 
period is the rotation period in seconds, 
prf is the nominal operating PRF in pulses per second, 
range is the nominal operating range in metres, 
beamwidth is the radar beam width in degrees. 
 
If the first character in the line is ‘#’, the line is assumed to be a comment. 
A sample file is: 
 
# Radar database file 
'SPQ-100D' 500 500 30 2.0 500 100.0 2.0 
'TPZ-99DZ' 250 500 31 2.0 300 250.0 3.5 
# End of file 
 
 
SPxRadar * SPxRadarDatabase::FindRadarByName(const char *name) 
 
Find a radar by name and return an SPxRadar pointer that represents the 
radar. The specified name is the one defined in the radar database file. 
 
 
SPxRadar * SPxRadarDatabase::FindRadarByID(int id) 
 
Find a radar by ID and return a SPxRadar pointer that represents the radar. The 
specified ID is the one defined in the radar database file. 
 
 
void SPxRadarDatabase::AddEntry(const char *name, double x,  
 
 
 
 
 
  double y, int id, 
 
 
 
 
  
  double period, double prf,  
 
 
 
 
 
  double range, double beamWidth) 
 
Add a new entry into the radar database. The form of the entry is consistent 
with lines added from a file in the Open member function. 
 
 
SPxRadarDatabase::SPxRadarDatabase(void) 
 
Delete a radar database. 
 
11.10.2. SPxRadar Class 
 
The SPxRadar class holds information on a specific radar. 
 
SPxRadar::SPxRadar(const char *name, double x, double y, int id,  
 
 
       double period, double prf, double range,  
 
 
       double beamWidth);  
 
Creates a new SPxRadar object. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 482 =====
Page 482 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
char * SPxRadar::GetName(void) 
 
Returns the name of an SPxRadar object 
 
 
void SPxRadar::SetRadarName(char * radarName) 
 
Sets a new name for the radar. Take care not to confuse this function with 
SetName(), which is the mechanism for naming an SPx instance. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetRange(void) 
 
Returns the maximum range of the radar in metres. 
 
 
void SPxRadar::SetRange(double newRange) 
 
Set a new range in metres for a radar object. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetX(void) 
 
Get the current X world position of the radar. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetY(void) 
 
Get the current Y world position of the radar. 
 
 
void SPxRadar::SetPosition(double x, double y) 
 
Set a new x, y position for the radar. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetBeamwidth(void) 
 
Get the beam width of the radar. 
 
 
void SPxRadar::SetBeamwidth(double beamwidth) 
 
Set a new beamwidth for the radar object. 
 
 
int SPxRadar::GetID(void) 
 
Get the ID of the radar. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetPeriod(void) 
 
Get the period of the radar. 
 
 


===== PAGE 483 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 483 
void SPxRadar::SetPeriod(double period) 
 
Set a new period for the radar. 
 
 
double SPxRadar::GetPrf(void) 
 
Get the PRF of the radar. 
 
 
void SPxRadar::SetPrf(double prf) 
 
Set a new PRF for the radar. 
 
11.11. SPx Time Functions 
 
The section describes a set of utility functions for management of time. These are 
used widely within the SPx library and are also available to the application 
programmer. The classes are a wrapper to the native Linux and Windows time 
functions and provide a convenient cross-platform interface. 
 
The basic SPxTime_t structure represents an epoch time (time since 1st January 1970) 
as follows: 
 
typedef struct SPxTime_tag { 
    UINT32 secs;  
 
/* Whole seconds since epoch */ 
    UINT32 usecs;  
 
/* Microseconds to add */ 
} SPxTime_t; 
 
The following functions are provided in the SPx library: 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxTimeSleepMsecs(unsigned int msecs) 
 
Implement a sleep of the specified number of milliseconds. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxTimeGetTickerMsecs(void) 
 
Get a ticker count since a refreence point, which is unspecified. The value 
returned from this function may be supplied to a subsequent SPxTimeGetDiff 
to get the time difference between two points. 
 
 
UINT32 SPxTimeGetDiff(UINT32 firstMsecs, UINT32 secondMsecs) 
 
Compute and return the time difference between the first time and the second 
time. The time difference is returned in milliseconds.  
 
 
int SPxTimeGetEpoch(SPxTime_t *now) 
 
Get the time now in the SPxTime_t pointer provided as input. An error code is 
returned. 
 
 


===== PAGE 484 =====
Page 484 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
double SPxTimeGetDiffEpoch(const SPxTime_t *first, 
 
 
 
 
   const SPxTime_t *second) 
 
Calculate the difference between the first and second epoch times and return 
the difference in seconds. 
 
 
int SPxTimeCopyEpoch(SPxTime_t *dest, const SPxTime_t *src) 
 
Copy the src time into the dest time. An error code is returned. 
 
 
int SPxTimeAddEpoch(SPxTime_t *dest, int usecs); 
 
Add the specified number of microseconds, usecs, to the destination time, 
which is modified. An error code is returned. 
 
11.12. SPx Plugins 
 
SPx supports the use of plugins.  Plugins are pre-compiled shared object files that 
may be loaded at runtime and provide additional functions and C++ classes to an 
application.  A plugin generally provides specialised or user-defined processing that is 
not supported in the standard SPx library. 
 
On Linux, a plugin is a shared object file (*.so).  On Windows, a plugin is a dynamic-
link library file (*.dll).  The SPx library provides C++ classes from which a plugin may 
be derived and a utility class called SPxPluginHandler to load and interface with 
plugins. 
 
As well as general purpose plugins, SPx supports a specialised type of plugin for 
performing radar processing referred to as a ‘process plugin’.  Process plugins have a 
well-defined interface and are used to encapsulate a user-defined processing module.  
A process plugin may be used to add user-defined processing to an application such 
as SPxServer, without the need to modify or rebuild the application itself. 
 
Plugins may contain C++ classes used to create plugin objects.  All plugin classes are 
derived from the SPxPlugin base class.  Process plugin classes are derived from the 
SPxPluginProcess class, which itself is derived from SPxPlugin.   
 
To use a general purpose plugin class, an application must have access to the plugin’s 
public header file as this provides details of the classes and methods supported by the 
plugin.  This is analogous to using a dynamically linked library.  For an application to 
use a process plugin class, the application only requires the SPxPluginProcess base 
class header file, which defines a common interface to all plugin process classes. 
 
11.12.1. When to Use Plugins 
 
If you are developing an SPx-based server or client application, there is no need to 
use plugins. You can always embed the code in your application, including standard 
SPx Processing or custom processing that you provide. The normal mechanisms for 
supporting user-defined processing support this. The plugin concept allows you to 
write a client or server application that is extensible (perhaps by a third party, for 


===== PAGE 485 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 485 
example), without the need to recompile or relink your application. Your application 
code can be configured to read one or more plugin modules that get dynamically 
loaded. 
 
The additional role of a plugin is to expand the capabilities of a standard Cambridge 
Pixel application. For example, SPx Server is shipped as a pre-built executable, but 
since it supports plugins, it is possible to expand its processing options without 
changing the standard executable. 
 
 
11.12.2. SPxPluginHandler Class 
 
The SPxPluginHandler class is used to load a plugin, create and destroy plugin 
objects, and access public functions and variables contained within the plugin. 
 
 
SPxPluginHandler::SPxPluginHandler(void) 
 
Create a new instance of the plugin handler. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginHandler::Open(const char *name) 
 
Load a plugin. The plugin file name must be defined without a file extension 
(.so or .dll).  If the filename is relative then the current working directory is 
checked first followed by the $(SPX)/SPxPlugins directory.  If the filename is 
absolute (starts with either ‘/’, ‘\’ or ‘.’) then the filename is used as defined.  If 
the plugin file cannot be opened an error is returned. 
 
The version of the SPx library used to compile the plugin is checked against the 
current version of the SPx library.  If the versions differ then an error is 
returned. 
 
The same plugin may be opened more than once by multiple instances of 
SPxPluginHandler, however the plugin is only loaded into memory once and a 
reference count maintained on the number of times it has been opened.  The 
plugin is only deallocated once it has been closed as many times as it has been 
successfully opened.  Care should be taken as all SPxPluginHandler objects 
have access to the same static functions and variables within the plugin. 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginHandler::Close() 
 
Close the current plugin. If the plugin has been used to create any plugin 
objects then all objects created by the plugin handler are destroyed.  An open 
plugin file is automatically closed when a plugin handler is destroyed.  As 
described for Open(), a plugin is only loaded once and not deallocated until it 
has been closed as many times as it has been successfully opened. 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 486 =====
Page 486 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxPlugin *SPxPluginHandler::CreateObj(const char *className,  
                                       SPxErrorCode *errRtn) 
 
This function is used to create a plugin object using the currently loaded plugin.  
A plugin may define multiple C++ classes used to create plugin objects.  Once 
created, a plugin object may be used in the same way as a conventionally 
created object.  The only difference is that the object must not be destroyed 
using the C++ ‘delete’ operator.  A plugin object may only be destroyed using 
the DestroyObj() function. 
 
If the address of a variable is passed to errRtn then it is set to SPX_NO_ERROR 
on success or an error code on failure.  Set errRtn to NULL if not required. 
 
The className argument is used to pass a string defining the name of the 
plugin class that will be used to create the object.  If the class name is not 
recognised then NULL is returned and an optional error code is returned via 
errRtn. 
If successful, a pointer is returned to the plugin object.  The pointer may be 
cast to the required plugin object type.  For generic plugin objects, the pointer 
is cast to a class defined in the plugin’s public header file.  For process plugin 
objects, the pointer is cast to SPxPluginProcess, as shown in the following 
example: 
 
 
SPxErrorCode errorCode = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
SPxPluginProcess *obj; 
 
obj = (SPxPluginProcess *) pluginHdlr->CreateObj(“SPxPluginProThresh”, 
                                                &errorCode); 
 
if (obj == NULL) 
{ 
 
/* Handle error */ 
 
printf(“Failed to create object, err = %d\n”, errorCode); 
} 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginHandler::DestroyObj(SPxPlugin *obj) 
 
Destroy a plugin object created using CreateObj(). This function must be used 
in place of the C++ ‘delete’ operator. Failure to do so will cause undefined 
behaviour. 
 
 
void *SPxPluginHandler::LoadSymbol(const char *sym, SPxErrorCode *errRtn) 
 
This function provides raw access to symbols within a plugin. In general, most 
plugins will be used to create plugin objects and therefore there is no need to 
access symbols directly, however this function is included for completeness. 
 
Symbols may be function or variable names with C code linkage. If using C++ 
then the plugin function or variable definition must be prefixed with ‘extern 
“C”’. Due to the way C++ symbols are modified by the compiler (often referred 
to as C++ name mangling) it is not possible to access C++ symbols reliably 
using this function. 
 


===== PAGE 487 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 487 
If the address of a variable is passed to errRtn then it is set to SPX_NO_ERROR 
on success or an error code on failure. Set errRtn to NULL if not required. 
 
The name of the symbol to load is defined by the sym argument. If successful, a 
pointer to the symbol is returned, otherwise NULL is returned. An optional error 
code may be returned via errRtn. The address returned by this function should 
be cast to a suitable variable pointer or function pointer type. 
 
 
int SPxPluginHandler::TestSymbol(const char *sym) 
 
This function may be used to check if a plugin symbol exists before an attempt 
is made to load it. If the symbol exists then this function returns TRUE, 
otherwise it returns FALSE. This function is useful as unlike LoadSymbol(), no 
error is reported by the SPx library if the symbol does not exist. 
 
 
11.12.3. Plugin Interface Functions 
 
Plugins must define a number of static interface functions.  The interface functions 
must have C linkage and are used by the SPxPluginHandler class to interface with 
the plugin. 
 
The following functions must be provided by all plugins: 
 
SPxPluginOpen() 
SPxPluginClose() 
 
The following functions are only required if a plugin provides C++ classes: 
 
SPxPluginCreateObj_<className>() 
SPxPluginDestroyObj() 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginOpen(const SPxLibInfo_t *libInfo) 
 
All plugins must define a function called SPxPluginOpen() with C linkage, as 
shown in the following example:  
 
extern “C” SPxErrorCode SPxPluginOpen(const SPxLibInfo_t *libInfo) 
{ 
 
SPxErrorCode err = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
 
 
/* Initialise the plugin and return on error */ 
 
if ((err = SPxPluginInit(libInfo)) != SPX_NO_ERROR) 
{ 
 
return err; 
} 
 
/* Do custom initialisation here if required */ 
 
return err; 
} 
 


===== PAGE 488 =====
Page 488 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The SPxPluginOpen() function is called each time a plugin is opened.  This 
function must call the following SPx library function using the provided libInfo 
pointer: 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginInit(const SPxLibInfo_t *libInfo) 
 
If SPxPluginInit() fails, then the error code must be returned by 
SPxPluginOpen().  A plugin is statically linked with the SPx library and 
therefore has its own private version of the SPxLibrary which is separate from 
the application’s SPx library (i.e. the code that is using the plugin).  The call to 
SPxPluginInit() ensures that any static data within the SPx library, e.g. the 
installed error handler, is synchronised with the application’s SPx library.  It 
also checks that the plugin has been built using the same version of the SPx 
library as the application. 
 
The SPxPluginOpen() function should be used to perform any static 
initialisation required by the plugin.  It should also check to ensure that the 
plugin is supported, for example all resources are available, and return a 
suitable error code if any problems are encountered.  Be aware that this 
function may be called more than once if the plugin is opened multiple times.  
Any code within SPxPluginOpen() that may only be executed once should be 
protected using a static variable, as shown in the following example: 
 
static int OpenCount = 0; 
 
. . . 
 
if (OpenCount == 0) 
{ 
 
/* Do one-off initialisation operations here */ 
}  
 
/* Increment open count */ 
OpenCount++; 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginClose(void) 
 
A plugin must define a function called SPxPluginClose() with C linkage, as 
shown in the following example:  
 
extern “C” SPxErrorCode SPxPluginClose(void) 
{ 
 
SPxErrorCode err = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
 
/* Do custom shutdown here if required */ 
 
return err; 
} 
 
The SPxPluginClose() function is called each time a plugin is closed and is 
used to perform any custom shutdown operations, such as freeing resources 
used by the plugin.  If the plugin has been opened multiple times then the 
plugin is only deallocated when it has been closed as many times as it has been 
opened.  Any one-off operations may be performed the last time the plugin is 
closed using the same static OpenCount variable described for 
SPxPluginOpen(). 
 
 


===== PAGE 489 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 489 
/* Decrement open count */ 
OpenCount--; 
 
if (OpenCount == 0) 
{ 
 
/* Do one-off shutdown operations here */ 
}  
 
 
<className> *SPxPluginCreateObj_<className>(void) 
 
Any plugin that that provides C++ classes must define this function.  This 
function must be defined for each class supported by the plugin.  Each function 
must have C linkage. 
The SPx library provides a macro used to generate the source code for this 
function.  The following macro should be called in the plugin source file, ideally 
just before the first class constructor is defined.  It must be called for each 
different class supported by the plugin, in each case replace <className> with 
the name of the class. 
 
SPX_PLUGIN_CREATE_OBJECT_FUNCTION(<className>); 
 
 
void SPxPluginDestroyObj(SPxPlugin *obj) 
 
Any plugin that that provides C++ classes must define this function. This 
function must be defined once only, irrespective of the number of classes 
supported by the plugin. The function must have C linkage. 
 
The SPx library provides a macro used to generate the source code for this 
function. The following macro should be called in the plugin source file. It must 
be called once only and takes no arguments. 
 
SPX_PLUGIN_DESTROY_OBJECT_FUNCTION(); 
 
 
11.12.4. Plugin Classes 
 
A plugin may provide one or more C++ classes. Any plugin that provides classes must 
define the static object create and destroy functions in the plugin source, as described 
in section 11.12.3. 
 
11.12.4.1 
Generic Plugin Classes 
 
General purpose plugins may or may not provide C++ class definitions.  All generic 
plugin classes must be derived from SPxPlugin and must be defined in a public 
header file to enable the plugin to be used by an application.  Apart from the need to 
provide a constructor and destructor for each class, there are no mandatory functions 
that must be provided by a generic plugin class.  The only caveat is that, with 
exception of the constructor, all public class functions must be declared as virtual in 
the plugin header file to ensure that an application using the header file can be built 
without link errors.   
 
 


===== PAGE 490 =====
Page 490 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
The following code shows an example derived class in a plugin header file: 
 
/* SPx library headers */ 
#include <SPx.h> 
 
/* Define the plugin class */ 
class MyPlugin : public SPxPlugin 
{ 
Public: 
MyPlugin(void); 
 
virtual ~MyPlugin(void); 
 
virtual int MyFunc(int arg1, int arg2); 
 
}; /* MyPlugin */ 
 
11.12.4.2 
Process Plugin Classes 
 
A process plugin provides C++ classes used to perform user-defined radar processing.  
A process plugin may be used to add custom processing to SPxServer, for example, 
without modifying or rebuilding the application.  Details of the process plugin classes 
are simply defined in the server’s configuration file. 
 
A process plugin must provide at least one class derived from SPxPluginProcess.  
The plugin may have a public header file but this is not a requirement. 
 
Each plugin process class must create an SPxProcess object.  This object is used to 
receive spokes of radar data from a PIM and invoke a user-defined processing 
function.  The processing function is installed when the SPxProcess object is created 
and is used to process the radar data. For further details on the SPxProcess class 
refer to Section 6.4. 
 
Some additional points to be aware of when using an SPxProcess within a plugin: 
 
 
The name assigned to the SPxProcess object (passed as an argument to the 
constructor) will be displayed in the process list in the SPxServer GUI.  If 
longer than 16 characters then the name will be truncated. 
 
 
The name assigned to any process parameters will be displayed in the 
SPxServer process control dialog.  The length of the parameter name is not 
restricted but should be limited to 16 characters. 
 
Each plugin process class must override the following function in the 
SPxPluginProcess base class: 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginProcess::Init(const char *name) 
 
This function is used to create and initialise an SPxProcess object. 
 
The name argument defines the instance name of the plugin object.  The object 
may use the instance name to identify any SPx parameters associated with the 
object as they will be prefixed with the instance name. 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 491 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 491 
The SPxPluginProcess base class provides the following function used to 
retrieve the SPxProcess object handle: 
 
SPxProcess *SPxPluginProcess::GetProcess(void) 
 
11.12.4.3 
  Standard Process Plugin 
 
A pre-built process plugin, SPxPluginProStd, is included in the standard release tree, 
containing all the standard linear SPx processes. By using this plugin, it is simple to 
add standard processes to the process chain of the RDC. The RDC supports up to 20 
plugin processes per channel. See the “Radar Display Coprocess User Manual” for 
further details. 
 
The following processes are supported by the plugin: 
 
Process Name 
Plugin Class 
SPxProAdaptiveNoiseRemoval 
SPxPluginProAdaptiveNoiseRemoval 
SPxProAverage 
SPxPluginProAverage 
SPxProAziSpokes 
SPxPluginProAziSpokes 
SPxProBoost 
SPxPluginProBoost 
SPxProDebug 
SPxPluginProDebug

===== PAGE 492 =====
Page 492 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
11.12.4.4 
 Example of Process Plugin 
 
The best way to understand how a process plugin works is to look at an example.  An 
example plugin called SPxPluginProThresh is included in the SPx library Examples 
directory. This code also provides a good starting point when creating a new process 
plugin. A simplified version of the code is included in this section. 
 
 
SPxPluginProThresh.h: 
  
/* SPx header files */ 
#include <SPx.h> 
 
/* 
 * Define the class itself which is derived from SPxPluginProcess. 
 */ 
class SPxPluginProThresh : public SPxPluginProcess 
{ 
public: 
 
    /* Constructor */ 
    SPxPluginProThresh(void); 
 
    /* Destructor */ 
    ~SPxPluginProThresh(void); 
     
    /* This function overrides base class initialise function */ 
    SPxErrorCode Init(const char *name); 
 
}; /* SPxPluginProThresh */ 
 
 
 
 


===== PAGE 493 =====
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Page 493 
SPxPluginProThresh.cpp: 
 
/* Local headers */ 
#include "SPxPluginProThresh.h" 
 
/* The actual function that implements our process. */ 
static void applyThreshold(SPxRunProcess *rp, SPxReturn *r, 
                           unsigned firstAzi, unsigned size); 
 
/*  
 * Plugin object interface functions  
 */ 
 
/* Include this macro for each class defined in this plugin */ 
SPX_PLUGIN_CREATE_OBJECT_FUNCTION(SPxPluginProThresh) 
 
/* Include this macro once only */ 
SPX_PLUGIN_DESTROY_OBJECT_FUNCTION() 
 
 
extern "C" SPxErrorCode SPxPluginOpen(const SPxLibInfo_t *libInfo) 
{    
    SPxErrorCode err = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
 
    /* Initialise the plugin and return on error */ 
    if ((err = SPxPluginInit(libInfo)) != SPX_NO_ERROR) 
    { 
        return err; 
    } 
 
    /* Do custom initialisation here if required */ 
 
    return err; 
 
} /* SPxPluginExample::SPxPluginOpen */ 
 
 
extern "C" SPxErrorCode SPxPluginClose(void) 
{ 
    SPxErrorCode err = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
 
    /* Do custom shutdown here if required */ 
 
    return err; 
 
} /* SPxPluginExample::SPxPluginClose */ 
 
 
SPxPluginProThresh::SPxPluginProThresh(void) 
{ 
    /* Initialise variables (m_process is defined in the base class) */ 
    m_process = NULL; 
 
} /* SPxPluginProThresh::SPxPluginProThresh */ 
 
 
SPxPluginProThresh::~SPxPluginProThresh(void) 
{ 
    /* Destroy the process */ 
    delete m_process; 
 
} /* SPxPluginProThresh::~SPxPluginProThresh */ 
 
 
SPxErrorCode SPxPluginProThresh::Init(const char *name) 


===== PAGE 494 =====
Page 494 
SPxLib Programming Manual 
Cambridge Pixel Ltd. 
{ 
    SPxErrorCode err = SPX_NO_ERROR; 
    char param[256]; 
 
    /*  
     * Create the SPx process and register processing callback. 
     * The name assigned to the SPxProcess is the name that 
     * will be displayed in the application's process list. 
     * The name should be restricted to a maximum of 16 characters. 
     */ 
    m_process = new SPxProcess("Threshold Plugin", applyThreshold); 
 
    /*  
     * Read default threshold from a global parameter (usually 
     * read from the application's config file). Plugin parameters 
     * are prefixed with the plugin instance name. For example if 
     * the instance name of this plugin is "ProThreshPlugin" then 
     * the name of the parameter that defines the default threshold 
     * value for this instance will be "ProThreshPluginThreshold". 
     * If the parameter is not defined then we default to a threshold 
     * value of 32. 
     */ 
    SPxSnprintf(param, sizeof(param), "%s%s", name, "Threshold"); 
    int threshVal = SPxGetSafeInt(param, 32); 
 
    /*  
     * Add process parameters and set default values. Process  
     * parameters enable a user to change the process attributes 
     * using the application's GUI. 
     */  
    m_process->AddParameterI("Threshold", threshVal); 
 
    return err; 
 
} /* SPxPluginProThresh::Init */ 
 
static void applyThreshold(SPxRunProcess *rp, SPxReturn *r, 
                           unsigned firstAzi, unsigned size) 
{ 
 
/* Do processing */ 
 
    /* Threshold. Data less than or equal to the threshold value is 
     * replaced by zero. 
     */ 
 
SPxPIM *inPIM  = rp->GetPIMA(); 
 
SPxPIM *outPIM = rp->GetPIMB(); 
    int threshold = rp->GetParamValueI("Threshold");   
    for (unsigned  i = firstAzi; i < (firstAzi + size) ; i++) 
    { 
 
/* Get the azimuth number, allowing for crossings of North. */ 
 
int azimuth = i % inPIM->GetAzimuthDim(); 
 
SPxReturn * rtn = inPIM->GetStoreMemory()[azimuth]; 
 
unsigned char *inPtr = rtn->data; 
 
unsigned char *outPtr = outPIM->GetStoreMemory()[azimuth]; 
 
/* Threshold all samples in the return. */ 
 
for (int j = 0 ; j < rtn->header.thisLength ;  
 
 
 
 
j++, inPtr ++, outPtr++) 
 
{ 
 
    if (*inPtr <= threshold) 
 
 
 
*outPtr = 0; 
 
    else 
 
 
 
*outPtr = 0xff; 
 
} 
    } 
} /* applyThreshold */
